sections.title: Format of the Story
sections.teaser: The format of the story of Winifred Eloise Coss’ Family Tree merges important historical events and notes within the family history of Winifred Coss’ relativ…

blocks.text: The format of the story of Winifred Eloise Coss’ Family Tree merges important historical events and notes within the family history of Winifred Coss’ relatives.  Winifred’s family tree is separated into two branches.  The people covered in the first branch of Winifred Coss’ Family are from England starting with George Alcock who was born in 1581 in Cambridge, Cambridgeshire, England.  The first branch ends with the marriage of Warren Coss and Edith Powers.  The early members of the Power’s family arrive in the ships of the Winthrop Fleet in 1630 in the new Massachusetts Bay Colony.  A second branch of Winifred Eloise Coss’ Family Tree begin in 1575 when Louis Hébert was born in Paris, Île-de-France and ends when Joseph Warren Coss marries Mary Goodwater.  The early members of the Goodwater family help establish the Québec settlement in Canada, New France.  Many other direct family members will be covered as other marriages occur.
The early members of the family tree generally only include individuals who traveled to or were born in North America.
All starting tree individuals were born in England, Scotland, Ireland, France or Native North America.
Female members in the family tree are identified by their maiden name until their marriage.  After marriage their maiden name is include in parenthesis ( ).
Names of the direct family members are presented in Bold Type in the story.
Most locations in family story are identified with their current location names so they can be easily found on an Internet map applications.

Information on individuals’ birth, marriage and death are not always available and a number have separate documents with conflicting dates and locations on official records.  Typed official records before 1874 have to be retyped records from handwritten records since the typewriter was not available before that date.  Some surviving hand written records are hard to read and list many individuals under different spelling of their names.  When records are not available for known events, the dates are estimated.  When this occurs, the events are listed as “about”, or “before”, “after” and “late”.

Information on a family members’ death is only included when their death is unusual and adds to the family history.  The dates of individual deaths are included in the Index where birth and death are shown for each family tree member.
Fires in building, churches and towns, wars and Native American attacks have all destroyed family historical records that in many cases cannot found or replaced.

Information on each family tree member varies.  Many are barely known by a single even or fact.  Others are famous and have had books written about them and the historical events they impacted.  Some are so famous they now have their own Internet Web Pages written about them and their life exploits.  Most, individuals however, are somewhere in between.

Many of early books on family histories are available that were written closer to the time the events occurred, when memories were fresh and legible hand written records were more available.  When their information is available it is used.

Footnotes have not been used to keep the document uncluttered.  Information on sources is available in the Family Tree information in Family Tree Maker.  A list of the important historical references is included at the end of this document.

sections.title: Early History of England, France and the Protestant Reformation
sections.teaser: The story of Edith Powers’ family history begins in England during the Tudor period.  In 1485, Henry Tudor, the Earl of Richmond, landed in west Wales, forme…

blocks.text: The story of Edith Powers’ family history begins in England during the Tudor period.  In 1485, Henry Tudor, the Earl of Richmond, landed in west Wales, formed an army and marched into England.  At the “Battle of Bosworth Field”, in Leicestershire, he defeated King Richard III’s forces and King Richard III was killed.  The dead King’s crown was picked up and placed on the head of Henry Tudor making him King Henry VII of England.  In 1509, King Henry VII died and his eighteen-year-old son Henry became the famous King Henry VIII (Henry the 8th) of England.

In 1517, Martin Luther, a Catholic monk in Germany, wrote a document challenging the Catholic Church’s practice of selling “indulgences”.  The indulgences process involved Catholic priests selling a piece of paper to an individual that forgave their sins, which then allowed them go the haven after death, as a church money-making scheme.  Martin Luther, could read Latin, and had read the Christian Bible many times and had not found any mention of the need to buy an indulgences piece of paper to go the heaven.  The Bible states Jesus preached: “One must repent (sincere regret for wrongdoing) so the Holy Spirit can dwell in a person to prepare one for heaven after death.”

In 1521, Pope Leo X and the Holy Roman Emperor Charles V ordered Martin Luther’s writings to be burned.  Martin Luther went into hiding where he translated the New Testament of the Bible from Latin into German, which was then printed on the new Johannes Gutenberg printing press, allowing common German people to read the bible themselves for the first time.  Martin Luther’s writings were responsible for sparking the “Protestant Reformation”.  The Protestant Reformation was a reform movement that swept across Europe, creating a new branch of Christianity called “Protestantism” to refer to the many religious groups that separated from the Roman Catholic Church due to differences in religious doctrine (beliefs).  The Protestant reforms challenged Catholic Church’s authority based on its perceived errors, biases and disagreements with the teachings of Jesus.
In 1534, French explorer Jacques Cartier sailed up the large St. Lawrence River and claimed the area in North America for France

In 1536, because of England’s King Henry VIII’s desire to remarry, which the Pope in Rome would not approve, King Henry VIII left the Catholic Church and create a new “Church of England”.  The king’s actions started a conflict between the new Protestant Anglican Church of England and the older Catholic Church of Rome.  The religious split in England along with the Protestant Reformation that was occurring at the same time in Germany, started wars in the name of religion that went on for hundreds of years.

Between 1536 and 1541, King Henry VIII who was in the need of money enacted the “Dissolution of the Monasteries” that removed the Catholic monasteries (building occupied by a community of monks living under religious vows), which were the wealthiest properties in the England that were owned by the Catholic church.  The monastery holdings represented over a quarter of all the cultivated land in England.  The monastery buildings and lands were sold off to families who supported the King Henry VII’s actions.  The majority of Catholic monks, friars and nuns were given money or pensions to live on after the monasteries were closed.  However, there were some abbots and religious leaders who refused to comply and they were executed and their monasteries destroyed.
In 1542, King James V of Scotland died and his only surviving legitimate child was his six days old daughter Mary who ascended to the throne of Scotland as Queen Mary.

In 1547, England’s King Henry VIII died and his nine-year-old son Edward, from his third marriage became King Edward VI of England.  The new king was a sickly boy who may have had tuberculosis.  King Edward VI uncle Edmund Beaufort, the Duke of Somerset, ran the English government as the “Lord Protector”.

In 1549, the Church of England published its new version of the “Book of Common Prayer” and the English Parliament passed the first “Act of Uniformity” that established that the English language, rather than the Catholic Latin language, was to be used in the public church worship in England.  The ‘Book of Common Prayer” was a “how-to-book” that prescribed the process, which were to be used to conduct the daily and Sunday Church of England’s worship services in English.  It contained Morning Prayer, Evening Prayer, Holy Communion and the procedures to be used in conducting Baptisms, Confirmations, Marriages, “", and at funeral services.  The new prayer book also set out in full the “proper’s” (that is the parts of the service which varied week by week, or at times throughout the Church's Year).  The “proper’s” included the introits (a psalm or antiphon that was sung or said while the priest approached the church alter), the collects process, epistle (singing of a psalm) and gospel readings for the Sunday service of Holy Communion.  Old Testament and New Testament readings for daily prayer were specified in tabular format as were the Psalms and canticles, mostly biblical, that were provided to be said or sung between the readings.  The new “Book of Common Prayer” prescribes how the Church of England was to operate in proving all its Christian serves.

In 1553, England’s King Edward VI died and Lady Jane Grey was named next in line for the English throne.  Fifteen-year-old Lady Jane was a Protectant granddaughter of King Henry VII and the daughter of King Henry VIII’s sister.  Lady Jane lasted only 9 days before Mary, the daughter of King Henry VIII and his first wife Catherine of Aragon, took over and became Queen Mary I of England.  Lady Jane Grey was arrested and taken to the Tower of London and held there until she was executed twelve years later.  Queen Mary I, was a devout Catholic, married to Philip the son of King Charles V of Spain.  Queen Mary I, tried to convert England back to Catholicism.  Queen Mary I, forced the English Parliament to nullify all the religious legislation passed by former King Edward VI and removed the Church of England “Book of Common Prayer”.  An English inquisition (investigation) was initiated to identify, exile, convert, or punish anyone not conforming to Catholic procedures.  The inquisition identified over 300 Protestant dissenters (rebels) who were branded as heretics (religious outcasts), and 280 of them were burned-at-the-stake, and many more Catholic dissenters were exiled from England in the 5-year reign of Queen Mary I.  England was plunged into a bitter blood bath, which was why Queen Mary I was remembered as “Bloody Mary”.
Note:  The Queen Mary of Scotland and the Queen Mary I of England are different individuals who both rule their countries at the same time.

In 1555, John Calvin’s religious ideas and writing were published, which criticized both Catholic and the new Anglican Church of England for failing to address God’s true will.  John Calvin’s religious beliefs were brought to England where they inspired many people who heard them in large public preaching events.  John Calvin believed in the importance of the individual and that one was happiest when one worked hard, was thrifty and accountable for one’s own actions.  Those who believed in Calvin’s arguments were labeled “separatists” and fell into two groups the “Puritans” and “Pilgrims”.  England became a country strongly divided by different religious faiths.  There were two different types of “Puritans” at the time: separatists and non-separatists.  The non-separatist Puritans wanted to remain in the Church of England and reform it from within.  The “Separatist Puritans” on the other hand felt the church was too corrupt to be reform and instead wanted to separate completely from it.  This was problematic for the separatists because, at that time, the church and state were one in England and the act of separating from the Church of England was considered treasonous.  This problem persuaded many of the strong Puritan separatists to leave England for the New World in order to escape potential punishment for their beliefs and to be able to worship more freely.

In 1558, Catholic Queen Mary I of England died and twenty-eight-year-old Elizabeth Tudor, daughter of Henry VIII and his second wife Anne Boleyn, became Queen Elizabeth I of England.  Queen Elizabeth I, was a Protestant and returned England to the church of her father, while being generally tolerant of those who remained Catholic.  To minimize bloodshed in her kingdom between the factions of Catholics and Protestants, Queen Elizabeth I, encouraged the development of a new “Book of Common Prayer”, including Thirty-Nine Articles, the Ordinal, and two Books of Homilies.  These works, issued under Archbishop Matthew Parker, became the basis of all subsequent Anglican Church of England doctrine, which was substantially the same as the original “Book of Common Prayer”.  The bulk of the English population complied to Elizabeth's religious beliefs and practices with varying degrees of enthusiasm or resignation.

In 1558, the “Act of Supremacy”, later referred to as “Penal Laws” was passed by the English Parliament, which confirmed Queen Elizabeth I, as Supreme Governor of the Church of England, and imposed an “Oath of Supremacy”, requiring any person taking public or church office in England to swear allegiance to the queen as Supreme leader of the Church of England.  The act also made it a crime to proclaim the authority of any foreign prince, church leader, or other authority over the English Queen, and abolished the authority of the Catholic Pope in England.  The new religious doctrine was approved by a narrow vote in the English Parliament, in which all the Roman Catholic bishops, who were not imprisoned, voted against the new law.  Extremists on both sides, those who continued to believe in Catholic Papal Supremacy (the Pope) and those who favored the more militant Protestant or Puritans approach as it became more widely known, were not happy with the new religious compromise that both groups opposed.  It took about 20 years before the Church of England’s Protestantism became the religion of a majority of the English people.

On 25 Feb 1570, the Catholic Pope Pius V issued a papal bull (public decree) excommunicating England’s Queen Elizabeth I.  The Pope referred to Queen Elizabeth I, as “the pretended Queen of England and the servant of crime”, declaring her as a heretic (religious outcast), and released her subjects from their allegiance to her, even those who had "sworn oaths to her", and excommunicated any who obeyed her orders: "We charge and command all and singular the nobles, subjects, peoples and others afore said that they do not dare obey her orders, mandates and laws.”

In 1571, Queen Elizabeth I’s unwillingness to give up the symbols and processes, which she liked, of the Catholic Church and embrace the godlier lifestyle being preached by the country’s more radical Protestant leaders, the “Puritans”, created new brotherhoods and congregations that became increasingly detached from the mainstream Anglican Church of England.  The identification of “Puritans” started in 1564 when Archbishop Matthew Parker used the word to identify protestant neighbors who were more intensely protestant than the members of the Church of England.

On 9 Jul 1575, Louis Hébert was born in Paris, France beginning the start of the Mary Ann Goodwater branch of the French Québec chapter of  “Story of  Winifred Eloise Coss’ Family’s ”, which will be covered later in this paper.  Later in 1608, Louis Hébert became involved in the establishment of the Québec settlement in New French.

Note:  See the “Long Road to the Constitution” a separate paper for information on the history of the Native American people who were and had been living in New England area where the Massachusetts Bay Colony was started by the British Puritans looking for a safer place to practice their new Protestant religion.

In 1578, eighty-six years after Columbus discovered North America, the English finally started their own efforts in exploring and establishing settlements in the New World.  The monarchy (Kings and Queens) and the early legislative government (Parliament) of England had been distracted by wars with Scotland, Ireland and France.  Additionally, the Protestant vs Catholic fighting followed by the Church of England vs Puritan fighting, had left little time and energy to pursue settlement efforts in the New World.

In 1578, Queen Elizabeth I granted a charter to Sir Humphrey Gilbert to explore the territories in the New World.  The terms of the charter were vague, though Sir Humphrey understood it to give him rights to all territory in the New World north of Spanish Florida.  In Mar 1580, Englishman Sir Humphrey Gilbert sent Azorean-born pirate Simon Fernandes on a voyage to the mid-Atlantic North American coast to explore a good location to establish an English colony.
In 1580, Francis Drake completed a three-year circumnavigation sailing trip around the world and in 1581 he was knighted by England’s Queen Elizabeth I.

Note:  While the Spanish were busy defeating the New World’s major advance native civilizations, the Aztec and Inca, England’s Queen Elizabeth I, was content in funding privateers to robbed Spanish treasure ships laden with captured gold and silver returning to Spain.

sections.title: Edith Powers’ branch members start
sections.teaser: On 25 Mar 1581, George Alcock was born to Protestant Rev. John Richard Alcock and Alice Emily (Abrich) in Cambridge, Cambridgeshire, England.

blocks.text: On 25 Mar 1581, George Alcock was born to Protestant Rev. John Richard Alcock and Alice Emily (Abrich) in Cambridge, Cambridgeshire, England.
In 1582, John Leavens Sr. was born in Bocking, Essex, England to Peter Leavens and Elizabeth (Porter).

In 1583, Sir Humphrey Gilbert, sailed over to North America and claimed the Island of Newfoundland, as the first English territory in North America.  Sir Humphrey and his ship were lost a sea on the return trip and the initial attempt to settle Newfoundland was abandoned.  Queen Elizabeth I, divided the charter she had given to Sir Humphrey between his brother Adrian Gilbert, and his half-brother Walter Raleigh.  Adrian's charter gave him the patent for Newfoundland and all points north, where geographers expected to find the long-sought Northwest Passage to Asia.  Walter Raleigh was awarded the lands to the south of Newfoundland though much of it was already claimed by Spain.

Richard Hakluyt, was an ordained priest and an English writer who promoted English colonization of North America.  Richard was aware of Italian explorer Verrazzano’s discover of the "isthmus" with the large ocean beyond “Pamlico Sound”, located within Walter Raleigh's claim in North America, and campaigned for England to capitalize on the opportunity and explore the area.  In Mar 1584, Queen Elizabeth I agreed to establish a colony in the “remote heathen and barbarous lands” of North America, which Walter Raleigh name in her honor, “Virginia”.  The identification of “Virginia” was do to the fact Queen Elizabeth never married or had children.
On 20 Apr 1584, Susan Riddlesdale was born to John Riddlesdale and Dorcas (Black) in the Assington, Suffolkshire, England.
In 1584, Thomas French Sr. was born in Assington, Suffolkshire, England to Jacob French and Anna Agnes (Olmstead).  On 11 Oct 1584, Thomas French Sr. was baptized by his parents born in Assington.

Note:  See the “Long Road to the Constitution” and the “Story of Carolee Afton McCance’s Family” for the history of the Roanoke Colony, England’s first effort to establishes a colony in North America in 1584.

A precedent (standard) was set by the Roanoke Colony leaders who offered 3 acres and then 500 acres of free land to families and men willing to join an effort in establishing a new settlement in the New World.  The offer of free land was an important moment in the history of the English colonization of North America.  From the very beginning the most daring Englishmen and Englishwomen saw a unique opportunity to own and control their own land, without government officials watching and overseeing how the land was used and taxing them for the privilege of using one’s own land.
On 8 Jun 1586, Anne Hooker was born to Thomas Hooker and Susan (Garbrand) in Birstall, Leicestershire, England.

In the late 1500s a new Christian theory developed based on Christian theology, cannon-laws and philosophical (truth-seeking) ideas.  The new theory was that someone who became a witch had made a deliberate pact with the devil and that witches did not act alone.  The new theory led to persecution of people believed to be witches.  The act of helping an injured or sick person was considered good white witchcraft, but still witchcraft.

The wholesale persecution of witches started in 1590 in Scotland when James VI was king.  James VI will later become King James I of England.  Witchcraft had been a criminal offence in Scotland prior to 1590 but action against suspected witches had been limited.  Starting in 1590, many of the Scottish Christians over the next thirteen years of the reign of King James VI, fully accepted the Christian theory of witchcraft which believed that when one witch was found, there would be others in the area who needed to be hunted down.

In 1590, over hundred individuals in Scotland were accused of being witches who were plotting the death of King James VI.  The king had a morbid fear of violent death and became very interested in the witch trials and demonology (study of demons) behind it.  The king’s interested started back in 1589 while he was in Denmark meeting his future wife and queen.  King James VI met a number of Danish intellectuals and philosophers including the astronomer Tycho Brahe.  Witches were actively hunted down in Denmark where the theory of a demonic pact (group of demons) had been widely accepted.  King James’ sea journey back to Scotland encountered a very rough stormy and one of the ships was lost.  Witches performing witchcraft were blamed for the storm and the loss of the people on the ship that sank.

Evidence used in witch trials was very patchy at best.  An example was the theory that witches  had called up the storm that sunk one of the ships carrying King James VI and his party.  Other charges include trying to kill King James VI by melting a wax effigy of him.  Scottish people were accused of performing perverted rituals in a church in Berwick Scotland, although it was not clear what this activity had to do specifically with trying to kill the king.  Over one hundred individuals were accused of performing witchcraft and were put on trial in Scotland.  A large number of accused witches were found guilty and executed, but no accurate figure of the number was recorded.
In 1590 Elizabeth Sale is born in to unknown parents in Chesham, Buckinghamshire, England.
In 1590, George Wyllys was born to unknown parents in Fenny Compton, Warwickshire, England.
In 1591, William Preston was born in to Adam Preston and Isabel (Braithwaite) in Chesham, Buckinghamshire, England.
In 1592 Edward Peter Hurst was born to John Hurst and Joane (Andrews) in the village of Leckhampstead, Buckinghamshire, England.
On 17 Nov 1594, eight-year-old Anne Hooker was baptized in Birstall, Leicester, England by her father Thomas Hooker.
In 1594, Joanna Blessing was born to John William Blessing and Joanna (Preaste) in Greater Yarmouth, Norwich, Norfolkshire, England.
In 1594, Edmund Rice was born to Sir Henry Rice and Margaret (Baker) in Berkhamsted, Hertfordshire, England.
In 1596,  John Upham was born to Richard Upham and Maria Agnes (Underwood) in Bicton, Devonshire, England.
In 1596, John Bent was born to Robert Bent and Agnes (Gosling) in the village of Weyhill, Hampshire, England.  John was baptized on 20 Nov 1596 in Penton by his parents.

In about 1596, Thomas Lamb was born to Thomas de Lambe and Elizabeth (Aylett) in London, England.  Thomas Lamb was baptized on 19 Apr 1597, in the Collegiate Church of St Katherine by the Tower in London, England.

In 1597, King James VI of Scotland wrote an eight-page book called “Deamononlogie”, which expounded on his views on the topic of witchcraft.  Between 1603 and 1625, about twenty witchcraft trials were held in Scotland, identifying nearly 450 possible witches, with half the accused being found guilty and executed.
On 24 Sep 1598, Martha Blanchard was born to Pierre-Jean Blanchard and Martha (Baker) in Penton Grafton, Hampshire, England.
On 29 Oct 1598, Edward Wood was born to John Wood and Mary (Allen) in Nuneaton, Warwickshire, England.
About 1598, Richard Palgrave was born to Rev Edward Palgrave and Amy (Sturmer) in Barnham, Norfolkshire, England.  Richard Palgrave was baptized on 29 Jan 1598 by his parents in Barnham.
On 18 Mar 1599, William Towne was born to Richard Towne and Elizabeth Clarke (Harrison) in Greater Yarmouth, Norwich, Norfolkshire, England.
In May 1599, Arthur Gary was born to Nathaniel Gary and Joane (unknown) in Bishop's Stortford, Hertfordshire, England.  On 20 May 1599, Arthur Gary was baptized in Bishop’s Stortford.
On 11 Aug 1600, Thomasine Frost was born to Edward Frost and Thomasine (Belgrave) in Stanstead, Suffolk, England.
On 25 Aug 1601, Ralph Farnham Sr. was born to Robert Farnham and Elizabeth (Monk) in London, England.
In 1601, Frances Warman was born to unknown parents in the village of Hadham, Hertfordshire, England.
In 1601, Solomon Johnson was born to John Johnson and Hannah (Throckmorten) in Clerkenwell, London, Middlesex, England.
In 1601, Elizabeth Sale was born to unknown parents in the village of Wingrave, Buckinghamshire, England.
On 5 Mar 1602, Joseph Hills was born to George Hills and Mary Wade (Symonds) in Great Burstead, England.  Father George Hills was a “linen draper” (buyer and seller of linen cloth).
On 8 Oct 1602, Elizabeth Webb was born to unknown parents in market town of Axminster, Devonshire, England.
On 19 Oct 1602, Nicholas Holt was born to Sir Thomas Holt and Annice (Bowden) in Ramsey, Essex, England.
In 1602, John Ramsdell Sr. was born to John Ramsdell and Susan (Waterhouse) in Halifax, West Yorkshire, England.
In 1602, Rose Clarke was born to unknown parents in Great Burstead, Essex, England.
On 3 Jun 1603, Ralph Shepard was born to Thomas Shepherd and Margaret Anne (Holliwell) in Derbyshire, England.
On 16 Jun 1603, John Leavens Sr. and Elizabeth Stebbins were married in St. Mary's, Bocking, Essex, England.

In 1603, William Ward was born to Johannes Edward Warde and Marie Hatton (Adams) in York, England.  The Ward family traces its family heritage back to knight of William I, ”the Conqueror ”.  The family name Ward first appears in1066.  In 1175, William de la Ward was listed as a resident in Chester, England.
In 1603, Mary Brisbey was born to unknown parents in Fenny Compton, Warwickshire, England.

In 1603, Queen Elizabeth I died childless and her half-sister, Scottish Queen Mary I’s son, King James VI of Scotland, became King James I of England.  King James I, unlike his Catholic mother,  was a strong supporter of the Protestant Anglican Church of England.

In Jan 1604,  I convened the “Hampton Court Conference”, where a new English version of the Bible was conceived in response to the problems of the earlier Latin Bible translations perceived by the .  King James I, gave the translators instructions intended to ensure that the new version of the Bible would conform to the nature and structure of the Church of England beliefs and practices.  The translation was done by 6 panels of translators (47 men in all, most of whom were leading biblical scholars in the Church of England) who had the rewrite work divided up between them.  The Old Testament was entrusted to three panels, the New Testament to two panels, and the  to one panel.  The New Testament was translated from , the Old Testament from  and , and the  from Greek and  documents.
In 1605, Elizabeth Short was born to Henry Shorte and Mary (Ashley) in Harwich, Essex, England.
On 8 Jul 1605, Walter Powers Sr. was baptized in Stanton St. Quintin, Wiltshire, England by his father John Powers.

About 1606, Thomas Green Sr. was born to Sir Thomas Green and Mary (Jermyn) in St Albans Hertfordshire, England.  Sir Thomas Green was a member of the English Parliament and later was Vice Chamberlain to King Charles I.

Note:  In the 1600s the last name Green was a very common English last name.  There were three different Thomas Greens who all arrived in the Massachusetts Bay Colony about the same time.  The information for the Thomas Green in Edith Powers’ family tree comes from a book written by Samuel S. Green in 1858 in Boston, Massachusetts.  One Thomas Green lived in the town Roxbury, another in Ipswich and a third in Malden.  There are two more Thomas Green’s living in the Virginia Colony at the same time.  The Thomas Green in Edith Powers’ family was the Thomas Green who lived in the town of Malden in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.

On 10 Apr 1607, England’s King James I, after sixteen years had passed since the failure of the Roanoke Colony, became interested in exploring and settling in the New World and he granted competing royal charters to the “Virginia Company of London” and the “Plymouth Company”.  At the time the entire east coast of North American north of Florida was called “Virginia”.

Note:  See the “Long Road to the Constitution”, the “Story of Carolee Afton McCance’s Family” and the “Story of Frederick Garnet Popham’s Family” for the history of the Popham Colony and Jamestown Colony in North America in 1607.
In 1606, Alice Harris was born to Sir Thomas Harris and Mary (Harman) in Assington, Suffolk, England.
On 14 Mar 1607, Elizabeth Lynde was born to Deacon Thomas Lynde and Elizabeth (Stratton) in Padiham, Lancashire, England.
On 25 Aug 1607, William Perkins was born to William Perkins Sr. and Katherine in All Hallows, Bread Street, London, Middlesex Co., England.
In 1607, Priscilla Chase was born to Aquila Chase and Martha (Jelliman) in London, Middlesex, England.

In Aug 1607, the Plymouth Company’s ships arrived at the mouth of the  (then called the Sagadahoc River) where they established the Popham Colony settlement to honor financial backer Sir .  The colonists faced a harsh winter, the loss of supplies following a storehouse fire, and struggled with mixed relations with the local Native people in the area.  The Colony leader Captain  died in the winter, and Raleigh Gilbert the second in command, decided to return to England to take up an inheritance left by an older brother—at which point, all of the other colonists decided to return to England.  In Aug 1608, the remaining Popham Colony colonists sailed back to England on the ship “Mary and John” and on a new ship built by the colonists named “Virginia of Sagadahoc”, a 30-ton ship that was the first sea-going ship ever built in North America.

The other charted company the “Virginia Company of London’s” or “London Company” charter granted the company the eastern coast of America from the 41st longitude (in , New York) down to the 38th longitude ( North Carolina).  The company was permitted by its charter to establish a 100-square-mile settlement within its assigned coastal area.  The portion of the company's territory north of the 41st longitude was shared with the “Plymouth Company”, with the stipulation that neither company would establish a colony within 100 miles of each other.  See a map below showing the two company’s overlapping areas.  The Popham Colony is “Po” and Jamestown is “J”.

blocks.caption: The other charted company the “Virginia Company of London’s” or “London Company” charter granted the company the east…

blocks.text: On 26 Apr 1607, the London Company’s three ships made landfall at the mouth of the , which they named , near present-day , Virginia.  After exploring the Chesapeake Bay area, on 13 May 1607, the London Company leaders, selected a lowland marsh peninsula, 40 miles up the James River for the new Jamestown Colony.
Starting in 1607, settlers that sailed to the English colonies in North America paid for their ocean crossing in one of four ways:
(1).  Those that could pay for the cost for themselves and their family members directly did so.
(2).  Those that had a needed profession could pay for the trip by using their trade skills to work off the trip’s cost once they arrived in America.

(3).  Those who could only pay for a portion of the cost for the trip could pay the remaining portion by agreeing to work for three shillings a day after arriving until the remaining portion of the trip’s cost was repaid.
(4).  The last way of paying was to agree to travel as an Indentured Servant.

The Virginia Company of London developed the system of “indentured servitude” to attract workers to their new Virginia Colony.  Indentured servants typically worked for four to seven years in exchange for their ship passage, room & board (meals) in the colony.  At the completion of the servitude period a, “freedom package”, which included new clothes, and a years’ worth of supplies was given each individual.  Indentured servant’s contracts were extended if one tried to run away or if a woman became pregnant.  Indentured servants became vital part of the Virginia colony’s economy.

If an individual or family also wanted to bring items with them on their voyage to the Virginia Colony such as household furniture, farm animals or tools, the cost for the shipping was about $2 a pound (in today’s money).  The mustang horse of the time weight about 800 pounds and would cost $1,600 each to ship to the New World.  The high cost of shipping made bringing large items nearly prohibited accepted for the very rich.  A new life in the New World offered a glimmer of hope and two-thirds of the early immigrants who came to the American colonies arrived as indentured servants.

In 1608, of the 500 plus new colonists that had arrived in the three ships and were living in Jamestown just eight months earlier were now down to only 60 people, just 12%, and many of the 60 were now sick.  Imagine volunteering to go to Mars if you only have a 12% chance of surviving the first eight months.
Note:  See the “Story of Carolee Afton McCance’s Family” for more on the history of the Jamestown Colony.
On 3 Jul 1608, Thomas Richardson was born to Thomas Richardson Sr. and Kathrine (Duxford) in Westmill, Hertfordshire, England.
On 8 Jul 1608, Margaret Huntington was born to John Huntington and Anna (Inglefield) in Bebington, Cheshire, England.
On 5 Sep 1608, Thomas French Sr. and Susan Riddlesdale were married in Assington, Suffolk, England.

On 27 Nov 1608, Thomas French Jr. was born Thomas French Sr. and Susan (Riddlesdale) in Assington, Suffolk, England.  On 27 Nov 1608, Thomas Jr. was baptized by his parents in St. Edmunds church in Assington.

In 1608, John Winthrop was living in Assington, Suffolk, England before leading the Winthrop Fleet that took 2,000 Puritans to the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  Thomas French Sr. and his wife Susan Riddlesdale and their new born son Thomas Jr. were also lived in Assington, England at the same time.

The 1608 failure of the Popham Colony limited interest in settling the area, but did not deter interest the amazing fishing-grounds in the Gulf of Maine and the Grand Banks off Newfoundland where large schools of cod could be found and caught.  A steady stream of European fishing boats from many countries sailed to fish in the abundant fishing grounds.  Small fishing settlements were established along to coast to support the European fishing operations.
In 1608, Samuel de Champlain established a settlement at the large rapids, which prevented further navigation up the St. Lawrence River.  The Native people living in the area call the spot “Kebec”.
On 6 Jan 1609, Thomas Barrett was born to Christopher Barrett and Elizabeth (Clarke) in the market town of Thornbury in Gloucestershire, England.

Note:  In 1609, Galileo Galilei, an Italian astronomer, physicist and engineer learned about a telescope built by a Dutch eyeglass maker.  Galileo soon developed his own telescope, which he used to observe the moon and the planet Venus.  Galileo discovered that Venus went through phases just like the moon, which meant it had to be rotating around the Sun, just like the moon rotates around the Earth.  The idea that the Sun not the Earth was the center of the visible night sky was a major challenge to the doctrine of the established Catholic Church, which believed the Earth was the center of the universe.

sections.title: Dutch Colony
sections.teaser: In 1609, Henry Hudson, an English sea explorer, was hired by the “Dutch East India Company” to explore North America and look for a passageway to sail to Asi…

blocks.text: In 1609, Henry Hudson, an English sea explorer, was hired by the “Dutch East India Company” to explore North America and look for a passageway to sail to Asia.  Henry Hudson sailed down the east coast of North America from Nova Scotia to the Chesapeake Bay where he explored Delaware Bay and the mouth of the Hudson River that flows by Staten Island where New York City is today.  The area Henry Hudson explored was four-hundred miles north to the English Virginia Colony, located down on the south end of Chesapeake Bay.  As an explorer Henry Hudson claimed the area he had explored for his employer, the “Dutch East Indian Company”.

A year later in 1610, Henry Hudson, while searching for England this time, sailed the ship “Discovery” north past Labrador looking for the Northwest Passage to Asia.  Henry discovered the Hudson Straits and the immense Hudson Bay.  In 1611, after the Hudson Bay froze, Henry and his crew were forced to spent the winter on the shore of James Bay.  It wasn’t until June the next year that sea ice finally melted and Henry ordered the crew to press on to the west to continue the search for the passage to Asia, but his crew mutinied.  The mutineers cast Henry, his son, and seven others adrift in small shallop sailboat and the sailed the “Discover” back to England.  Henry Hudson and his companions were never seen again.  See a map on the next page showing Henry Hudson’s two voyages to North America.

blocks.caption: A year later in 1610, Henry Hudson, while searching for England this time, sailed the ship “Discovery” north past Lab…

blocks.text: In 1610, the Dutch established a series of seasonal trading posts on the Hudson, Connecticut and Delaware rivers at the eastern edge of Iroquois Mohawk Native Americans’ territory.  The Mohawks were one of the five nations that made up the “Iroquoian-speaking” Iroquois Confederacy.  The new Dutch presences gave the Iroquois’ direct access to European fur markets.  The Dutch soon also established trade with the Lenape and Susquehannock tribes that were both Algonquian-speaking tribes in the area.  The Dutch supplied the Iroquoian-speaking and Algonquian-speaking tribes with guns and other valuable metal tools that the Native people wanted in exchange for animal pelts (skins).  The European demand for animal fur pelts resulting in large-scale Native American hunting and trapping operations to gather fur animal pelts to trade for the European products the Native people wanted.  About this time, conflicts began to grow between the Iroquoian-speaking Iroquois Confederacy and Algonquian-speaking Native tribes over control of the lucrative fur trading business.  See the map below that shows in yellow highlighted area the Dutch settled areas in America.

blocks.caption: In 1610, the Dutch established a series of seasonal trading posts on the Hudson, Connecticut and Delaware rivers at t…

blocks.text: In 1610, Elizabeth Wooton was born to Dr. Thomas Wooton and Sarah (Jennings) in the village of Ufford, Northamptonshire, England.  On 2 Oct 1610, Elizabeth Wooton was baptized in Ufford, Northamptonshire, England by her parents.
In 1610, William Douglas was in Scotland to unknown parents.
In 1610, Ann Mattle was born to Thomas Mattle and Agnes (de Aston) Kings Lynn, Norfolkshire, England.
In 1610 Richard Robbins was born to John Robbins and Hester Anne (Pitt) in England.  On 7 Aug 1610 Richard Robbins was baptized in the town of Thane, Oxfordshire, England by his parents.
In 1611 Richard Sawtelle was born to John Sawtell and Agnes (Pittard) in Aller, Somersetshire, England.  On 7 Apr 1611, Richard Sawtelle was baptized by his parents in Aller.
In 1611, Jacques Vezina was born to Michot Vezinat and Jeanne (Menard) in Puyravault, Vendêe, France.
On 27 Oct 1611, Elizabeth Pople was born Stephen Pople and Jane (Neame) in High Ham, Somerset, England.

Starting in 1611, the Dutch followed up on Henry Hudson explorations with explorer Adriane Block who made four voyages on the behalf of a group of Amsterdam merchants.  Adriane Block explored the Connecticut River.  The Dutch quickly established trade with the Native American Pequot tribe in the Connecticut River Valley area.
On 15 Feb 1612, Eleanor Crofts (Elinora Crofte) was baptized in Shelve, Shropshire England.  No information has been found on Eleanor’s birth.
On 4 Apr 1612, Joane Newman was born to John Newman and Elizabeth (Latham) in Stanton St. Quintin, Wiltshire, England.  Joan was baptized 27 Aug 1612 in Melksham, Wiltshire, England.
On 20 Jun 1612, Thankslorde Perkins (Thank Ye Lord) was born to Thomas Perkins and Dorothy (Bird) in Stepney, London Borough of Tower Hamlets, London, England.
In 1612, Mary Baldwin was born to Richard Baldwin and Johane Blythe in England.
In 1612, Mary Scudamore was born to William Scudamore and Frances Lechmore in Gloucestershire, England.

In 1613, Governor Sir Thomas Dale of the Jamestown Virginia Colony began to grant three-acre parcels to English colonists willing to stay after their seven-year indentured servitude was completed.  Those who stayed could work for themselves on their private three-acre plot.  The change gave the colonists a path to acquiring their own land something that was not possible back in England, unless one was the oldest son and able to inherited land from his father.
On 11 Oct 1613, William Preston and Elizabeth Sale were married in Chesham, Buckinghamshire, England.
On 9 Dec 1613,  Elizabeth Storey was born to William Storey and Caterina (Tharbie) in Sudbury, Derbyshire, England.

In 1614, Captain John Smith, who had led the Virginia Colony from Sep 1608 to Aug 1609 before returning back to England returned and explored the coasts from Maine down to the Cape Cod Bay named the Atlantic coast area “New England”.
In 1614, Roger Preston was born to Roger Preston and Alice (Parry) in Hadlow, Kent, England.

Note:  After the failure of the Popham Colony questions arose about the survivability of living through a winter on the coast north of Cape Cod.  Sir Ferdinando Gorges sent a group of adventures in 1616 to camp at the mouth of the Saco River south of the Popham Colony location on the coast of Maine.  The test worked the adventures survive proving the area could be settled.

Note:  In 1616, the Catholic Church placed Nicholas Copernicus’ book “De Revolutionibus,” the first modern scientific argument for a heliocentric (sun-centered) universe, on its index of banned books.  Pope Paul V summoned Nicholas Copernicus to Rome and told him he could no longer support his radical ideas publicly, just as had been done seven years earlier to Galileo Galilei, an Italian astronomer.  The reason the Catholic Church took this position was the sun-center universe conflicted with the Church’s interpretation of the Bible, which it believed correctly placed the earth at the center of the universe.
In 1617, Marie Boisdon was born to Jean Boisdon and Marie (Bardin) in La Rochelle, Normandy, France.

In 1618, English authorities came to Leiden in Holland to arrest  a leader of the Pilgrims after he published comments highly critical of the King of England and the  of England.  William Brewster escaped arrest, but the events spurred the Pilgrim congregation to move farther from England.
On 15 Oct 1618, Edmund Rice and Thomasine Frost were married in Bury, Suffolk, England.  Edmond Rice was spoken of as a “Yeoman” someone in the middle class of England.  He had a fair education.

In Jun 1619, the Pilgrims (someone who journeys to a foreign land) group leaders obtained a land patent from the “Plymouth Company” that had previously sponsored the earlier Popham Colony.  The Pilgrims had declined the opportunity to settle in , located north of the English Virginia Colony, because of their desire to avoid the Dutch influence.  Their new land patent allowed them to settle at the mouth of the Hudson River, where the Dutch had already established a trading port and were trading with the local Native Americans in the area.  The Pilgrims sought to finance their venture through working with the “London Merchants Adventures”, a group of businessmen who principally viewed the establishment of a new English colony as a means of making a profit on their investment.

In 1619, a capture group of African people were sold to Portuguese slave traders and forced onto the Portuguese slave ship, the "São João Bautista" that then sailed to the new world to deliver the enslaved Africans to the Spanish in Mexico.  Before arriving in Mexico, the Portuguese ship was captured by the English ship the “White Lion” and twenty of the Africans were taken aboard the English ship, which then sailed on to the Jamestown Colony where the Africans captives were sold.  At that time Jamestown had no slaves or slave laws and the new Africans were treated as indentured servants and given the same opportunities for freedom after their servitude as was the same opportunity enjoyed by  European indentured servants.
On 14 Nov 1619, Edward Preston was born to William Preston and Elizabeth (Sale) in Chesham, Buckinghamshire, England.
In 1619, Rachell Wright was born to Giles Wright and Elizabeth (Porter) in London, England.
In 1619, Dorothy Harbottle was born to William Harbottle and Alles (Baelle) in Horton, Gloucestershire, England.

On 25 Mar 1620, William Towne married Joanna Blessing in St. Nicholas Parish, Great Yarmouth, Norfolk, England.  William and Joanna’s had six children all of which were baptized in the St. Nicholas Church.
In 1620, twenty-four-old Thomas Lamb entered Cambridge University in England.
In 1620, Francis Gould was born to John Gould and Judith (Marks) in Hemel, Hempstead, Hertfordshire, England.

sections.title: Events Leading to the English Civil War
sections.teaser: On 13 Nov 1620, King James I summoned his 3rd, Parliament.  After the disappointment of his 2nd Parliament, when King James I had attempted to rule without a…

blocks.text: On 13 Nov 1620, King James I summoned his 3rd, Parliament.  After the disappointment of his 2nd Parliament, when King James I had attempted to rule without a Parliament, he was now forced to by events that occurred in continental Europe to restore the English Parliament.  His son-in-law Frederick V, the Elector of Palatine, had accepted the throne of Bohemia that triggered an invasion of Bohemia (the western part of the present-day Czech Republic) by a Spanish army.  King James I, want to help his son-in-law, but needed money to do so.  To his relief the English Parliament voted to give the king additional money, but with the stipulation the Parliament could investigate the abuse of monopolies in the English government.  During their investigations the House of Commons discovered that the Lord Chancellor, Sir Francis Bacon, had been taking bribes, which resulting in his dismissal from his office and being fined and imprisoned.  Further investigation revealed the King’s favorite ,the Duke of Buckingham and his associates, were also monopoly abusers.  King James I, reacted by threatening to dissolve the English Parliament and demanded further subsidies (money).  Parliament reluctantly acceded to King James I’s demand on condition that they should be allowed to finish their other business and also suggested that the king’s son, , should seek a Protestant wife rather than the Spanish marriage King James had in mind.  King James I, view the potential marriage of his son Charles to Maria Anna, the daughter of the King of Spain, as a financial windfall with the expected large Spanish wedding dowry.  King James I, furiously asserted that the royal marriage was none of the English’s Parliament business.  Parliament next arrested additional government officials they found to be involved in government scandals and King James I downgraded the Parliament to a convention (i.e., a Parliament without royal approval) and finally dissolved it altogether by Proclamation on 6 Jan 1622.  House of Commons representatives of the 3rd English Parliament included Edward Popham from Bridgwater in Somersetshire and Sir Francis Popham from Great Bedwyn in Wiltshire.

sections.title: Plimoth Plantation
sections.teaser: In 1620, the separatists Pilgrims, who were back in England after returning from Holland, decided England was no longer a place where their controversial bel…

blocks.text: In 1620, the separatists Pilgrims, who were back in England after returning from Holland, decided England was no longer a place where their controversial beliefs could be freely practice so on 6 Sep 1620, the Pilgrims and a group of London Merchants Adventures left Plymouth England on the ship “Mayflower” and headed for America.  The Pilgrims had already been living onboard the “Mayflower” for six weeks.  The voyage across the Atlantic Ocean took 66 days.  The first half of the voyage went smoothly; the only major problem was sea-sickness.  But by October, the ship began to encountering strong Atlantic storms that made the voyage treacherous.  Several times, the strong wind forced ship’s captain to let ship drift where the weather took it because it was not safe to use the ship's sails.  The ship was heading to the Northern Virginia Colony.  As the Mayflower approached land, the crew spotted Cape Cod just as the sun rose on 9 Nov 1620.  The Pilgrims wanted to head south but encountered very rough seas, and nearly shipwrecked.  The Pilgrims decided, rather than risk another attempt to go south to Virginia; they would just stay and explore Cape Cod.  They anchored in what is now Provincetown Harbor.  See below a replica of the living area in the Mayflower:

blocks.caption: In 1620, the separatists Pilgrims, who were back in England after returning from Holland, decided England was no long…

blocks.text: Note: The settlements the English first established in North America were called “Plantations” not colonies during the 1600’s.  The Pilgrims established the “Plimoth Plantation” - not the “Plymouth Colony”.  From Maine in the far north to Virginia in the south the men who came to settle in this new land adopted the name “planters”.  They were not planters in the agricultural endeavor, but instead were planters seeking to create a new spiritually orientated civilization.

On 11 Nov 1620, the pilgrims of Plimoth Plantation didn’t have a charter or a patent to settle in Cape Cod.  A “Charter” was a document from the British government that gave a plantation the legal right to settle an area and establish local laws.  The Pilgrims originally planned to settle in the Northern Virginia Plantation, where they did not need a patent.  The pilgrims worried they didn’t have a legal right to settle Cape Cod and were concerned they would offend the King and the English government by ignoring the law.  The non-pilgrim passengers on the “Mayflower” were not separatists, like pilgrims, and they questioned the pilgrims’ authority which concerned many of the pilgrim members.  As a result, the two groups decided to draw up a social contract, now known as the “Mayflower Compact”, to establish a foundation for a new local government.  The new compact was the first example of a colony self-governing itself and is considered by historians to be the beginning of American democracy.  The “Mayflower Compact” laid a set of laws that were used to govern the new “Plimoth Plantation”.

The London Merchant Adventures and the Pilgrims spent the rest of Nov and Dec of 1620 exploring Cape Cod, trying to decide where they would build their new plantation settlement.  On 23 Dec 1620, they had finally decided to settle in an abandon Native Wampanoag village site called “Patuxet”.  The location was selected because it was suited for winter building since the forest trees had already been cleared off and the tall hills in the area provided good defensive positions to provide protection for the new plantation.  The “little Ice Age” was still ongoing and the winters in Cape Cod were cold, dark and frequently stormy.  With the unforgiving weather and the desolate coastal area that had selected to build their new plantation, the London Merchant Adventures and the Pilgrims decided to stay and live on the ship while their houses and protective plantation fortifications were built.  The cold, damp and crowded conditions on the ship created an unhealthy environment and people soon began to get sick with an influenza type of disease.  The lack of fresh fruits and vegetable also led to some of the people coming down with scurvy (a disease caused by insufficient vitamin C, the symptoms of which include spongy gums, loosening of the teeth, and bleeding of the skin and mucous membranes.).  On 25 Dec 1620, they began construction of the first buildings for the new Plimoth Plantation.  See below the reconstruction of the Plimoth Plantation in todays’ Living History Museum:

blocks.caption: The London Merchant Adventures and the Pilgrims spent the rest of Nov and Dec of 1620 exploring Cape Cod, trying to d…

blocks.text: Note:  Only 57 of the 102 Pilgrims that arrived in the Plimoth Plantation in 1620 survived the first winter.

In 1621, the London Company sent a second ship the “Fortune” with more settlers and supplies to the Plimoth Plantation and a third ship the “Anne” later in 1623.  New settlements were established in Weymouth and Braintree, in 1623 and in Winnisimmet, Nantasket, Naumkeag and Shawnut in 1624 and Mishawumin in 1625.  The later arriving settlements were independent of each other and represented a population of about 500 settlers.
Note:  See the “Long Road to the Constitution” and the “Story of Morris Rankin Batie’s Family” for additional information on the Plimoth Plantation and the later Plymouth Colony.

The ships that were being used to carried settlers to the New World were originally designed to carrier freight not people, since there was no business in carrying people before the migration to America occurred.  The ships like the “Mayflower” and the “Arabella” were designed and built to carried wine between ports around the Mediterranean Sea.  The ships were called “sweet ships” because they were well caulked to keep their cargo day.  See below a photo of a reconstructed Mayflower replica:

blocks.caption: The ships that were being used to carried settlers to the New World were originally designed to carrier freight not p…

sections.title: Native American People
sections.teaser: In 1620, when the London Merchant Adventures and Pilgrims arrived in Cape Cod, they found a coastline that was empty of native people.  The native inhabitant…

blocks.text: In 1620, when the London Merchant Adventures and Pilgrims arrived in Cape Cod, they found a coastline that was empty of native people.  The native inhabitants of the region around Cape Cod were various tribes of the Wampanoag people, who had lived in the area for over 10,000 years.  Between 1615 and 1619 the Wampanoag people suffered an epidemic (smallpox or leptospirosis, a bacterial infection) brought over from Europe by early explorers that killed almost all of the native people in the area.  As a result, the London Merchant Adventures and Pilgrims found the Cape Cod area empty and abandon.

Soon after their arrival Plimoth settlers began building fortifications around the area where houses were going to be built.  During the building process the new settlers came in contact with Squanto, an English-speaking Native American from the Patuxet tribe.  Back in 1614, Squanto had been seized by the explorer Thomas Hunt and taken back to Spain.  Squanto managed to escape from Spain and got over to England where he learned to speak English, and was later able to return to his native homeland in 1619, where he found most of his tribe had died.  In addition to interpreting and mediating between the Plimoth Plantation’s leaders and the local Native American chiefs (including Massasoit, chief of the Pokanoket), Squanto taught the new settlers how to plant maize (Indian corn), which became an important crop and showed them where to fish and hunt around Cape Cod.  In the fall of 1621, the Plimoth settlers famously shared their harvest feast with the Pokanokets; the meal is now considered the basis for the “Thanksgiving holiday”.

Other Native tribes, such as the Massachusetts and Narragansets, were not friendly towards new Europeans, and their Massasoit’s alliance.  The arrival of the Plimoth settlers had disrupted relations among Native American people living in the greater Cap Code area.  Over the next decades, relations between settlers and Native Americans deteriorated as more new English settlers arrived and spread out and occupied more of the Native peoples’ ancestorial lands.  The map below identifies the major Native Tribes in the Massachusetts area:

blocks.caption: Other Native tribes, such as the Massachusetts and Narragansets, were not friendly towards new Europeans, and their M…

blocks.text: About 1621, Dr. Richard Palgrave and Anne unknown were married in England.
About 1621, Sarah Palgrave was born to Dr. Richard Palgrave and Anne (unknown) in Wymondham, Norfolkshire, England.

In 1621, Daniel Cheever was born to Richard Cheever and Katheren (Williams) in Canterbury, Kent, England.  On 29 Apr 1621, Daniel Cheever was baptized in St. Andrew’s church in Canterbury, Kent, England by his parents.

In 1621, the “Dutch West India Company” of the Dutch Republic claimed the land from southern Cape Cod down to the bottom of the “Delmarva Peninsula”.  See the map below showing the Delmarva Peninsula that later would become the Delaware and Maryland Colonies and the eastern shore of the Virginia Colony.

blocks.caption: In 1621, the “Dutch West India Company” of the Dutch Republic claimed the land from southern Cape Cod down to the bot…

blocks.text: On 24 Jan 1622, Rose Tyrill  was born to Thomas Tyrill and Ellenor (Parnes) in Buckingham, England.
On 19 Jun 1622, Martha Blessing Towne was born to William Towne and Joanna (Blessing) in Great Yarmouth, Norfolk, England.
On 20 Oct, 1622 Edward Rice was born to Edmund Rice and Thomasine (Frost) in Stanstead, Suffolk, England.
In 1622, George Alcock graduated from Cambridge, England’s St. John’s College.
On 23 Jul 1623, Edward Peter Hurst and Susanna Tristram were married in St Mary church in Hitchin, Hertfordshire, England
In 1623, Margaret Hurst was born to Edward Peter Hurst and Susanna (Tristram) in Kent, England.

sections.title: New Hampshire Colony
sections.teaser: In 1622, the Council of New England, with King Charles I approval, granted land to Captain John Mason, the former governor of Newfoundland, on the coast nort…

blocks.text: In 1622, the Council of New England, with King Charles I approval, granted land to Captain John Mason, the former governor of Newfoundland, on the coast north of Massachusetts Bay.   Captain Mason named the area “New Hampshire”.  Ferdinando Gorges was also given land that is now the State of Maine.
In 1623, Esther Hester was born in England to unknown parents.

In 1623, Captain John Mason gave David Thompson, the leader of a group of English fisherman, permission to settle at Odiorne’s Point (present day Rye Beach, New Hampshire).  The New Hampshire area was called the Upper Plantation (comprising modern-day city of Dover.  See below a map of the later Province of New Hampshire:

blocks.caption: In 1623, Captain John Mason gave David Thompson, the leader of a group of English fisherman, permission to settle at…

blocks.text: In 1623, a grant was issued to a group of merchants from England who wanted to establish a trade settlement overseas.  John White, a reverend in Dorchester Dorset England at the time, aided the merchant group in securing the needed government patent.  The “Dorchester Company”, as it was then called, sent 14 fishermen across the Atlantic Ocean.  They landed on Cape Ann and set up a fishing outpost called Gloucester.  This was the first of many “Old Planter” colonies in New England that were not a part of either the Plimoth Plantation or the later Massachusetts Bay Colony, which were established by Puritans purely for financial reasons, mainly to catch fish to sell to England and Spain for profit.
On 22 Jul 1624, Joseph Hills and Rose Clarke were married in Burstead, Essex Co., England.
On 19 Aug 1624, William Peacock was born to John Peacock and Mary Marie Miller in Ardeley, Hertfordshire, England.
In 1624, William Perkins is admitted to the Emmanuel College in Cambridge, England.  A year later William enrolled in Cambridge where he received his B.A degree in 1628 from Christ’s College.

Note:  The Plimoth Plantation used a system where the families in the settlement share common fields for growing crops and raising animals.  After the harvest the crops and animals were shared equally with all the families.  The result of the communal system was poor harvests and hard time for the entire Plantation.  The sharing of common fields was implemented by the joint-stock company that funded the Plimoth Plantation.  The London Merchants Adventures considered themselves the owners of the entire planation effort and were expecting profits from their investments.  Governor Bradford, the leader of the Plimoth Plantstion, received a petition from the settlement members complaining about the common field system and he agreed to assign individual families their own field area based on the size of each family.  The change resulted larger harvests, happier farmers and improved chances of having profits that could be share with the London investors.

On 18 Apr 1625, Arthur Gary and Frances Warman were married in Bishops Stortford, Hertfordshire, England.  Arthur and Frances lived in the village of Hadham, Hertfordshire, England after their marriage.
In 1625, John Bent and Martha Blanchard were married in Sudbury, Suffolk, England.
About 1625, George Alcock and Anne Hooker were married in hamlet of Marefield, Leicestershire, England.

In 1625, King James I died and his son Charles became King Charles I of England.  King Charles I, immediately had problems with the lack of money to cover his plans, which led to conflicts with the growing power of the English Parliament.  King Charles I, forced his nobles to lend him money, force others to accept knighthood at high fees, and raised “Ship Money” taxes higher. “Ship Money” was a special tax on people in England living by the sea.  King Charles I, had a Catholic wife and favored the Catholic religion, causing hostility towards the Puritans and other Protestants to greatly increase.  Rumors spread throughout England that King Charles I was planning to force the county back to the Roman Catholic Church, which was a major concern for the Puritans.

In 1626, the Gloucester settlement at Cape Ann failed and the settlers headed by Roger Conant moved to the nearly abandoned Naumkeag Native American settlement in the area, which later was renamed Salem, without first obtaining permission from the King Charles I.  Although the Cape Ann settlement was established as a business venture, one of the founders of the Cape Ann settlement, Reverend John White, also wanted the settlement to be a place of refuge for Puritans escaping religious persecution back in England.
On 1 Jan 1626, John Douglas Alcock was born to George Alcock and Anne (Hooker) in Markfield, Leicestershire, England.
On 1 Nov 1626, Deacon John Upham and Elizabeth Webb were married in Bicton, Devonshire, England.
In 1626, Robert Smith was born to Robert Smith Sr. and Margaret Deane in Boston, Lincolnshire, England.
In 1626, Solomon Johnson and Eleanor Crofts were married in London, Middlesex, England.

In 1627, the bickering directors of the London Merchants Adventures Company sold out their interests in the “Plimoth Plantation” to the settlers living in the plantation for £1,800.  The settlers spent the next fifteen years paying off the debt.
On 5 Feb 1627, Richard Sawtelle and Elizabeth Pople were married in High Ham, Somerset, England.
On 10 Oct 1627, Ralph Farnham Sr. and Alice Harris were married in Southampton, England.

Note:  New England's political and social evolution during the 1600s did not occur under tight centralized control.  Vague grants, poorly written charters, a lack of close British supervision, in many cases caused by the English Civil War, created an independent self-reliant attitude within the English colonies.  Territorial boundaries of the various colonies underwent later substantial change.  The first colonial entity to emerge was Plimoth Plantation (1620), which exercised control over what is today the southeastern portion of State of Massachusetts.

In Mar 1627, John Endecott was one of seven signatories to a land grant given to "The New England Company for a Plantation in Massachusetts" (or the New England Company) by the Earl of Warwick on behalf of the Plymouth Council for New England.  The Plymouth Council was at the time the umbrella organization overseeing English colonization efforts in North America between 40- and 48-degrees latitude on the coast of North America.

In 1628, the “London Plantation” obtained a patent to settle the Salem area in Cape Cod and took over the illegal settlement established there by the colonists from the failed Gloucester settlement back in 1626.
On 20 Jun 1628 the ship “Abigail” sailed from Weymouth, Dorset, England and arrived on 6 Sep 1628 in Salem, with a new government for the “London Plantation”, under Governor Captain John Endicott.
In 1628 Thomas Green Sr. and Elizabeth Lynde were married in England.

sections.title: Massachusetts Bay Colony
sections.teaser: In Mar 1629, King Charles I dismissed the English Parliament.  It would not be restored for eleven years, which greatly limited the king’s ability to raise f…

blocks.text: In Mar 1629, King Charles I dismissed the English Parliament.  It would not be restored for eleven years, which greatly limited the king’s ability to raise funds for his government.

On 4 Mar 1629, the “London Plantation” renamed itself the “Massachusetts Bay Company” after the tribe of Massachusetts Native people living in New England.  The new company was granted a charter by King Charles I to officially engage in trade in New England.  King Charles I was happy to rid England of the Protestant trouble makers that were an obstacle his desire to return England to the traditional Catholic Church religious practices.  The King Charles I hoped most of the militant Protestant Puritans would leave England for the colonies in America and many did.

In Aug 1629, when the charter was issued to the new Massachusetts Bay Company for the “Massachusetts Bay Colony”, the charter neglected to say the company members had to stay in England to conduct their meetings.  The company held a series of meeting in Cambridge England where they voted to take advantage of the omission in the charter and move the entire company over to New England.

sections.title: Puritans Arrive in the Winthrop Fleet
sections.teaser: In Oct 1629, John Winthrop the spiritual and political leader of the Massachusetts Bay Company was elected by the company’s General Court as the first govern…

blocks.text: In Oct 1629, John Winthrop the spiritual and political leader of the Massachusetts Bay Company was elected by the company’s General Court as the first governor of the “Massachusetts Bay Colony”, while he was still in England.  The General Count was made up of the company’s investor shareholders (called “Freemen”) and operated as a governing body for the company.  John Winthrop would serve twelve annual terms as governor and was a force of comparative moderation in the religiously conservative colony.  John Winthrop was a respected political figure, his attitude toward governance was somewhat authoritarian: he resisted attempts to widen voting and other civil rights beyond a narrow class of religiously approved individuals, (Freemen) and opposed attempts to codify a body of laws that the colonial magistrates would be bound by, and also opposed unconstrained democracy, calling it "the meanest and worst of all forms of government".

In April 1630, the Puritans, led by John Winthrop, left their homes in Boston, Lincolnshire, England and set sail from Southampton, England to the New World.  King Charles I, had given the Massachusetts Bay Company the rights to the land between the Merrimack River on north and the Charles River on the south.  The territory ostensibly administered by the Massachusetts Bay Colony covered much of central New England, including portions of Massachusetts, , , and .  See a map below of the new Massachusetts Bay Colony.  The original Native tribes in the area are also identified on the map.

blocks.caption: In April 1630, the Puritans, led by John Winthrop, left their homes in Boston, Lincolnshire, England and set sail fro…

blocks.text: In Jun 1630, the first of eleven ships of the Winthrop Fleet arrived in Salem in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  About 2,000 Puritans plus livestock and provisions arrived from England over the summer during this first phase of the “Great Migration”.  The settlement in Salem, which was occupied by members of the failed Cape Ann settlement quickly realized it did not have enough food or shelter to accommodate the newly arriving Puritans.  The Puritans were forced to move on and initially settled in Charlestown, across the river from the Shawmut peninsula.  Although they had a location, the colony still suffered due to a lack of fresh water.  By chance a college friend of John Winthrop, William Blackstone, was living on the Shawmut peninsula.  When William Blackstone heard of Winthrop’s arrival, he invited the Puritans to live with him on the Shawmut peninsula.  John Winthrop accepted the offer and the Puritans relocated and began construction of their new settlement, in what later would become the town of Boston.  Six new towns were laid out by the olony for the new settlers: Boston, Watertown, Lynn (Saugus), Newton (Cambridge), Dorchester, and Toxbury.

The charter of the Massachusetts Bay Company provided for the Governor, his deputy, and eighteen assistants to be chosen from among the colony’s “freemen”.  At first only the twelve members of the Massachusetts Bay Company were “freemen”.  As time passed, new “freemen” were gradually admitted.  As the governmental body for the colony the “Freemen” met together as a “General Count”, which elected the colony’s leaders, passed governing laws for the colony and performed as the highest court to judge legal matters.  The “General Court” was the colony’s government.

Between 1630 and 1640, an estimated twenty-five thousand Puritans left England and settled in the Massachusetts Bay Colony because they believed the corrupted morals and doctrines of the Church of England needed "purifying”.  Imprisoned and brutally persecuted for conscience’s sake, and seeing no hope for religious freedom in England, the braver of the Puritans sailed over to the New World.  The difference between the ‘Pilgrims’ and ‘Puritans’, who were also Protestants, differed in their opposition to the Anglian Church of England.  Both groups followed the teaching of John Calvin, but the Pilgrims were Puritans who had abandoned their local parishes and formed small congregations of their own because the Church of England was not holy enough to meet their standards.  The Pilgrims were labeled “Separatists”.

In addition to looking for religious freedom the “yeoman” (men working on rented land of others or servants in a royal or noble households) left England and suffered the extreme hardships found in the new world to escape the life of “tenantry” that they and their families had been suffered for generations.  Tenantry came from the “Middle Ages” when all land was owned by the King and managed by the king’s nobles who swore allegiance to the king in exchange for their noble positions.  The nobles in turn allowed men and their families to live as tenants on the land in exchange for the work they provided.  The tenants did not live they simply survived, hopeless of any improvement in their condition socially or materially, and domed to support indefinitely the privileged class place above them by the king or queen.  This “Manorial System” in England perpetuated a social slavery whereby landowners drained the earnings of their tenants, whose lives were spent working for their masters and who died as poor as they began life.  The Catholic clergy supported this life style by preaching to the tenant families the doctrine of “Christian Resignation”, the acceptance of one’s lot in life in which Providence had placed them and then receiving their reward, if they were obedient, in the after-life, in heaven.

Note:  John Calvin’s beliefs differed from the Roman Catholic Church in the 16th century, which believed in the real-presence of Christ when one is receiving the Holy Communion (the Christian service, ceremony commemorating Christ’ Last Supper, in which bread and wine are consecrated and consumed).  The Calvin's reformation did not endorse Christ's body being present during this religious sacrament, but did advocate for the importance of the Holy Communion ritual.  Calvin believed Holy Communion aided in the spiritual commune of God and man by allowing man to rise up to God rather than having God descend down.  Calvin also rejected worship of images of saints and the presents of an image of the body of Christ on the crosses in churches, but did allow the use of a plain cross and music in church services.  He condemned both icon-worship (worshiping man-made objects) and iconoclasm (the rejection or destruction of religious images).

In England in the 1630s, the fortunate few that had managed to own their own land “Free-holders” were now caught in King Charles I squeeze of higher and higher taxes to support his ongoing religious wars.  The opportunity to own one’s own land without fear of not being able to pay the taxes on the land encouraged even the very successful land owners to leave England and restart their lives over in New England.

The newly arriving Puritans, who came in the 1630’s migration, were shocked at the primitive conditions facing them.  The ships they sailed on were freight-carrying vessels that were not designed for human passengers.  Two hundred of the Puritans who arrived in New England immediately went back to England.  The more steadfast remained true to the cause for which they had come.  John Winthrop sent a ship back to England for food and more colonists.  The ship returned in February 1631, in time to save the starving colony.  For several years the new settlers were severely tested by more than the ordinary sufferings of frontier life.  Only their great faith in God enabled these people to survive.  It was necessary to carry guns for protection from the Native warriors, even on the way to church, where they met to hear the same famous ministers who had once preached to them in some of England’s loveliest churches.  Now they shivered in barn-like meeting houses as they gave attention to the words of the great scholars who were their leaders.  The fine clothing, they loved; silk hose, crocked hats, and gaily colored silk, satin, and velvet dresses, cloaks, and breeches were out of place in the tangles of the New England’s forests.  Men of the “gentry class”; former justices of English counties, members of the English Parliament, merchants, artisans, and shopkeepers now all labored with their hands to wrest a bare living from this harsh new land they were now living in.  Wolves killed precious young calves.  Women and children, and sometimes even men got lost in the woods and froze to death.  Others drowned in storms out at sea while out fishing.

In 1630, Thomas French Sr., his wife Susan (Riddlesdale) and their seven children, including twenty-two-year-old son Thomas Jr. sail from England on the ship “Arabella” as part of the Winthrop Fleet and settled in the Salem community in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.
In 1630, Thomas French Jr. and Mary Scudamore were married in Boston shortly after Thomas arrived with his parents and siblings from England.

In 1630, Dr. Richard Palgrave, his wife Anne (unknown) and their four children, including nine-year-old daughter Sarah Palgrave sailed from England as part of the Winthrop fleet and arrived and settled in Charlestown, in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  Dr. Richard Palgrave was the first physician in Charlestown and was granted 200 acres in the area, which later became part of the town of Woburn north of Charlestown on which Richard farmed.  Dr. Palgrave was made a “freeman” on 18 May 1631 in Charlestown.

In 1630, Thomas Lamb, his first wife Elizabeth (Smith) and their children sailed from England with the Winthrop fleet and arrived in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  Thomas owned a home lot of eighteen acres and thirty-seven acres of meadow and marsh lands. Thomas Lamb opened and operated the first rock quarry on Squantum Island in Boston Harbor.  The stones were used extensively for cemetery purposes and as material for buildings.  On 18 May 1631 Thomas was made a “freeman”.

sections.title: The old map on the next page that shows the major towns in the early Massachusetts Bay Colony.

blocks.caption: From the family document

blocks.text: In 1630, George Alcock, his wife Anne (Hooker) and their 4-year-old son John Douglas Alcock left England and sailed to Roxbury in the Massachusetts Bay Colony as a member of the early planters of the Winthrop fleet.  George worked as a “butcher” in Roxbury.  He was admitted as the 51st member to the Roxbury church.  In 1631, George was made a “freeman”.  The towns of Roxbury and Boston were only three miles apart.

In 1630, Thomas Richardson and his two brothers Samuel and Ezekiel sailed with the Winthrop fleet and arrived and settled in Charlestown, Suffolk Co., Massachusetts Bay Colony.  Thomas was the youngest of the three brothers.
In 1630, Thomas Green Jr. was born to Thomas Green Sr. and Elizabeth (Lynde) in Kegworth, Leicestershire, England.

sections.title: Rhode Island Colony
sections.teaser: In 1630, Roger Williams was a chaplain in the Church of England, who believed in religious freedom, tolerance and the separation between church and state, ho…

blocks.text: In 1630, Roger Williams was a chaplain in the Church of England, who believed in religious freedom, tolerance and the separation between church and state, however, he did not believe in forcing others to agree with him.  He thought that it was a sin to punish or kill anyone in the name of Christianity and he thought that only church members should pay to support their church.  On 5 Feb 1631, he and his wife arrived on the ship the “Lyon” and settled the Nantasket settlement in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  Roger was offered serve in the church of Boston while the Rev. John Wilson traveled to England to bring his wife over to Boston.  Roger declined the offer and began speaking and writing about his ideas.
