He wrote a book saying it was wrong to punish people for having different beliefs.  Then he said that the European settlers were stealing the Native Americans' land.  He said the king of England had no right to permit people to settle on land that was not his.

The Puritan leaders of the Massachusetts Bay Colony were not pleased with Roger William’s attack on the Church of England and England’s king.  The Colony’s General Court in Boston ordered Roger Williams to leave the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  Roger bought land south of the colony from local Native people and started a new town named “Providence”.  The English Parliament gave Roger permission to establish a new colony of Rhode Island, with the town Providence as its capital.  As a new colony, Rhode Island accepted people of all religious beliefs, including Catholics, Quakers, Jews and even people who denied the existence of God.  Roger Williams also believed that governments should have no connection to a church.

In 1630, Sir Richard Saltonstall paddled up the Charles River from the town of Charlestown and established the site of the new Saltonstall Plantation, one of the earliest settlements in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  The site had been inhabited for thousands of years before the arrival of English settlers.  The Native American tribes; the “Massachusetts”, the “Pequossette” and the “Nonantum”, had settlements communities on the banks of the Charles River.  The Pequossette people built a fishing weir to trap the annual spring migration of both alewife and blueback herring fish that swim upstream from the ocean to spawn in fresh water.  The new Saltonstall plantation was officially incorporated a year later as “Waterton” that later was renamed “Watertown”.  The new settlement quickly grew and its first gristmill was installed in 1632.  Watertown was the chief horse and cattle raising market in New England and was known for its fertile gardens and fine estates.  In 1632, the residents of Watertown refused to pay a tax to support the new settlement of Cambridge that was located 5 miles east because the tax was levied on them without their permission.  Thus, the cry of “No taxation without representation” originated there.
In 1631, George Wyllys and Mary Brisbey were married in Stoke, Warwickshire, England
In 1639, Mary Willis was born to George Wyllys and Mary (Brisbey) in Roxbury, Massachusetts Bay Colony.  No information has been found on when George and Mary arrived in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.
About 1630, Hugh Maxwell was born to Edwin Hugh Maxwell and Marian (Anderson) in Airth, Stirlingshire, Scotland.
On 5 Jun 1631, Elizabeth Wooton sailed from England in the Winthrop fleet to the Massachusetts Bay Colony.
In 1631, Nathaniel Gary was born to Arthur Gary and Frances (Warman) in the village of Hadham, Hertfordshire, England.
In 1631, Agnes Bent was born to John Bent and Martha (Blanchard) in the village of Wayhill, Hampshire, England.

Note:  The relative income of Massachusetts Bay settlers was far more equitable than that of other regions, such as the Virginia Colony.  The climate of the Massachusetts Bay did not lend itself to a particular cash crop.  There were no large plantations with teams of workers working for a powerful large land owner.  Instead, the people of Massachusetts Bay were mostly small family farmers and small fishing, lumber, and fur family businesses.  Skill craftsmen set up shops in the new farming towns to provide local services for the town and surrounding farms.

Note:  In 1631 the Massachusetts Bay Colony passed a law that limiting eligibility for one to become a “freeman” to those men who were members of Orthodox Congregational Churches.  (Congregational churches were Protestant Christian churches practicing Congregationalist Church governance, in which each congregation independently and autonomously rans its own affairs.).  Freemen were the only ones eligible to hold township positions and were eligible to vote.  Only about 20% of the male colonists at the time were “freemen”.
On Mar 1632, William Perkins sailed from England on the ship "William & Francis" in the Winthrop fleet to the Massachusetts Bay Colony, where he settled in the town of Boston.
On 21 May 1632, Ralph Shepard and Thankslorde Perkins were married in St. Bride Church on Fleet St., London, England.

On 5 Jun 1632, John Leavens Sr. and his wife Elizabeth (Stebbins) arrived in the Massachusetts Bay Colony on the ship “William and Francis”.  On 4 Mar 1634, John Leavens Sr. was made a “freeman” in Roxbury, Massachusetts Bay Colony.
On 6 Nov 1632, Thomas French Jr. was made a “freeman” in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.

In 1632, John Ramsdell Sr. left England and sailed with the Winthrop fleet to the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  John was an indentured servant to Captain Nathaniel Turner.  Later John settled in Rumney Marsh in Lynn (now Saugus) where he farmed.

Note:  The town of Lynn, in the Massachusetts Bay Colony was an early center of leather making tanning operations and shoe-making in New England.  The boots the worn by the Continental Army during the Revolutionary War were made in Lynn.
In 1632, George Alcock was made a deacon in the Dorchester church, since there were not enough people living yet in Roxbury to constitute a church in the new community.

Note:  In 1632, Galileo published his “Dialogue Concerning the Two Chief World Systems,” which proved from Galileo’s observation through his new telescope that mathematically the sun had to be at the center of the universe to explain the movement of the planets.  Galileo had also observed that Venus went through phases just like the Earth’s moon, which only could occur if Venus was circling the sun.  Galileo was summoned before the Roman Catholic “Inquisition” in 1633 and was convicted of “vehement suspicion of heresy” and under threat of torture Galileo was forced to express sorrow and curse his errors.  This was one of the many actions taken by the Catholic Church that weaken its support and strengthen the Protestant’s positions.

On 12 Jun 1630, the fleet’ flagship the “Arabella” of the Winthrop fleet was investigating the region around the earlier Cape Ann settlement and met and entertained, the Native chief of the Agawam Native tribe, Chief Masconomet.  The event was recorded in John Winthrop's journal.  The newly arrived Puritans spent the first winter in Charlestown where previous English settlers were living.  That first winter hundreds of the new Puritan colonists died from malnutrition and disease.  They also experienced their first “nor’easter” storm,  which cost them some fingers and toes, and many of their houses were destroyed in fires.  On 16 Sep 1630, just as John Winthrop was handing out the last handfuls of their grain supply to the famished settlers the ship “Lyon” from England entered Boston Harbor with fresh supplies of much needed food for the starving Puritans living in Charleston in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  John Winthrop Sr. had sent for his family back in England and his wife Margaret their children, including eldest son, John Jr. from John Winthrop’s first wife, Mary Forth, arrived back on the rescue ship the “Lyon”

John the Younger, the name given to John Winthrop Jr. resided with his father and stepmother In Charleston until 1633, when he resolved to settle in Agawam Native area, with the permission of the General Court of Massachusetts.  Captain John Smith had previously written about the Agawam area back in 1614, calling it "an excellent habitation, being a good and safe harbor”.  John the Younger and William Clerk, Robert Coles, Thomas Howlet, John Biggs, John Gage, Thomas Hardy, William Perkins, John Thorndike, William Sergeant, and three others left Charleston and sailed into Ipswich harbor and took up residence there.  Two men continued up the river (now River Road) to a large meadow, which they called New Meadows, now Topsfield.  That same year Agawam was incorporated on August 5, 1634, as Ipswich, after the town of Ipswich back in England.  While Chief Masconomet survived, most of his Agawam people did not as they succumbed from the European diseases introduced by the newly arriving English settlers.
On 4 Aug 1633, Ralph Farnham Jr. was born to Ralph Farnham Sr. and Alice (Harris) in St. Nicholas Church, Rochester in Kent, England.
In 1633, Nicholas Holt and Elizabeth Short were married in Romsey, Hampshire, England.

In 1633, England’s King Charles I appointed William Laud as the new Archbishop of Canterbury and head of the Anglian Church of England.  William Laud immediately began rooting out nonconformity in the church.  His actions resulted in an increase number of Puritans leaving England and sailing to the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  One of the targets of Archbishop Laud’s was Rev. Thomas Hooker a Puritan who had fled to the city of Rotterdam in the Netherlands to avoid being captured.  Thomas Hooker was the older brother on Anne Hooker who married George Alcock back in 1625 in England.  Rev. Thomas Hooker immigrated to the Massachusetts Bay Colony in 1632 aboard the ship “Griffin” that carried 200 Puritans to Boston.  Rev. Thomas Hooker settled in Newtown (later remained Cambridge, Massachusetts) where he became the pastor of the earliest established church there, known to its members as "The Church of Christ at Cambridge."  His congregation, some of whom may have been members of congregations he had served in England, became known as "Mr. Hooker's Company".
On 2 Mar 1634, Mary French was born to Thomas French Jr. and Mary (Scudamore) in Boston, Massachusetts Bay Colony.

In 24 Apr 1634, Archbishop William Laud was persecuting non-conformists (a Protestant who did not conform to the authority of the Anglican Church of England).  Ralph Shepard was summoned before a Court of High Commissions.  There is no record of the results of the court appearance, but shortly after the summons Ralph Shepard and his wife Thankslorde (Perkins) left England and sailed to America.

On 4 Jun 1634, Deacon William Ward and Elizabeth Storey were married in St. Sepulcher Church in London, England.  Elizabeth was William’s second wife.  His first wife had recently died leaving William along to raise his three young children.

On 3 Sep 1634, William Perkins was made a “freeman” in the town of Roxbury in the Massachusetts Bay Colony where he owned twenty-five acres of land.  William was a Sergeant who was promoted to Ensign (the first officer rank in the English military) in the Militia Company of Roxbury.  William was admitted to the Roxbury church as member #114.
In Feb 1634, Elizabeth (Sale) wife of William Preston and mother of their eight children including their son fifteen-year-old son Edward Preston, died in Chesham, Buckinghamshire, England.
In 1634, widower William Preston married Mary Seabrook in Chesham, Buckinghamshire, England.
In 1634 ,Rebecca Hills was born to Joseph Hills and Rose (Clarke) Malden, Essex, England.
About 1634, Thomas Barrett and Margaret Huntington were married in England.

Note:  In 1634, a document (book) titled “Wood’s New England’s Prospect” was published that described the contemporary account of life in the New England.  The first addition appeared in 1634 and in 1635 & 1637 more volumes followed.  The books talked about the weather, the area soils, the wild beasts, birds and fishes on the several “plantations” that had established in New England.  It provided a list of what a new colonist should bring with them when they sailed across the ocean.  The books were in great demand in England and were responsible for creating excitement for the many looking for a better life.  Previously information on life in the New World had been limited to stories from those who wrote letters back to family and friends in England and what had been told to them by their church leaders.
A copy of the Wood’s New England’s Prospect can be view at   See a sample on the next page:

blocks.caption: A copy of the Wood’s New England’s Prospect can be view at See a sample on the next page:

blocks.text: In Feb 1635, twenty-three-year-old Mary Baldwin, whose parents had both died when Mary was just three-year-old, arrived in Charlestown from England and joined the church in Charlestown.  Mary’s future husband Thomas Richardson and his brother Samuel both joined the church in Charlestown on 18 Feb 1637 and were later admitted as “freemen” in the Massachusetts Bay Colony on 2 Mar 1638.
On 21 Feb 1635, Thomas Richardson and Mary Baldwin were married in Charlestown, Massachusetts Bay Colony.

On 3 Apr 1635, twelve-year-old William Peacock left on the ship “Hopewell” from London, England with sixty-six-year-old Lawrence Whittemore and his fifty-seven-year-old wife and sailed to the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  William Peacock lived with Lawrence Whittemore and his wife, possible as an indenture servant or a “binding out” child, in Roxbury in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.

Note:  The American colonies did not invent the practice of “binding out” poor children; they inherited the practice.  From the early 1600s, local authorities in both back in England and in America Colonies regarded “pauper apprenticeship” as an acceptable, and even a desirable, way to raise the children of the poor.  Communities on both sides of the Atlantic shared similar assumptions about the powers of the local authorities—to decide whether a poor family was able to raise its own children; to identify and to negotiate terms with an appropriate master and then place the child in the master’s home.  Orphans’ courts monitored estate administration for the unfortunate and bound out poorer children to caregivers who benefited from the children’s labor.

On 8 Apr 1635, Roger Preston left England and sailed on the ship “Elizabeth” as part of the Winthrop fleet and arrived in Boston, in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  Roger settled in new town of Ipswich, in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.

On 20 Apr 1635, Thomas French Jr. and his wife Mary (Scudamore) and their one-year-old daughter Mary French, who had left Boston and moved to Ipswich in the Massachusetts Bay Colony were granted a town lot in Ipswich on Bridge Street just off Mill Street.

On 5 May 1635, sixteen-year-old Edward Preston, arrived in the Massachusetts Bay Colony with James Buskett age 28 and Thomas Cooper age 18, on the ship “Christian” from Weymouth, Dorsetshire, England with Rev. Joseph Hull.  The ship had twenty families and servants from the Batcombe and Broadway parishes in Somersetshire, England.

On 6 May 1635, John Upham, his pregnant wife Elizabeth (Webb) and their three children along with John’s sister Sarah left Weymouth, England and sailed to the Massachusetts Bay Colony, with Rev. Joseph Hull and twenty other families.  On 2 Sep 1635 John Upham was admitted as a “freeman” in Weymouth, Massachusetts Bay Colony.

Note:  On 7 Jun 1635, Rev. Joseph Hull arrived in Dorchester in the Massachusett Bay Colony with a congregation of 104 church members from Weymouth, England.  Rev. Hull's willingness to preach without approval from Church of England church officials and his efforts to chart a middle course between Anglicans and Puritans resulted in repeated conflicts with religious and colonial authorities.  On 8 Jul 1635 Rev. Joseph Hull and twenty-one family followers were given permission by the Massachusetts Bay Colony’s General Court to establish the Wessagusset Plantation 15 miles south of Boston.  Almost all of the Native Americans Wessagusset tribal members who had lived in the area had recently died in a smallpox epidemic.  On 2 Sep 1635, the plantation was renamed Weymouth after the town in England where most of the new arrivals had left from.

Note:  In 1635, the first settlers sailed up through Plum Island Sound and up the Quascacunquen River, which was later renamed the Parker River, to arrive in the new Newbury settlement location.  There had been a few fishermen occupying the banks of the Merrimack and Quascacunquen Rivers before this, but they were not permanent settlers.  On 6 May 1635, before the settlers had moved from Ipswich to Newbury, the Massachusetts General Court passed a resolution that Quascacunquen was to be established as a plantation and its name was to be changed to Newbury.  The settlers of Newbury, Massachusetts Bay Colony were not religious enthusiasts or pilgrims who fled from religious persecution in England.  They were substantial, law abiding, loyal English tradesmen, of the staunch middle class that were the backbone of England society.  The new settlers arrived in Newbury Plantation in May and June of 1635.  Governor Winthrop Sr., in his history of New England under the date of June 3, 1635, records the arrival of two ships full of Dutch cattle along with a third ship the "James", from Southampton, England bringing new settlers.  Newbury settlement was begun as a stock raising enterprise and the settlers came to raise cattle and to establish homes for themselves.  In total fifteen ships came in June and one each in August, November and December bringing still more families to the growing Newbury settlement in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  See the map on the next page of the Plum Island and the town of Newbury:

blocks.caption: Note: In 1635, the first settlers sailed up through Plum Island Sound and up the Quascacunquen River, which was later…

blocks.text: On 3 Jun 1635, Nicholas Holt and his wife Elizabeth (Short) left England and sailed to the Massachusetts Bay Colony on the ship “James”.  The family settled in the new town of Newbury, 38 miles north of Boston, where Nicholas established and operated a tannery (tanning animal hides  to make leather), worked as a cooper (someone who makes and repairs wooden barrels), and performed dish turning (someone who makes wooded dishes).  Nicholas built a house on the four-acres of land he was allotted.  Nicholas also received forty acres of land over the Little River and thirty acres of upland and meadow land bounded by the Great Creek.  Later in 1636, Nicholas was elected the town of Newbury’s representative to the General Court in Boston and reelected to the General Court the next two years.

In 1635, Ralph Farnham Sr. sailed from Southampton, England also on the ship “James” in Jun 1635 in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  Ralph and wife Alice (Harris) and their three children: eight-year-old daughter Mary, four-year-old son Thomas and two-year-old son Ralph Jr. settled in the new town of Ipswich, 30 miles north of Boston, and Ralph Farnham Sr. made his living as a “barber”.  Barbers in the 1600s in addition to cutting hair and giving men a shave, perform minor surgical procedures such as bloodletting, teeth pulling and cupping therapy (an ancient form of alternative medicine in which special cups were placed on one’s skin for a few minutes to create suction to help relieve pain and inflammation, increase blood flow to the area to improving one’s overall well-being as a type of deep-tissue massage).  Ralph was chosen to ring the church bell, keep the town meetinghouse clean and published such things as the “Town shall appointments” in Ipswich.

On 4 Jul 1635, Ralph Shepard, wife Thankslord (Perkins) and their two-year-old daughter left the Stepney Parish in London, England and arrived in the town of Boston in the Massachusetts Bay Colony on the ship “Abigail of London ”, which was carrying passengers infected with smallpox.  Ralph and Thankslorde who were both Puritans and Calvinists settled in the town of Weymouth where Ralph worked as a “tailor” making cloths.

Arthur Gary his wife Frances (Warman) and their four-year-old son Nathaniel Gary also arrived on the ship “Abigail of London” with the smallpox infected passengers in the town of Boston in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  Arthur Gary and his family moved 4 miles west from Boston and settled in the town of Roxbury in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  Arthur and his wife Frances were early members of the First Church of Roxbury and Arthur was one of the first subscribers to the Roxbury town grammar school, the first free school in the English American Colonies.  Arthur Gary was granted seven acres of land next to the great pond and Edward Bugbie’s property towards the path to the town of Dedham abutting Phillip Elliott property.

On 19 Sep 1635, William Preston his new wife Mary (Seabrook) and their four children from William’s first marriage to Elizabeth (Sale) arrived from England on the ship “Truelove” with the Winthrop fleet in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  Their sixteen-year-old son Edward Preston had arrived earlier on the ship “Christian” and their fourteen-year-old son Daniel Preston arrive later on the ship “Elizabeth & Ann”.  It appears the family sent their two oldest sons on separate ships to avoid losing all of the family if a ship disaster occurred at sea.
On 15 Oct 1635, Walter Powers Sr. and Joane Newman were married in Bremhill, Wiltshire, England.

In 1635, Thomas Green Sr., his wife Elizabeth (Lynde) and their son Thomas Jr. also arrived on the ship “Hopewell” to the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  They settled in the town of Malden were Thomas farmed on 63 acres.
In 1635, Mary Baldwin and her brother John Baldwin left England and sailed on the ship “Speedwell” to the Massachusetts Bay Colony.
In 1635, John Johnson was born to Solomon Johnson and Eleanor (Crofts) in London, Middlesex, England.

In 1635, William Towne his wife Joanna (Blessing) and their thirteen-year-old daughter Martha sailed from England as part of the Winthrop fleet and settled in the town of Salem in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  William initially farmed 40 acres in the Topsfield area, ten-miles northwest of Salem.  William Towne later received a land grant "a littleneck of land right over against his house on the other side of the Waters River" in an area called North Fields, that was just three miles north of Salem in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.

In 1635, Phineas Upham was born to Deacon John Upham and Elizabeth (Slade) in the town of Weymouth, Massachusetts Bay Colony.  During this period Deacon John Upham was one of three residents in Weymouth who were selected to represent Weymouth in the Massachusetts Bay Colony’s General Count.

About 1635, Thomas Barrett and his wife Margaret (Huntington) arrived in the town of Watertown, up the Charles River in Massachusetts Bay Colony as part of the Winthrop Fleet.  Thomas later settled in the town of Braintree in the Massachusetts Bay Colony down near the border with the Plymouth Colony.

Note:  The name Rhode Island came from the name of "Aquidneck Island" derived from the  name for the island “aquidnet”.   was an authority on the Narragansett language and said: "Aquethneck shall be henceforth called the Ile of Rods."  See map below of the new Rhode Island Colony.  The city of Newport is on the Aquidneck Island.

blocks.caption: Note: The name Rhode Island came from the name of "Aquidneck Island" derived from the name for the island “aquidnet”.…

blocks.text: On 30 Mar 1636, Elizabeth Holt was born to Nicholas Holt and Elizabeth Short in the new town of Newbury in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.
On 12 Jun 1636, at a town meeting in Weymouth, Massachusetts John Upham was awarded 30 acres of land for himself and his family to build a home and farm.

In Jun 1636, Ruth Wood was born to unknown parents in England.  At a later date, Ruth Wood became a “child ward” along with Mary Grove of Ralph Mousall and his wife Alice and their family in the town of Charlestown in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  Ralph Mousall and his wife Alice and their two sons had arrived in the town of Charlestown in 1630 from England.  Ralph Mousall was a church Deacon of the Charleston and a successful farmer in Charleston.  The English process of giving children of poor families to other successful families was called “binding out”.  Ruth Wood fits the patten of a “binding out” child.
On 26 Jul 1636, William Douglas and Ann Mattle were married in Ringstead, Northamptonshire, England.

On 30 Aug 1636, William Perkins and Elizabeth Wooton were married in the town of Roxbury in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  No information has been found on when Elizabeth Wooton arrived in New England.
On 9 Sep 1636, Deborah Ward was born to Deacon William Ward and Elizabeth (Storey) in Clerkenwell, Middlesex, England.

In 1636, Richard Sawtelle and his wife Elizabeth (Pople) left England and sailed to the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  (More than five ships arrived in the Massachusetts Bay Colony in 1636, but no records of their passengers were kept.)  Richard and Elizabeth settled in the town of Watertown and in Jul 1636, received 25 acres of land in Watertown.

sections.title: Connecticut Colony
sections.teaser: The beginning of the Connecticut Colony began back in 1610 when the Dutch established the first trading post on the Connecticut River Valley in what would be…

blocks.text: The beginning of the Connecticut Colony began back in 1610 when the Dutch established the first trading post on the Connecticut River Valley in what would become the town of Hartford.  The word "Connecticut" is a French corruption of the Native American word “quinetucket”, which means "beside the long, tidal river”.  The later move into the Connecticut River Valley was part of a general movement out of the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  By the mid-1630s, the population in and around Boston had grown so dense that settlers began to move out throughout southern New England, concentrating on navigable river valleys such as Connecticut River.  The man credited as the founder of the English Connecticut Colony was Rev. Thomas Hooker, an English yeoman, and clergyman born in 1586 in Markfield, Leicester, England.  He was educated at Cambridge College in England and was one of the most well-educated and powerful preachers of both old and New England.  He was a nonconformist  who was targeted for suppression by the English government under King Charles I and was forced to flee to Holland.  Governor John Winthrop of the Massachusetts Bay Colony wrote to Rev. Hooker in 1628, asking him to come to Massachusetts.  Rev. Thomas Hooker arrived in 1633 and was made pastor of the town church in Newton in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.

Voting in 1633 in the Massachusetts Bay Colony was limited to “Freemen”, individuals who had been formally admitted to their church after a detailed interrogation of their religious views and experiences.  Rev Thomas Hooker disagreed with this limitation of voting suffrage, putting him at odds with the influential Massachusetts Bay Colony Pastor .  By May of 1634, Rev. Hooker and his congregation in Newtown, Massachusetts Bay Colony petitioned to leave for Connecticut.  In May 1636, they were allowed to go, and they were provided a commission by the General Court of Massachusetts for establishing a new colony.

Note:  On 13 Dec 1636, the National Guard started when the General Court of the Massachusetts Bay Colony ordered the colony's militia companies into three regiments: the North, South and East Regiments.  The colonists had adopted the English militia system which obligated all males, between the ages of 16 and 60, to possess arms and participate in the defense of the community.  The early colonial militias drilled once a week and provided guardsmen for details each evening to stand watch and sound the alarm in the event of an attack.

In 1637, Rev. Thomas Hooker, his wife, and about 100 members of his church congregation left Boston and drove 160 head of cattle overland and establishing the towns of Wethersfield, Hartford and Windsor on the Connecticut River.  The new Connecticut colonists used the Massachusetts' “civil and ecclesiastical laws” to set up their initial government but discarded the requirement that only members of approved churches could become “freemen”.  Most people who came to the  came as indentured servants.  According to English law, it was only after a man had paid or worked off his indentured servant contract that he could apply to become a member of the church and own lands.  In Connecticut whether a man was indentured servant or not, if he entered a colony as a free person and successfully completed a 1-to-2-years probationary period to make sure he was an upright Puritan, he would be accepted as a “freeman” in Connecticut.  If he failed during his probationary period, he could be forced to leave the colony.  Only 229 men were admitted as “freemen” in Connecticut between 1639 and 1662.  Rev. Thomas Hooker is connected to Edith Powers’ family tree through his sister Anne Hooker who married George Alcock in 1625 in England.  See map below of the early Connecticut Colony.

blocks.caption: In 1637, Rev. Thomas Hooker, his wife, and about 100 members of his church congregation left Boston and drove 160 hea…

blocks.text: In 1637,  William Preston, wife Mary (Seabrook) and their children left the Massachusetts Bay Colony and moved to the New Haven settlement, 138 miles southwest of Boston in the Connecticut Colony, that was established by Rev. .  William Preston worked a “solicitor” (English lawyer) in New Haven.  No records were kept of who were included in the 100 plus members of Rev. Hooker’s church congregation that walked with the cattle to New Haven, but based on the times and locations involved there is a good chance William Preston and his family left as part of “Mr. Hooker’s Company” that walked to New Haven.

sections.title: Pequot War
sections.teaser: Between Jul 1636 and Sep 1638, the “Pequot War” occurred when various Native American tribes fought for control of beaver fur pelts trading business in the N…

blocks.text: Between Jul 1636 and Sep 1638, the “Pequot War” occurred when various Native American tribes fought for control of beaver fur pelts trading business in the New England area.  In the 1630s, the whole Connecticut River Valley region was in an uproar.  The Pequot Native tribe aggressively extended its control over the Connecticut River Valley at the expense of the Wampanoag Native tribe that lived north of the valley, the Narragansett Native tribe that lived to the east, Mohegan Native tribe that lived to the west and the Algonquian Native tribe that lived in Connecticut River Valley itself.  The Lenape Algonquian Native people that lived out on Long Island were also impacted.  The Pequot Native tribe aligned itself with the Dutch and its fur trading company against English colonists and their Mohegans tribe allies for control of the beaver fur trading business with European fur buyers.

In the 1630’s, a series of European disease epidemics occurred, which severely reduced the number of Native people living in and around the Connecticut River Valley and a power vacuum resulted.  The Pequot Native tribe’s territory was the southeastern area of what today is the State of Connecticut.  The Pequot’s, with support of the Dutch fur traders, tried to prevent other tribes from trading with the English fur trading companies.  Fighting started after English fur trader John Stone and a member of his crew were murdered by the Pequots in the summer of 1634.  The Pequots admitted killing the traders, but defended their actions as a retribution (payback) for the killing on one of their people by the Dutch.  The Pequot viewed the English and Dutch as the same outside intruders on their land.

In Jul 1636, another English fur trader John Oldham was murder by Narragansett warriors who were allied with the Pequot tribe.  News of John Oldham's death became the subject of sermons in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  In Aug 1636, the Massachusetts Bay Colony’s Governor Vane sent John Endecott, with 90 militia soldiers, to exact revenge on the Pequot Natives for the killings and a small scrimmage occurred with the Pequots on Block Island, off the coast of the Rhode Island Colony, however the Pequot warriors and their families were able to escape the attack by leaving on their boats.  The Massachusetts Bay militia burned the Pequot village on the island to the ground.  They also carried away the Pequot harvested crops, which had been stored in the island for the upcoming winter.  John Endecott and his soldiers next continued on to Fort Saybrook on the Atlantic coast where the Connecticut River flows into the ocean, where he acquired additional English colonists’ soldiers familiar with the area as guides.  John Endecott’s military militia then sailed west along the coast to a Pequot village, where he repeated the previous year's demand for identification of those responsible for the death of John Stone, and now also for those who murdered John Oldham.  After some discussion, John Endecott concluded that the Pequots were stalling and attacked their village, but most of the Pequots were able to escaped into the woods.  John Endecott again had his soldiers burn down the Pequot’s village and took their stored crops before sailing home.

The Pequots attempted to get other tribes to join them in the fight with English colonists, but were only partly successful in getting their traditional enemies to help, and the Mohegan and the Narragansett tribes openly sided with the English colonists.  The Narragansetts had lost the fur trapping territory to the Pequots back in 1622.  Through the autumn and winter of 1636, Fort Saybrook was effectively besieged by the Pequots warriors.  When spring arrived in 1637, the Pequots stepped up their raids on Connecticut towns.  They attacked a number of English towns killing men, women, cattle and horses, and took two young girls’ captive.  (The girls were daughters of William Swaine and were ransomed back by Dutch traders.)  In all, the English Connecticut towns lost about thirty settlers.

On 17 Nov 1636, Rev. Nathaniel Rogers, the Vicar of Assington in Suffolk, England, which was the home of John Winthrop, immigrated to New England and became the Pastor of the Ipswich town Church in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  The town of Ipswich had a number of residents from the Assington, England whom had moved to New England to escape the religious harassment back in England.

On 20 Feb 1637 , Sergeant Thomas French Jr. was granted ten acres of upland property at the “end of Neck” in Ipswich beyond Reed Salt Marsh and three additional acres of adjoining meadow land.  The land grant process provides the first indication that Thomas French Jr. was a sergeant in the Massachusetts Bay Colony militia.  Thomas French Jr’s parent Thomas French Sr. and Susan (Riddlesdale) and his three sisters soon join Thomas Jr. in the town of Ipswich.
In 1637, Sergeant Thomas French Jr. fought in the “Pequot War”, for which he 1672 was later granted land northwest of Salisbury along with eight other Ipswich’ town men who fought in the war.

In May 1637, leaders of Connecticut River towns met in the town of Hartford and raised a new militia, and placed Captain John Mason in command.  Captain Mason set out with ninety militiamen and seventy Mohegan warriors to attack the Pequots at their fort.  At Fort Saybrook, Captain Mason was joined by John Underhill with another twenty militiamen.  John Underhill and Captain Mason then sailed east from Fort Saybrook to Narragansett Bay in Rhoda Island, a tactic intended to mislead Pequot spies along the shoreline into thinking that the English were not intending an attack.  After gaining the support of an additional 200 Narragansett warriors, Captain Mason and John Underhill marched their combined forces approximately twenty miles towards Pequot stronghold at Fort Mystic.  They briefly camped at Porter's Rocks near the head of the Mystic River before mounting a surprise attack just before dawn.  See a map on the next page showing the major events of the Pequot War:

blocks.caption: In May 1637, leaders of Connecticut River towns met in the town of Hartford and raised a new militia, and placed Capt…

blocks.text: The “Mystic Massacre” started in the pre-dawn hours of 26 May 1637, when colonial forces, along with their Mohegan and Narragansett warriors, surrounded one of two main fortified Pequot villages at Mystic.  Twenty militiamen successfully breached the Pequot fortified village’s palisade's gate, but they were quickly overwhelmed and had set a fire to create chaos to facilitate their escape.  The majority of the Pequots who managed to escape the spreading fire were soon slain by the colonial soldiers and their supporting Native warriors who quickly surrounded the fortified village.  An estimated 500 Pequots were killing in the fighting and the fire and only seven were taken prisoner and another seven escaped to the woods.

The Narragansetts and Mohegans warriors who had fought with Captain Mason and Underhill's colonial militia were horrified by the savage killings that occurred in the attack.  The Narragansetts attempted to leave and return to their home but were cut off by Pequots from the other village and had to be rescued by the colonial militia causing them to reluctantly rejoined the colonists for protection and were utilized to carry the wounded, thereby freeing up more militia soldiers to fend off the numerous attacks that occurred along the withdrawal route.

The destruction of Native Pequot people and their villages at Mystic Fort and the additional losses that occurred during the withdrawal pursuit broke the remaining roughly 400 Pequot warriors’ spirit, and they decided to abandon their villages and flee westward to seek refuge with the Mohawk Native tribe.  In mid-Jun 1637, Captain John Mason next set out from the town of Saybrook with 160 colonial militia solders and 40 Mohegan warrior scouts and caught up with the Pequot refugees at village near present-day Fairfield Connecticut.  The Colonial militia force surrounded the Pequot village allowed several hundred Pequot to surrender, mostly were women and children, but before dawn eighty Pequot warriors escaped and continued west.  Sassacus, who was the leader of the escaped Pequot War Party, hoped to gain refuge among the Mohawk in present-day New York.  However, the Mohawk instead murdered him and his bodyguard and sent his head and hands back to Hartford Connecticut (for reasons which were never made clear).  The Pequot War finally ended, but colonial officials continued to call for hunting down the remaining Pequots warriors after war's end.  Mind were changed and asylum was granted to any Pequots who went to live with the Narragansetts or Mohegans tribes.

Note:  Private initiative and capital had always been involved in the operations of the British colonies, and the colonists were expected to provide for their own defense.  By 1636, ten “Train Bands” (volunteer military companies) were formed in Boston and nearby towns.  In 1637 the “Military Company of the Massachusetts” was organized to provide training for the officers in the volunteer military companies.  In 1638, a charter of incorporation was issued under the authority of Governor John Winthrop and the colony’s General Council.  Many of the original colonial members of the military training company had been members in England of the ‘Honorable Artillery Company of London” (chartered by King Henry VIII in 1537 as the “Fraternity of St. George”).  Around the time of the American Revolution, the “Military Company of the Massachusetts” change of name to the “Ancient and Honorable Artillery Company of Massachusetts.”  The company still exists today and is the oldest military organization on the North American continent.
In 1637, William Sterling was born to David Stirling and Sarah (Watson) in London, England.
On 17 Jul 1637, Joseph Hill, his wife Rose (Clarke) and their daughter Rebecca sailed on the ship “Susan & Ellen” from London to the Massachusetts Bay Colony.
In 1637, Anne Mattle Douglas was born to William Douglas and Ann (Mattle) in England.

In 1637, Nicholas Holt was one of ten men from the town of Newbury who traveled 40 miles on foot to qualify for the “freeman oath” so they could vote for John Winthrop in the election for Governor of the Massachusetts Bay Colony to prevent Sir Henry Vanc from being re-elected.  Their efforts succeeded and John Winthrop won the election.

In 1637, Thomas Richardson and Samuel Richardson were granted a house-lot, clearly indicating that they had recently become citizens of the town of Charlestown.  Thomas Richardson with his brother had a lot assigned on "Mystic River side and above the Ponds" that became the town of Malden in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.

Note:  On 1 Sep 1638, Governor John Winthrop stopped recording information on the passengers arriving in ships in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  That year over twenty ships arrive in the colony.  The greatest success of the New England colonies was their achievement in attracting a continuous flow of settlers to their shores.  The Dutch and French colonies both suffered from a lack of new arriving settlers causing them to fall behind England in establishing their new colonies.
On 1 May 1638, Elizabeth Sawtelle was born to Richard Sawtelle and Elizabeth (Pople) in the town of Watertown in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.

On 20 Apr 1638, the three Richardson brothers, Ezekiel, Samuel and Thomas were assigned home lots in the town of Charleston on “Mystic River Side above the pond”, which later became the town of Malden.

On 17 Jul 1638, Joseph Hills, his wife Rose (Clark) and their daughter Rebecca left England and sailed on the ship “Susan & Ellen” to the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  Joseph was an “undertaker” (one of stockholder who finances the voyage).  Joseph Hills opened the first woolen draper shop (retailer/wholesaler of wool cloth) in Charlestown.  In 1645, Joseph Hills was admitted as a “freeman” in the town of Charlestown.  Joseph and his family next moved north of the Mystic River to the new town of Malden.  In 1646, Joseph was elected the “Deputy” from the town of Malden to the Massachusetts Bay Colony’s General Court and a year later was selected as “Speaker” of the House of Deputies in the General Court.  Joseph Hills was on many committees, but his greatest public service was that of leading a committee that developed the “first written codification of laws” for the new Massachusetts Bay Colony at the request of the colony’s General Court.

On 10 Oct 1638, Ensign William Perkins signed his name as a member of a surveying party led by Nathaniel Woodward.  The surveying party calculated the latitude of the most southernly part of the Charles River in an effort to the determination and identify the boundary line between the Massachusetts Bay Colony and the Plymouth Colony.

Note:  In the 1600’s one’s latitude was determined by measuring the angle between the horizon and the North Star.  An instrument called an “astrolabe” had been invented that consisted of a large brass ring fitted with an alidade (sighting ruler).  The user held the astrolabe from a loop at the top and then turned the alidade so that one could see the North star along its length, and then read the altitude off the scale engraved on the ring.  The North Star, however, was often obscured by clouds, fog, or daylight so navigators and surveyors learned to use the astrolabe with quadrant (quarter-circle made of wood or brass), and across-staff (square staff of wood 4 feet long with scales on each side.  A piece of smoked glass was frequently used to keep the user from blinding oneself when looking at the sun.  Highly prized and valuable declination tables and astronomical charts were next used to convert the angle that was determined to the distance of one’s current location from the Earth’s equator in miles so one’s North/South location could be found on a map.  The other East/West location “longitude” that is needed to determine one’s location east and west was not available until much later when accurate clocks were developed.  See a photo of an astrolabe on the left and an astrolabe with quadrant on the right on the next page:

images.caption: Note: In the 1600’s one’s latitude was determined by measuring the angle between the horizon and the North Star. An i…
Note: In the 1600’s one’s latitude was determined by measuring the angle between the horizon and the North Star. An i…

blocks.text: In 1638, Ensign William Perkins was admitted to the “Ancient and Honorable Artillery Company” of Massachusetts.
In 1638, Edmund Rice, his wife Thomasine (Frost) and their sixteen-year-old son Edward Rice sailed from England and arrived in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.

Note:  A book “Edmund Rice and his Family” by Elsie Hawes Smith describes the life Edmund Rice and Thomasine (Frost) faced when they arrived in America.  King Charles I, had recently become king of England.  The new king was viewed as selfish and ill-advised and made life for the English an unhappy experience for most people.  Taxes were high and opportunities to better one’s self were practically non-existent.  A few of the more adventurist souls looking for better opportunities chose to take a long hazardous voyage to the New World hoping for a new start and a better life.  Edmund Rice, wife Thomasine and their children set sail in 1638 for the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  The ship they sailed on had limited space for baggage and Edmund’s family was limited to only taking a few head of cattle, goats and some farm tools.  They also brought guns and ammunition and seeds for growing crops.  Thomasine brought a carved chest filled with blankets, linens, extra clothing and utensils.  They arrived with other families with similar belongings ready to make their new home in America.  Most of their home family furniture had to be left in England.  English Puritans had been arriving the Massachusetts Bay Colony since 1630 and Edmund and Thomasine had friends in the colony, they were able to stayed with until they had their own home to live in.  Edmund noticed new fortifications around the towns of Dorchester and Charlestown had recently been built to defend against a military invasion by England, which King Charles I might launch, to gain control over the Protestant Puritan colony.  At this time the Massachusetts Bay Colony was operating under its own self-government that was busy handing out land to new arrivals and putting down Native American warriors’ attacks.

A group of men, including Edmund Rice, who were living in the town of Watertown had recently been granted the right to establish a new plantation called “New Concord” in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  Edmund Rice with other men established the bounders for the new settlement.  The first house lots comprised a few acres each.  The homes were designed to be built close together for security and safety.  Before Edmund started to build his new home, he sold his property in New Concord and bought a house and six acres from widow Axdell.  Edmund next leased for six years a farm of Henry Dunster, the first president of Harvard College.  The lease required Edmund to pay Henry Dunster thirty bushels of corn a year for two years, then fifty bushels each for the next two years and finally one hundred bushels for the last two years in equal proportions of wheat, Indian corn and rye.  At the end of the six years Edmund bought the farm from Henry.  Later Edmund bought nine more acres and a house that expanded his homestead.  The homestead stayed in the Rice family for many generations.  The town of Concord, later in 1775, would be the sight of the first battle of the American Revolution.
In 1638, twelve-year-old Robert Smith arrived in Boston from England.  Robert Smith was an “indentured servant” of Mr. Whittingham.
In 1638, Thomas French Sr. and his wife Susan (Riddlesdale) were farming in the “Reedy Marsh” out of the town of Ipswich in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.

On 11 Arp 1638, John Bent, his wife Martha (Blanchard) and their five children, including eight-year-old daughter Agnes Bent left Southampton, England and sailing on the ship “Confidence” to the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  John was an original settler of the town of Sudbury located out on the frontier sixteen miles west of Boston.  Sudbury had fifty-four settlers living when John and his family arrived.  John was given 6 acres for his home lot and nearby farming.  Over the next two years John Bent joined the Puritan church in Sudbury, which allow him to be made a “freeman” and eligible for thirty-five additional acres farm land.  John Bent was active in the Sudbury town affairs serving as a selectman responsible for laying out a highway over to the town of Watertown, which was eight miles away.

On 14 Mar 1639, Arthur Gary was admitted as a “freeman” of the town of Roxbury, Massachusetts Bay Colony.  Arthur was a town landowner in Roxbury and he and his wife Frances (Warman) were early members of the First Church of Roxbury.  Arthur was one of the first subscribers to the Roxbury grammar school, the first free school in the English America colonies.  Arthur and Frances’s home was west of Stony Brook River and south of Heath Street, next to the home of William Heath another pioneer settler of Roxbury.
On 10 Oct 1638, John Leavens wife Elizabeth (Stebbin) died in Roxbury in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.

On 25 Mar 1639, Sergeant Thomas French Jr. was put in for a promotion to the rank of Lieutenant in the Massachusetts Bay Colony’s militia by Major Daniel Dennison in a letter he wrote to John Winthrop.  Daniel Dennison’s letter included the following: “Our company wanting some officers, have according to their liberty, made choice of some, whom they desired me to propound to the Court or Council.  They were willing to express their love and liking to Sergeant French and Sergeant Howlett proposing the former for Lieutenant, the other for Ensign.”
On 28 Jun 1639, Walter Powers Jr. was born to Walter Powers Sr. and Joane (Newman) in Essex, England.  Walter Powers Jr. begins the Edith Powers branch of Winifred Eloise Coss’ family tree.

On 5 Jul 1639, fifty-seven-year-old widower John Leavens Sr. and twenty-year-old Rachell Wright were married in the town of Roxbury in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  Rachell was a “maid servant” who had just recently been admitted to the Roxbury town church.  No record has been found indicating when Rachell arrived in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  It is likely she arrived with John Leavens Sr. and his wife Elizabeth (Stebbin) back in 1632 as their thirteen-year-old “maid servant”.

In 1639, Edmund Rice, wife Thomasine (Frost) and their children including seventeen-year-old son Edward Rice left England and sailed on one of the three ships that left England for New England in 1639.  The family settled in the town of Watertown in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  On 3 Sep 1639, Deacon Edmund Rice was a member of the committee appointed by the Massachusetts General Court to lay out the land in Sudbury for the new community.  Edmund received parcels of land in Sudbury in three land distributions.  Edmund built his home on the east side of the Sudbury River in what is now the town of Wayland, Massachusetts.  Edmund next expanded his farm by renting, buying and selling land near “Dudley Pond”, which was now south of the town of Wayland, and which became known as “Rice’s End”.  Edmund Rice became a town selectman in 1644 and a church decan later in 1648.

In 1639, William Ward, his wife Elizabeth (Storey) and their daughter Deborah sail from England in the ship “Diligent” to the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  William was one of the founders of the town of Sudbury, in the Massachusetts Bay Colony where he had his farm.

In 1639, Solomon Johnson, his wife Eleanor (Croft) and their four-year-old son John Johnson sailed from England and arrived in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  Solomon was an original proprietor in the town of Sudbury, having received twenty-eight acres.  He was made “freeman” in 1645.  Having bought land in the “Great River Meadow” of West Sudbury, in 1651, he was called "Taylor of Watertown ".  In 1658, Solomon received a grant of one hundred thirty acres in the "two-mile strip" of Sudbury, and later he was given ten more acres when a new road was built running through his property.
In 1639, Joseph Barrett was born to Thomas Barrett and Margaret (Huntington) in the town of Braintree in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.

In 1639, Francis Gould arrived in Boston in the Massachusett Bay Colony on an unknown ship from England.  Francis Gould was first mention in 1639 in the town of Boston’s records a “watchman before the count” (a guard or policeman).

In 1639, Dr. John Douglas Alcock was working as a physician in Boston, in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  Dr. Alcock graduated from Harvard College in 1646.  The Harvard College was started in 1636 in the Massachusetts Bay Colony as the first college in the American English colonies.  Dr. Alcock had received medical training back England, which he used in Boston before attending Harvard College.  Dr. Alcock was often employed by the Massachusetts Bay Colony for public service, such as surveying and locating grants of lands.  For his public service Dr. Alcock was later granted one-thousand acres of land known as “The Farm”, which became part of the new Marlborough community in the Massachusetts Bay Colony. Below are artist’s sketches of young  Dr. John Douglas Alcock:

images.caption: In 1639, Dr. John Douglas Alcock was working as a physician in Boston, in the Massachusetts Bay Colony. Dr. Alcock gr…
In 1639, Dr. John Douglas Alcock was working as a physician in Boston, in the Massachusetts Bay Colony. Dr. Alcock gr…

blocks.text: In 1639, twenty-nine-year-old Richard Robbins left England and sailed to the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  Richard first settled in the town of Charlestown in the colony.
On 27 Apr 1640, John Leavens Jr. was born to John Leavens Sr. and Rachel (Wright) in the town of Roxbury in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.

On 16 Jul 1640, widower Thomas Lamb and Dorothy Harbottle were married in Roxbury, Massachusetts Bay Colony.  Dorothy Harbottle was admitted to the Roxbury church in 1638 indicating she had arrived then in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  No record of Dorothy’s voyage from England has been found.  Thomas Lamb’s first wife Elizabeth (Lambert) had died ten months earlier in Roxbury.
In 1640, Francis Gould was as a “blacksmith” living in Plymouth Colony who had eight-year-old boy John Eddy working for him as a “blacksmith apprentice”.

In 1640, William Douglas his wife Ann (Mattle) and their daughter Anne Mattle Douglas emigrated from England and sailed to the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  The family first settled in the town of Gloucester, but soon moved on to the larger town of Boston, where William worked as a “Cooper” (a person trained to make wooden casks, barrels, vats, buckets, tubs and troughs).  On 31 Jun 1640, William was made a “freeman” in Boston.  Later in 1641, William and his family moved on to the town of Ipswich where he was entitled to a share of public land.  He stayed in Ipswich for four years before returning back to Boston in 1645.
In 1640, William Preston and his wife Mary (Seabrook) were members of the New Haven Church, in the town of New Haven in the Connecticut Colony.

In 1640, John Ramsdell Sr. and Priscilla Chase were married in the town of Lynn in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  John had a home and farm in “Rumney Marsh” near Lynn.  Information on Priscilla Chase’s arrival in the Massachusetts Bay Colony has not been found, but had to occur before her marriage to John Ramsdell Sr.
Note:  By 1640, over 40,000 English colonists were living in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.
On 10 Feb 1641, Tryall Shepard was born to Deacon Ralph Shepard and Thankslorde (Perkins) in the town of Weymouth in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.

On 16 Mar 1641, William Perkins, because of a £50 gift his father William Perkins Sr. made back in London England, to the Harvard College in the town of Cambridge in the Massachusetts Bay Colony, William Jr. was given 400 acres of land near the town of Roxbury in the colony.

In 1641, Thomas Richardson, his two brothers, and four other men joined together to form the Woburn town church in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  The three Richardson brothers lived near each other on the same street.  Thomas owned a house, a barn and one hundred acres of land, situated in the town of Woburn, of which twenty-five acres were plowed land and ten acres were meadow grazing land.

In 1641, Edmund Rice was appointed a “magistrate” (a judicial officer who hears cases in a lower court) in the town of Sudbury in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  Edmund was assigned to hear cases that involved infringement of regulations regarding personal behavior and local welfare.  The colonists, the majority of whom were solid practical middle-class craftsmen and farmers, were building their new community based on laws and insisted on high standards of morality and honesty regarding the rights of others.

In 1641, John Ramsdell Sr. served as a “juror” for both the Massachusetts Bay Colony’s General Court and Grand Jury.  John was called as a witness in a number of cases.  One of particular interest was the case against Robert Burgis of the town of Lynn for "bad and course grinding of both English and Indian corn".

Note:  The word “corn” has various meanings depending upon different geographical regions.  It stands for any local grains that are safe to eat and harvested in large areas.  In English, the word “corn” pertains to any cereal grain crop.  The Latin the word “grain” and the Germanic word “corn” represent any edible plant-seed like millet, barley, rye, wheat, maize, oats etc.  For the British people, corn was “wheat” the chief grain that was most available in their county as a food crop.  When the English entered the New World, they attributed the term corn to the local grain namely, “Zea mays” or “Indian corn”.  Indian corn does not grow wild anywhere in the world.  It is a domesticated plant that evolved sometime in the last 10,000 years, through human intervention, from “teosinte”, a form of wild Mexican grass.  Originally cultivated in the Americas, corn was brought back to Europe by Christopher Columbus.

sections.title: English Civil War
sections.teaser: In 1642, King Charles I of England, who believes he rules by “Divine Right”, was challenged by the English Long Parliament, which resulted in a four-year “En…

blocks.text: In 1642, King Charles I of England, who believes he rules by “Divine Right”, was challenged by the English Long Parliament, which resulted in a four-year “English Civil War” called the “Great Rebellion”.  The English Parliament had grown in power and influence overtime, limiting the power of the country’s kings and queens.  With King Charles I’s absolute powers in check and the beheading of Archbishop William Laud and Earl Strafford; John Winthrop’s Journal noted sudden decline in the number of Puritans arriving in New England from England.  Before 1642, the flow of immigrants was about 2,000 a year, after 1642, the flow dwindled to only a few hundred Scots-Irish immigrants each year.
On 9 Apr 1642, Joshua Lamb was born to Thomas Lamb and Dorothy (Harbottle) in the town of Roxbury in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.

On 16 Apr 1642, John Upham and five other men from Weymouth signed a treaty with a local Native American tribe that clarified their ownership of their land in the Weymouth community.  Prior to the treaty, the settlers in the town of Weymouth in the Massachusetts Bay Colony were considered “squatters or trespassers”.
In 1642, Roger Preston and Martha Blessing Towne were married in the town of Ipswich in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.

In 1642, Richard Sawtelle was granted 67 acres in addition to his 5-acre home lot that was located east of “Fresh Pond”, in the furthest eastern part of the town of Watertown, near the town of Cambridge.  Richard had four additional parcels of land in the area.  Richard was also the town of Watertown’s “town clerk” (record keeper) in charge of keeping the town’s records.

In 1642, William Perkins and his wife Elizabeth (Wooton) moved from the town of Roxbury to the town of Weymouth in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  Shortly after the move William Perkins was promoted from Ensign to Lieutenant and the commander of Weymouth town’s militia.

On 5 Apr 1643, Elizabeth Preston (Sale) was given the task of sweeping the Connecticut Colony’s town of New Haven meetinghouse each week.  Her husband William Preston was assigned the task of shutting the meetinghouse doors and making sure they stayed shut.  William was also responsible for making sure the town’s meetinghouse doors were open on Lord’s days and lecture days before the first drum beat called the town’s people to a town meeting.
On 6 Jul 1643, William Preston was made a “freeman” in the town of New Haven in the Connecticut Colony.

On 23 Jul 1643, Deacon Ralph Shepard was one of the original proprietors of town of Rehoboth in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  Ralph’s estate was assessed at £120.  The town of Rehoboth, which is located 45 miles south of Boston, was founded in 1643 by Walter Palmer and William Sabin.  The word Rehoboth means “enlargement or broad places” in the Hebrew language.
On 18 Jun 1643, Elizabeth Perkins was born to William Perkins and Elizabeth (Wooton) in the town of Weymouth, Norfolk Co., in the Massachusetts Bay Colony .

On 10 May 1634, William Ward was made a “freeman” in the town of Sudbury in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  William was one of the leaders of the town of Sudbury, which was located 23 miles west and inland for the city of Boston in the colony.  A year later William was made a “deputy” (member) of the Massachusetts Bay Colony’s General Court from the community of Sudbury.  William was also a “selectman” in Sudbury and was most of the time the “chairman” of the town’s selectman board.  William was one of the nine men who petitioned the Massachusetts Bay Colony’s General Count for a grant to create new community of Marlborough in the Massachusetts Bay Colony, named after the Marlborough a town in England.  The grant request was approved.
In 1643, Francis Gould was listed as someone who was allowed to carried a musket (gun) in the town of Duxbury in the Plymouth Colony.

On 13 Apr 1644, the new town of Woburn, Massachusetts held its first Town Meeting.  Edward Johnson, Edward Converse, John Mousall, William Learned, Ezekiel Richardson, Samuel Richardson and James Thompson were selected as the new town’s “Selectmen”. William Learned was also selected as the town’s Constable.  Michael Bacon, Ralph Hill and Thomas Richardson were chosen as Surveyors of Highways for the new community.

On 29 May 1644, William Perkins was promoted from Lieutenant to Captain in the Militia and the Weymouth town’s representative in the Massachusetts Bay Colony’s General Court.  William was also the Weymouth town’s “schoolmaster”.  Captain William Perkins was made a member of the “Ancient and Honorable Artillery Company” in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.

In 1644, the Massachusetts General Court gave John Upham, who was a Weymouth town’s “selectman” at the time, the power and responsibility to judge and settle small issues between Weymouth town’s members.

In 1644, Nicholas Holt and his family left the town of Newbury and moved to the town of Andover as one of the first settlers and officers of the new town in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  The Andover was incorporated as a town in 1646 and was named after Andover, England where many of the new residents originally came from.  Nicholas Holt, set up a “tannery” in Andover and operated a “ferry crossing” on the Shawsheen River for the town.  Nicholas Holt received a large area of land in Andover, a portion of which was located on top of a hill that was called “Holt Hill”.  Nicholas gave his son Timothy Holt Sr. one-hundred acres of land on Holt Hill.  Timothy Holt Sr’s son Timothy Holt Jr. built a house on top of Holt Hill, located today at 89 Prospector Road in Andover, Massachusetts, on the highest point in Essex County.  Timothy Holt Jr’s house still exists today and is listed in the National Register of Historic Places.  A portion of the Holt farm is now part of the 704 acre “Charles W. Ward Reservation” open space reserve.  See a photo the Holt’s house below:

blocks.caption: In 1644, Nicholas Holt and his family left the town of Newbury and moved to the town of Andover as one of the first s…

blocks.text: In 1645, the “Parliamentarians Army” led by Puritan Oliver Cromwell, referred to as his “New Model Army”’ defeated the English Royalist Army of King Charles I at the “Battle of Naseby” ending the “English Civil War”.  The recently developed musket was effectively used by the “New Model Army” in the fighting especially against the King’s horse cavalry.  An estimated 200,000 soldiers and civilians were killed in the English Civil War representing about 3.6% of the population of England.  (Compared to 2% of the population in America was killed in the American Civil War.)
In 1645, William Taylor was born to William Johns Taylor and Anne (Wyeth) in England.

sections.title: In1645, Thomas Barrett was made a “freeman” in Braintree in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.
sections.teaser: Note:  The period between 1645 and 1715 was the coldest period of the “Little Ice Age”.  This cold period impacted farming and made life challenging for the…

blocks.text: Note:  The period between 1645 and 1715 was the coldest period of the “Little Ice Age”.  This cold period impacted farming and made life challenging for the people living in New England.
On 1 May 1646, William Douglas purchased a dwelling house with a shop and land in Boston, Massachusetts Bay Colony, where he conducted his trade as a “cooper” making wooden barrels.

In 1646, Ralph Shepard and his family left the town of Rehoboth and relocate to the town of Malden, Massachusetts where Ralph served as the Deacon of the Church in Malden.  The Malden area was a hilly woodland area north of Mystic River that had been purchased from the Pennacook Native tribe back in 1629.  The area had been called “Mistick Slide”, the name was changed by Joseph Hills to Malden after the town of Malden, Essex, England.
In 1646, Daniel Cheever and Esther Hester were married in England.
On 10 Mar 1647, William Preston was given the first seat of the men in the New Haven meetinghouse.  His wife Elizabeth (Sale) was given the fourth seat of the cross seats at the end.

On 26 May 1647, Nicholas Holt representing the new town of Andover along with Capt. Johnson of Woburn and Thomas Danforth of Cambridge were tasked with defining the boundaries of the new Andover settlement in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.
In 1647, Daniel Cheever and his wife Esther (Hester) sailed from England and arrived in the town of Cambridge in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.

In 1647, Nicholas Holt and Sergeant Marshall were tasked with laying out a road from the town Reading to the new town of Andover in the Massachusetts Bay Colony, including locations of bridges needed to cross the rivers in the area.
In 1647, John Ramsdell Jr. was born to John Ramsdell Sr. and Priscilla (Chase) in Lynn, Massachusetts Bay Colony.
In 1647, Edward Rice and Agnes Bent were married in Sudbury, Middlesex Co., Massachusetts Bay Colony.

Note:  In 1648, the Massachusetts Bay Colony tried and executed an accused witch for the first time.  The accused was a midwife (helper of woman giving birth) named  from Charlestown who was hanged at Gallows Hill in Boston after she was accused of being a witch by some of her pregnant women patients.  Margaret and her husband Thomas Jones were one of the earliest settlers in the town Charlestown.  Margaret was an herbalist (used herbs and plants as medicine) and midwife who treated her sick neighbors with various teas and herbal concoctions.  People who had the power to heal also have the power to harm, and eventually some of Margaret's patients began to murmur against her.  They claimed she said if they refused to buy her medicines they would never get well.  Soon some of her neighbors began to openly accuse Margaret of witchcraft, claiming she "was found to have such a “Malignant Touch”, as many persons were taken with deafness, or vomiting, or other violent pains and sickness."  About eighty people throughout New England were accused of practicing witchcraft between 1648 and 1693. Thirteen women and two men were executed.

In Feb 1648, Mary Maxwell was born to Hugh Maxwell and Helen Marie (Comerford) in the town of Chelmsford, Middlesex Co., Massachusetts Bay Colony.  No additional earlier information has been found on Hugh Maxwell and his wife Helen Marie (Comerford).
About 1648, Richard Robbins and Mary Rebecca Wells were married in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  No information has been found on Mary Rebecca Wells prior to her wedding.
In 1648, Robert Robbins was born to Richard Robbins and Mary Rebecca (Wells) in the town of Groton in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.

In 1648, John Upham and his family moved from town of Weymouth to the new town of Malden, on the north side of the Mystic River.  John Upham was elected a “selectman” in the town of Malden in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.
In 1648, Dr. John Douglas Alcock and Sarah Palgrave were married in Roxbury, Massachusetts Bay Colony.

In 1649, Francis Gould left the town of Duxbury in the Plymouth Colony and moved to the town of Braintree in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  Francis Gould and Rose unknown were married in Braintree shortly after Frances’ arrival.

In 1649, as part of the English Civil War, King Charles I of England was captured and imprisoned.  The English House of Commons tired King Charles for treason.  The king was found guilty and was beheaded on 30 Jan 1649.  The king’s son Charles Jr. fled to France.  General Oliver Cromwell declared Britain a republic “The Commonwealth”.  Oliver Cromwell used his army between 1649 to 1651 to crush the Irish clans killing over 40% people living in Ireland and totally defeated the ’s son Charles.  In 1653 Oliver Cromwell finally expelled the corrupt English Parliament and with the agreement of army leaders became Lord Protector (King in all but name) of Great Britain.

Note:  Even with the lack of large number of new emigrants after the removal of King Charles I, the equal balance of males and females living in New England, and the healthy environment led to longer life spans and the trend of couples to marry at a young age, resulted in the population boom in New England.  The average family typically had seven to eight children, with at least six surviving to adulthood.
On 25 Jun 1650, John Ramsdell Sr. was sworn in as the “constable” for the town of Lynn in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.
In 1650, Joseph Hills was appointed a Captain and commander of the Malden town militia company in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.

Note:  By 1650, the clashing of the Native Americans people, living in the New England area, with the Europeans settlers and their new advanced technologies and their deadly European diseases, totally disrupted Native American people’s way-of-life that had maintained fairly a stable population as an advanced hunter-gather society for thousands of years.  The number of Native American People living in the New England area had falling from an original estimated 60,000 people down to only about 6,000, a 90% loss in numbers by 1650.  The native population had no immunity to diseases such as: (, , , , , , , , , , , ,  and).  The sharing of advanced European weapons with the Native people also increased the number of native people who were killed in wars between Native tribes themselves and wars against the advancing European settlers.

Note:  Without modern medicines that were not developed until hundreds of years later, the catastrophe that occurred to the Native American people, who had no immunity to the old-world diseases was going to occur no matter which old-world explorers they came in contact with first.  The old-world explorers and settlers had immunities to the old-world diseases only because their ancestors had survived and live through the plagues that had sweep across Europe again and again for thousands of years.  The surviving old-world people then passed on their immunities on to their children.

On 8 Nov 1651, David Stirling, the father of William Sterling and his brother John Stirling sailed from Gravesend, the port of London, on the ship “John and Sarah” to the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  The two Stirling brothers were part of 272 Scottish prisoners captured in the English Civil War.  Oliver Cromwell spared the lives of the King Charles I’s Scottish soldiers in the English Civil War, but had imprisoned them.  On 20 Oct 1651, the English Parliament freed the imprisoned Scottish soldiers and had shipped to the English American Colonies as “indentured servants”.  Having been a supporter of the dethroned King Charles I, the Stirling family must have been Catholics.  The success the family had in melding in with the New England Puritan society indicates they were willing to do more than just change the spelling of their last name to fit in with the other protestant New English settlers in the colony.

sections.title: Praying Indian Villages
sections.teaser: In 1651, the village of Littleton in the Massachusetts Bay Colony was one of the original “Praying Indian Villages”.  Rev. John Eliot, known as the “Apostle…

blocks.text: In 1651, the village of Littleton in the Massachusetts Bay Colony was one of the original “Praying Indian Villages”.  Rev. John Eliot, known as the “Apostle to the Indians”, began an effort to organize the Massachusetts’ Native American people into Christian Villages.  With the backing of Oliver Cromwell and £12,000 sterling given him by the English government, John Eliot began a long-term mission to translate the Bible into the Algonquin language.  Although he was a Puritan, Eliot was also a humanitarian and he felt that the best way to assure the Native American peoples’ survival in the midst of heavy English land-pressure was to organize the Native Americans into English towns and English lifestyles.  The Native people were to convert to Christianity, receive deeds to their property in new towns, live in English houses, wear English clothes, and worship Puritan style in church meeting houses.  Between 1651 and 1658, John Eliot and his assistant Daniel Gookin organized seven Praying Indian Villages in Massachusetts Bay Colony.  All told there were over 14 such villages established in Massachusetts Bay Colony each with between 45 to 60 Native inhabitants.

Rev. John Eliot allowed the Native people to choose the sites for their new villages.  The local Concord Native people led by an early Christian convert, Chief Sachem Tahattawan, requested the "Nashoba" lands close by for their new praying village.  The new Native Plantation of Nashoba was formally granted by Massachusetts Bay Colony General Court in 1651 and was laid out in rectangle 3 x 4 miles.  The area encompassed most of current day Littleton and a portion of Boxborough in Massachusetts.  The Nashoba Praying Village thrived until “King Phillip’s War” in 1675-76, when the Nashoba Native people were rounded up and dumped on Deer Island in the middle of Boston Harbor to freeze and starve.  More on the “King Phillip’s War” will be covered later.  Only a handful of the Native Nashoba people survived the ordeal and were able to return to Nashoba Village.  The Nashoba’s Plantation land was sold to English settlers and by 1714 it was completely in English settlers’ hands and re-incorporated as the Town of Littleton in 1715.  The surviving Native Nashoba people were given a new Native town on a 500-acre tract of rocky hills between Nagog Pond and Fort Pond (which was named after the Native fort located in the area).
On 2 Jan 1651, Nathaniel Richardson was born to Thomas Richardson and Mary (Baldwin) in the town of Woburn in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.

In 1651, Edward Preston and twenty-eight-year-old Margaret Hurst were married in the town of New Haven in the Connecticut Colony.  No record has been found for Margaret’s voyage from England to New England.  Margaret’s father Edward Peter Hurst had emigrated to the town of Boston in the Massachusetts Bay Colony, prior to his death in Boston on 16 Jun 1656.  Margert’s mother Susanna (Tristram) had died in 1670 back in Hitchin, Hertfordshire, England.  From these facts it appears Margaret and her father Edward migrated to Boston sometime before Margaret’s marriage to Edward Preston in 1651.

In 1651, William Towne purchased land in the neighboring town of Topsfield in the Massachusetts Bay Colony from William Paine, which consisted of forty acres divided into plow land, meadow, and unplowed land.  William sold his Salem property to Henry Bullock in 1652 and bought additional land in Topsfield in 1656.
In 1652, Nathaniel Gary was admitted to the Roxbury town church, in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.

In 1652, Mary Alcock was born to Dr. John Douglas Alcock and Sarah (Palgrave) in the town of Roxbury in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  Mary Alcock was baptized by her parents on 15 Aug 1652, in Roxbury.
In 1652, Thomas Green Jr. and Rebecca Hills were married in Malden, Middlesex Co., Massachusetts Bay Colony.

On 12 Aug 1653, William Peacock and Mary Willis were married in Roxbury, Massachusetts Bay Colony.  William Peacock was working as a “trader” with the Native Americans living in the Massachusetts Bay area.
On 15 Oct 1654, Marth Gould was born to Francis Gould and Rose (unknown) in Braintree, Norfolk Co., Massachusetts Bay Colony as the second child of thirteen children.

In 1654, after her husband Walter Powers Sr. died in England, forty-two-year-old Joane (Newman) and her fifteen-year-old son Walter Powers Jr., sailed from England and arrived in Salem settlement in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.

In 1654, John Upham, Ralph Shepard, Joseph Hills and four other men living in the town of Malden incurred the displeasure of the Massachusetts General Court for the offense of electing their own town minister, without consulting with other churches in the area.

In May 1655, the Massachusetts Bay Colony’s General Court approved the creation of a new community called Chelmsford near the towns of Concord and Woburn in the colony.  The settlement of Chelmsford was a horrible example of taking land from the Native American people.  The Pennacook Native people (meaning bottom of the hill) had lived in the area selected for the new Chelmsford community for thousands of years.  The Native Americans were Algonquian-speaking people who operated as a network of politically and culturally allied communities rather than a single large tribe.  The major Pennacook villages were built along the rivers in the area.  Life revolved around the seasons with Native women beginning in the spring collecting maple sap and making maple sugar.  Men would burn the forests to add nutrients in the soils for later cultivation.  In late spring the rivers and creeks would swell as the salmon and shad fish making their way up the rivers to spawn.  Many Pennacook villages were built above natural waterfalls that gathered the spawning fish and made it easier to catch them in the late spring.  The delicious “fiddlehead fern” season occurred next followed by other seasons known today, like blueberry and the raspberry seasons.  During the summers, the Native families would disperse out to summer villages and hunting camps.  Women did most of the work of building and maintaining homes as well as farming.  Their main crops were varieties of corn and squash, which they planted along rivers and in meadows.
