While they found it difficult to clear the massive old-growth forest trees, the Pennacook’s were experts at manipulating beavers to move their dams and build ponds up and down creeks and brooks, thereby clearing and opening up land for farms that would be essential to the first Europeans settlers who arrived and found cleared fields ready for cultivation.

The Pennacook Native people tried to live peacefully with the new arriving Europeans.  When the first Europeans arrived, there were an estimated 16,500 Pennacook Native people living in the Chelmsford area.  As occurred to most of the Native American people, the Pennacook people suffered badly from European introduced diseases.  By the end of the 1600s, 90% of the Pennacook Native people were gone killed by diseases.  Successive Pennacook tribal leaders tried to maintain a friendly relations with the new European settlers who came into the Pennacook Native territory.  Despite this determinedly pro-peace stance, the new Chelmsford European settlers became increasingly violent towards the tribe, often forcing the Pennacook’s to flee north temporarily and later permanently.  On one horrible occasion, a handful of Pennacook native people who were too sick or elderly to flee with their kin and remained behind were killed when their homes were set on fire.
On 29 Sep 1655, Elizabeth Preston was born to Edward Preston and Margaret (Hurst) in the town of New Haven in the Connecticut Colony.
On 8 Nov 1655, Daniel Rice Sr. was born to Edward Rice and Agnes (Bent) in the town of Sudbury in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.
In 1655, John Preston was born to Roger Preston and Martha Blessing (Towne) in the town of Ipswich in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.

On 26 Mar 1656, Robert Smith and Mary French were married in Rowley Village in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  Robert had completed his indentured servitude period and was now a successful “tailor” in the Boxford and Topsfield townships and owned his own farm in the area.
On 6 Oct 1656, Mary Cheever was born to Daniel Cheever and Esther (Hester) in the town of Cambridge in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.

In 1656, eleven-year-old William Taylor arrived in the ship "Speedwell" alone with a number of other unaccompanied young boys.  William was probably an apprentice of one of the older passengers on the ship.  Many of the passengers on the ship “Speedwell” settled west of the town of Sudbury in what later became the town of Marlborough in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.

On 30 Apr 1657, Deacon Ralph Mousall died in the town of Charleston in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  In Ralph’s will he gave his “child ward” Ruth Wood, who he called his daughter, £20.  Ralph also gave his other “child ward” he called his daughter, Mary Grove £15.
On 19 Nov 1657, John Johnson and Deborah Ward were married in the town of Sudbury in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.

On 29 Nov 1657 Mary Peacock was born to William Nathaniel Peacock and Mary (Willis) in Dedham in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  The town of Dedham was located 20 miles southwest of the city of Boston.
In 1657, Arthur Gary owned 1,000 acres near the town Dedham in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.

In 1658, Oliver Cromwell died and his third son Richard was appointed the second ruling Lord Protector of England, Scotland and Ireland.  Unlike his father, Richard Cromwell lacked military experience and as such failed to gain respect or support from the New Model Army.  In 1659 Richard was ‘persuaded’ to resign from his position as Lord Protector and exiled himself to France.
On 14 Feb 1658, Phineas Upham and Ruth Wood were married in the town of Malden in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.
On 14 Oct 1658, Nathaniel Gary and Anne Mattle Douglas were married in the town of Roxbury in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  Anne was later admitted on 22 Sep 1661, to the town church of Roxbury.
On 26 Oct 1658, Ralph Farnham Jr. and Elizabeth Holt were married by Rev. Simeon Broadstreet in the town of Andover in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.

On 27 Nov 1658, Bethiah Ward was born to Deacon William Ward and Elizabeth (Storey) in the town of Sudbury in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  Bethiah and Deborah Ward were sisters that were born to the same parents twenty-one years apart.  In total William and Elizabeth had fifteen children.

In 1658, brothers David Stirling and John Stirling left the Massachusetts Bay Colony where they had been forced into indentured servitude and returned to England after Oliver Cromwell died and his son Richard Cromwell fled to France.  David Stirling was an exiled  Scottish soldier who had been sent to the New England Colonies to get him out of England and was the father of twenty-two-year-old William Sterling who had immigrated to the New England Colonies and was now living in Haverhill in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.
Note:  In 1658, the English Parliament passed an ‘Act of Uniformity’ defining anyone not belonging to the Anglican Church (Church of England) as being a nonconformist.
On 23 May 1659, Elizabeth Upham was born to Phineas Upham and Ruth (Wood) in the town of Malden in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.

In 1659, twenty-two-year-old William Sterling and Elizabeth Sawtelle were married in the town of Haverhill in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  The record of the marriage is the first record identifying William Sterling was now living in the English American Colonies.  Elizabeth Sawtelle was the first of William’s four wives.  William Sterling appears in the 1662 records of Essex County in the Massachusetts Bay Colony, where the birth of his first child was entered as born in the town of Rowley in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  The town of Rowley was 13-miles east of Haverhill.  Between 1660 and 1664, William Sterling was taxed each year three shillings and ten pence by the town of Rowley.  William worked as a ship ”carpenter and grain miller”.  He was living in the Rowley in 1669, when he sold his property to Stephen Kent in exchange for the latter's house, barn and orchard in the town of Haverhill, on the Merrimac River, near a small stream called Little River.  William Sterling secured another twelve acres of land sometime prior to 1683 on the site where the city hall of the town of Haverhill, Massachusetts now stands, and built on the land a two-story house.  In the town of Haverhill, William was a “carpenter” by trade and built ships on the Merrimac River for the New England coastal traders.  He was a member of the “Board of Selectmen” in Haverhill.  William offered his house and land to the town as a home for the town’s minister.  He received in payment, ten acres of land on Fishing River, and the use of ten more acres for a grain mill he erected.  William was prominent in Haverhill town’s affairs, and from 1693 to 1696 he was one of the town’s “tithingman” (English Peace Officer) and also held the office of the town’s “Constable”, responsible for serving court documents, assisting at executions of writs (collection court fines) and maintaining order during court sessions

In Dec 1659, William Douglas purchased the house of William Hough in the town of New London in the Connecticut Colony.  Soon after in 1660, William his wife Ann (Mattle) and their three children left the city of Boston in the Massachusetts Bay Colony and moved to the town of New London.  The town granted William a tract of land by the Thames River that runs through the town and he later received a grant for his services of 69 more acres of land, 3 miles west of the town New London.

In late 1659, Francis Gould, wife Rose (Whitehall) and their children, including five-year-old daughter Martha Gould, left the town of Braintree in the Massachusetts Bay Colony and moved northwest out to the western frontier of the colony to the new community of Chelmsford, where Francis and Rose lived for the rest of their lives.

sections.title: Restoration of the English King
sections.teaser: On 30 Jan 1660, after the fall of the Cromwell’s dictatorships in England, the son of the dead King Charles I was placed on the throne and became King Charle…

blocks.text: On 30 Jan 1660, after the fall of the Cromwell’s dictatorships in England, the son of the dead King Charles I was placed on the throne and became King Charles II.  The new King was forced to agree to a restoration agreement with the English Parliament, which gave the king a standing army and allowed him to purge the officials responsible for his father’s execution.  In exchange King Charles II was force agreed to honor the “Petition of Right” and accept limited income.  The “Petition of Right” is a major document in England’s constitutional set of laws that sets out specific liberties of the English subjects (people) that the king is prohibited from infringing upon.  Passed on 7 Jun 1628, the Petition contains restrictions on non-Parliamentary taxation, forced billeting of soldiers, imprisonment without cause, and the use of martial law.

In 1660, following the , King  sought to streamline the administration of the English American colonies.  King Charles II and his government began a process that brought a number of the colonies under direct English crown control.  One reason for these actions was to reduce the cost of administration of individual colonies, but another significant reason was the regulation of trade.  Throughout the 1660s, the English Parliament passed a number of laws to regulate the trade of the colonies, collectively called the “Navigation Acts”, which forced all cargo coming and out of England to be carried only on English owned ships.  The American colonists’ owned ships were not considered English ships and their use in shipping goods was prohibited by the new “Navigation Acts”.  The American Colonies had established a significant trading networks with other English colonies and with other European countries and their New World colonies, especially Spain and the Dutch Republic.  The “Navigation Acts” in effect turned the existing American colonial merchants who transported goods in and out of the colonies into outlawed smugglers and significantly increasing the cost of doing business and the cost of imported goods into the American Colonies.

In 1660, the Massachusetts Bay Colony’s General Court ordered the name of the new town being established to be called Marlborough.  The court order read:  "It is ordered. That every person y' claims any interest in the town of Marlborough, shall pay to all public charge, both for the minister and for all other town charges that have arisen about the plantation to this day from the beginning thereof, according to their proportion in a rate now presented with said proportion due ; every person to pay at or before 10 Nov 1660 or else lose all legal interest in the aforesaid plantation; that is to say, four pence an acre for each acre of their house lots to the Minister, and three pence for all the estate that hath been kept or brought to keep, being found in the town or about the town ; and nine pence an acre for every acre of their house lots to town charges, till all the debts that are due from the town to them that have been employed by the town or the plantation thereof."  The Notice was signed by:  Edmund Rice, William Kerly, Thomas King, Henry Kerly, Solomon Johnson, John Howe, Richard Newton, Christopher Banister, William Ward, John Johnson, Thomas Goodnow and John Ruddocke.
On 27 Oct 1660, Roger Preston was allowed to keep and sell strong liquors for the entertainment of strangers as an “inn keeper”, in the town of Salem in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.

In 1660, William Ward moved his family inland nine miles west from the town of Sudbury to the new town of Marlborough in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  William Ward was chosen the first deacon for the new Marlborough town church.  William was given fifty acres of land for his home and farming in the new community.

On 11 Mar 1661, Walter Powers Jr. and Tryall Shepard were married in the town of Malden in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  It appears Walter had a limited education, but he overcame his limitations through hard work and good common sense.  Tryall on the other hand was fairly well educated for the times.  Walter Jr. and Tryall selected a track of land near the town of Concord in the Massachusetts Bay Colony, which Walter Jr. bought from Lt. Joseph Wheeler.  Walter Jr. and Tryall build a house on their new land on the north side of Quagany Hill about half mile from the town of Concord’s garrison house.
On 22 Sep 1661, Anne Mattle Gary (Douglas) was admitted to the Roxbury town church in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.
In 1661, Robert Smith and Mary (French) were living in the Boxford and Topsfield adjoining townships area.  Robert was working as successful “tailor” and farmer on land he had purchased.
On 1 Jun 1662, Ralph Farnham III was born to Ralph Farnham Jr. and Elizabeth (Holt) in the town of Andover in the Massachusetts Bay Colony as their second of seven children.

In 1662, Richard Sawtelle and his wife Elizabeth (Pople) were now living in the Groton settlement, 40 miles west of City of Boston in the Massachusetts Bay Colony’s western frontier.  Richard was appointed the Groton “town clerk” for the first three years after the new town was formed.  Richard Sawtelle was farming on 20 acres, which was formed out of the original Patatawag plantation.  Richard‘s home in the town of Groton was one of five fortified garrison houses to which the neighbors and Richard’s extended family would come for protection against Native American warrior attacks.  Groton was a frontier settlement when the “King Philip’s War” occurred and was attacked and burned on 13 Mar 1676, after which Richard and Elizabeth moved back to the town of Watertown, where Richard was elected a town “selectman” in 1689.
On 29 Oct 1663, Ephraim Smith Sr. was born to Robert Smith and Mary (French) in the Rowley Village in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.

In 1663, Thomas Barrett and Margaret (Huntington) left the town of Braintree and move 40 miles north to the town of Chelmsford in the Massachusetts Bay Colony where they bought a house and fifty-two acres of land from James Parker.  The land was situated on the southern slope of “Robin's Hill”.  The property included farmland, woodland and pasture.  The house on the property was built around a great central chimney that provided a fireplace in every room.  The house’s ceilings were low to conserve heat.
On 3 Jul 1665, Sarah Gary was born to Nathaniel Gary and Anne Mattle (Douglas) in the town of Roxbury in the Massachusetts Bay Colony as their fifth of ten children.

Note:  At the same time diseases were decimating the Native American people in the English American colonies, a breakout of ”Bubonic Plague” occurred in 1665 in England.  The disease quickly spread from parish to parish across England killing thousands of people.  Bubonic Plague was known as the “Black Death” and had been known in Europe for centuries.  It was a ghastly disease. The victim’s skin turned black in patches and inflamed glands or ‘buboes’ in the groin, combined with compulsive vomiting, swollen tongue and splitting headaches made it a horrible, agonizing killer.

The 1665 version of the “Black Death” plague began in London in the poor, overcrowded parish of St. Giles-in-the-Field.  It started slowly at first and by May of 1665, 43 had died.  In June 6,137 more people died, in July 17,036 people died and at its peak in August, 31,159 people died.  In all, 15% of the English population perished during that terrible 1665 summer.  Incubation period took a mere four to six days and when the plague appeared in a household, the house was sealed, thus condemning the whole family to death!  These houses were distinguished by a painted red cross on the door and the words, “Lord have mercy on us”.  At night the dead corpses were brought out in answer to the cry,” Bring out your dead” and put in carts and taken away to the .  The plague spread to most areas in England.  The city of York was badly affected.  King Charles II and his Court staff fled London and moved out to Oxford to avoid the plague.

On 14 Apr 1666, the town of Sudbury voted to pay Dr. John Chamber of Roxbury, on behalf of Joseph Noyes, for medical care of William Taylor, who had injured his hand.  William was working as an “apprentice carpenter”, most likely for Joseph Noyes.
On 9 Dec 1667, William Douglas was granted one hundred acres to farm four miles from the town of New London in the Connecticut Colony.
On 4 May 1669, Sarah Sterling was born to William Sterling and Elizabeth (Sawtelle) in the town of Haverhill in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.

In 1670, England’s King Charles II signed a treaty with French King Louis XIV in which he agreed to convert to Catholicism and support France’s war against the Protestant Dutch in return for money.  King Charles II’s wife Queen Catherine had failed to produce a child and the king arranged for his younger brother’s oldest daughter Mary, a Protestant, to married her first cousin, the Protestant Prince William III of the Dutch Republic known as “William of Orange”.  The arranged royal marriage will have a major impact on the future of England.  Mary’s father James the Durk of York later became King James II of England when his older brother King Charles II died, making his oldest daughter, Protestant Mary, the presumptive next heir to the English throne.  King James II was Catholics and when his son James Francis Edward was born, King James II wanted his new son not his oldest daughter to be the next king of England.  This conflict later in 1688 led to “Glorious Revolution” between the Protestants and Catholics believers in Great Britain.
On 31 May 1670, Thomas Green Jr. was admitted as a “freeman” in the town of Malden in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.
On 5 Oct 1670, Samuel Green was born to Thomas Green Jr. and Rebecca (Hills) in the town of Malden in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.

In 1670, William Douglas was chosen by the town of New London in the Connecticut Colony as the new Deacon of the New London’s town church.  Later in 1672, Deacon William Douglas served as a “Deputy” member of the Connecticut Colony’s General Court.

On 31 May 1671, John Ramsdell Jr. and Elizabeth Perkins were married in the town of Boxford in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  John Ramsdell Jr. joined with Henry Leonard in running an “Iron Works factory” in Boxford.  Henry Leonard and his brother James had established the Iron works factory in the town of Boxford, which was credited for making the first iron castings ever made in New England.

On 15 Nov 1671, William Taylor and Mary Johnson were married in the frontier town of Marlborough in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  On 11 Dec 1672, Mary died giving birth to their first child, son Samuel.  With no one to properly care for new baby Samuel he soon died a month later on 31 Jan 1673.
In 1671, Robert Robbins and Mary Maxwell were married in the town of Concord in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.
In 1671, Richard Sawtelle petitioned to be relieved from military training when he turned sixty-years-old, which was the usual age limit for military service.
On 17 Sep 1672, Joseph Barrett and Martha Gould were married in the town of Chelmsford in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.
In 1672, Nathaniel Richardson and Mary Peacock were married in the town of Woburn in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.

In 1672, Sergeant Thomas French Jr. petitioned the Massachusetts Bay Colony’s General Court for a grant of land northeast of the town of Salisbury on behalf of himself and eight other soldiers from the town of Ipswich, who had all fought in the “Pequot War”.  The General Court granted the land that was requested by the militia military veterans.
On 04 Jul 1673, Rebecca Barrett (spelled Rebekah) was born to Joseph Barrett and Martha (Gould) in the town of Chelmsford in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.

On 7 Oct 1673, at a town meeting in Chelmsford in the Massachusetts Bay Colony, a contract was approved to allow Joseph Barrett and his son-in-law of Francis Gould, to build a house on “Robins Hill”, outside of the town of Chelmsford.  The house was to have a single room; 16ft. by 18 ft. covered by “single thickness boards” and had only one window.  The purpose of the new house was to be used as a lookout, where members of the town would be posted, to watch and warn the town of approaching hostile Native warriors’ attacks.

On 30 Nov 1674, John Leavens Jr. and Elizabeth Preston were married in the town of New Haven in the Connecticut Colony.  Elizabeth was John’s second wife.  His first wife Hannah Woods had died eight-years earlier in the town of Roxbury Massachusetts Bay Colony.
On 28 Jun 1674, Walter Powers III was born to Walter Powers Jr. and Tryall (Shepard) in the town of Concord in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.
On 27 Jan 1675, Mary Ramsdell was born to John Ramsdell Jr. and Elizabeth (Perkins) in the town of Topsfield in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.
On 5 Apr 1675, Daniel Johnson in born to John Johnson and Deborah (Ward) in the town of Marlborough in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.
In 1675, Joshua Lamb and Mary Alcock were married in the town of Roxbury in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.

In 1675, William Taylor was assigned to the garrison house of Deacon William Ward in the frontier community of Marlborough in the Massachusetts Bay Colony at the beginning of the “King Philip’s War”.  The map below shows the tribal lands of the Native American tribes:

blocks.caption: In 1675, William Taylor was assigned to the garrison house of Deacon William Ward in the frontier community of Marlbo…

sections.title: King Philip’s War
sections.teaser: In 1675, Native-Americans in New England made their last major effort to drive the English colonists out of their ancestorial territory in the “King Philip’s…

blocks.text: In 1675, Native-Americans in New England made their last major effort to drive the English colonists out of their ancestorial territory in the “King Philip’s War”.  The war lasted a little more than a year.  In the war 12 of the colonial towns were destroyed and many more were damaged and the New England colonies’ economy were ruined.  The New Englanders lost one-tenth of all men available for military service in the war.  Over half of New England's towns were attacked.  The war was named for King Philip, the Wampanoag Chief who adopted the English name Philip due to the friendly relations between his father and the Mayflower settlers.  Chief Philip led a coalition of the Nipmuck, Pocumtuc and Narraganset tribes in an uprising against the English colonists and their allies, the Mohegans and the Mohawks tribes.  The war was disastrous for the native-Americans resulting in great losses of lives and the loss of much of their native lands.

In Sep 1675, Phineas Upham received a commission as a Lieutenant (Lt.) serving under Captain Wayte in the “King Philip’s War”.   On 1 Oct 1675, Lt. Upham was on the march into Nipmuck country in the company of Captain Gorham.  Lt. Upham documented in his journal the march the solders took through the back country looking for Native war parties.  None were encountered.  On 2 Nov 1675, “King Philip’s War” was formally declared against the Narragansetts Native American tribe.  On 19 Dec 1675, Josiah Winslow, Governor of the Plymouth Colony was appointed commander-in-chief of the Army of the United Colonies.  Lt. Upham was assigned to the 4th Company of the Massachusetts Regiment commanded by Captain Isaac Johnson.  The United Colonies’ Army had three Regiments, a Massachusetts, a Plymouth and a Connecticut regiment.

On 19 Sep 1675, Nathaniel Richardson who was a member in Captain Prentiss’ troop of horse soldiers and was one of eight Woburn men wounded on 19 Dec 1675 in the “Great Swamp Fight” in the King Philip’s War.

The major and decisive battle of the King Philip’s War occurred in the “Battle at the Great Swamp Fort”.  On 19 Dec 1675, hoping to prevent a spring Indian onslaught, Plymouth Colony’s Governor Josiah Winslow gathered the Army of the United Colonies and attacked a massive Narragansett and Wampanoag village fortification near the Great Swamp in West Kingston in the Rhode Island Colony.  The following account of the battle appeared in the New England Historical and Genealogical Register Jan 1886.

“About one o’clock in the afternoon on the 19 Dec 1675 the Army of the United Colonies came upon an Indian fortress built on a small island in the midst of a large cedar swamp.  Access to the fort was restricted to a strongly defended single-file path through the swamp land.  The severer cold of the previous day and night had frozen the muddy surface of swamp land near the fortified island allowing the colonial soldiers to use temporary alternate paths to approach the fortified camp.  The fort was surrounded by water and a clay wall covered with felled trees.  The barrier was impressive, but was not finished.  An unfinished opening in the wall provided a weakness in the defense the Colonial Army took advantage of in their attack.  If the wall had been finished and the ground was not frozen an attack on the fort would have been suicide.”

“Three of the companies of the Massachusetts Militia, including the Captain Johnson’s company with Lt. Phineas Upham, were the first to attack the Native fort.  Two Company Commanders, Captain Johnson and Captain Davenport were both killed at the start of the attack.  The Native warriors had constructed a fortified blockhouse near the opening in the wall that was attack by the colonial militia soldiers and the Native warriors were able to lay down a fury of musket fire on the militia soldiers that killed the two captains, many of their soldiers and wounded many others.  The colonial soldiers were force to fall back and stop the attack.  Lt. Upham took over command of Captain’s Johnson’s company.  The Massachusetts Regiment Commanded Major Appleton arrived with the remainder of the colonial soldiers that had just completed a thirty-four-mile march just as the initial attack fallback had been completed.  The larger colonial force regrouped and attacked the Native fort a second time.  The second charge of the colonial soldiers was able push inside the fort wall and take out the Native warriors that were firing from the fortified blockhouse.  The Plymouth soldiers soon joined in the fighting and after three hours of fighting the fort was set ablaze the Native warrior resistance finally stopped and many of the remaining Narragansett people fled into the swamp.  In the second attack Lt. Upham was wounded.  The exact number of Native warriors and other Narragansetts people in the fort that were killed is unknown, but was estimated to be about three hundred Native warriors and many other Narragansetts people were killed in the fires that destroyed the Native fortified village.”

In the “Battle at the Great Swamp Fort” sixty-eight colonial soldiers were killed and a hundred and fifty were wounded.  Twenty-two additional soldiers died of their wounds on the trip back to towns they came from.  Lt. Phineas Upham was carried over the night, after the attack, back to the town of Wickford in the Rhode Island Colony.  Wounded Lt. Upham was next taken back in the withdrawal of Governor Wilson forces from Rhode Island back to Massachusetts.  The march back in winter was extremely difficult.  The soldiers were forced to kill and eat many their own horses in what was known as the “Hungry March”.  The exact date is unknown, but in Oct 1676, Lt. Phineas Upham died of his battle wounds in his home in the town Malden in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.
In 1675, Deacon William Douglas and Mr. Willerby were appointed to deliver provisions to the military commissary for Colonial Army fighting in the “King Philip’s War”.
In 1675, John Preston served as a solder under Captain Gardner in the “King Philip’s War”.
In 1675, during the “King Philip’s War” fighting,  William Ward’s house was burned down, his cattle were killed and one of his son’s was killed.

In 1675, New England had about 80,000 colonists.  They lived in 110 towns, of which 64 were in the Massachusetts Bay Colony, which then included the southwestern portion of Maine.  New Englanders had about 16,000 men of military age who were almost all a part of the local town militias, as universal training was require in all New England towns.  Many towns had built strong garrison houses for defense, and others also had stockades wall enclosing most of the town’s houses.  All of the towns were strengthened as the “King Philip’s War” progressed.  Some poorly populated towns were abandoned, when they did not have enough men to defend them.  Each town had a local militia based on all eligible men in the town, who all had to supply their own arms.  Only those who were too old, too young, or were disabled or in the clergy were excused from military service.  The town militias were usually only minimally trained and initially did relatively poorly against the Native warriors, until more effective training and tactics were devised as the war progressed.  Joint forces of colonial militia volunteers and Native American allies fighting together proved to be the most effective way to fight the opposing warring Native warriors.
In 1676, Richard Sawtelle’s home in Groton in the Massachusetts Bay Colony was one of five garrison houses in Groton.

On 13 March 1676, the town of Groton was burned and the inhabitants were all forced leave.  Richard Sawtelle took his family back 30 miles east to the town Watertown near the City of Boston after the attack on remote frontier town of Groton.

On 16 Jul 1677, at the end of the “King Philip’s War”, widower William Taylor married again widow Hannah (Merriam), the widow of Henry Axtell, who had been killed by Native warriors on the Marlborough-Sudbury Road in April, 1676.  Hannah had five children from her first marriage with Henry.

On 19 Sep 1677, Joshua Lamb of Roxbury purchased half of the famous “Roanoke Island” in the County of Albemarle in the Province of Carolina for £150 from Nicholas Paige of Boston.  Nicholas had earlier purchased the island from of Sr. William Berkeley, the Governor of the Virginia Colony.

On 28 Jan 1678, forty-seven-year-old Nathaniel Gary died from smallpox in the town of Roxbury in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  Nathaniel’s wife Anne Mattle (Douglas) was left raising their ten children, the oldest of which were seventeen-year-old twins daughters Mary and Elizabeth.  Thirteen-year-old daughter Sarah Gary was one of the ten children.  Widow Anne Mattle (Douglas) remarried five-years later in 1683 a Thomas Bishop in the town of Roxbury.  Anne and Thomas had no children.

On 11 Apr 1678, Eliezer Taylor was born to William Taylor and Hannah (Merriam).  Eliezer was named after the son of Deacon William Ward who was slain during “King Philip's War”.  Later on, 21 Jan 1680, William and Hannah had a second son William Jr., but the trauma of childbirth took the life of Hannah on the same day.  Yes, this was the second wife of William Taylor who died in child birth.
On 8 Jun 1679, Dorothy Lamb was born to Joshua Lamb and Mary (Alcock) in the town of Roxbury in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.

In 1679, England’s King Charles II, issued a charter for a New Hampshire Colony, which had been a part of the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  New Hampshire remained a separate colony for seven-years until it was absorbed back into the Dominion of New England later in 1686.
See the map on the next page of 1677 New England.  The six of the Praying Villages are identified with white crosses on the map.

blocks.caption: See the map on the next page of 1677 New England. The six of the Praying Villages are identified with white crosses o…

blocks.text: Note:  In the 1680’s a few wealthy and respectable businessmen in the Boston area began to purchase large tracts of unsettled land out west of the existing established colonial settlements.   The areas purchased were large, containing a number of square miles of land, with loosely defined boundaries.  Once the purchase was completed the new owner(s) would approach the Massachusett Bay Colony’s Colonial Governor and the General Court to confirm the title on the purchased land.  Most of the time the title was granted based on the condition that a certain number of families within a specified number of years would settle in the new land and a portion of the new settlement would be set aside for church and school lands and town buildings.
On 10 Feb 1681, Daniel Rice Sr. and Bethiah Ward were married in the town of Marlborough in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.

On 28 Nov 1683, widower William Taylor married his third wife widow Mary Cheevers in the town of Cambridge in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.  The marriage was the second for Mary who had earlier married William Lewis when she was fifteen-year-old and he was fifty-one-years-old.  Mary did not have had any children with he first husband.  William Taylor and Mary (Cheevers) had nine children, including daughter Elizabeth Taylor.  Mary (Cheevers) died later on 28 Jul 1697 giving birth to her ninth child daughter Marcy Taylor, who also died five months later.  Yes, this was William’s third wife to die giving birth to a child.  William Taylor remarried for the fourth time 1699, widow Sarah Parker (Wheeler) and they had two more children.
On 3 Jun 1684, Daniel Rice Jr. was born to Daniel Rice Sr. and Bethiah (Ward) in the town of Marlborough in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.

In 1684, John Leavens Jr. and his wife Elizabeth (Preston) left the town of Stratford in the Connecticut Colony and move to the town of Roxbury in the Connecticut Colony where they were received into the Roxbury Church 22 Apr 1684.  John had become a “master carpenter”.
Note:  The colonies of Massachusetts Bay, New York, Maine, New Hampshire, Vermont, Connecticut, Rhode Island and New Jersey all had towns named “Roxbury”.

The town of Roxbury in the Connecticut Colony had been an Indian Praying village called Wabaquasset that got involved in the “King Philip’s War”.  The Native people on the north side of the village had sided with King Philip and join in the fight against the English colonists.  The Native people living in the south side of the village favored the Mohegan tribe and joined on the English colonist’s side.  The Wabaquasset village was deserted during the war.  In 1682, the Massachusetts Bay Colony bought a large track of land from the Mohegan Native people, which include the old Wabaquasset Praying Village’s land.  In 1686, a group of 13 men from  Bay Colony settled the new town and named it New Roxbury, 70 miles west of Boston in the Connecticut Colony.  Later in 1690, Judge Samuel Sewall suggested the town change its name to Woodstock in the Connecticut Colony.

On 23 Oct 1684, the “Massachusetts Bay Company Charter” was revoked by King Charles II after repeated violations of the charter’s terms, including discrimination against Anglicans and Quakers and running an illegal mint (making money).
In 1685, Nathaniel Robbins was born to Robert Robbins and Mary (Maxwell) in the town of Canterbury in the Connecticut Colony.

In 1685, the “Dominion of New England” was created by English Parliament through administrative action that united all the New England Colonies and Mid-Atlantic Colonies, except for the Pennsylvania Colony.  The colonies deeply resented the union since their rights and colony charters were revoked by the process.  The specific objectives of the new dominion included: regulation of trade, reformation of land title practices to conform more to the English methods and practices, coordination on matters of defense, and a streamlining of the government administration into fewer centers.

In 1685, King Charles II died and his younger brother James the surviving son of former King Charles I became King James II.  James had been in exiled following the “English Civil War” and had served in both the French and Spanish Armies.  As a Catholic, King James II persecuted the Protestant Church of England’s clergy and was generally hated by most of the English people.
On 9 Oct 1685, Ralph Farnham III and Sarah Sterling were married in in the town of Andover, Massachusett in the Dominion of New England.

On 7 Jun 1686, Joshua Lamb led a group of investors in the town of Roxbury, Massachusetts in the Dominion of New England who purchase a place called “Towriad” from the local Native Americans in the area that was 6 miles north of Roxbury.  The area was renamed “Strawberry-hill” because to the wild strawberry that grew there.

On 22 Dec 1686, Joshua Lamb led a second group of Roxbury, Massachusetts investors who purchase twenty square mile plot of land, 60 miles west of Roxbury from the local Native people for £23.  The sale was confirmed by the Massachusetts General Court on the condition that within seven years there would be 60 families settled on the land.  The new town was named Rutland, Massachusetts in the Dominion of New England.
In 1685, Edward Preston was listed as one of the original proprietors of the town of New Haven, Connecticut in the Dominion of New England.

In 1686, Robert Robbins was one of the first to purchase Nashoba native lands that had been taken from the Wampanoag and Narragansett Native tribes at the end of the “King Philip’s War” back ten years earlier in 1676.

In 1686, England’s King James II appointed the former , Colony governor Sir  as the new “Dominion of New England” governor.  The new Dominion was composed of the former Massachusetts Bay Colony, , , the , and the .  Two years later New York, East Jersey and West Jersey colonies were added to the “Dominion of New England”.

In 1686 John Leavens Jr. and his family left Roxbury, Massachusetts and with eight other families and became the first settlers of the New Roxbury community in Connecticut in the Dominion of New England.  Thirty-six 20 acres lots were distributed amount the new settlers.  John Leavens Jr. sold his property in Roxbury, Massachusetts to Joseph Warren. (Not the famous Revolutionary War hero, who lived a hundred years later).  In 1690, John Jr. was made a selectman and at the head of a committee formed to build a meeting house for the town of New Roxbury.  New Roxbury was later renamed Woodstock in Connecticut.
In 1686, Sir Edmund Andros, as the governor of the new Dominion of New England, proposed new taxes, pushing aside the Massachusett General Court and forbidding all town meetings.
On 15 Apr 1687, Thomas Richardson was born to Nathaniel Richardson and Mary (Peacock) in the town of Woburn, Massachusetts in the Dominion of New England.

On 2 Nov 1687, John Preston and widow Sarah Gary were married in the town of Andover, Massachusetts in the Dominion of New England.  The marriage is the second for Sarah, her first husband twenty-four-year-old John Holt had died earlier on 10 Mar 1687.

In 1687, Deacon Edward Rice was the elected deacon of the Marlborough town church in Massachusetts in the Dominion of New England.  Deacon Edward Rice and his family were farming in the Marlborough area.

Starting in 1688 a series of four French and Indian Wars were fought between Britain and France in North America.  The first war was the “King Williams’ War” that started in 1688.  The war began when the French and English could not agree on the border between French Acadia and northern Maine.  The English claimed the border was the Penobscot River and the French insisted it was the Kennebec River.  In addition, many of the Native Americans who were forced flee north to Maine area after “King Philip’s War” now sought revenge and joined with Wabanaki Confederacy tribes, which were allied with the French against the English colonists in Maine.

The Wabanaki Confederacy was formed from the Mi’kmaq, Maliseet, Passamaquoddy and Penobscot tribes of Native Easter Algonquian speaking people.  The confederacy tribes felt they were being undermined by the English who kept trying to take their guns away.  Tensions rose in the area after English sailors killed a Native American young child, and the English colonial administrator Edmund Andros led a raid on small coastal town of Castine in northern Maine.  The English destroyed the home of Baron Jean-Vincent de Saint-Castin, who was a powerful French fur trader and soldier, living in the disputed area.  The Baron Saint-Castin had cemented a French alliance with the Wabanaki Confederacy people by marrying the daughter of the confederacy’s great Wabanaki Chief Madockawando.  The fighting between the English and the French and their Native American allies continued and by 1713 there were no English homes left in Acadia (now Nova Scotia) .  For more information in the “King Williams’ War” see page 215 for the French point of view on the war.

In 1688, Ephraim Smith Sr. served for six weeks in the military expedition organized by Governor Sir Edmund Andros against the French and their allies the Wabanaki Native Confederacy in the beginning of the “King Williams’ War.”
In 1688, Elizabeth Leavens was born to John Leavens Jr. and Elizabeth (Preston) in the town of Woodstock, Connecticut in the Dominion of New England.
In 1688, Elizabeth Taylor was born to William Taylor and Mary (Cheevers) in the frontier town of Marlborough, Massachusetts in the Dominion of New England.

In 1688, the English Parliament ran out of patience with King James II and asked the Dutch Prince William of Orange to be the next King of England.  King William III was married to Mary the oldest daughter of King James II.  Mary was a strong Protestant in her beliefs.  The newly imposed “Dominion of New England” fell apart shorty after 23 De 1688, when King James II vacated the English throne and fled to France.

On 18 April 1689, the colonists in the City of Boston rose up against the “Dominion of New England” rule.  A well-organized "mob" descended on the City of Boston and arrested the English Dominion government officials.  Governor Sir Edmund Andros had his quarters in Fort Mary, a garrison house on the south side of Boston where he and a number of other former Dominion officials took refuge.  The old Massachusetts Bay Colony leadership headed by ex-governor Simon Bradstreet was quickly restored.  English Dominion Governor Sir Edmund Andros was ordered by the restored Massachusetts Bay Colony leadership to surrender, for his own safety.  He refused, and tried to escape to an English ship the “Rose” anchored in the Boston harbor.  A small rowboat sent from the “Rose” was intercepted by colonial militia, and governor Andros was forced back into Fort Mary.  Negotiations ensued, and Governor Andros agreed to leave the fort to meet with the Massachusett rebel council.  Promised safe conduct, Governor Andros was marched under guard to the townhouse where the rebel council had assembled.  There he was told that "they must & would have the Government in their own hands", and that he was under arrest.  Sir Edmund Andros was taken to the home of former dominion treasurer John Usher, and held under close watch.  When Fort Mary fell into rebel hand on 19 April 1689 Governor Andros was move out to Castle Island in the harbor.  On 2 Aug 1689, Andros escaped and fled to Rhode Island, where he was recaptured and sent to England for trial.  Massachusetts Bay Colony agents in London refused to sign the charges made against Sir Edmund Andros, so the English court summarily dismissed the charges.

sections.title: The Province of Massachusetts Bay
sections.teaser: On 7 Oct 1691, England’s new King William and Queen Mary II rechartered the Massachusetts Bay Colony into the new “Province of Massachusetts Bay”.  The new p…

blocks.text: On 7 Oct 1691, England’s new King William and Queen Mary II rechartered the Massachusetts Bay Colony into the new “Province of Massachusetts Bay”.  The new province charter allowed England to take control over the former Massachusetts Bay Colony.  A new English governor Sir William Phips was placed in charge of the new province.  The province included the Massachusetts Bay Colony, the Plymouth Colony, the Province of Maine, the islands of Martha’s Vineyards and Nantucket, Nova Scotia and New Brunswick.  Later in 1697, Nova Scotia and New Brunswick were separated from the Province of Massachusetts.  The new charter restricted religious-based laws, such as the church membership requirement to become a voter, extended religious tolerance to other Protestant denominations other than the Anglican Church of England , required oaths to be taken to the king and not the government of Massachusetts and tightened the English government’s overall control over the colony.  The Puritans in the colonies worried that their religion and themselves were once again under attack.

In 1691, the new “Province of Massachusetts Bay” that was formed did not include New Hampshire.  The separate New Hampshire became a new English Crown Colony.  Often, however the “Province of Massachusetts Bay” and the New Hampshire Colony shared the same English appointed governor.
See the map below of the new “Province of Massachusetts Bay”, the New Hampshire Colony and the disputed lands between Province of New York and the Massachusetts and New Hampshire Colony areas.

blocks.caption: See the map below of the new “Province of Massachusetts Bay”, the New Hampshire Colony and the disputed lands between…

blocks.text: On 28 Oct 1691, Samuel Green and Elizabeth Upham were married in the town of Malden in the Province of Massachusetts Bay.

sections.title: Salem Witch Trials
sections.teaser: In 1691, the Province of Massachusetts became famous for its “witch trials”.  The witch hysteria started in the town of Chelmsford, which was 30 miles west o…

blocks.text: In 1691, the Province of Massachusetts became famous for its “witch trials”.  The witch hysteria started in the town of Chelmsford, which was 30 miles west of Salem, when Martha (Barrett) Sparks was accused of witchcraft and on 28 Oct 1691 was sent to a Boston prison.  Martha remained in prison for over a year until 6 Dec 1692 at the start of the first legal actions leading to the “Salem Witch Trials”.  Martha Sparks who was the granddaughter of Thomas Barrett Sr. and Margaret (Huntington) was never brought to trial.  Thirty-five-year-old Martha was the daughter of Thomas Barrett Jr. and Frances (Woolderson) of the town of Braintree, Massachusetts.  Martha was married to Henry Sparks, who was away fighting in the “King Philip’s War”, when Martha was accused and put in prison on suspicion of witchcraft.  The History of Chelmsford provides an account of John Arnold, the Boston jailer, who not only played host to Martha Sparks for 58 weeks, but also had as his guests the more celebrated ladies from Salem, including Sarah Good, Sarah Osbourn and Rebecca Nurse.

On 8 Jan 1692, Ralph Farnham Jr. died in Andover in Province of Massachusetts Bay.  Ralph Jr’s death occurred right before the start of the witch trial of Mary Tyler, the wife of Hopestill Tyler, who lived in Andover.  Ralph Farnham Jr., was expected to be called as a witness in the trial of Mary Tyler based on the belief that witchcraft was causing Ralph’s illness.

On 30 Jul 1692, Ralph Farnham III and his brother John Farnham were summoned as a witness in another trial of Martha Carrier for being a witch.  No record has been found on the testimony Ralph III provided in the trial even though the trial was transcribed.  Martha Carrier was found guilty in her trial and was hanged on 19 Aug 1692.  The primary charge against Martha Carrier was that her family brought smallpox into the Andover community.  Martha’s father and two brothers died from smallpox and Martha’s husband and children also caught smallpox and two of her four children died.  Search the Internet, if interested in more information on the witch trial of Martha Carrier.
On 1 Nov 1692, Martha Sparks's father Thomas Barrett Jr. petitioned the Governor and Council for her release. The  petition read as follows from the Massachusetts Archives, Vol. 135 No. 64:

“To his Excy, Sr. William Phips, Knt. Capn Genll and Governor in Cheife of their Majties Province of the Massachusetts Bay in New England and to the Honed.  Council thereof.  The Humble Petition of Thomas Barrett Jr. of Chelmsford in New England, in behalf of his Daughter Martha Sparkes, wife of Henry Sparkes who is now a Souldier in their Majties Service att the Easterne Parts, and soe hath beene for a Considersble Time, Humbly Showeth That yor Petitionrs Daughter hath Layne in Prison in Boston for the Space of Twelve months and Five days, being Committed by Thomas Danforth, Esq the Late Depty Governor, upon suspicion of Witchcraft, Since which noe Evidence hath appeared against her in any Such matter, neither hath any Given bond to prosecute her nor doth any one att this day accuse her of any such thing as yor Petitionr knows of. That Yor Petitionr hath ever since Kept two of her children - - the one of 5 years ye other of 2 years old, wch hath been a considerable Trouble and charge to him in his poore & meane Condition; besides yor Petitionr hath a Lame antient & sick wife, who for these 5 yeares & upwards past hath beene soe afflicted: as that shee is altogether rendred uncapable of affording herself any help, wch much augments his Trouble. Yor Poore Petitionr Earnestly and humbly Intreates Yor E'cy & honrs. to take his distressed condition into yor consideracon, And that you will please to order ye releasemt. of his Daughtr. from her confinement.  Whereby shee may returne home to her poore children to look after them, haveing nothing to pay the charge of her Confinemt.”

In early 1692, the Massachusetts “witch trials” were occurred during a period of governmental disruption.  The new government in the Province of Massachusetts Bay was just getting started and the new governor sent from England, Governor Phips, had just arrived and immediately had to head off into the western territories of Province to handle Native American problem that had popped up, leaving a vacuum of leadership in the new Provincial Government.
What is “Witchcraft”?  Answer; “Witchcraft” in the 1600s in the English American Colonies was:
A capital crime, but was difficult to prove.

Formally accusing someone of being a witch, which was often punishment enough, if the accused had to spend time in jail since prisoners had to pay all of their own expenses whether they were guilty or innocent while they were in jail.
A crime handled by the regular court system and evidence of witchcraft had to comply with regular rules of evidence for the court.

A case where if the accused was found innocent, he or she would often sue the accuser for slander (insult) or defamation (smear), as a way to get back the costs that were incurred while living in jail.
Often a common curse not meant to be taken literally.  It was like calling someone a bad name at the time.

In the spring of 1692, the rules of “Witchcraft” were changed at the same time the new Governor Phips was gone handling Native American issues in the western frontier of the new Province.  The new rule changes made it much easier convict someone of being a witch.  The result was more people were found guilty of witchcraft.  The new rules were:

Witchcraft was still a capital crime, but became much easier to prove because the court now allowed “Spectral Evidence” to be used in the trial.  “Spectral Evidence” was a witness’s testimony that the accused person's spirit or spectral shape appeared to him/her in a dream at the time the accused person's physical body was at another location.
Accused witches were arrested and put in leg irons and shackles, were denied food and were even tortured to make them confess their accused crimes.  See a photo of a leg iron and shackles below:

blocks.caption: Accused witches were arrested and put in leg irons and shackles, were denied food and were even tortured to make them…

blocks.text: A new special “Court of Oyer and Terminer” (French for “To hear and to determine”) was created to handle “Witch Trials”.  The new court allowed “Spectral Evidence” and assumed the accused was guilty until proven innocent by the trial.

Since it was difficult to get evidence that would satisfy a jury, the new court tried to get the accused to confess their witchcraft grimes, by using intimidation(bullying), lack of food, lack of legal counsel and even the use of torture.
The accused still had to pay all court costs, and if found guilty had to forfeit all their property.

On 6 Dec 1692, Thomas Barrett Jr. appeared before the Court on 6 Dec 1692, and guaranteed a bond for Martha Carrier's appearance before the next session of the Court.  Two days later she was set free after 58 weeks in prison.  For more information on the history of the Massachusett “Witch Trials” see the “Story of Jessie Maud Paff branch of Morris Rankin Batie’s Family Tree”
On 4 Apr 1693, Elizabeth Green was born to Samuel Green and Elizabeth (Upham) in Malden in the Province of Massachusetts Bay.
On 6 Sep 1694, Ephraim Smith Sr. and Mary Ramsdell were married in Topsfield in the Province of Massachusetts Bay.
On 25 Jul 1695, Nathaniel Farnham was born to Ralph Farnham III and Sarah (Sterling) in the town of Andover in the Province of Massachusetts Bay as their seventh of ten children.

On 16 Dec 1696, Walter Powers III and Rebecca (spelled Rebekah) Barrett were married in the town of Littleton in the Province of Massachusetts Bay.  Walter and Rebecca located in the northerly part of Littleton, west of Spectacle Pond adjoining the town of Groton, near “Brown Hill”.  When Walter III’s older brother William died, Walter III became the owner of his father’s home in Concord.  Walter Power III worked as an “innkeeper” in Littleton.

On 15 Mar 1697, the town of Haverhill in the Massachusetts Bay Colony suffered a major attack by Native American warrior and later on July 31, 1697, William Sterling disposed of his mills, houses, and lands on Fishing River in Haverhill and moved to the town of Lyme in the Connecticut Colony.  Later, William bought lands in the town of Lyme located on Lieutenant River  and emptied into the Lower Connecticut River near Long Island Sound, on the, where he engaged successfully built and operated in “ship building business”.
On 30 Oct 1697, the “Treaty of Ryswick” ended the “King William’s War” between England and France.
On 23 Dec 1697, Daniel Johnson and Dorothy Lamb were married in the town of Framingham in the Province of Massachusetts Bay.
On 30 Jan 1698, Ephraim Smith Jr. was born to Ephraim Smith Sr. and Mary (Ramsdell) in the town of Boxford in the Province of Massachusetts Bay.
On 17 Jun 1698, Hannah Preston was born to John Preston and Sarah (Gary) in the town of Andover in the Province of Massachusetts Bay as John and Sarah’s sixth of seven children.
Note: In 1702, King William III died and Anne the second daughter of King James II and a staunch supporter of Protestant beliefs became Queen Anne of England.

In 1702, the second French and Indian War, called the “Queen Anne’s War”, broke out between England, France and Spain over control of land in North America.  See page 221 for information on the “Queen Anne’s War”.

In 1702, Robert Robbins exchanged the land he owned in the town of Nashoba for land eight miles northwest in the town of Groton in the Province of Massachusetts Bay.  Groton town officials voted to give Robert , three acres of meadowland and ten acres of upland in exchange for his lands in Nashoba.

In 1704, Walter Powers III took over responsibility of his sixty-five-year-old father Walter Jr., and his sixty-three-year-old mother Tryall, who were living in their home in the town of Nashoba in the Province of Massachusetts Bay.
On 23 Aug 1706, Zebadiah Johnson was born to Daniel Johnson and Dorothy (Lamb) in the town of Marlborough in the Province of Massachusetts Bay.
Note:  On 1 May 1707, the “Kingdom of Great Britain” was established and Queen Ann becomes its first monarch.  Great Britain now included England, Wales and Scotland.

On 22 Sep 1710, Josiah Powers Sr. was born to Walter Powers III and Rebecca (Barrett) in a “Garrison House” on Neshoba Hill in the town of Littleton in the Province of Massachusetts Bay as their seventh and last child.

Note:  Around 1712, the “Industrial Revolution” started in Great Britain.  At this time most people Britain resided in small, rural communities where their daily existences revolved around farming.  Life was difficult, incomes were meager and malnourishment and disease were common.  People produced the bulk of their own food, clothing, furniture and tools.  Manufacturing was done in homes or small, rural shops using hand tools or simple machines.  The change started in agriculture where improved tools and early machines increased the amount of food that could be produced using less manual labor.  Most of the power used in doing farm tasks had come from human power or animal power.  Where available, water power (water wheels) and wind power (windmills) had been used to power small early factories.  In 1712 Thomas Newcomen, unveiled his steam-driven piston engine, and everything began to change.  Steam engines improved rapidly as the century advanced, and were put to greater and greater use.  More efficient and powerful engines were employed in coalmines, textile mills and dozens of other heavy industries.
In 1712, Nathaniel Robbins and Elizabeth Leavens were married in Concord in the Province of Massachusetts Bay.
In 1712, Thomas Richardson and Elizabeth Green were married in Malden in the Province of Massachusetts Bay.
On 12 Feb 1713, Daniel Rice Jr. and Elizabeth Taylor were married in Marlborough in the Province of Massachusetts Bay.
On 8 Oct 1713, Esther Richardson was born to Thomas Richardson and Elizabeth (Green) in Malden in the Province of Massachusetts Bay.
On 20 Jun 1714, Hannah Rice was born to Daniel Rice Jr. and Elizabeth (Taylor) in Marlborough in the Province of Massachusetts Bay.
In 1714, Martha Mercy Anon was born in Concord in the Province of Massachusetts Bay.  No information beyond her marriage to Capt. Josiah Powers has been found for Martha.

On 1 Aug 1714, Queen Ann died and George, the son of Sophie the daughter of Elizabeth of Bohemia, became King George I of England.  Although there were over 50  related to Queen Anne, the “1701 Act of Settlement” passed by the English parliament prohibited a Catholics from inheriting the British throne and George was Anne's closest living Protestant relative.  King George I was an old German who did not speak English.  The government of England was left in the hands of Sir Robert Walpole who became the first ‘Prime Minister’ of England.  King George I, spent little time in England preferring to live in his home in Hanover Germany.  During King George I’s reign, the powers of the monarch diminish and the Kingdom of Great Britain began to transition to a cabinet form of government with a prime minister leading the country’s government.

Back in 1686 the land known as “Towtaid”, which was 50-miles west of Boston, had been purchased from the Nipmuc Native people by a Joshua Lamb and other businessmen from the town of Roxbury in the Province of Massachusetts Bay.  The area was renamed “Strawberry Hill” after its profuse wild strawberries.  Later the Massachusetts’s General Court renamed the area Leicester, after the shire in England that Samuel Green’s family came from.  Leicester's standing militia company at the beginning of the American Revolutionary War marched to Lexington and Concord to aid in the defeat of the British regulars.  The term "Minuteman" originated in Leicester, when Colonel William Henshaw declared at a 1774 meeting of the “Committee on Safety” said: "we must have companies of men ready to march upon a minute's notice."

In 1717, Samuel Green, his wife Elizabeth (Upham) and their children, including daughter  Elizabeth left the town of Malden north of the city of Boston and moved 60-miles west to an area known as” Strawberry Hill”.  Samuel was selected for a committee empowered to deed the lands in “Strawberry Hill” to new settlers.  Samuel built the first saw-mill and the first grist-mill in the new settlement.
See an engraving on the net page by John Warner Barber of the town of Leicester, Massachusetts:

blocks.caption: See an engraving on the net page by John Warner Barber of the town of Leicester, Massachusetts:

blocks.text: 1717 Thomas Richardson, wife Elizabeth (Green) and their children, including four-year-old daughter Esther Richard, were part of a group of families that left Malden, and move out to the new town Leicester in the Province of Massachusetts Bay.

In 1718, the “Transportation Act” was passed by the British Parliament.  As the Industrial Revolution progressed workers began to leave the farms and move into the cities looking for jobs.  The cities quickly became over crowed and crime became a major problem.  The  new “Transportation Act” had two purposes: first to take criminals off the streets and out of the crowed jails and second to provide cheap labor for the British American Colonies.  Less serious crimes were punished by 7 years of labor in a colony.  More serious crimes were punished with 14 years to life sentences as labors in a colony.  After completion of the punishment period a pardon was given to individual and they were free to located to where every they pleased.  The punishment for returning to England before completing the punishment period was death.

The “Transportation Act” allowed merchants to contract for the cost of transportation of jailed convicts, by putting up a security bond that paid for the transportation to the colonies and defined the term of service that would have to be completed.  To accomplish this, the act declared that the contractor owned the “convict” as property.  Significantly under section five of the new act, it was noted that many idle youth were "lurking" about London and elsewhere, who wanting jobs and would be surely soon be tempted toward crime, before an actual crime had been committed.  The new “Transportation Act” thus also allowed English merchants and others to contract with unemployed 15–20-year-olds, who were willing to be transported to the colonies to work up to eight years as “indentured servants” without having committed any crime .  The “Transportation Act” was temporary slavery.

Between 1718 and 1776, over 52,000 convicts and unemployed youth were transported to the American Colonies, primarily to Virginia and Maryland.  The outbreak of the American Revolution ended the process in 1776 in America.  From 1788 to 1869, more than 160,000 prisoners and unemployed youth were sent instead to the British Colony in Australia.
On 19 Mar 1719, Nathaniel Farnham and Hannah Preston were married in the town of Andover in the Province of Massachusetts Bay.

On 12 Jul 1721, John Robbins was born to Nathaniel Robbins and Elizabeth (Leavens) in the town of Canterbury in the Connecticut Colony.  John was baptized on 13 Aug 1721 in Canterbury by his parents Nathaniel and Elizabeth.

From 1722 to 1725, the “Dummer’s War” was fought between the British Province of Massachusetts Bay and the Wabanaki Confederation (specifically the Mi’kmag, Maliseet, Penobscot and Abenaki tribes), which was supported by French Colony of Canada.  The eastern theater of the war was fought primarily along the border between the British Province of Massachusetts Bay and the French Colony of  on island of .  A separate western theater of the war was fought in northern Province of Massachusetts Bay, which included New Hampshire and the Québec French Canada Colony.  The war was fought over disputes about the border between the French and English Colonies.  The 1713 “Treaty of Utrecht”, which had ended the “Queen Anne’s War” had transferred the Hudson Bay, Acadia and Newfoundland to Great Britain, but did not include the Wabanaki Confederation representatives.

On 13 Aug 1723, Gray Lock, an Abenaki warrior chieftain, began guerrilla raids against settlements in Northfield, Massachusetts.  In response, Governor Dummer ordered the construction of  in New Hampshire.  The fort became a major base of operations for scouting and punitive expeditions into Abenaki country.  Brattleboro became a town in the New State of Vermont later in 1777.

On 30 Apr 1723, John Robbins was one of eighteen men who received one-half share of the common land in the resolution over a dispute over land between the towns of Windham and Canterbury in the Connecticut Colony.
In 1723, Walter Powers III was given the second “pew place” in Littleton town church because he paid the next to the largest tax in the town of Littleton in the Province of Massachusetts Bay.

On 11 Jun 1727,  England’s King George I died and his son George, another German, became King George II of England.  Sir Robert Walpole, the de facto Prime Minister of Great Britain, continued to run the country under the second German king of Great Britain.  The fact that two successive Kings of Great Britain were Germans and lived out of the country, strengthen even more the power and control in the day-to-day operation of Great Britain government by the British Parliament.
In 1727, John Preston sold to his nephew land in the town of Windham in the Connecticut Colony he had received for his service with Captain Gardner in the “King Philip’s War”.

In 1727, Samuel Green owned 929 acres of land in the frontier town of Leicester in the Province of Massachusetts Bay.  Samuel was selected to be the town’s Captain of the first military company raised in Leicester.

On 7 Apr 1730. Rebecca Farnham was born to Nathaniel Farnham and Hannah (Preston) in the town of Hampton in the Connecticut Colony.  Rebecca was baptized on 12 Apr 1730 in Hampton by her parents Nathaniel and Hannah.
On 18 Nov 1731, Zebadiah Johnson and Esther Richardson were married in the town of Shrewsbury in the Province of Massachusetts Bay.

In 1731, Daniel Rice Jr., wife Elizabeth (Taylor) and their children, including seventeen-year-old daughter Hannah were living in the northeasterly part of Shrewsbury on their family farm.  The primary crop grown in the Shrewsbury area was apples grown in large planted  apple orchards.  The Rice family farm stayed on the family for the next three generations.

Note:  By 1732, all the British American colonies had put in place the requirement of either owning substantial amount of personal property or paying taxes to be eligible for a man to vote in elections in their colony.
On 16 Apr 1732, eighteen-year-old Hannah Rice requested to be baptized in Shrewsbury in the Province of Massachusetts Bay.

In 1732, Capt. Josiah Powers Sr. and Martha Mercy Anon were married in the town of Quabbin in the western frontier in the Province of Massachusetts Bay.  Josiah was a Captain in the local Militia.  Quabbin was renamed the town of Greenwich in 1739.  Today the town no longer exists as it was covered with water in 1939 when the Quabbin Reservoir was built to supply water to the City of Boston.
On 16 Aug 1733, Ephraim Smith Jr. and Hannah Rice were married in Shrewsbury in the Province of Massachusetts Bay.

In 1741, Benning Wentworth, a successful American merchant and colonial administrator was appointed the new governor of the New Hampshire Colony.  Governor Wentworth immediately laid claim on behalf of the New Hampshire Colony of all the lands west of the Connective River and east of the Hudson River.  Governor Wentworth began issuing controversial land grants to settlers that were challenged by the Province of New York, which also claimed the territory between the two large rivers.  The claim disputes eventually resulted in the formation of the separate Vermont Republic.  See a map of the Province of New Hampshire on the next page:

blocks.caption: In 1741, Benning Wentworth, a successful American merchant and colonial administrator was appointed the new governor…

blocks.text: On 28 Dec 1742, Josiah Powers Jr. was born to Capt. Josiah Powers Sr. and Martha Mercy (Anon) in the town of Littleton in the Province of Massachusetts Bay.

In 1744, the “King George’s War” started between Great Britain and its American colonies and France and its Canada colony, as the third of the “French and Indian Wars”.  The war took place primarily in the British provinces of New York and Massachusetts Bay, which included Maine, New Hampshire and Nova Scotia.  The war’s most significant action involved a 1745 military expedition organized by Massachusetts Governor William Shirley that besieged and captured the French fortress of Louisbourg on Cape Breton Island in in Nova Scotia.

The “King George’s War” was fought on the frontiers between the northern British colonies and New France Canada.  Each side had Native American allies and a number of outlying colonial villages were raided by native warriors and captives were taken for ransom, or adoption by Native American tribes.  As a result of the frequent raiding Governor William Shirley ordered the construction of a chain of frontier outposts along the border between Province of Massachusetts Bay out to the border with the Province of New York and the French Canada Colony.  On 28 Nov 1745, the French with their Native American allies raided and destroyed the village of Saratoga in the Province of New York, killing or capturing more than one hundred of the inhabitants of the village.  After the attack the British abandoned all their settlements in the Province of New York north of the town of Albany.

In 1746, the France launched a major military expedition to recover the Louisbourg Fortress.  The French soldiers were beset by storms, disease, and finally the death of their commander and the expedition's survivors gave up and returned to Canada in tatters.  The “King George’s War” took a heavy toll on the northern British colonies.  The Province of Massachusetts had estimated 8% of the military age men were killed the fighting.
On 2 Jul 1746, Thomas Johnson was born to Zebadiah Johnson and Esther (Richardson) in the town of Shrewsbury in the Province of Massachusetts Bay.
On 17 Feb 1748, Elizabeth Smith was born to Ephraim Smith Jr. and Hannah (Rice) in Shrewsbury in the Province of Massachusetts Bay.
On 21 Jan 1748 John Robbins and Rebecca Farnham were married in the town of Hampton in the Connecticut Colony.

Note:  In the 1750s, many landlords in the Scottish Highlands, who were facing bankruptcy over crippling debts, began to forcibly remove their farm working tenants from their open grazing fields.  The Scottish open pastures were then fenced allowing the creation of pastoral farms stocked with sheep, which were more profitable and required fewer farm workers.  The change resulted many Scottish families losing their source of income as fewer workers were required in fenced pastoral sheep raising system resulted in whole Highland communities leaving Scotland and immigrating, mostly to the British North America colonies.
Note:  In 1752, Great Britain and its colonies changed from the Julian to the Gregorian calendar.  The first day of the year changed from March 25 to January 1.
In 1752, Mary Robbins was born to John Robbins and Rebecca (Farnham) in the town of Leicester in the Province of Massachusetts Bay.

sections.title: French and Indian War
sections.teaser: In 1754, the “French and Indian War” broke out as the fourth and final war in North America between the British colonies and their Native American allies aga…

blocks.text: In 1754, the “French and Indian War” broke out as the fourth and final war in North America between the British colonies and their Native American allies against the French Canada colony with their own Native American allies.  At the start of the war, the France had only about 60,000 French settlers in Canada, compared to about 2 million people in the British colonies.  The British colonists were supported at various times by the Native American Iroquois, , and  tribes.  The France on the other hand were supported by the larger Native American Wabanaki Confederacy, which included members of the , , , , , , , and  Native tribes.  The outnumbered French soldiers and Canadian colonists were particularly dependent on their Native American allies.  The war started over a dispute for control of the strategic land at the confluence of the  and  called the “Forks of the Ohio”.  The French had constructed Fort Duquesne on the site, which today the city of  in Pennsylvania.  The fighting started on 28 May 1754, when a British military unit led by twenty-two-year-old Lt. Colonel , ambushed a small French force at the “Battle of Jumonville Glen” (present day Hopwood, Pennsylvania 50 miles south of Pittsburgh ).  The conflict quickly exploded across the British and French colonies in North America and extended to the seizure of hundreds of French merchant ships at sea by the British Royal Navy.  The British Navy next implemented an effective blockage of the French Canada colony preventing the France from sending additional French soldiers to fight against the British Army supporting the British Colonies.  With numbers on their side the British forces overwhelmed the French forcing them to surrender to the British Army after the fall of Quebec City in 1759.

In 1756, two years after the British and French starting fighting in North America the “Seven Years’ War” started Europe splitting the area into two coalitions: on one side was Great Britain, Prussia, Portugal and some small German states.  On the other side were France, Holy Roman Empire, Russia, Spain and Sweden.  Not much was accomplished in the conflict in Europe other than the rise of Prussia as a major European military power.  The war ended with the “Treaty of Paris” signed in 1763.  The treaty formally ended the “Seven Years' War” in Europe and the  “French and Indian War” in the North American, and marked the beginning of an era of British dominance outside of Europe.  Great Britain gained all the France's possessions in North America in the “Treaty of Paris”.  Spain also lost Florida to the British, but gained the French territories west of the Mississippi River.
See the story “Long Road to the Constitution” for additional historical information on the “French and Indian War” its impact on the forming of the United States of America.

In 1759, Zebadiah Johnson served as the “Church Assessor” for the town of Andover in the Province of Massachusetts.  The “Church Assessor” provided information on the local church needs to the Province of Massachusetts’ General Council to support the church’s requirements.  At this time all the British American Colonial governments were proving finical support to the Protestant Anglican churches in their colonies.

On 25 Oct 1760, King George II died and his twenty-two-year-old grandson George, the first English-born and English-speaking king in 46 years, became King George III.  King George III’s father, the Prince of Wales, had died from a lung injury nine years earlier leaving young George the next in line to be king.

On 20 Oct 1761, a new town was charter by Capt. Josiah Powers Sr. and 65 associates on 23,000 acres, in the New Hampshire Colony that was initially named Neshoba.  Capt. Powers Sr. named the new town Neshoba after his boyhood home of Neshoba in the Province of Massachusetts Bay.  Later in 1784, the town was renamed Brandon in the State of Vermont where Capt. Powers Sr. owned over 3,000 acres of land.

In 1763, George Grenville became the new British Prime Minister.  Great Britain had just won the “Seven Year War” and the “French and Indian War” against the French.  The victories were great, but Great Britain was in a desperate need of money to pay off its large war debts and the ongoing in the cost of the maintaining 10,000 British troops in North America.  The primary reason for retaining such a large British military force in North America after the war was to prevent the demobilizing the army and putting some 1,500 loyal British military officers out of work.  It became politically prudent to retain a large peacetime British army, but citizens back in Great Britain were averse to maintaining a standing army at home, so it was necessary to leave and garrison of troops in North America.  Raising taxes in Britain was also out of the question, since there had been virulent (strong) protests when a tax on the production of cider had been proposed.  Another source of money was need to pay the British army garrison in North America.

On 7 Oct 1763, King George III issued a “Royal Proclamation” that forbade all settlements west of a line drawn along the center of the Appalachian Mountains, which was delineated as the eastern boundary of a Native American Reserve.  Exclusion from the vast region of land in the Ohio River Valley created great discontent between the British government and wealthy colonial land speculators and new settlers looking for land to settle on and farm.  The proclamation and its probation of access to western lands was one of the first significant areas of dispute between Britain and its American Colonies and will become a contributing factor leading to the later American Revolution.  See the map below that shows the “Proclamation Line of 1763”.

blocks.caption: On 7 Oct 1763, King George III issued a “Royal Proclamation” that forbade all settlements west of a line drawn along…

blocks.text: On 20 Jul 1764, King George III established a boundary line between  Colony and the Province of New York along the west bank of the , north of the Province of  Bay, and south of .  The Province of New York refused to recognize the land titles known as the  (towns created by land grants sold by the New Hampshire Colony Governor ).  The New Hampshire settlers quickly organized in opposition to the Province of New York wishes to absorbed the New Hampshire area.  In 1770, , his brothers  and Levi, and the Allen’s' cousins  and Remember Baker, recruited an informal militia known as the “Green Mountain Boys” to protect their interests in New Hampshire in opposition to Province of New York wishes.

In 1765, the “Stamp Act” was enacted by the British Parliament that imposed a direct tax on the colonies of British America and required that many printed materials in the colonies had to be produced on stamped paper made in London carrying an embossed revenue stamp as a tax to raise money.
See the story “Long Road to the Constitution” for additional historical information on the steps Great Britain took to force their American Colonies to revolt.
On 8 Jan 1769, Josiah Powers Jr. and Mary Robbins were married in the town of Westmoreland in the New Hampshire Colony.
On 24 Sep 1771, Thomas Johnson and Elizabeth Smith were married in the town of Shrewsbury in the Province of Massachusetts Bay.

sections.title: American Revolution
sections.teaser: On 5 Mar 1770, the “Boston Massacre” occurred in the City of Boston in the Province of Massachusetts Bay.  The massacre occurred when a group of nine British…

blocks.text: On 5 Mar 1770, the “Boston Massacre” occurred in the City of Boston in the Province of Massachusetts Bay.  The massacre occurred when a group of nine British soldiers shot five American colonists in a crowd of three to four hundred colonists who were harassing the soldiers verbally and throwing various projectiles at the solders.  The event was heavily publicized as "a massacre" by leading American Patriots.  British troops had been stationed in the Province of Massachusetts Bay since 1768 in order to support the British crown-appointed officials and to enforce unpopular British Parliamentary legislation.
