Three years later on 16 Dec 1773, the “Boston Tea Party” occurred as a political protest at Griffin's Wharf in the city of Boston, in the Province of Massachusetts.  The American colonists dressed as Native American warriors demonstrated their frustration and angry at Britain for imposing a new tax on tea and dumped 342 chests of tea into the Boston harbor.

On 20 May 1774, the British Parliament passed the “Massachusetts Government Act” that revoked the 1691 charter of the Province of Massachusetts Bay and appointed a new British Royal governor over the former province with wide-ranging powers.  The Massachusetts colonial leaders argued the act altered the basic historical structure of American colonial government and vehemently opposed the new act and would not let it operate.

In Oct 1774, the “Province of Massachusetts Bay” came to an end, when members of the General Court of Massachusetts met in violation of the “Massachusetts Government Act” and established the “Massachusetts Provincial Congress”.  Although the new British Governor Gage continued military rule in Boston, the new provincial congress had in effect rule over the rest of the former province.  Hostilities between the British troops and rebel militia colonists six months later started the in April 1775 at the towns of Lexington and Concord, and continued with the siege of the City of Boston.

On 17 Mar 1776, the British were forced to evacuate the City of Boston, ending the siege of the city and bringing the city under colonial rebel control.  On May 1, 1776, the provincial congress adopted a resolution declaring the Province of Massachusetts to be independent of the crown; this was followed up on 4 Jul 1776 by the “Declaration of Independence”, declaring the independence of all thirteen of the American Colonies.  In Oct 1779, a new Constitution of the  was agreed upon and went into effect in Jun 1780.  In elections held in in Oct 1780, John Hancock was elected the first  along with representatives to the commonwealth's first General Court.

See the story “Long Road to the Constitution” for additional historical information on the events leading to the American Revolution, the creation of the United States of America’s Constitution and the formation of the States’ governments and the later National Government.
On 29 Nov 1774, Nathaniel Powers was born to Josiah Powers Jr. and Mary (Robbins) in the town of Chesterfield in the New Hampshire Colony.

In Apr 1775, Nathaniel Farnham enlisted in Captain Jonathan Wentworth’s company of Colonel Enoch Poor’s 2nd New Hampshire regiment, which in Aug 1775 was designated the “11th Regiment of Foot” in the Continental Army for eight months.  A month later he participated in the “Capture of Fort Ticonderoga” where Ethan Allen and his “Green Mountain Boys” under the command of Colonel Benedict Arnold defeated the British soldiers at the fort and captured the fort’s British cannons.  The cannons and other armaments were  back to the City of  by Colonel  and used to  and break the British s.

In June 1775, Josiah Powers Jr. enlisted in Captain Jacob Hinds Company, of Colonel James Reed’s 3rd New Hampshire Regiment, which in Aug 1775 was designated the “3rd Regiment of Foot” in the Continental Army and fought against the British forces during the American Revolutionary War.  The regiment saw action on 17 June 1775, at the “Battle of Bunker Hill”, which is more properly known as the “Battle of Breeds Hill”, in the Siege of Boston by the British.
Josiah Powers Jr was in the Col. James Reed’s “3rd Regiment of Foot” defended Breeds Hill in the middle Continental defense line of troops.  See a map of Battle on the next page:

blocks.caption: Josiah Powers Jr was in the Col. James Reed’s “3rd Regiment of Foot” defended Breeds Hill in the middle Continental d…

blocks.text: On 28 Oct 1775, a census was conducted of the people living in the town of Temple, New Hampshire.  Zebadiah Johnson was listed as: 5 males under age 16, 1 male age 16-50, 0 males over 60 years old, 1 Person in Army, 4 females, 0 guns, 0 powder.

In 1775, Zebadiah Johnson was selected as one of two men to serve as the town of Temple’s; Municipal, Political and Religious Officers.  Zebadiah also served a one of the town’s two “Tithingman” (English Peace Officer).

In Apr 1776, Nathaniel Farnham was involved in the “Battle of Cedars” (Cedars was a community north of the Saint Lawrence River 28 miles west of City of Montreal in British Canada).  Brigadier General Benedict Arnold, commanding the American Continental military garrison camped at Montreal, and in Apr 1776 placed a detachment of his troops in the community of Cedars, after hearing rumors that the British and their Native American allies were making military preparations to the west of Montreal.  The Continental garrison surrendered on 19 May 1776 after a confrontation with a combined force of British and Native American warriors led by Captain George Forster.  American reinforcements that were sent to Cedars were also captured after a brief skirmish the next day.  All of the Continental Army captives were eventually released after negotiations between British Captain Forster and General Arnold, who had arrived with a sizable Continental force into the area.  The terms of the agreement required the American Continental Army to release an equal number of British prisoners, but the deal was renounced by the Continental , and no British prisoners were freed.  Colonel Timothy Bedel and Lieutenant Issacs Butterfield, leaders of the American Continental force at the Cedars, were both court martialed (a military trial) and cashiered (a ritual dismissal from the Continental Army) for their roles in the Cedars garrison surrender.

On 4 Jul 1776, Zebadiah Johnson was one of 84 men living in the town of Temple in New Hampshire who pledged their support for a Continental Congress’ resolution professing one’s wiliness to risk one’s life and fortune, and take up arms in resistance to the hostile attacks of the British Fleets and Armies against the United American Colonies.

On 18 Jul 1776, Zebadiah Johnson was serving as a Sergeant in the Colonel Isaac Wyman’s New Hampshire Militia that had mustered two days earlier at Keene, New Hampshire and marched to Fort at Number 4 (A stockade fortification protecting Plantation Number 4 located along the Connecticut River in New Hampshire) and then on to Fort Ticonderoga where the regiment stayed until Dec 1776, when the regiment returned to New Hampshire for winter quarters.

sections.title: State of Vermont
sections.teaser: Back in 1763, after the “French and Indian War” and the signing of the “Treaty of Paris” the French lost all their North American possessions.  The end of th…

blocks.text: Back in 1763, after the “French and Indian War” and the signing of the “Treaty of Paris” the French lost all their North American possessions.  The end of the war did not however solve the issues over Vermont.  In 1775, after the outbreak of the American Revolutionary War, the “Green Mountain Boys” assisted a force from Connecticut, led by Benedict Arnold, that capturing the British fort at Ticonderoga.  Thereafter, the Continental Congress in Philadelphia directed the New York colony's revolutionary congress to fund and equip Allen's militia as a ranger regiment of the Continental Army, which it did.

The New York, Massachusetts and New Hampshire Provinces all made claims on Vermont.  The issue was finally settled in a second Continental Convention on 8 Jul 1777 when the Constitution of Vermont was approved, in the middle of the Revolutionary War.  At the end of the War, settlers began to enter Vermont.  See the Map of Vermont on the next page:

blocks.caption: The New York, Massachusetts and New Hampshire Provinces all made claims on Vermont. The issue was finally settled in…

blocks.text: In 1776, Josiah Powers Jr. moved his family to the town of Brandon, which is 16 miles north of Rutland Vermont.  Josiah Powers Jr. was a member of the convention with his father that formed the State Constitution of Vermont and later was a member of the 1778 Vermont State Legislature.

On 4 Jun 1777, Josiah Powers Sr. represented the towns of Neshobe and Whiting, which are 24 miles north of Rutland at the Vermont State Convention in the city of Windsor, Vermont, which 14 miles south of Hartford.  The purpose of the convention was to determine how to form a new State Government.
On 2 Mar 1778, Josiah Powers Jr. enlisted as a private in Capt. William Ellis’s company the 3rd Battalion of the New Hampshire Continental Troops fighting in the American Revolutionary War.
On 8 Jun 1778, Esther Johnson was born to Thomas Johnson and Elizabeth (Smith) in the small town of Phillipston in the Commonwealth of Massachusetts.

On 16 Aug 1777, as part of the Continental Army’s Saratoga campaign, Josiah Powers Jr. fought in the “Battle at Bennington” in Walloomsac, New York, about 10 miles west from Bennington in Vermont.  A Continental force of 2,000 men, primarily New Hampshire and Massachusetts militiamen, led by General John Stark, and reinforced by Vermont militiamen led by Colonel Seth Warner and members of the “Green Mountain Boys”, decisively defeated a detachment of General John Burgoyne's army led by Lieutenant Colonel Friedrich Baum.
In Oct 1778, Josiah Powers Sr. died when he drowned crossing in the swollen Connecticut River on horseback, while he was returning from the Vermont State Convention in Windsor, Vermont.
On 1 Jan 1779, Josiah Powers Jr. re-enlisted as a private in Capt. William Ellis’s company the 3rd Battalion of the New Hampshire Continental Troops.

On 2 Aug 1790, the first census of the entire United States was conducted on “Census Day”, as mandated by Article I, Section 2 of the United States Constitution and applicable laws.  In the first census, the population of the United States was enumerated (counted) to be 3,929,214 people.  The U. S. Congress assigned responsibility for the 1790 census to the marshals of United States judicial districts under an act, which with minor modifications and extensions, governed census taking until the 1840 census. "The law required that every household be visited, that completed census schedules be posted in 'two of the most public places within (each jurisdiction), there to remain for the inspection of all concerned and that the aggregate amount of each description of persons for every district be transmitted to the President of the United States”.  President Washington wanted the census taken to better understand the number of military age men were living in the new country, if war broke out again with Great Britain.  Both Secretary of State Thomas Jefferson and President Washington expressed skepticism over the census results, believing that the true population had been undercounted.

In the 1790 U. S. Census, Zebadiah Johnson and his wife Esther (Richardson were living in Shrewsbury, Worcester Co., Massachusetts.  Two other men over 16 and two women were living with Zebadiah who were probable their adult children living their parents`.
In the 1790 U. S. Census, Thomas Johnson, wife Elizabeth (Smith) and twelve-year-old daughter Ester Johnson were living in Westminster, Worcester Co. Massachusetts.
In 1790, Josiah Powers Jr. was living in the town of Chesterfield, New Hampshire, with a wife and four children.  No 1790 U. S. Census record has been found for Josiah and his family.
In 1791, Warren Coss was born to unknown parents in the town of Lowell in Massachusetts.  No information of who Warren’s parents has been found.
In the 1800 U. S. Census, Josiah Powers Jr. was living in the town of Marlboro in Vermont, with a wife and four children.
In the 1800 U. S. Census, Nathaniel Powers was living in the town of Westminster in Vermont, with a wife and one child.
On 13 Jul 1800, Nathaniel Powers and Esther Johnson were married in the town of Westminster, Vermont.
On 30 Oct 1800, Edith Powers was born to Nathaniel Powers and Esther (Johnson) in the town of Athens in Vermont.  Westminster and Athens in Vermont are about eight-miles apart.
No 1800 U. S. Census record has been found for fourteen-year-old Warren Coss.  Warren’s parents’ names are unknown.

In 1800, in the secret treaty of “Third Treaty of San Ildefonso”, France regained the western area of North America from Spain, which France had lost after the “French and Indian war” back in the 1763 “Treaty of Paris”.

In 1803, four years after acquiring the Louisiana Territory, Napoleon Bonaparte sold, it to the United States for $15,000,000 or 3 cents per acre, doubling the size of the United States.  See a map below showing the Louisiana Purchase area.

blocks.caption: In 1803, four years after acquiring the Louisiana Territory, Napoleon Bonaparte sold, it to the United States for $15…

blocks.text: The 1810 U. S. census, which was the third census for the United States, most of the census records were destroyed in a building fire.  None of the members of this family tree story have 1810 U. S. Census records.
In the 1820 U. S. Census, Nathaniel Powers was living in the town of Westminster in Vermont, with a wife and seven children.
In the 1820 U. S. Census, widow Elizabeth Johnson (Smith) was living in Phillipston, Worcester Co., Massachusetts.
No 1820 U. S. Census record had been found for twenty-nine-year-old Warren Coss who had been living in of Lowell, Massachusetts before the census and again four years later when he married.
On 30 Sep 1824, Warren Coss and Edith Powers were married in Lowell, Massachusetts.

In Jul 1827, Joseph Warren Coss was born to Warren Coss and Edith (Powers).  The location of the birth is in question.  No birth certificated has been found and different documents identify different locations in Massachusetts and New Hampshire for the place of Joseph’s birth.

In the 1830 U. S. Census, Warren Coss was listed as living in Lowell, Massachusetts.  A female age 20-30 (thirty-year-old wife Edith) and a male under 5 (two-year-old son Joseph Warren Coss) were living with Warren Coss.

In the 1830 U. S. Census, Nathaniel Powers was listed as living in Westminster, Vermont with a 50-59-year-old female (fifty-two-year-old wife Esther).  A second set of adult male and female and six children are also listed, indicating a separate family is living with Nathaniel and his wife Esther (Johnson), probably one of their adult children’s family.
In the 1840 U. S. Census, sixty-six-year-old Nathaniel Powers was listed as living in Westminster, Windham, Vermont with a 60-69-year-old female sixty-two-year-old wife Esther (Johnson).

In the 1840 U. S. Census, Warren Coss was listed as living in Athens in Vermont.  A female age 30-39 (thirty-nine-year-old wife Esther) and a male 10-14 (twelve-year-old son Joseph Warren) and a male 5-9 were living with Warren Coss.  Warren was farming for his living.

After 1840, sixty-six-year-old Nathaniel Powers and sixty-one-year-old Esther (Johnson) left their home in Westminster Vermont, where they were married and lived for forty-years and picked up and move from Westminster over 1,000 miles west to the town of Palmyra in Wisconsin.
On 13 Jul 1846, seventy-two-year Nathaniel Powers died in the new town of Palmyra in Wisconsin.

Palmyra in Jefferson Co. Wisconsin was first settled 1846 by Cyrus Norton.  Three years later a saw mill was built by the David Johnson Powers and his brothers who named the new settlement “Palmyra” after a city in the Syrian desert, because of the abundance of sand in the area.  A general store and tavern were soon opened and the area was surveyed and family lots were platted.  In 1852 the railroad arrived in the new small town.

This ends the story of Edith Powers family tree, which is major branch of Winifred Eloise Coss’ Family tree.  The next section of this Winifred’s family story will cover the Mary Ann Goodwater’s family history.  After covering the history of Mary Ann Goodwater’s family history, this paper will continue on from the marriage of Edgar Putnam “Ed” Coss and Sarah Mary “Sadie” Pearson on down to Winifred Eloise Coss and her marriage to Morris Rankin Batie.

sections.title: Early History of Canada
sections.teaser: In 775, a Viking settlement was established at the top of Newfoundland called “L'Anse aux Meadows”.  It was a Viking boat repair settlement that had iron mak…

blocks.text: In 775, a Viking settlement was established at the top of Newfoundland called “L'Anse aux Meadows”.  It was a Viking boat repair settlement that had iron making capability.  The iron came from the peat bogs in the area.  The repair facility was in operation for over 300 years.   Interestingly the 755 date is 38 years before the first Viking raid on England when the church on Lindisfarne Island off the northeast coast of England was attacked starting the Viking invasion of Saxon England.  755 is also 245 years before when Viking Leif Eriksson first explored North America in about 1000.

Note:  The date of 775 for the “L'Anse aux Meadows” settlement has been determined by using a new science.  In the year 775, a supermassive solar flare erupted on the Sun that was recorded in scientific papers of-the-day that were left by European observers who were following Sun’s sunspot activates.  The supermassive solar flare that occurred created 1.2% more carbon-14 than usual leaving the Sun that reached the Earth and was absorbed into living trees.  The increase in carbon-14 was stored in the tree rings of trees growing at the time.  Recently wood that has been found in the wood of trees that were cut down and used in the building of the Vikings “L'Anse aux Meadows” settlement have been studied and the tree rings of the year 775 have been found to have higher carbon-14 indicating the impacted tree ring was grown in 775 when the supermassive solar flare occurred.  See a photo below of a reconstructed structure at the National Historic Site in Newfoundland, Canada.

blocks.caption: Note: The date of 775 for the “L'Anse aux Meadows” settlement has been determined by using a new science. In the year…

blocks.text: In 1386, upon the death of England’s King Henry IV his son became King Henry V of England.  King Henry V was knighted at age 12 and had honed his soldiering skills putting down rebellions launched against his father.  He pleased his nobles later by renewing the war with France in 1415.  In the face of tremendous odds, he beat the French at the “Battle of Agincourt”, losing just 400 of his own soldiers while killing more than 6,000 French soldiers.  On a second expedition in France, King Henry V of England captured the city of Rouen and was recognized as the next king of France.  King Henry V was married Catherine of Valois, the daughter of French King Charles VI.  In their short two years of marriage Henry V and Catherine had a son Henry.  In 1422, King Henry V died of dysentery whilst campaigning in France before he could receive the French throne, leaving his 9-month-old son Henry as his only child.

In 1422, as the only child of King Henry V, the nine-month-old son Henry succeeded to the English throne becoming King Henry VI of England and then two-months later succeeded to the throne of France.  As a young baby, others ruled England and France on the infant king’s behalf.  The “Hundred Year’s War” which had already been going on for 85 years was currently underway.  The war was between the House of Plantagenet, the family of the Kings of England and the House of Valois, the family of the Kings of France over the control of France.  Tensions between the crowns of France and England traced back to William the Conqueror, who was a French Norman, who ruled England.  For this reason, English monarchs had historically held not only the English crown, but also titles and lands within France.  At this time a civil war was being fought in France between forces wanting to restore a French King and forces aligned with the English.  The civil war dramatically improved towards the side wanting to restore a French King when Joan of Arc led French troops and lifted the siege of Orleans.  The French victory was soon followed by the “Battle of Castillon”, in 1453, where the French expelled the English from all of France, except for the area around the coastal city of Calais.

In 1453, England’s King Henry VI, had a series of mental breakdowns, and tensions mounted between his Queen Margaret and his uncle, Richard the Duke of York, over control of the English government, and who would be the next king.  Civil war broke out in 1455, called the “Wars of the Roses”.  King Henry VI was deposed on 29 Mar 1461 after a crushing defeat at the “Battle of Towton” by the Duke of York’s son Edward, who took the throne as Edward IV.  Two ineffective Kings then followed: Edward V and Richard III.
In 1469, Queen Isabella of Castile and King Fernando of Aragon married uniting their two Christian kingdoms creating the new country of Spain.

In 1483, French King Louis XI died and his thirteen-year-old son became King Charles VIII of France.  France was rules by King Charles VIII’s older sister Anne and her husband Peter of Bourdon until 1491.  Seven years later King Charles VIII died after hitting his head on the heading of a doorway.
In 1485, England’s King Richard III was killed in the “Battle of Bosworth” and his crown was picked up and placed on the head of Henry Tudor, who became King Henry VII of England.

In 1492, King Fernando and Queen Isabella of Spain led their Spanish Christian Armies in the defeat of the last Moorish Islamic forces holding the city of Granada, thus ending the 781-year Moorish occupation and control over most of the Iberian Peninsula (modern Portugal and Spain).  With the defeat of the Islamic forces, King Fernando and Queen Isabella ordered all Spanish Jews to convert to Christianity or face expulsion from Spain.  Four years later in 1492, the Spanish Muslims were given the same order.  That same year Christopher Columbus, an Italian explorer sponsored by Queen Isabella and King Ferdinando, successfully crossed the Atlantic Ocean between the Canary Islands off Africa and the Bahamas Island off North America in 34 days.  In addition to proving that it was possible to sail from Europe to the New World, Columbus discovered the “Atlantic Ocean Trade Wind system” that made it possible to sail ships back and forth across the Atlantic Ocean.  The unification of Christian Catholics in Spain, the discovery of New World and the way the Atlantic Ocean could be crossed gave Spain a major head start in the New World over the later rival European country’s France, England and the Dutch Netherlands.  See a map below showing the important “Atlantic Ocean Trade Wind system”:

blocks.caption: In 1492, King Fernando and Queen Isabella of Spain led their Spanish Christian Armies in the defeat of the last Moori…

blocks.text: Note:  The word “Indian”, given to the people living in the New World, originated when Christopher Columbus returned to Spain after his first trip and described the indigenous people he found to King Ferdinand and Queen Isabella on the first island he visited.  Unfortunately, there is no clear record of what island in the Bahamas was the island Columbus first landed on he called San Salvador.  The island that has that name today was called Watlings Island prior to 1920.  The description given to the King Ferdinand and Queen Isabella of the indigenous people living on the island was “Los Ninos de’la’endeo” or “children of god” a term later corrupted to los nineos de la Indio” that, later became “Indian”.  The term “children of god” was used to describe the well-mannered, praiseworthiness, straight-forwardness and honesty of the indigenous people that Columbus found in the New World.  The word “Indian” was meant as a complementary term for describing the native people of the New World.

In 1494, the Pope Alexander VI divided the newly discovered New World lands between the Catholic Portuguese and Spanish Empires along the 370-meridian line.  Spain got all of the New World except Brazil and Portugal got Africa and Asia.  See the map below of the world with Spain’s lands shaded.

blocks.caption: In 1494, the Pope Alexander VI divided the newly discovered New World lands between the Catholic Portuguese and Spani…

blocks.text: In 1497, a Genoese (a maritime republic on the northwest coast of Italy) navigator John Cabot sailed to the new world and claimed Newfoundland for England.

In 1508, only 16 years after the first voyage of Christopher Columbus, Thomas Auber a French explorer from Dieppe France, followed fishermen from Breton France on their annual fishing trip off Newfoundland and brought back the first American Native people, with their weapons and canoes to France.  The Natives people in their native dress caused a great stir in Rouen, France.
In 1509, eighteen-year-old King Henry VIII (8th) was crown king of England and would rule England as a powerful king for the next 38 years.
In 1515, twenty-one-year-old King Francis was crowned king of France and would rule also as a powerful king for 32 years creating a balance rivalry between the English and French.

Starting in 1530, Spain was sending 15 ships a summer to Red Bay (Labrador) to hunt for whales.  They melted the whale blubber into whale oil that was used in indoor oil lamps for indoor lighting and for making soap.  Each ship that returned to Spain with the whale oil was worth $8 million (in today’s dollars).

In 1534, France King Francis, authorized navigator Jacques Cartier, a French-Breton maritime explorer, to led a voyage to the New World in order to seek gold and other riches, as well as look for a new sea route to Asia.  In 1535, Jacques Cartier and his crew sailed up the St Lawrence River and visited an Iroquois Native settlement of about 500 persons called “Stadacona” that was located on the bluff where Québec City is located today.  Jacques Cartier named the large river the St. Lawrence River.  While in the New World Jacques claimed the large St. Lawrence River valley for France, calling the area “Canada”, after the Huron-Iroquois word “kanata”.

In 1536, Jacques Cartier on his second trip to the New World named an island in the St. Lawrence River where wild grapes were growing the Île d’Orléans in honor of the Duke of Orleans, son of French King Francis.

In 1541, Jacques Carter returned to the New World a third time with 400 people, and established Fort Charlesbourg-Royal on the then abandon Iroquois Native settlement site of “Stadacona” on the north shore of the St. Lawrence River where the Cap-Rouge River flows in from the north.  An upper fort located about 40 yards above the St. Lawrence River was built in a good strategic defensive position and a less protected lower fort was constructed down on the river shore to provide an anchorage for arriving ships.  The forts were failures and were abandoned after two years due to hostilities with the local Native Iroquoian people and a large number of deaths that occurred over the long harsh winters caused by scurvy a disease caused due to the lack of eating fresh fruits and vegetables.

Jacques Cartier’s three other expeditions along the St. Lawrence River and the building of Fort Charlesbourg-Royal would later enable France to lay claim to the lands that would become the French colony of Québec.  French King Francis, however, lost interest and refused to fund further explorations of the New World.  See a drawing of Fort Charlesbourg-Royal below:

blocks.caption: Jacques Cartier’s three other expeditions along the St. Lawrence River and the building of Fort Charlesbourg-Royal wo…

blocks.text: In 1562, France wanted to capture Spanish treasure ships that were carrying looted gold and silver taken from the Aztec and Inca Empires, which Spain had conquered and was now bringing back home to Spain.  To catch the eastward trade winds needed to sail back across the Atlantic Ocean to Spain the Spanish treasure ships had to sail through the Straits of Florida, a narrow passage between the Florida peninsula and the Bahamas Islands, where the ships caught the ocean gulf-steam current and the easterly blowing trade winds.  France established a small coastal settlement on what is today, Parris Island, South Carolina and built a fort they called “Charlesfort”.  Spain quickly responded in 1565 with fleet of ships with solders from Cuba that destroyed the France fort and settlement.  Spain next built their own outpost and fort at St. Augustine on the Florida peninsula, the first permanent European settlement in what is today the United States, to protect their shipping.

Towards the end of the 1500s, the great Spanish Empire in the New World began to decline as the gold and silver plunder began to run out.  The Spanish’s economy had become dependent upon to steady flow of new wealth coming back from their New World colonies .  The decline of Spain allowed other rival European powers begin to snatch up Caribbean islands and establish new colonies themselves in the North American.

Between1562 and 1598, France’s efforts in colonization of North America were disrupted by religious wars back in France between the established French Roman Catholics and the new rising French Protestants Huguenots.  The “Huguenots” were French people who followed the teachings of Protestant leader John Calvin, who back in 1530 began to challenge the Roman Catholic Church doctrines.  The French government led by Catherine de Medici, the mother of French King Charles IX apposed the “Huguenots” and their Protestant beliefs.  On 18 Aug 1572, the Margaret of Valois, the daughter of Catherine de Medici and sister of French King Charles IX, married Protestant King Henry III of Navarre.  Many of the wealthiest and most prominent Huguenots gathered in Paris France for the Protestant wedding.  Five days later, French King Charles IX ordered the killing of all the Huguenots leaders that had gathered for the wedding of his sister.  A slaughter called the “St. Bartholomew’s Day Massacre” followed that lasting several weeks and spread throughout France.  An estimated 5,000 to 30,000 Protestant Huguenots in France were hunted down and killed.  The Huguenots were not only murder; some were gruesomely torture and mutilation.  The violence and murders occurred in 12 cities over a two-month period after the “St. Bartholomew’s Day Massacre”, leading to the first wave of French Protestant Huguenots departing France to England, Germany and the Netherlands.

Note:  The locations of places where individuals were born and married are identified in terms of modern French Cities and Regions to make the places easy to find on a map or Web app.  Locations identified as being in Perche are the exception.  Perche was a region that no longer exists and is mostly now in the Region of Normandy.

Note:  French Canadians had an unusual naming process call “dit name”.  The dit names were commonly used by early French Canadians until the 1800s.  The dit names pose challenges to genealogists and anyone searching the French-Canadian family trees.  A dit name can or cannot be used for the same individual.  Dit name translate as "called".  A French name such as Adolphe Guillet dit Tourangeau can translate as "Adolphe Guillet, called Tourangeau", where both "Guillet" and "Tourangeau" are used as surnames, sometimes together and sometimes individually in different situations.  The dit name carried the same legal weight as the original family name with regard to land transfers and the naming of children.  Dit names developed for a variety of reasons, such as distinguishing one family from another nearby family with the same surname, or allowing an adopted child to retain both their birth and adopted family names.  In some cases, both the original name and the dit name are retained.  A family's dit name often derived from a personal attribute, such as: Leblond, Leblanc or Leroux, a place of origin, or as a profession such as: Chartier, Meunier or Vanier.  Children often adopted a dit name, sometimes dropping their original family name.  As is obvious dit names make it more challenging to identify different individual using the same dit name.  In this paper, when possible, “dit names” are not used.

sections.title: Mary Ann Goodwater’s branch members start
sections.teaser: On 9 Jul 1575, Louis Hébert was born in Paris, Île-de-France, France at the start of the Mary Ann Goodwater branch of Winifred Eloise Coss’s family tree.

blocks.text: On 9 Jul 1575, Louis Hébert was born in Paris, Île-de-France, France at the start of the Mary Ann Goodwater branch of Winifred Eloise Coss’s family tree.
On 15 Feb 1576, Mathieu Abraham Côsté was born to Jean Baptiste Francois Côsté in Mortagne, Perche, France.

Note:  The French language adds accent marks to some letters of the alphabet to help explain how to pronounce words in a system called International Phonetic Alphabet.  Louis Hébert’s and Mathieu Abraham Côsté last names contains two of the five possible pronunciations of the letter e.  The others are è é ê ë.  Where possible the accent letters are used in this paper.

Note:  In 1578, eighty-six years after Columbus discovered North America, the English finally started their own efforts in exploring and establishing settlements in the New World.  The monarchy and the early parliament government of England had been distracted by wars in Scotland, Ireland and France.  Additionally in England, the Protestant vs Catholic fighting followed by the Church of England vs Puritan fighting, left little time and energy to pursue efforts in the New World.

In 1578, England’s Queen Elizabeth I granted a charter to Sir Humphrey Gilbert to explore the territories in the New World.  The terms of the charter were vague, though Sir Humphrey understood it to give him rights to all territory in the New World north of Spanish Florida.
In 1580, Marie Rollet was born to Jean-Claude Roulette and Anne (Cogu) in Paris, Île-de-France, France.
About 1580, Pierre Desportes was born to Louis Desportes and Anne (Du Poteau) in Lisieux, Normandy, France.
On 15 Apr 1581, Francoise Genevieve Loisel was born to Louis Loisel and Judith (Charlot) in Perche, France,

In 1584, England sent ships to the New World and started their first colony in the New World the “Roanoke Colony”.  See the Story of Carolee Afton McCance Family and the Long Road to the Constitution, for more details on the “Roanoke Colony” failure.
On 11 Oct 1588, Guillaume Couillard was born to Andre Couillard and Jehanne (Basset) in Saint-Malo, Brittney, France.
Note:  France is divided into thirteen Administrative Regions, much like States.  See the map below showing the French Administrative Regions:

blocks.caption: Note: France is divided into thirteen Administrative Regions, much like States. See the map below showing the French…

blocks.text: In 1588, King Philip of Spain attacked England with an Armada of 130 large galleons warships carrying 19,000 soldiers.  Queen Elizabeth I of England responded with 60 warships.  Many of the English warships were a new design with new armament (cannons & guns) that had recently been developed during England’s sponsorship of pirating of Spanish ships returning with treasures from the New World.  The new English ships were designed for firing cannons.  They were smaller, faster and able to quickly reload their cannons.  England has recently developed a process of making cannons out of wrought iron, which cost only 10% as much to build as the older brass cannons used by the Spanish.  The Spanish warships were large, slow and designed as floating castles to come up and grapple an opposing ship and then attached using bows and arrows and small handguns by soldiers perched on the tall castle like structures on the front and back of the ship.  The Spanish ships did have cannons, but they were large, heavy and slow to reload.  The Spanish also did not have standardized cannon ball sizes, which resulted in confusion and slowing in reloading.  In the two sea battles off the south coast of England, the English fired over 2,000 cannon shots to the Spanish only 750 shots.

The Spanish ships had to first go to Calais France to pick up 27,000 soldiers they were taking for the invasion of England.  When the ships arrived, the soldiers were not ready to board so the Spanish fleet had to anchor and wait  The English warships followed the Spanish ships and at night sent 7 of their ships loaded with explosives and set ablaze into the anchored Spanish fleet.  A number of the Spanish ships were set afire and the Spanish ships pulled their anchors and headed north to escape the English.  The English attached the scattered Spanish ships the next morning and destroy and damaged many forcing the Spanish fleet to sail back to Spain by circling up and out around England, Scotland and Ireland.  Only 60 of the 130 Spanish ships made it back to Spain.  70 Spanish ships and over 6,000 Spanish soldiers were lost.  Spain never again challenged the English at sea again and within 10 years Spain was a bankrupt country and England and France were free to explore and colonize the New World.
On 27 Nov 1589, Abraham Martin was born to Jean Galleran Martin, known as “The Merchant of Metz” and Isabelle (Côté) in La Rochelle, Nouvelle-Aquitaine, France.
In 1590, Zacharie Cloutier was born in Mortagne, Perche, Normandy, France.
On 18 Sep 1592, Jean Guyon was born to Denis Cloutier and Renee (Brière) in St-Aubin, Mortagne, Perche, France.
In 1592, Mathurine Robin was born to Eustache Robin and Madeleine (Avrard) in St. Jean, Mortagne, Perche, France.
Note:  As has already been mentioned Perche was a former province in France that now is in the southeast corner of the Administrative Region of Normandy.
In 1592, Marguerite Langlois was born to Guillaume Langlois and Jeanne (Millet) in Montpellier, Occitania, France.
On 25 Jan 1594, Jacques Gaudry was born to Rene Gaudry and Leonarde (Blondeau) in Feings, Normandy, France.

In 1595, Jean Bonneau was born to Jean Bonneau Sr. and Catherine (Roi) in Mesnil-Saint-Laurent, Hauts-de-France, France.  Jean Bonneau was the 9th great grandfather of Winifred Elois Coss and the beginning of the Goodwater family branch of Winifred’s family tree.
In 1596, Saintes Dupont was born to Paul Michel Dupont and Perrine (Rousseau) in Perche, France.
On 15 May 1597, Gervais le Normand was born to François Normand and Radegonde (Gadois) in Mortagne, Perche, France.
In 1598, Jean Nicolet was born to Thomas Nicolet and Marguerite (De La Mar) in Cherbourg, Normandy, France.

During the 1590’s, shortages of food and much suffering occurred in many parts of France.  Armies on both sides of the conflict between the French Catholics vs French Protestants Huguenots set upon cities and villages, taking farmers’ crops and livestock and forcing residents from their homes.  French life was in complete disarray, destruction was everywhere.  In 1598, at the end of the religious wars, French King Henry IV was crowned king and began focusing on rebuilding France by profiting from the valuable resources in North America.  French commercial trade monopolies were set up to encourage business in the New World.

In 1599, Samuel de Champlain sailed with his uncle on a large French ship chartered by the Spanish government.  They travel extensively in the Caribbean and visited several Spanish ports in the New World.  Samuel Champlain gained a reputation as a strong navigator.  In 1601, Samuel de Champlain was named royal geographer in the court of French King Henry IV.  Samuel visited several French seaports, where he learned about northern coast of North America from fishermen who had gone there to fish.
In 1599, Francoise Langlois was born to Guillaume Langlois and Jeanne (Millet) in Lisieux, Normandy, France.  Francoise was the younger sister of Marguerite Langlois.
In 1600, Thomine Chastel was born to Benoit Chastel and Perrine (Prodhomme) in Tourouvre, Perche, France.
In 1600, Miotte Lecombe was born to Mahiel Lecombe and Collineau (de Montaguerre) in Dieppe, Normandy, France.
On 15 Jan 1601, Mathieu Abraham Côté and Francoise Genevieve Loisel were married in Mortagne, Perche, France.

On 19 Feb 1601, Louis Hébert and Marie Rollet were married in the Saint-Sulpice church in Paris, Île-de-France, France.  Louis ran a Paris apothecary shop selling drugs, spices and herbs he grew.  During the early years of their marriage, Louis associated with men who were interested in starting a French colony in North America.  Marie had to manage their household in Paris on her own while Louis was off exploring in America.  Before he left in 1606, Louis signed power-of-attorney over to Marie so she could sign documents involving their finances during his absence.  There were only three known children born to Louis and Marie.  The two oldest children, son Guillaume and daughter Anne, were born before Louis’ first trip to America, and their third child, daughter Guilleumette Hébert was born later in 1608.
On 9 Mar 1601, Jean Bigot was born to Jacques Bigot and Anne Pestel (Bataille) in Rennes, Brittany, France.

Note:  Brittany is a unique area with its own and culture and language.  The people of Brittany left from Cornwall, England in 600 AD and migrated to the peninsula in the Frankish kingdom to escape the invading Anglo-Saxons.  See the map below showing the Brittany peninsula:

blocks.caption: Note: Brittany is a unique area with its own and culture and language. The people of Brittany left from Cornwall, Eng…

blocks.text: In 1601, Charlotte Chevalier was born to Gilles Chevalier and Nicole (Pineau) in Feings, Normandy, France.

In 1603, Samuel de Champlain joins Captain François Gravé du Pont on a fur-trading expedition to North America.  Samuel serves as unofficial geographer and cartographer (map maker) on the journey.  Their ship arrived in the summer at a Native American trading post called “Tadoussac” located where the Saguenay River joined the large St. Lawrence River (140 miles east of what will become Québec City.)

In 1603, Jeanne Bahmahmaadjimiwin Giisis was born in a Nippissing Native settlement at Lake Nippissing.  Lake Nippissing is located northeast from Lake Huron.  More will be discussed about the Nippissing Native people later when Jean Nicolet is sent to live with them.

On 4 Jun 1603, Noël Langlois Sr. was born to Guillaume Langlois and Jeanne (Millet) in Alençon, Normandy, France.  Noël was the younger brother of sisters Marguerite Langlois and Francoise Langlois.  Noël, Marguerite and Francoise and their spouses will all be directly involved in the early establishment of the Québec settlement.
On 1 Jan 1604, Raymond Pagé was born to Guillaume Pagé and Marie Claud (Auge) in Quiercy, Hauts-de-France, France.
In 16 Mar 1604, Françoise Garnier was born to Guillaume Garnier and Michelle (Mabille) in Alençon, Normandy, France.
In 1604, Guillaume Hébert was born to Louis Hébert and Marie (Rollet) in Paris, Île-de-France, France.
In 1604, Jean Dumay was born to Adrien Dumay and Cathérine (Pougnye) in Dieppe, Normandy, France

In 1605, the France establish its first permanent settlement in North America at Port-Royal on what will later be called the island of Nova Scotia (Latin for “New Scotland”).  The French called the area “Acadia”.  The French and English would fight over control of the area for the next 153 years.  See a map below of the island of Nova Scotia and a photo of the replica of the fort of Port-Royal National Historic Site:

images.caption: In 1605, the France establish its first permanent settlement in North America at Port-Royal on what will later be cal…
In 1605, the France establish its first permanent settlement in North America at Port-Royal on what will later be cal…

blocks.text: On 10 Apr 1606, Great Britain’s King James I, after sixteen years had passed since the failure of the Roanoke Colony in Virginia, became interested again in exploring and settling in the New World and granted competing royal charters to the “Virginia Company of London” and the “Plymouth Company”.  At the time the entire east coast of North American north of Florida was called Virginia.  See the “Carolee Afton McCance Family Story” and the “Frederick Garnet Popham Family Story” for information of the English Popham Colony and the English Jamestown Colony.

In 1606, Louis Hébert sailed with his mother’s nephew, Jean de Biencourt to the French community of Port-Royal in Acadia.  During the summer of 1608 Louis Hébert explored with Samuel de Champlain along southeast coast of North America.
On 4 Aug 1607, Robert Drouin was born to Robert Drouin and Marie (Dubois) in Mortagne, Perche, France.
In 1607, Jean Baptiste Halay was born to unknown parents in Chartres-de-Bretagne, Brittany, France.
In 1607, Mathurine Valet was born to Paschale Valet and Julienne (Royer) in Chartres-de-Bretagne, Brittany, France.
In 1607, Nicolas Bonhomme was born to Nicolas Bonhomme Sr. and Marie (Gayon) in Rouen, Normandy, France.

sections.title: Québec Settlement Established
sections.teaser: On 3 Jul 1608, Samuel de Champlain, who was exploring the upper St. Lawrence River, was force to stop at the large rapids that blocked his ship from travelin…

blocks.text: On 3 Jul 1608, Samuel de Champlain, who was exploring the upper St. Lawrence River, was force to stop at the large rapids that blocked his ship from traveling further up the St. Lawrence River, about 500 miles upstream from the rivers’ ocean mouth.  The area of the large rapids was called “Kebec” by the Native Algonquin people, which became “Québec”.  Back in 1534, French explorer Jacques Cartier, who was the first explorer in the St. Lawrence River valley had named area Canada, from the Native Iroquoian word “Kanata” meaning village or settlement.  The site Samuel Champlain now selected seventy-four years later was the same site Jacques Cartier had selected to build his failed Fort Charlesbourg-Royal.  The Iroquois Native tribe that had been in the area when Jacques Cartier built his fort had left the area and were replaced by the Innu and Algonquin Native tribes.  Samuel Champlain was able to build a wooded fort, which he called the “habitation”, within a few days of their arrival.  The new Québec settlement became an important French trading post with the Native people in the area.  Samuel and twenty-seven other men spent the first winter in the new Québec settlement, but only eight survived until the spring.  See a photo on the next page of a model of the “habitation fort”:

blocks.caption: On 3 Jul 1608, Samuel de Champlain, who was exploring the upper St. Lawrence River, was force to stop at the large ra…

blocks.text: During the next summer of 1609, Samuel de Champlain attempted to establish better relations with the local native tribes.  He made alliances with the Wendat (called Hurons by the French) and with the Algonquins, the Montagnais and the Etchemins, who lived in the large St. Lawrence River Valley.  These tribes demanded that Samuel de Champlain help them in their ongoing war against the aggressive Iroquois native tribe, who lived farther south and had a history of raiding the native tribes living in the St. Lawrence River valley area.  Samuel set off with nine French soldiers and 300 local native warriors to explore the “Rivière des Iroquois” (now known as the Richelieu River in New York), and became the first European to map Lake Champlain.  Having had no encounters with the Iroquois, Samuel Champlain sent many of his men back, leaving only 2 Frenchmen and 60 natives with him.  On 29 Jul 1609, somewhere in the area near today’s city of Ticonderoga on the southern end of Lake Champlain New York, Samuel’s remaining party encountered a large group of Iroquois warriors.  A battle occurred the next day, two hundred Iroquois warriors advanced on Samuel's position.  One of the native guides with Samuel Champlain pointed out the three chiefs leading the Iroquois war party.  In his account of the battle, Samuel talked about firing his arquebus long gun, which he had loaded with 4 musket balls, which killed two of the Iroquois chiefs in a single shot, after which one of Algonquins men killed the third Iroquois chief.  The Iroquois warriors turned and fled the area.  This action set the tone for poor “French-Iroquois” relations for the rest of the century.  See a drawing below of a soldier firing a large heavy arquebus long gun.

blocks.caption: During the next summer of 1609, Samuel de Champlain attempted to establish better relations with the local native tri…

sections.title: Cyprien Tanguay’s Genealogical Dictionary
sections.teaser: The history of the French people who came and settled French Canada was fortunately captured by Cyprien Tanguay, a French-Canadian Catholic priest and histor…

blocks.text: The history of the French people who came and settled French Canada was fortunately captured by Cyprien Tanguay, a French-Canadian Catholic priest and historian.  Cyprien’s

early taste for genealogical studies fully manifested itself after his official appointment in 1865 to the Agriculture, Immigration, and Statistics Ministry for Québec, Canada.  After his appointment, Cyprien spent rest of his life doing genealogical research on the people of Canada by gathering data from the Catholic parish registers (births, baptisms, marriages and deaths) throughout Canada.  He also twice visited Europe for the same purpose to gather more information on people who were born in France and immigrated to Canada.  As the result of his untiring labors, he published (1871–90) his “Dictionnaire généalogique des familles canadiennes françaises depuis les origines de la colonie jusqu'à nos jours,”, which comprised seven large double column volumes of over six hundred pages: a colossal undertaking that documented the people who lived from 1608 to 1760 in Canada.  See below a sample page from Cyprien’s work for Louis Hébert and Marie Rollet:”

images.caption: early taste for genealogical studies fully manifested itself after his official appointment in 1865 to the Agricultur…
early taste for genealogical studies fully manifested itself after his official appointment in 1865 to the Agricultur…

blocks.text: In 1608, Guilleumette Hébert was born to Louis Hébert and Marie (Rollet) in Paris, Île-de-France, France.

In 1608, Louis Hébert, with his family and a group Recollects (French Order of Catholic Franciscans Friars) and a few Catholic Jesuits Priests started to farm the land up behind the “Habitation” fort in the new Québec settlement.  Both wheat and rye grains were planted.  The area had a good natural spring for water and rich fertile soil.  Only Louis Hébert and Samuel Champlain had any interest in cultivating the land in the Canada colony.
In 1608, Lêonarde Élêonore Joinault was born to Guillaume Joinault and Gabrielle (Coiste) in Bellême, Perche, France.

Note:  The climate of the St. Lawrence River valley involved a long harsh winter and a warm short summer.  Cold winds, came with the first snow fall usually about the first week of November that lasted through May the next spring.  The rivers in the great valley, including the large St. Lawrence River itself, iced over in November and stayed frozen until warming weather arrives in late spring.  The five warm months of summer and early fall had to be used to grow the food the settlers needed for the entire year.  The northern latitude of the area resulted in long summer days when everyone needed to work from first light until dusk to completed as much work as possible.  There was plenty of time in the long dark cold winter to rest.  The warm summers, however, brought out hordes’ mosquitoes, biting black flies and stinging insects which greatly tormented the colony’s settlers.  See a photo below of black flies sundering someone in a hooded jacket that occurred in a current Canadian canoe expedition:

blocks.caption: Note: The climate of the St. Lawrence River valley involved a long harsh winter and a warm short summer. Cold winds,…

blocks.text: In the warm summer days, the Native Americans went around all but naked, wearing only a strip of animal skin around their waist and smeared their bodies with animal grease, mainly bear fat, in order to protect themselves and their children from sun, wind and the insects.

Note:  Bread was the staple of the French-Canadian diet.  The grains used to make bread were barley, oats, millet, rye and wheat.  A French peasant family could spend half their income buying flour and bread.  Soups and porridges were the staple food items eaten in the morning, noon and evening.  Vegetables were added to the soups and porridge from the family gardens that included peas, cabbage, carrots, turnips, leeks and radishes.  Mushrooms from the forests, and a bit of fish or meat made up the rest of the diet of the early French people living in Canada.
On 21 Mar 1609, Charles Guillebourg was born to Charles Guillebourg Sr., and Jeanne (LeMelse) in Tourouvre, Perche, France.
In 1609, Noël Morin was born to Clade Morin and Jeanne (Moreau) in Brie-Comte-Robert, Île-de-France, France.
In 1610, Catherine Poulet was born to Pierre Poulet and Marie (Sorgeault)in Mesnil-Saint-Georges, Hauts-de-France, France.
On 14 May 1610, a Catholic fanatic (extremist) stabbed to death French King Henry IV.  King Henry IV had been baptized a Catholic but raised by his mother in the Protestant faith.

On 19 Jun 1610, Samuel de Champlain supported, by the new young French King Louis XIII and Samuel’s native allies the Wendat, Algonquin and Innu tribes fought against the Mohawk-Iroquois tribe in an area of present-day Sorel-Tracy, Québec, 50 miles upstream from Trois-Rivières.  Champlain's forces armed with the arquebus long guns engaged and slaughtered or captured nearly all of the Mohawk-Iroquois warriors, the most eastern tribe of the Iroquois Confederacy, living in the Hudson and Mohawk River valleys of present-day upper New York State.  The battle ended major hostilities with the Mohawks-Iroquois for the next twenty years.

Note: The French who arrived in North America discovered the American Native people had dogs in their camps that were descended from wild wolves; who were the only animal domesticated in North American before the arrival of the Europeans.

In 1610, French Catholic Jesuits assumed responsibility for converting the Hurons natives living in Canada to Christian Catholicism.  The French Jesuits priests were unprepared for the hardships of the frontier life and the lack of interest by the native people in European Christian salvation beliefs.  Over the next 30 years, half of the Hurons who were contacted, died from diseases acquired from Europeans they came in contact with.  The Huron people became convinced the Jesuits priests had cast sells over them making them get sick, which was half true.  In retaliation the Hurons killed and mutilated (scalped) seven Jesuit priests.

On 30 Dec 1610, forty-three-year-old Samuel de Champlain married twelve-year-old Hélène Boullé, the daughter of French King Louis XIII’s Lord Chamberlain (the person who runs the king’s living quarters).  Hélène brought an enormous dowry of 6,000 livres (French money) to the marriage, which Samuel used to finance his explorations and business dealings.  Samuel is not a member of the Mary Ann Goodwater’s Branch of the Winifred Elois Coss family tree.

Note:  The livre (French for "pound") was the currency of Kingdom of France.  The livre was established by King Charlemagne as a unit equal to one pound of silver.  It was subdivided into 20 sous per livre and 12 deniers per sous.

Back in 1608, John Guy and other members of the city of Bristol in England’s “Society of Merchant Ventures”, decided to act upon a letter they had received from England’s Chief Justice Sir John Popham concerning the colonization of the large island of Newfoundland off the east coast of North America.  Since its discovery by the Italian explorer John Cabot, who was working for England’s King Henry VII at the time, Newfoundland had been formally taken into possession in 1583, by Sir Humphrey Gilbert for England’s Queen Elizabeth I.  The English merchants in the seaport of Bristol England became interested in exploiting and colonizing the island of Newfoundland in North America.  In 1608, John Guy visited the Newfoundland and selecting Cuper’s Cove as a site for a new colony.  The next year John Guy put forward a proposal to establish a colony at Cuper’s Cove, which the Bristol “Society of Merchant Ventures” embraced with great enthusiasm.  In 1610, John Guy was appointed governor of the new colony and sailed over in twenty-one shorts days to Cuper’s Cove arriving in August with colonists and livestock.  Thirty-nine colonists spent the winter of 1610–1611 in the colony.  During John Guy’s governorship the colonists built fortifications for the settlement, explored the area and planted crops.  In 1611, John Guy returned to England leaving William Colston, one of his brothers-in-law in charge of the colony.  The next year more settlers with more livestock and female settlers arrived in the colony.

The Newfoundland colonists built a boat called the “Endeavour”, which John Guy used to lead a voyage into Trinity Bay where they meet with members of the Beothuk Native tribe.  It is quite likely that the Beothuk Native people were the same people the Vikings encountered in Newfoundland around 600 years earlier.  There is no historical evidence that the Vikings encountered the Beothuk people, but there is evidence that the early Viking settlements in Newfoundland had been attacked by Native people and that the Vikings eventually abandoned the area leaving it in the hands of people they called “Skraelings”.  The Vikings used the term “Skraelings” to refer to all the native people they encountered in Greenland and the North American Arctic lands.  There is a good chance the Beothuks were native people that are known today as “Inuit’s”.

In 1612, John Guy sailed back to Trinity Bay where he spotted a campfire burning on shore.  John Guy sent one of his man ashore in a rowboat with a pocket full of trinkets and carrying a white flag.  As instructed, the man stood quietly near the campfire and waited.  Eventually, the man was approached by two Beothuk natives carrying a white wolf skin.  The Beothuk’s held out the wolf skin as a gift and the Englishman offered the trinkets in exchange.  John Guy next went ashore with a few more men and they were soon joined by eight more Beothuk natives.  Using hand signs, John Guy indicated that he wished to share the food he had brought ashore with the Beothuk people  They feasted on bread, butter, raisins, beer, and aqua vitae ('water of life', brandy) supplied by Guy and dried deer meat supplied by the Beothuk people.  Communications were surprisingly easy and the group of new friends made plans to meet again the following year.  The Englishmen went to sleep together on shore.  However, when they awoke the next morning, the Beothuk’s had disappeared.  This is the first and only recorded encounter with the Beothuk’s, unless they were in fact Inuit natives.  The winter of 1612 was very severe and eight more English colonist died in the small Cupid's Cove settlement.  John Guy resigned as Governor and returned to England.  Investors in England lost interest in the Newfoundland Colony and the Cuper’s Cove settlement was abandon.
In 1610, Catherine Poulet was born to Pierre Poulet and Marie ) Sorgeault in Mesnil-Saint-Georges, Hauts-de-France, France.

In 1610, twenty-one-year-old Abraham Martin, who was employed by the “Company of Rouen” as a sea pilot, travel to the New World and visited the Québec settlement that was struggling to establish itself.
In 1611, Olivier Charbonneau was born in Marans, Charente-Maritime, France.  The name “Charbonneau” means “coal merchant” in French.

The city of Marans in Charente-Maritime, France was located in the middle of the Poitevin marsh 12 miles from the port of La Rochelle on the Atlantic coast.  The English when they controlled the area built a chateau (Medieval fortress) at Marans.  French King Henri IV took possession of the area in 1588.  French King Louis X III later had the chateau razed (taken down) in 1638 during the time when Oliver Charbonneau was still a young man living in the area.  Next Cardinal Richelieu, the French Superintendent for Navigation and Commerce, began to fortify the Marans area.  In 1643, the citizens of Marans decided to drain their immense marshland that surrounded the city.  Olivier Charbonneau certainly ate the eggs laid by the local hens, a breed called “Marans”, famous for dark reddish-brown large eggs with thick shells.  See a photo of a Marans chicken below:

blocks.caption: The city of Marans in Charente-Maritime, France was located in the middle of the Poitevin marsh 12 miles from the por…

blocks.text: On 13 Sep 1612, Louis Gagne was born to Louis Gagne Sr. and Marie (Launay) in St. Martin, Ige, Perche, France.
On 7 Oct 1612, François Bélanger was born to François Bélanger Sr. and Francoise (Horlaysz) in Tougues, Normandy, France.
In 1612, Robert Caron was born to René-Edouard Caron and Catherine (Fortin) in La Rochelle, Normandy, France.
About 1612, Catherine Gouge was born to Leonard Gouge and Catherine (Dufrencoys) in Thury-Harcourt, Normandy, France.
See a map below made by Samuel de Champlain in 1612 of Canada:

blocks.caption: See a map below made by Samuel de Champlain in 1612 of Canada:

blocks.text: On 23 Jul 1613, Jean Côté was born to Mathieu Abraham Côté and Francoise Genevieve (Loisel) in Mortagne, Perche, France.

In 1613, Guillaume Couillard, a “carpenter”, “seaman”, “caulker” and later “farmer” from Saint-Malo, Brittany, France arrived as a member of the Samuel de Champlain’s first group of settlers in the Québec settlement in Canada.  Guillaume was one of the few new settlers to survive the harsh winters and improper diet as the new settlement struggled.

In 1613, the English capture and destroyed the tiny French settlement of Port-Royal.  Louis Hébert who was living in Port-Royal at the time returned back to Paris, France and reopened his apothecary (medicine and drug) shop in the city of Paris.

In 1613, Abraham Martin and Guillemette Couillard were married in Champagne, Nouvelle-Aquitaine, France.  Abraham Martin used the dit name “L’ecossais”, which meant Scot or Scottish, resulting in him being called “the Scot”.  Why Abraham selected his dit name is unclear.  It may have been the name of the street he lived on or associated with some military service he was involved in.  Another possible reason for the dit name may have been to hide his identity.  Regardless Abraham was French not Scottish.

Note:  From 1613 to 1620, the “Compagnie des Marchands“ was a monopoly company supported by the French government that operated in Canada but failed to fulfill their contractual obligations and therefore lost their monopoly rights in 1621 to the “ Compagnie de Montmorency”.  Six years later in 1627, French Cardinal Richelieu, the real leader of France withdrew the monopoly of the “Compagnie de Montmorency”, and established in its place the Compagnie de la Nouvelle France” as part of a plan to develop and expand trade in Canada.  Throughout all of this period the monopoly holders were frequently troubled with rogue traders (from France and other nations) in North America on one side, and politically connected opponents of their monopoly in France on the other side.  Many of the directors of these companies were more interested in trade than in colonization, which was usually a drain on the company's finances.  Samuel de Champlain, who championed the colonization efforts, worked tirelessly to make sure the French colonies survived amid political and corporate changes of power.
On 23 Mar 1614, Anne Martin was born to Abraham Martin and Guillemette (Couillard) in La Rochelle, Normandy, France.

On 2 Jun 1615, Jean Guyon and Mathurine Robin were married in Mortagne, Perche, France.  As a “master mason” Jean Guyon built a thirty-step stone staircase up to the bell tower for the new Saint-Aubin church in Mortagne.  In Jul 1625, he was given the job of working on the fortification of the town of Mortagne.  It was there that Jean’s skills as a “stone-cutter” brought him to the attention of Robert Giffard who was then in the process of establishing a seigneury (feudal lordship) in Beauport, Canada.
In 1616, Madeleine Bergeron was born to Jean-Baptiste Bergeron and Collineau (DeMontaguerre) in Quierzy, Hauts-de-France, France.
On 18 Jul 1616, Zacharie Cloutier and Saintes Dupont were married in Mortagne, Perche, France.

In 1616, Samuel de Champlain, who had been names Lieutenant General of Canada, engaged Louis Hébert as a resident physician and surgeon for the “Company of Québec”.  Louis was granted 4 hectares (10 acres) of land for his homestead and paid 200 crowns per year during a 3-year contract.  Louis Hébert later learned Samuel de Champlain had overstepped his authority and the “Company of Québec” reduced his annual salary to 100 crowns a year and forbid Louis Hébert from getting involved in the fur trade or farming for profit.  The “Company of Québec” tried to prevent all new settlers in Québec from farming to limit self-reliance and ensure the settlers bought everything they needed from the French company.

In 1617, Louse Hébert sailed from France to the Québec settlement with his thirty-seven-year-old wife Marie (Rollet) and their children, including thirteen-year-old son Guillaume Hébert, eleven-year-old daughter Anne Hébert and nine-year-old daughter Guilleumette Hébert.  The small Québec settlement was operating as a trading post and depended on supplies from France to sustain the small group of settlers living there.  Marie Hébert (Rollet) was the first European women to migrate to Canada, the first women to farm and was the Québec colony’s first teacher.  Marie took on the role of teaching the Native American girls to convert them to Catholicism.  She developed a warm relationship with Native people, and took girls into her home, adopting them while they learned the French language.  Marie continued to care for Native American children in her home into her later years.  She died in Québec in May of 1649.  See photo on the next page of monuments to Marie Hébert (Rollet) and her three children and Louis Hébert Monument in Québec City:

images.caption: In 1617, Louse Hébert sailed from France to the Québec settlement with his thirty-seven-year-old wife Marie (Rollet)…
In 1617, Louse Hébert sailed from France to the Québec settlement with his thirty-seven-year-old wife Marie (Rollet)…

blocks.text: Note:  In the 1600s and 1700s, the inhabitants in Canada in the Saint Lawrence River Valley were subject to the laws and customs of France.  Each person had a family name and a given name.  Children had the family name of their father.  Married women kept their family name from birth, on official documents, although they were commonly known by their husband’s family name.
In 1618, Jacques-Hugues Picard was born to Gabriel Picard and Michelle (Clavier) in Nantes, Pays de la Loire, France.
In 1618, Guillaume Fournier was born to Gilles Fournier and Noelle Gargon in the small town of Coulmer in Normandy, France.

Starting in 1618, the new “Québec Trading Company”, funded by investors from the cities Rouen and Saint Malo in Normandy France, began discouraging permanent settlement in Québec because the investors were primarily interested in making profits from fur trading, not in creating a new French settlement.  The company made no effort to clear land around the small Québec settlement and refused to give settlers land for farming.  They refused to give Louis Hébert a plough and forbade him from trading with the native people in the area.  Louis ignored the company rules and continued to clear land, without the benefit of a horse or plough, which were both available, and continued to raise more food for his family and the others in the settlement.  Over the next 4 years Louis created a 10-acre farming plot with only a spade (shovel).  Louis also built a 20 by 40-foot stone house on the top of the plateau above the river shore in the Québec settlement.  Louis planted an apple orchard, grape vines and vegetable garden on his cleared land.

Note:  Many of the early French fur trappers and traders took native women as wives/partners.  A number had two families, one in the French settlements where the union was blessed by the Catholic church and their children were duly baptized and another second native family living in and around the French trading posts and forts around of the Great Lakes.  The children of all mixed French/Native families were known as “Métis children”.

In 1618, Jean Nicolet left Cherbourg, Normandy, France and sailed to the Québec settlement in the service of the “Compagnie des Marchands” a trading monopoly owned by members of the French aristocracy.  Jean Nicolet was sent to Canada so he could live among the Native American people in order to learn their language and customs and explore the regions they inhabited (where they lived).  Samuel de Champlain instructed Jean Nicolet, when he arrived, to go and spend the winter on Allumette Island, a large island 375 miles up the Ottawa River.  The Allumette Island was a rallying point for the Algonquin Native people (Indigenous inhabitants of North America who spoke the Algonquin language.).  The Allumette Island was at a strategic spot on the Ottawa River, that was used as a main native fur-trading route.  It was important, for the sake of trade, that the tribes living on the shores of the Ottawa River should be friendly with the French.  Jean Nicolet stayed two years with the Algonquin Native people on Allumette Island and carried out his mission very well.  He learned the Algonquin and Huron languages, lived with the native people, learned their customs, and explored their native region.  The Native people soon accepted him as one of their own.  In fact, they made him a chief, allowed him to attend their tribe councils, and even took him along with them when they met with Iroquois tribe to negotiate a peace treaty.
On 10 Dec 1619, Paul de Rainville was born to Jean de Rainville and Jeanne (Burchet) n Touques, Normandy, France.
In 1619, Guillemette (Couillard) wife of Abraham Martin died in France, leaving Abraham Martin alone to raise their five-year-old daughter Anne Martin.
In 1619, widower Abraham Martin married Marguerite Langlois in France.
In 1619, Pierre Desportes and Francoise Langlois were married in Lisieux, Normandy, France.  Francoise Langlois was the younger sister of Marguerite Langlois who had recently married Abraham Martin.

In 1619, Pierre Desportes and his new twenty-year-old wife Francoise (Langlois) and Francoise’s sister Marguerite (Langlois) and her husband Abraham Martin and six other French settlers left Dieppe, France and sailed to Canada and Québec settlement.  Pierre Desportes was a French lawyer, an investor and an employee of the “Company of One-Hundred Associates”, which was funding the Samuel de Champlain’s Québec Colony.  After arriving in the Québec settlement Pierre Desportes, as an employee of the “Company of One-Hundred Associates”, managed the warehouse in the Québec settlement and worked as the settlement’s baker tending the care and operation of a public oven.  Abraham Martin had previously worked back in 1610 as a river pilot on the St. Lawrence River and made the first maps of the river although he could neither read or write.  He placed channel buoys to mark dangerous areas on the river and was known as the “king’s pilot”.
In 1619, Léonard Pilote was born to George Pilote and Philomene (Charest) in La Rochelle, Normandy, France.

In 1619, Samuel Champlain persuaded Sieur de Monts and his partners in the “French Fur Trading Company” to support the ongoing needs of the fledgling Québec Colony.  They drew up a statement whereby the fur company agreed to send sufficient provisions to sustain a total of eighty settlers in Québec Colony.  Supplies were to include one mattress, one straw bed, two blankets, three pairs of new sheets for each two people.  Each settler was also to receive two coats, six shirts, four pair of shoes and a cloak.  No pants were listed.  For defense of the new colony forty muskets, twenty-four pikes, one thousand pounds of fine gun powder, one thousand pounds of cannon powder, one thousand pounds of cannon balls, six thousand pounds of lead and a good supply of cannon-match (safe slow-burning twin fuse) were also provided.  To aid in the construction of buildings, four thousand pounds of iron, two barrels of steel, ten thousand roof tiles, and ten thousand pounds of bricks were to be sent to the new colony.

Samuel Champlain welcomed the supplies provided by the “French Fur Trading Company”, but felt the fur trading actives greatly hindered his efforts build a working successful colony.  During the first half of the 1600s a succession of commercial monopolies were started and then were replaced.  The French colony’s fur trading business competed and drew new recruiting settlers away from the less successfully agriculture actives needed to sustain and grow the new Canada Colony.

Note:  The restrictions that were put in place in the new Québec colony by the French government through monopolist French trading companies greatly hinder the Québec colony’s growth.  The French government desire to seek short term profits from trading with the Native American tribes to acquire valuable fur pelts to sell in Europe, while limiting the expenses of supporting a growing French colonists in New France, will later result in the loss of New France to England.  France was looking for quick wealth in the New World like Spain had recently achieved.
Note: In 1619, the Quebec settlement had a total of sixty individuals of which only six were women.

In Sep 1620, King James I of England granted all of what is now eastern Québec to William Alexander, a Scottish poet and tutor to the King’s oldest son, Prince Henry.  William Alexander created the “Knights Baronet of Nova Scotia” (Latin for New Scotland) and began to recruit investors.  Anyone who would put up money or would settle in Nova Scotia was given a title and granted a parcel of land 6 miles by 3 miles.  The settlement development began slowly at first, with only a few small settlements established along the Bay of Fundy, much to the chagrin of the French Acadians who already living there.  In 1625, England’s King James I died and another religious war started between England and France.

William Alexander surged ahead with his enterprise and recruited more aristocrats into the “Knights Baronet of Nova Scotia”.  Merchants and financiers from London joined the enterprise and gathered together £60,000, enough to purchase sailors and supply for 3 ships which would be used against the French.  The ships were captained by the three Kirke brothers; David, Louis and Thomas, sons of one of the enterprise’s investor Gervase Kirke.  More will be heard from the Kirke brothers later.

On 7 Jul 1620, Hélène Desportes was born to Pierre Desportes and Francoise (Langlois) as the first child born in the Québec Settlement.  At the time Champlain’s “Habitation” fort, Hélène’s first home, consisted of a few decrepit wooden structures surrounded by a wooden palisade.  Hélène’s godmother, who she was named after, was Madame Hélène’s Boullé, the young twenty-two-year-old wife of Samuel de Champlain.

In 1620, Samuel de Champlain returned to the Québec settlement with full authority over the Canada colony.  He found the Québec settlement in shambles and the settlers living in squalid conditions.  The few cottages within the “Habitation” were in great disrepair.  Roofs leaked and the flimsy walls did little to protect against the cold and wind of winter.  The settlement’s storehouse was on the verge of collapse.  The lower area down by the river shore had been abandoned and the area on top of the bluff only thriving around Louis Hébert’s home and his farming operation.  Samuel appointed Louis Hébert to oversee the administration of justice in the colony.  Louis was already providing medical services to the colony’s French settlers and the local Native Americans that were in contact with the settlement.  Samuel immediately set out to rehabilitate the family cottages and rebuilt storehouse this time with stones.  Samuel also started construction of a new Fort Saint-Louis on the cliff above river to better defend the new Québec settlement.

Note:  By 1620, the French, along with Basques, Spanish, English and Portuguese had all been making regular trips across the North Atlantic Ocean to the North American coast for more than one hundred years.  Beginning early in the 1500s, French fishermen sailed from the ports of Dieppe, Le Havre, La Rochelle and Saint-Malo, in the late spring of each year.  They fished for Atlantic cod, mainly on the Grand Banks of Newfoundland and also off the coast of North America.  By 1580, 10,000 European fishermen in 500 boats were making annual voyage across the Atlantic each summer.  In addition to the fish, the fishermen also brought valuable North American animal fur pelts back to Europe.

sections.title: Life in the Canada Colony
sections.teaser: In the new Canada Colony, there were no privileged orders, which was a marked contrast to the social organization structure of back in France.  There were so…

blocks.text: In the new Canada Colony, there were no privileged orders, which was a marked contrast to the social organization structure of back in France.  There were social distinctions in Canada, to be sure, but the boundaries between different classes of the population were not rigid; there were no privileges based upon the laws of the land, and no impenetrable barriers separating one class from another as was the case back in France.  Men could rise by their own hard work and efforts or come down through their own mistakes and lack of effort.  Everyone’s place in the colony was not determined for them by the accident of their birth parents.  Some of the most successful figures in the public and business affairs of Canada, came from relatively humble parentage.

In France in 1500s and 1600s, the chief French government officials, the seigneurs (the landlord) of the estates in France were held through a feudal tenure (swooned allegiance to the king).  The higher church officials and even the officers of the army and navy, were all drawn from the noble privileged classes of France.  In the Canada colony things were very different.  Canada colonists were able to rise up to higher ranks of responsibility through distinguished services in the colony.  The great majority of colonial leaders came from the ranks of the very day people.  This led to social camaraderie (friendship), a spirit of fraternity, and a feeling of universal companionship among the colonists, which was altogether lacking back in France proper.

The center of the social life in Canada was in the settlement of Québec.  It was the colonial capital and seat of the government and had all the trappings and glitter of a well-rounded social society.  Many of the French colony’s seigneurs who farmed out in cleared forest lands moved back into the Québec settlement to spend the long cold winter months.  Royal colonial officials, the officers of the military garrison, the colony’s leading merchants and a few other professional men and their families managed to echo, even if somewhat faintly, the pomp and glamor back in the French king’s court in Versailles.  Québec was a lively place in the long winter months.
