The residents of the colony were shut off from all contact with Europe for many long months at a time and they soon learned the art of providing for their own recreation and amusement.  Life in Québec in the winter included a society involved in dinners, dances and receptions.  But the Catholic clergy, however, frowned upon all this cheerfulness, of which they believed there was far too much.  In the smaller towns of Ville Marie and Three Rivers, the social circles were smaller and less brilliant.  Québec City indeed had no rival in New France.

A later census showed less the 2% of the France colonists lived in the colony’s three main towns.  Most of the colonial people lived along both banks of the St. Lawrence River, chiefly on the northern shore, in houses grouped into “cotes” (little hamlets), with each family’s property laid out with a narrow width of shore line that stretched back into the forested interior, which had to be cleared to farm.  Each of these hamlets usually had a manor-house or home of the area’s seigneur, although not a mansion by any means, which was the focus of hamlet’s social life.  The manor-house was sometimes built of timber but more often of stone, with dimensions rarely exceeding twenty feet by forty feet.  The manor-house was not much showier than the homes of the more prosperous and thriftier colonist farm houses.  All houses were built of either felled timber (log-cabin) or rough-hewn stones, which made the houses solid.  All the houses were low in height with a single door and few small windows.  Roofs were steep-pitched with dormer windows on the ends and roof eaves that projected out to handle heavy snow in the winter.  The manor-house generally had three or four spacious rooms with comfortably furniture which in many cases had been brought from France.  With almost religious punctuality the colonist in the spring whitewashed the outside of their house walls making the community look neat and proper.  A roadway was built between the houses on the river bank allowing movement between the farms of the individual families.  The family houses were lined up along the river bank only a few yards apart giving the appearance from the river of a long sprawling village along the river’s shore.

Behind each family farm house was a small hut that was used as a storeroom.  Not far away were the barn and the stable, built with untrimmed logs with clay or mortar closing the gaps.  There was also a root-house, half-sunk in the ground or burrowed into the slope of a hill, where the family kept their potatoes and vegetables secure from the frost through the winter.  Most of the farmers likewise had their own bake-ovens, located a convenient distance behind the house and rising four or five feet from the ground.  The ovens were built with boulders and plastered with clay.  The family would build a roaring fire in the oven and finish the whole week’s baking at one time.  The farmers would often enclose a small plot of ground surrounding the house and outbuildings with a fence of piled stones or split rails, and in one corner they would plant a kitchen-garden of vegetables, berries, potatoes, squash and spices .

Within the family dwelling-houses there were usually two, and never more than three rooms on the ground floor.  The single doorway opened into the great room of the house with the parlor, dining-room, and kitchen all combined.  A “living” room it surely was!  In the better houses, however, the main room was divided, with the kitchen partitioned off from the rest.  Most of the furnishings were the products of the colony and chiefly of the family’s own workmanship.  The floor was of hewn timber, rubbed and scrubbed to smoothness.  A woolen rug or several of them, always of vivid colors, covered the greater part of the rooms’ floors.  There was a family dinner-table of hewn pine, chairs made from pine saplings with seats of rushes or woven under-bark, and often in the corner a couch that would serve as an extra bed at night.  Pictures of holy Catholic saints hung on the walls, sharing the space with a crucifix, but often having for ominous companion the family’s flint-lock gun and powder-horn hanging from the ceiling beams.  At one end of the room was the fireplace and hearth, the sole means of heating the house, and usually the only means of cooking as well.  The fireplace also provided the light for the room at night or when it was too cold to open the windows or door.  Around the fireplace were hung the array of pots and pans, almost the only things in the house which the family was not able to make for themselves.  The lack of colonial industries had the advantage of throwing each home upon its own resources, and the people developed great versatility in the cruder arts of craftsmanship.

Upstairs in the attic, which was reached by a ladder, was a loft running the full-length of the house, that was so low one had to crouch to move around.  Here the family children, often a dozen or more were stowed away at night on mattresses of straw or feathers laid along the attic floor.  As the windows were securely fastened, even in the coldest weather the attic was usually warm, if not altogether hygienic.  The love of fresh air in homes was not among the home’s virtues.  Everyone went to bed shortly after darkness and all rose with the sun in the morning.  In the 1600s most of the world went to bed at nightfall because there was nothing else to do, and no easy or inexpensive artificial light was available.  Candles were in use, but burned on the altars in the churches than in the homes of the people.  Reading was not taught and the colonists relied upon their Catholic priest to provide information in the holy scriptures.

Individual’s clothing was almost all made at home.  The clothing was warm and durable, as well as somewhat distinctive and picturesque (charming).  Every parish (settlement with a church) had spinning wheels and handlooms in some of the homes on which the women would turn out heavy druggets (a coarse woolen felted fabric or woven woolen fabric colored or printed on one side) from which most of the men’s clothing was made. The men’s clothing was fashioned into roomy trousers and a long frock coat reaching down to the knees which was tied around the waist with a belt of leather or of knitted yarn.  The women also used woolen fabric for their skirts.  Summer dresses were made of calico cloth (plain-woven coarse rough fabric textile, made from half-processed and unbleached cotton fibers).  As for the children, most of them ran about in the summer months wearing next to nothing at all.  A single garment without sleeves and reaching to their knees was all that covered their naked body.  For all ages and for both sexes there were animal furs of plenty for winter use.  Beaver skins were cheap, in some years about as cheap as wool cloth.  When properly treated they were soft and pliable, and easily made into clothes, caps, and mittens.  Most of the footwear was also made at home, usually from deer hides.  In winter every one wore the “bottes sauvages” (a slipper shoe made from leather with a wooden sole and leather laced up halfway or more to the knees.  The shoes protected against the cold and were serviceable for use with snowshoes.  Within the slipper shoe heavy woolen socks were worn made from coarse homespun woolen yarn.  In the summer the women and children of the rural communities usually went barefooted so that the soles of their feet grew as tough as pigskin; the men sometimes did likewise, but more frequently they wore, in the fields or in the forest, clogs made of cowhide.

On the week-days in summer everyone wore a straw hats which the women of the household spent part of each winter plaiting (weaving).  In cold weather the knitted “tuque” (a knitted hat or cap with a narrow or no brim) made in vivid colors was the great favorite.  It was warm and colorful.  Each section of the Canada colony had its own color; the habitants in the vicinity of Quebec wore blue “tuques”, while those around Ville Marie preferred red ones.

On Sundays and festival days everyone brought out and wore their best attire.  Men wore clothes made of better texture and high beaver hats, the women appeared in their brighter plumage of dresses with ribbons and laces imported from France.  In the towns the officials and the well-to-do merchants dressed elaborately on all occasions of ceremony, with scarlet cloaks and perukes (wigs), buckled slippers and silk stockings.  In early French Québec there was no austerity rules like those imposed on the colonist in Puritan dominated English New England.

As for his daily fare, the Canada colonists were never badly off even in the years of poor harvests.  The food that was available was more nourishing and more abundant than what the French peasants received back in France.  Daily bread was made from both wheat and rye flour.  Corn cakes were baked in the Native American fashion from ground maize (Indian corn).  Fat salted pork was a staple during the winter, and nearly everyone laid away each autumn a supply of smoked of eels from the St Lawrence River.  Wild game animals of all sorts were also available including wild ducks, geese and partridges.  In the early winter it was only the unlucky colonist who could not also kill a deer or caribou.  Following the Native American’s custom, the deer and caribou meat were both smoked and then hung on the kitchen beams, where the smoked meat kept for months until needed.  Salted or smoked fish was also plentiful, since the Catholic Church prohibited the eating of meat on numerous fast-days.

Vegetables of many varieties were grown in Canada in the summer where the warm sandy virgin soil of the St. Lawrence River valley region was splendidly suited for growing vegetables.  Peas were the great stand-by and the national dish of the French Canadians.  Beans, cucumbers, melons, and a dozen other vegetables  were also grown in the family gardens.  Potatoes, which the French colonists called “palates”, were common.  Wild fruits, chiefly raspberries, blueberries, and wild grapes, grew in abundance among the foothills and were gathered in great quantities every summer.

As for beverages, the French colonists were very fond of sour milk (milk that is made by allowing bacterial fermentation to occur when the milk is keep in a warm place for a day, often near a stove.  Tea was scarce and costly.  Brandy was imported from Europe in huge quantities.  Every parish in the colony had its grog-shop (place to buy and drink rum), which the French King in 1725 had to control by ordering that no parish could have more than two grog-shops.  There is no doubt that the people of Canada were also very fond of the “flagon” (wine).  Idleness due to the numerous holidays and to the long winters combined with the tradition of hospitality led to drinking large quantities of flagon.

Tobacco was used freely and every French colonial farmer planted a quantity of tobacco near their house because it was universally smoked.  Boys of twelve years of age often ran about with the pipe in their mouths smoking.  The women were smokers, too, but more commonly they used tobacco in the form of snuff (finely ground tobacco place into one’s nose to deliver a quick kick of nicotine).  The French-Canadian tobacco was very strong as it was cured in the sun till the leaves turned black, and when smoked emitting an odor that scented the whole parish community.  The art of smoking a pipe with tobacco was quickly acquiring from the colonists’ Native American friends.

The Canadian colonists did not spend all their energies and time at hard labor.  From October, when the crops were finally in, until May when the crop seeding time came again, there was little hard work to do.  Aside from the cutting of firewood and the few household chores, the day was free, and the colonists therefore spent much time visiting with their neighbors, drinking and smoking, dancing and playing cards.

The chief festivities occurred at Michaelmas, Christmas, Easter, and May-Day.  Of these, the first and the last were closely connected with the traditional French seigneurial system.  On 29 September, the end of the harvest season and the beginning of fall, Michaelmas or the Feast of St. Michael holiday was celebrated.  It was the time the colonists came to pay their annual rental free to their seigneurial for their land they lived and farmed on.  May-Day (the first day on May) was when Maypole homage occurred when each French male colonist publicly pledge allegiance to their seigneurial lord.  Christmas and Easter were the great festivals of the Catholic Church and as such were celebrated with religious dedication and seriousness.  Sundays were a day not only of worship but of recreation.  Clad in their best clothes, everyone went to Catholic Mass, regardless of the weather.  The Catholic parish church was a symbol of village’s solidarity, for it gathered within its walls each Sunday morning all sexes, ages and ranks.  The colonists did not separate their religion from their work or amusements.  The Catholic church and its priests were the center and soul of Canada communities.  The whole countryside gathered about the church doors after the service while the “capitaine de la cote”, the local representative of the intendant (highest ranking government official in the area) read the decrees that had been sent to him from the governor of Canada.  That duty over, there was a garrulous interchange of local gossip with the retelling of news that had dribbled in from the rest of the colony and the home country of France.  The crowd outside the church then melted away in groups to spend the rest of the day in games, dancing or in friendly family visits.

Especially popular among the young people of each parish were the “corvees recreative” (free labor activities) in the rural communities.  There was corn-husking, wheat shucking ,flax-beating jobs that needed to be done.  Dancing was by all odds the most popular pastime, and every parish had its fiddler.  Every wedding was the occasion for dancing festivities which lasted all day long.  The colonists liked to sing, especially when working with others in the woods or when on a march.  The most popular were folksongs that came over France.  They were easy to learn, simple to sing, but lively and musical.

Socially, the seigneur and his family did not stand apart from his neighbors.  All went to the same area church, took part in the same amusements on days of festivals, and frequently worked together on the common task, such as clearing forested land, building homes and animal shelters, constructing churches and river loading docks.  Sons and daughters of the seigneurs often intermarried with local colonists or with traders and craftsmen in the towns.  There was no social “bottleneck”, which existed back in in France, to advancement in one’s wealth and responsibility in the Canada communities.
In 1620, Marie Marguerie was born to Francois Marguerie and Marthe Roma, in Le Havre, Rouen, Normandy, France.
In 1620, Jean Leblanc was born to Clement Leblanc and Anne Jeanne (Lefebvre) in Saint-Lambert, Bayeux, Normandy, France.
In 1620, French King Louis XIII, on the advice of his minister Richelieu, led an eager fight against the Protestants Huguenots in France.

In 1620, the Recollects (Franciscans Catholic Friars) built the first hospital, the “Hotel Dieu”, a small stone house that provided medical services for the French settlers and the native people in the Québec settlement area.

In 1620, French King Louis XIII ordered Samuel de Champlain to cease exploration and to return to Québec , and to devoted himself to the administration of the Canada.  In every way but having a formal title, Samuel de Champlain was serving as Governor of Canada, a title that may have been formally unavailable to him owing to his non-noble status.  Samuel established the French North America trading companies that sent goods, primarily fur, back to France, and oversaw the growth of Canada Colony in the St. Lawrence River valley until his death in 1635.

Note:  Samuel de Champlain and many other earlier explorers kept looking for a northern sea rout to Asia.  Initially the St Lawrence River had looked promising as early explorers soon found the large river was connected to the Great Lakes.  There is no evidence the discovery of Hudson Bay was known to Samuel Champlain and the French at this time.

In 1620, the newly arrived French quickly established trading posts throughout their Canada territory, trading for fur with Native American hunters.  The French traders also explored much of the area themselves.  The map below that shows the French, English and Spanish claimed areas in North America and the dates the important towns were established.

blocks.caption: In 1620, the newly arrived French quickly established trading posts throughout their Canada territory, trading for fu…

blocks.text: In 1620, Jean Nicolet returned to the Québec settlement after living with Algonquin Native people for two years.  Jean Nicolet made a report on his two-year mission and was given a new mission: to make contact with the Nipissing Native people who lived on the shores of the lake of the same name.  The Nipissing’s were each year assuming a more important role in the fur trade, acting as intermediaries between the French and the other Native American tribes to the west and north.  It was Jean’s new mission to enhance the Nipissing fur trading alliance with the French, and to see that the furs did not find their way to English.  In the summer of 1620, Jean Nicollet travel 170 mile farther up the Ottawa River by canoe to Lake Nipissing and met and lived with the Nipissing Native people for the next nine years.  Jean Nicolet had his own lodge and a store where he traded with all the Native people of the various tribes and questioning them about their lives and country.

In 1621, Robert Giffard, a gentleman-surgeon and apothecary, made his first trip to the Quebec settlement Canada.  Robert Giffard, as a naval surgeon (a ship’s doctor responsible for the health of the people about the ship) who was living in Tourouvre, Perche, France at the time.

On 26 Aug 1621, Guillaume Couillard and Guilleumette Hébert, the youngest daughter of Louis Hébert and Marie (Rollet) were married in the Québec settlement.  Guillaume, who had been a carpenter, seaman and caulker now took up “farming” with his new wife.
On 8 Dec 1621, Jacques Gaudry and Charlotte Chevalier were married in Mortagne, Perche, France.
On 8 Dec 1621, Nicolas Gaudry was born to Jacques Gaudry and Charlotte (Chevalier) in Mortagne, Perche, France.
In 1621, Marie Gendre was born to Moïse Gendre and Jeanne (Cosse) in Surgères, Nouvelle-Aquitaine, France.
In 1621, Marie Crevet was born to Jean Mercier and Jeanne (Chesnée)in Bénouville, Normandy, France.  Bénouville is where the famous WWII Pegasus Bridge is located.  See the famous bridge below:

blocks.caption: In 1621, Marie Crevet was born to Jean Mercier and Jeanne (Chesnée)in Bénouville, Normandy, France. Bénouville is whe…

blocks.text: Note:  Early before daylight on 6 June 1944, during the Second World War, the Pegasus Bridge was taken from the Germans by a small elite glider-borne American force of 6th Airborne Division.  Once captured, the bridge was held against repeated attacks by a much larger German force, preventing the Germans from launching a large counterattack on the Allied troops on the Normandy beaches.
In 1621, Pauline Rolline Poête was born to Jean Poete and Marie (Michel) in Touques, Normandy, France.
In 1621, Anne Fosse was born to Francois Foissey and Marguerite (Cler) in Rouen, Normandy, France.

In 1622, Louis Hébert petitioned Samuel de Champlain, the Viceroy (leader) of Canada, for the title of his land and on 4 Feb 1623 he received a grant guaranteeing his possessions, which later became the site of the Notre-Dame Cathedral in Quebec City located on Hébert and Couillard streets.
In 1622, Marie Michel was born to Pierre Michel and Louise (Gory) in St. Martin du Vieux- Bellême, Normandy, France.
In 1622, Jean Baptiste Bourgery was born to Marie Bourgery and Jeanne (Le Moine) in La Rochelle, Normandy, France.
In 1622, Denise Gauthier was born to Philippe Gauthier and Madeleine (Pichon) in La Rochelle, Nouvelle-Aquitaine, France.
In 1622, Quentin Moral was born to Antoine Morel and Antoinette (Fleury) in Valence, Auvergne-Rhone-Alpes, France.

In 1623, the first Roman Catholic bishop of Québec (Titular Viceroy of Canada), appointed by Pope Alexander VII, gave Louis Hébert the Québec colony’s first “title deed” for a parcel of land Louis had begun to work on.  Louis Hébert was made a seigneur (lord of the manor).

In 1624, the Dutch established a permanent settlement of New Netherland, (present-day New York City).  They quickly set up an extensive fur trade operation out of Fort Orange (present-day Albany, New York).  The Dutch next moved into present-day Connecticut.  Tensions gradually increased between the Dutch and the Algonquian Native people in the area.  The Dutch settlers’ cattle and hogs got lose and invaded the Algonquian villages’ cornfields.  The Algonquian retaliated by killing the invading animals.  A series of wars followed between the Dutch settlers and the Algonquian natives.
On 18 Mar 1624, Marie Guyon was born to Jean Guyon and Mathurine (Robin) in Tourouvre, Normandy, France.
On 11 Jul 1624, Jean Dumay and Miotte Lecombe were married in parish of Saint-Jacques in Dieppe, Normandy, France.
On 7 Dec 1624, Louis Gesseron Sr. was born to Nicolas Gezeron and Maria (Guitard) in La Rochelle, Normandy, France.
In 1624, Mathieu Choret was born to Thomas Choret and Jeanne (Serre) in La Rochelle, Normandy, France.
In 1624, Marie Guitard was born to Nicolas Guittard and Catherine (Boul) n La Rochelle, Normandy, France.
Note: In 1625, the population of the new Québec settlement was only 56 people.
On 22 Dev 1625, Marie Marguerite Garnier was born to Charles Garnier and Jeanne (La Braye) in Marans, Charente-Maritime, France.
In 1625, Claude Metru was born to unknown parents in Paris, Île-de-France, France.

Note:  The France, England, Dutch and Spanish who were exploring and colonizing North America and fishing in the North Atlantic Ocean were all constantly in fear of being captured by Muslim pirates associated with the Ottoman empire.  Between 1612 and 1620 more than one thousand fishermen were captured off the Newfoundland’s fishing banks and forced into galley slaves (a death sentence) by the Muslim pirates.  See the “Story of Carolee Afton McCance’s Family” for more information on the history and impact of the Muslim pirates on Europe and North America.
On 15 Apr 1626, Françoise Morin was born to Jean Morin and Jeanne (Desnquets) in Gahard, Brittany, France.
On 19 Jun 1626, Anne Cloutier was born to Zacharie Cloutier and Saintes (Dupont) in Mortagne, Perche, Normandy, France.
In 1626, Pierre Tremblay was born to Philibert Tremblay and Jeanne (Coignet) in Randonnai, Tourouvre au Perche, France.
In 1626, Pierre Saint-Denis was born to Paschal Saint-Denis and Rosalie (Daoust) in Dieppe, Normandy, France.
In 1626, Vivienne Brunel was born to Louis Brunelle and Nathalie (Campeau) in Rouen, Normandy, France.
In 1626, Etienne Dumay Sr. was born to Jean Dumay and Miotte (Lecombe) in Dieppe, Normandy, France.
In 1626, Sébastienne Veillon was born to Maxient Veillon and Bernarde (Venet) in Verdille in Poitou, which is 10 miles from La Rochelle, Normandy, France.
On 6 Aug 1626, Marguerite Couillard was born to Guillaume Couillard and Guilleumette (Hébert) in the French Québec settlement.
On 1626, Louis Hébert was granted additional land and along the Saint Charles River in recognition of his “painful labors” by the new Viceroy Samuel de Champlain.
On 1626, Samuel de Champlain established the first farm area in Château-Richer community on the north side of the St. Lawrence River across from the Île d'Orléans Island.

In 1626, the Native American Iroquois Confederation destroyed the Mohicans tribe and became the dominate native force in eastern North America south of the St. Lawrence River.  They next set their sights on the land north of the St. Lawrence River.

On 25 Jan 1627, fifty-two-year-old Louis Hébert, the first farmer of Quebec, died from a fall on ice and his widow wife Marie was left to manage the farm with help from son-in-law Guillaume Couillard, the husband of her daughter Guilleumette (Hébert).
On 26 Dec 1627, Jean Guay was born to Jean Guay Sr. and Marie (Dumont) in Berneuil, Nouvelle-Aquitaine, France.

In 1627, following the death of Louis’s Hébert, his second son Francois Hébert and Guillaume Couillard each inherited half of Louis Hébert’s and estate.  Samuel de Champlain granted Guillaume Couillard an additional one hundred acres of land bordering on the Saint Charles River.  The next spring Guillaume became the first person to use a plow in the colony as he prepared his new land for farming.

Note:  The initial Canada settlements were set up as a part of a proprietary colony granted by the French Crown (King) to various corporations (companies) to rule, provided they fulfilled the terms of their royal charters, like supporting the missionary work of the Roman Catholic Church amongst the Native people.

In 1627, France’s Catholic Cardinal Richelieu, the chief minister and powerful authority with great influence over France’s King Louis XIII, got interested and became involved in the Canada Colony.  During this period Robert Giffard left the Québec settlement and returned to France to encourage others from the Perche area in Normandy France to immigrate to Canada and start a new life.  Robert Giffard received approval from Cardinal Richelieu, to form the “Company of the One Hundred-Associates” with a charter that gave the company a monopoly on the French business enterprise actives in Canada, to manage the booming fur trade and control land rights in the Canada colony.  Canada quickly became a new source of beaver fur, which was used to make the popular wide-brimmed felt hats for the French aristocracy and the Parisian bourgeoisie (those in the county with wealth and ownership of the production of goods).  The fur hats even became part of the standard French military uniform.  The process of felt making involved converted the beaver fir into a material that was warm, soft, pliable and weather-resistant.  The “Company of the One Hundred-Associates” as a government monopoly, was required to send 4,000 settlers to Canada over the next 15 years.  The Company largely ignored the settlement requirements and focused on the lucrative fur trade instead.

In 1627, Catholic Cardinal Richelieu implemented through the “Company of the One Hundred-Associates” a new restriction allowing only Catholics to immigrate to the Canada Colony.  This new rule resulted in all French Protestant Huguenots leaving Canada and going to the Dutch and English colonies in North America, limiting a source of energetic hardworking settlers who were just the people needed to grow the French Canada Colony.

Robert Giffard became in effect, a subcontractor of the “Company of the One Hundred-Associates” and recruited colonists for the Québec settlement in exchange for a three-mile-long tract of riverfront land, which extended four and a half-miles back into the forest on the north bank of the St. Lawrence, ten-mile east and downstream from the Québec settlement.  Robert’s new property was called the Seigneury (manor) of “Beauport”.  As a seigneur, Robert would allocate a section of his land to a settler, who was expected to clear the land, build a home, pay a modest rent and give ¼ of all the grain grown to Robert for his flour mill.

Robert Giffard lived in the French village of Mortagne in the Perche, famous for its Percherons draft horses.  Robert was able to persuade skilled craftsmen from Mortagne and in some cased whole families to join him in the adventure of building a new settlement community in Canada.  Robert required everyone coming with him to work three years, clearing Robert’s land and build Robert a manor house before receiving their own assigned land.

In 1627, the Québec settlement had grown to 100 people, of which only 12 were women.  A new wooden fort had been constructed.  After the last supply ship from France arrived, no additional supply ships or new emigrants reach Canada over the next two years.  War with England had broken out and France forgot about its Canada Colony.

In 1627, the “Anglo-French War” broke out, between France and England when the English attacked the fort at La Rochelle in France in support of the French Protestant Huguenots.  The Huguenots were fighting against King Louis XIII of France, who was trying to bring the Huguenots under his Catholic control.  The city of La Rochelle had become the stronghold of the French Protestant Huguenots.  It was the center of Huguenot Sea power and the strongest center of resistance against the King Louis XIII and his central Catholic government.  The English used the war to launched a campaign against France's new Canada colony in North America, which led to the English capture of the Québec settlement.  The clashes between the Catholics and Protestants in France resulted in fierce violence, despite the “Edict of Nantes” signed back in 1598 by French King Henry IV that granted considerable religious and civil liberty to French Protestant Huguenots.  The “Siege of La Rochelle” lasted fifteen months.  The town resisted in a heroic fashion but had to surrender to the French Army in 1628 after 15,000 of its 20,000 inhabitants had died.
Note:  Seventeen members of Winifred Elois Coss family tree were born of lived in La Rochelle before leaving for Canada.

sections.title: Seigneurial System
sections.teaser: In 1627, the “Seigneurial System” (Manor System) was instituted in Canada that granted land to seigneurs: usually military leaders or aristocrats just below…

blocks.text: In 1627, the “Seigneurial System” (Manor System) was instituted in Canada that granted land to seigneurs: usually military leaders or aristocrats just below the French Ruling Class, including high ranking church leaders.  The seigneurs were charged with the task of subdividing large parcels of land and renting out sections to peasant families who would engage in subsistence farming to meet most of their food, heating, and shelter needs.  Under regulations set up by the French government, the seigneur could also set up a court of law and a mill on his land and organize a collective farm.  The seigneurial system was based on the European feudal system, were plots of land were given to noblemen - who were called seigneurs - in return for their loyalty to the king and a promise to perform military service when necessary.  The system created a traditional peasant-lord relationship and established an upper group of landed elite.  This system of land distribution was regulated by French law.  Its purpose was to promote settlement in a systematic way.  Seigneuries areas, which were usually (3 miles by 9 miles) in size, were generally divided into river lots.  The long, rectangular strips facilitated interaction between neighbors and provided multiple points of access to the river, the principal communication route of goods and people.  The seigneur’s system doomed the French in North America to their eventual defeat against the British who allowed their settlers to own their own land and get ahead through hard work.  The ability to acquire and own land in the English American Colonies attracted millions of settlers hoping for a better life.  Canada on the other hand struggled to attract new settlers, which in the end caused them to be overwhelmed by the large number English.  See the map below of the Île-d’Orléans Island showing how the typical Seigneur’s land was subdivided into land parcels with waterfront access.

blocks.caption: In 1627, the “Seigneurial System” (Manor System) was instituted in Canada that granted land to seigneurs: usually mil…

blocks.text: In April 1628, the “Company of the One Hundred-Associates” sent a fleet of ships from Dieppe France with supplies and settlers heading to Canada.  The French fleet was intercepted by English ships commanded by David Kirke, Thomas Kirke and Louis Kirke “the Kirke’s brothers”.  A number of the French ships were sunk, and the rest were forced to return to France.  No French ships made it to Canada in 1628.

In 1628, Robert Giffard with his new wife Marie Renouard sailed back to Canada to prepare for the immigrants they would soon be bringing from the Perche area of France.  Their ship was also intercepted by English pirates and forced to return to France.

In 1628, Euphrosine Madeleine Nicolet was born as a Métis child (mixed Native and European blood child) to Jean Nicolet and Jeanne Bahmahmaadjimiwin Giisis in a Nipissing tribe settlement.  Folklore claims Jeanne B. Giisis was a Native Princess, the daughter of the Nipissing Chief.  The Nipissing Native people lived around Lake Nipissing, which is now in Ontario Québec, north of today’s city of Toronto and east of Lake Huron.
In 1628, Jeanne Crissot was born to unknown parents in Paris, Île-de-France, France.
About 1628, Jean Bonneau and Catherine Poulet were married in Mesnil, Hauts-de-France, France.
In 1628, Jean Lemarché was born to Jean Lemarché and Marie (Blondeau) in Paris, Île-de-France, France.

In the summer of 1628, the English ships appeared in the harbor of the Tadoussac outpost, 130 miles down the St. Lawrence River from the Québec settlement.  In July the English ships sailed up the St. Lawrence River and attacked the French farm at Cap Tourmente, 30 miles east of the Québec settlement.  The Frenchmen at the farm were all killed  and their farm structures were plundered, the livestock slaughtered and their homes were burn down.  On 10 Jul 1628, the English leaders, the three Kirke brothers, sent a letter to Samuel de Champlain demanding the surrender of the Québec settlement.  Samuel was able to bluff the English into thinking the small Québec settlement was stronger than it was and stopped English from attacking for the rest of the year.  The English were however successful in stopping all French support and supply ships from aiding the beleaguered and desperate Québec settlement.

Note:  Today “Cap Tourmente” is an National Wildlife Area located on the north shore of the  in the  of Québec.  It is one of the critical habitats for the  during their annual migrations.  Flocks of tens of thousands of the birds stop over to feed on the bullrushes in the spring and fall.  The  was recognized as a wetland of international significance in 1981, making it the first North American site to receive that designation.
On 21 Aug 1629, Jacques Ménard was born to Jean Ménard and Anne (Savinelle) in Fontenay, Normandy, France.

On 9 Aug 1629, Samuel de Champlain formally surrendered his beloved Québec settlement to the English Kirke brothers.  When the English came ashore to raise the English flag, they found the colony had only one tub of potatoes and roots left to feed the 67 people living in Colony.   The settlement’s residents were close to starving.  On 14 Sep 1629, Samuel and most settlers, including  Abraham Martin, wife Marguerite (Langlois) and their fifteen-year-old daughter Anne Martin boarded ships and left Québec and sailed back to England.  The French settlement captives in the ships stopped at the Tadoussac outpost before setting sail across the Atlantic Ocean where Samuel learned that two Frenchmen had helped the Kirke’s navigate up the St. Lawrence River in their quest to seize Canada from the French.  Samuel was outraged by the discovery that Jacques Michel was a traitor to the country but, even more so, by the fact that Samuel’s own former servant, Étienne Brulé, was the other traitor!  Champlain felt deeply betrayed by his fellow countrymen.

Unknown to the Québec French colonists and the English captors was that peace had been declared between the French and English earlier in late April a month after the Kirke’s had left England to seize the French colony.  As soon as the French Government received word of the seizure of Québec Colony, they made an immediate demand that the colony be returned to them and that restitution be made for all the losses the French had suffered.  King Charles II of England agreed to this demand.  The agreement to return Canada to the French, however, did not happen immediately.

A few of the French settlers including Guillaume Couillard’s family had remained in the settlement under the Kirke brothers when they took control.  The Marie Hébert (Rollet) was the matriarch of the remaining French setters.  Even though her husband Louis Hébert had died, she refused to leave her home in the tiny French settlement her husband had help build.

In 1629, when Québec was captured by the English, Jean Nicolet, who was loyal to France, took refuge with Nipissing Native people in the Huron country.  He thwarted all the English plans to get the Native tribes to trade with them.  Jean Nicolet’s partner (lived together with but was not married) Jeanne Bahmahmaadjimiwin Giisis, a native Nipissing woman, with whom he had a daughter Euphrosine Madeleine Nicolet the year before.  Euphrosine  grew up to be an important native interpreter and intermediary between the New French and the local native tribes of the St. Lawrence River Valley.

In the fall of 1629, after returning to France, nine-year-old Hélène Desportes was living in the port city of Dieppe, France, the birth place of Hélène’s mother Françoise Langlois, who had passed away a few months earlier, and her aunt, Marguerite Martin (Langlois).  Marguerite and her current husband Abraham Martin were also living in Dieppe.
On 1 Jan 1630, Paul Vachon was born to Vincent Vachon and Sapience (Vateau) in La Copechagnière, Pays de la Loire, France.

On 4 Mar 1630, Pierre Bonneau was born to Jean Bonneau and Catherine (Poulet) in Vernoux-sur-Boutonne, Nouvelle-Aquitaine, France, which the Romans called “Brigiosum” that was located on the Boutonne River where lead ore was mined by the Romans.  The town of Vernoux-sur-Boutonne was located 50 miles east from the coastal port city of La Rochelle.
On 17 May 1630, Pierre Gaudin was born to Claude Gaudin and Marie (Bardin) in Châtillon-sur-Seine, Bourgogne-Franche-Comté, France.
On 13 Jun 1630, Jean Bigot and Thomine Chastel were married in La Ventrouze, Normandy, France.  Jean Bigot will die in France in 1639.
On 23 Dec 1630, Pierre Maheu was born to Jean Maheu and Michelle (Chawvin) in Mortagne, Perche, France.
In 1630, Marie Anne Le Laboureur was born to Thomas Laboureur and Marguerite) in Bayeux, Normandy, France.
On 17 May 1631, Francoise Bigot was born to Jean Bigot and Thomine (Chastel) in Tourouvre au Perche, France.

sections.title: Perche Migration
sections.teaser: In three decades starting in 1632, a large proportion of immigrants to Canada came from Perche area, in what has been called the “Percheron Immigration Movem…

blocks.text: In three decades starting in 1632, a large proportion of immigrants to Canada came from Perche area, in what has been called the “Percheron Immigration Movement”.  Many Percheron’s had been recruited to work in seigneuries being establishing along the Saint Lawrence River Valley.  The Beauport seigneurie, Canada's first agricultural-oriented seigneurie, was granted in 1634 to Robert Giffard by the “Company of Hundred Associates”.  While the total number of emigrants was small, Perche had a much higher rate of emigration to Canada than most other regions of France.  (Mary Anne Goodwater has 16 family tree members who were born in Perche area of France.)

sections.title: Return to Québec
sections.teaser: On 29 Mar 1632, Québec settlement that had been lost to the Kirke brothers three years earlier, was returned to France by the “Treaty of Saint-Germain-en-Lay…

blocks.text: On 29 Mar 1632, Québec settlement that had been lost to the Kirke brothers three years earlier, was returned to France by the “Treaty of Saint-Germain-en-Laye” with England.

In 1632, with the Québec settlement back in French control; Guillaume Couillard worked unsparingly for the colony.  He fought against the frequent Iroquois warrior attacks, piloted boats between the Québec Settlement and the Tadoussac settlement 130 miles down the St. Lawrence River where the Saguenay River entered the St. Lawrence River, and he also became the churchwarden for a small church built on his land.

In 1632, following the freeing of Canada by the British, Robert Giffard persuaded Noël Langlois Sr. to become a ship's pilot (navigator), like his wife’s sister Marguerite (Langlois) husband’s Abraham Martin.
In 1632, Pierre Duchesne was born to Jean Duchesne and Catherine (Paulet) in Mesnil-Saint-Laurent, Hauts-de-France, France.

In 1632, the Catholic “Franciscans Friars”, the “Récollets”, were not allowed to return to Canada.  French Cardinal Richelieu reserved Canada to the Catholic “Jesuit Order” to convert the North American natives to the Catholic faith.  The Jesuit Orders' founder was a Spanish soldier who formed the Jesuit Order back in 1540.  The Jesuit Order was based on soldier's discipline and the zeal for training.  The Franciscans, on the other hand, were founded by Francis of Assisi.  Francis was the son of a wealthy merchant and had renounced his wealth and devoted himself to a life of service to the poor.  His followers, like Francis, strive to divest themselves not only of wealth but also of ambition, arrogance and power.  They live among the poor and needy, and spend their lives in service.  The Jesuits, on the other hand were renowned for their scholarship and educational work.

French Catholic Church was fraught (troubled) with change.  The fear of hell was stronger than ever; Christians had a duty to combat Satan’s work on all fronts.  The French elite believed the world as they knew it was coming to an end.  There were very strong religious undercurrents such as Protestant reforms occurring.  The world was on the road to ruin and the final judgment was approaching.  If the Native pagan people in Canada were not converted to Christians the French settlers in Canada would surely go to hell.  The whole world needed to be converted for the elite were accountable to God for all souls.  The Jesuits perceived themselves to be God’s soldiers.

On 22 Mar 1633, Samuel de Champlain returned to the Québec settlement and began construction of a new settlement based on the framework of a traditional French “ville” (town) that was common in France at the time.  The Québec settlement’s population was split into two groups.  The first group were the colonial elites, including clergy, government officials, skilled craftsmen who lived in the upper town.  The second group were the merchants, sailors and craftsmen that live in the lower town down on the river edge.  The old small settlement of Québec soon began to grow into a real town.
On 18 Jul 1633, Anne Ozanne Achon was born to Jean Achon and Helene (Regnault) in La Rochelle, Normandy, France.  The Achon family was of Moorish decent.

The Moors were the original people who occupied the Northwest African Berber area for over 12,000 years.  Starting in the late 600s an Arab migration began, which expanded the Muslim faith across north Africa from Egypt over to Atlantic coast.  In 711, the Berber Muslims invaded Hispania, today modern Spain and controlled the area until 1492 when King Fernando and Queen Isabella led their Spanish Christian Armies and defeated of the last Moorish Islamic forces holding the city of Granada, thus ending the 781-year Moorish occupation and control over most of the Iberian Peninsula (modern Portugal and Spain).  The fact that Anne Ozanne Achon and her family were living in western France indicates they had probably assimilated into the French Catholic cultural and were viewed as regular ordinary people of France in a period when non-Catholics were under assault in France.  The map on the next page shows the areas the Berber tribes held before that were defeated by the Spanish Christian Armies.  The Berber African Anne Ozanne Achon fits with DNA finding of Winifred Eloise Coss’ daughter (Gloria Joan), who shows >1% Senegal African DNA.

The French became interested in the Senegal area, particularly the Senegal River and the large river valley.  In 1626 France set up of a monopoly company the “Norman Company” made up of merchants from Dieppe and Rouen areas of France to exclusive trade with the people of Senegal.  The “Norman Company” was dissolved later in 1658 and replace by new “Company of Cape Vert and Senegal”.  The start and stop of a series of monopole trading companies occurred in Senegal the same way it occurred in Canada during the same period.  Just as happened in Canada, Frenchmen who went to work in the trading monopolies in Senegal were not allowed to take French wives and Frenchwomen were also not allow to travel to the French Colony.  The young adventured single Frenchmen partnered or married native women in Senegal as occurred in Canada.  See a map on the next page showing the areas where the Moors/Berber people lived.

blocks.caption: The French became interested in the Senegal area, particularly the Senegal River and the large river valley. In 1626…

blocks.text: On 2 Nov 1633, Toussaint Giroux was born in Réveillon, Perche, France to Jean Giroux and Marguerite (Quilleron).

Opposition to the French settlement in Canada existed from rich French merchants in the communities of La Rochelle, Rouen, St-Malo and other cities on the west of France.  The opposition came from the French merchants that had received French monopiles in the New World in the fur trading business.  The rich merchants worried that the establishment of French settlements in Canada would upset the current trade agreements that were in place with the powerful Native Iroquois Nation.  The merchants also worried that establishment of new French settlements would drive the fur bearing animals away from the area ending the source of supply of the valuable animal furs that were making the French merchants rich.  Young French King Louis XIII saw how the riches Spain had gained from the capture gold and silver in the New World had increased the wealth and power of Spain and King Louis XIII wanted France to also benefit from its New World possessions.  The French merchants were opposed to the establishment of French settlement of Canada, but were not totally successful.  The opposition however, was another barrier that had to be overcome that slowed French settlement of their colony in Canada.

Samuel de Champlain, the first governor of Quebec, had an ambitious vision for Canada.  He was planning on creating a whole new country, a new French country in North America where French people could prosper through farming, fishing, trading and living comfortable off the abundant natural resources in the new land, while at the same time living in harmony the Native people living in the area.  Samuel had secretly visited the Spanish controlled Caribbean and had been stunned by the cruelty of the Spaniards toward the Native Caribbean people.  Samuel viewed the Native people living in the New World as equal to the Europeans.  He consistently worked to maintain good relations with the various Native tribes he encountered.  His efforts were mostly successful with the Montagnais, Etchemin’s, Abenaki and the Hurons Native tribes, which lived in the St. Lawrence River valley, but were far less successful with the Iroquois living south of the Great Lakes.

sections.title: Immigration Encouraged
sections.teaser: In the early 1600’s France’s young men who wanting to be tradesmen had limited opportunities because most trades were already overloaded with qualified Frenc…

blocks.text: In the early 1600’s France’s young men who wanting to be tradesmen had limited opportunities because most trades were already overloaded with qualified French workers.  The ability to become a “masters” in a trade was restricted and was also required to be able to own one’s own business.  Many young men who want-to-be French tradesmen signed up with recruiters for a new program of “Volunteers for Canada”, agreeing to a three-year contract working for a landlords or religious community who were seeking skilled workers in Canada.  The recruiters of the new young men volunteers were responsible for arranging everything: cash advances for the trip and all other transportation expenses.  The volunteers were to earn an annual salary of 20 to 150 livres, according to their level of skills.  At the end of their service, the volunteers were free to stay in the Canada or return back home to France with their new trade skills.

On 15 Jan 1634, Robert Giffard got permission from the “Company of the One Hundred-Associates”, to take people living in France to the French colony in Canada.  Robert recruited his first colonists from his home area of Perche France.  For his efforts, Robert Giffard was granted one of the first royal seigneuries (manor) of land in Canada, which stretched three miles along the northern shore of the St. Lawrence River, becoming a new settlement and later the town of Beauport Canada.

Note:  The Perche area was located 100 miles west of Paris.  Perche was a country that had been created back in 1115 and was later united with France in 1525.  Later in 1792, in a major reorganization of the French county system occurred and Perche was lost and absorbed into the region of Normandy.

In March 1634, Robert Giffard, his wife Marie and their children plus thirty Perche colonists he had recruited left France and sailed over to Canada.  They arrive on 4 Jun 1634, in the Quebec settlement.  Traveling with Robert Giffard on the 4 ships sent by the “Cheffault-Rozee Company”, were: Noël Langlois Sr., the ship’s pilot and navigator; Jean Guyon, a master mason and his wife Mathurine (Robin) and their seven children, including ten-year-old daughter Marie Guyon; Zacharie Cloutier a “master carpenter” and his oldest son Jean; Robert Drouin a “brick and tile maker”; François Bélanger a “mason”; Jean Côté and his son Nicolas Côté; and Robert Caron and his two brothers.  The new commers were temporarily lodged in Samuel de Champlain’s new Fort Saint-Louis , which was still under construction.  The newly arriving men, under their contracts, immediately got to work building a new manor for Robert Giffard.  Robert Drouin used his brick making and building skills to quickly construct a brick manor house and supporting manor outbuildings.  A brick church was also built along with more modest houses for the other newly arrived French colonists, before the harsh Canadian winter set in.

Catholic Jesuit superior (leader) Paul Le Jeune wrote about how hard the new men worked in building the new manor for Robert Gifford, doing the jobs that usually were performed by horse or oxen, both of which had not yet arrived.  Trees were cutdown and removed and stones gather for use in construction and farm fields were prepared for planting all by hand.

The winters of 1634/35 was very severe.  Ice formed in the St. Lawrence River blocking all navigation (movement up and down the river) for six months starting in October and lasting to April, cutting Quebec off from the outside world.  Periods of intense cold followed by heavy blizzards (whiteouts), lasted for three to four days forcing everyone inside.  Deep fresh snow up to twelve feet deep made all outside activity impossible.  The new houses being built had to hold up during the deep snow periods and barns had to be strong and big enough to house all the farm animals over the six-month winter.  See a map below of the Quebec settlement in 1634.

blocks.caption: The winters of 1634/35 was very severe. Ice formed in the St. Lawrence River blocking all navigation (movement up and…

blocks.text: Over the next 30 years Robert Giffard brought an additional 116 colonists from the Perche area to Canada.  The colonists from the Perche area represented about 5% of all the early colonists in Canada.

On 14 Mar 1634, Zacharie Cloutier and Jean Guyon signed a “contract of servitude”, in favor of Robert Giffard.  The contract stipulated that Giffard would pay their passage plus food and lodging in Quebec, for Zacharie Cloutier and Jean Guyon plus one family member each, for a period of three years.  After two years the two men would be allowed to send for the rest of their families, also at the expense of Robert Giffard.  Giffard also agreed to give each man a few head of livestock to get them started farming, plus 845 acres of land with the right to build on it and the right to hunt, fish and trade with the native people in the area.  Zacharie had agreed to leave France with his seventeen-year-old son only, but he changed his mind and decided to bring his wife Saintes (Dupont) and their children, including eight-year-old Anne Cloutier, which he was allow.  By 22 Jul 1634, master-carpenter Zacharie Cloutier and master-mason Jean Guyon were hard at work on the construction of a manor house for their lord, Robert Giffard as well as a parish church and a new Fort Saint-Louis for Quebec.  The new Fort Saint-Louis was an expanded the original Habitation Fort built earlier by Samuel Champlain.  See below a photo of a model of Fort Saint-Louis as it looked in 1635.

blocks.caption: On 14 Mar 1634, Zacharie Cloutier and Jean Guyon signed a “contract of servitude”, in favor of Robert Giffard. The co…

blocks.text: Note:  Arpent is an old French unit of land. One arpent is equal to .85 acres and was the standard measure of land in France until the 1970s.
On 4 Jun 1634, Nicolas Massard was born to Jean-Francois Massard and Marguerite (Pimpante) in Vouillé, Nouvelle-Aquitaine, France.

On 4 Jul 1634, a new settlement of Trois-Rivières was established in the Canada Colony, 80-miles up the St. Lawrence River from the Québec settlement, becoming the second permanent settlement in Canada.  Member of the Jesuits religious order established a mission in the new settlement.  Back in 1603, when Samuel de Champlain was surveying the St. Lawrence River, he had recommended establishing a permanent settlement where the Saint-Maurice River flows into the St. Lawrence River.  The first initial residents of the new Trois-Rivières settlement included:  Pierre Boucher, Jacques Le Neuf, Jean Godefroy de Lintot, Michel Le Neuf du Hérisson, François Hertel, Quentin Moral, Sieur de St. Quentin, François, Marguerie, René Robineau, and Jean Sauvaget.  Trois-Rivières played an important role in the Canadian fur trade, because of its strategic trading location near the Native tribes providing the fur pelts that were highly prized back in Europe.

In 1634, thirty-year-old Françoise Garnier sailed with Robert Giffard’s sponsored “Percheron Immigration” to the Québec settlement as the intended wife of Noël Langlois Sr.  Françoise was one of the first of 262 filles à marier women.

sections.title: Filles à marier or “Marriageable Girls”
sections.teaser: Between 1634 and 1663, 262 filles à marier or “Marriageable Girls” immigrated to Canada representing one quarter of all the single girls arriving in Canada t…

blocks.text: Between 1634 and 1663, 262 filles à marier or “Marriageable Girls” immigrated to Canada representing one quarter of all the single girls arriving in Canada through 1673.  They were recruited and chaperoned by religious groups or individuals who had to assure and account for women’s’ safety and wellbeing to the king of France.  They were promised nothing but the possibility of a better life.  The filles à marier girls were to meet the following requirements and commitments:
Must be of marriageable age (ages 12-45).
Must have immigrated before Sep 1663 (any girls later were considered “Filles du Roi).

sections.title: Must have either signed a marriage contract to get married in the Canada colony.
sections.teaser: Cannot be accompanied by both parents.

blocks.text: Cannot be accompanied by both parents.
Cannot be accompanied by or joining a selected husband.

If the filles à marier women survived the perils of the crossing the North Atlantic Ocean , they still had to live with the daily threat of death by the hands of the Iroquois native warriors.  If they survived the Iroquois, they had to deal with the hard life of subsistence farming, harsh winters spent in a log cabin that they may have had to help build, survived epidemics of smallpox, plagues and the difficulties of often dangerous childbirths.  The “filles à marier” women chose to immigrate under perilous conditions to a wilderness of the Canada colony because the advantages offered by the colony were great enough to make them forget the dangers of the crossing and the rude character of colonial life.  In France, the girls had little or no choice in their marriages because marriages were arranged.  Many young French girls were placed in convent schools to await a marriage in which they had no choice.  In Canada, these women could choose whom they married and had the freedom to change their minds before the marriage took place.  Most of the “filles à marier” women belonged to the rural class and many were unwanted daughters of single women.  Their average age was 22, and more than one-third had lost at least one of their parents.  Of the 262 “filles à marier” women Nineteen (19) were members of Mary Ann Goodwater’s Branch of Winifred Elois Coss’ Family tree.
In 1634, Noël Langlois Sr. left France and sailed to the Quebec settlement as part of the Robert Giffard’s “Percheron Immigration” to the Quebec settlement in Canada.

On 25 Jul 1634, Noël Langlois Sr. and Françoise Garnier, a “filles à marier”, were married in the church of Our-Lady of Reconveyance, by Jesuit priest Father Charles Lallemant in the Quebec settlement.  They were the fourth Christian marriage in the Canada colony.

In 1634, fourteen-year-old Hélène Desportes sailed back to her place of birth, the Québec  settlement, with her husband Abraham Martin and their twenty-year-old daughter Anne Martin accompanied by Hélène’s aunt Marguerite (Langlois).  Hélène’s father Pierre Desportes and mother Francoise (Langlois) had both died in France after they had been forced by the English to leave the Québec settlement.

Note:  From the beginning of the Québec settlement, tensions existed between the Roman Catholics and the Protestant Huguenots settlers in Canada.  Back in France the Huguenots were generally better off financially and were hated for their economic successes.  The seaports of Dieppe and La Rochelle were Huguenot strongholds.  The protestant seafarers from these French ports enjoyed a life of freedom and independence not available to the rest of the French Catholic people.

Note:  The French people that sailed to the New World wanted to transplant the legacy of their own civilization.  The French settlers organized their colonies under the French King’s sponsorship filled with the spirit of order and guided by rigid norms French culture.  The settlers’ imported models and practices into Canada with no thought of adapting to the local conditions.  Instead of using logs to build log cabins, the French cut squared lumber poles connected with wooden dowels.  The French built a better home than the English, but spent much greater effort in the construction process.

In 1634, while living with the Huron native people Jean Nicolet leaned about a tribe known as the Winnebago or “People of the Sea”, whom he believed might help him find the long sought Northwest Passage to the Orient and its riches.  In the summer of 1634 Jean Nicolet canoed down French River from Lake Nipissing to Lake Huron, one of the Great Lakes.  On the way he recruited an escort of seven Huron native men.  The group paddled their canoes west passing through the Mackinaw straits and entering the large Lake Superior.  They continued following the shoreline until they reached Green Bay on the west side on Lake Superior.  Jean Nicolet and his small group of Huron native men met with the Native Winnebago people living in the Green Bay area.  In the meeting with the Winnebago’s Jean Nicolet wore a bright damask silk robe and caried two gold pistols Jean was taking as gifts for the Emperor of China, once he found the “Northwest Passage” to the Orient.  Jean momentarily struck terror into the Winnebago people, who took him for a god.  See on the next page a painting of the first encounter with the Winnebago native people:

blocks.caption: In 1634, while living with the Huron native people Jean Nicolet leaned about a tribe known as the Winnebago or “Peopl…

blocks.text: Jean Nicolet assembled about 4,000 people of the different native tribes in the Green Bay region, and shared long-stemmed peace pipes, with which they concluded a peace agreement.  Jean next continued from Green Bay down the Fox River to Lake Winnebago and the Winnebago village of Mascouten, which was just a short walking distance from the Wisconsin River, a tributary of the Mississippi River, which later French explores would lean was a route to the center of North America and Gulf of Mexico.

Note:  The map on the next page of Wisconsin shows the interconnecting rivers systems in the area.  The left side of the map is the Mississippi River that separates Minnesota and Wisconsin.  The Mississippi River travels south down to the Gulf of Mexico.  On the way it merges with the Missouri River that flows from the Rocky Mountains out in Montana.  Looking closely at the Wisconsin River map one can see how close the Fox River and the Wisconsin River come to each other just above Lake Mendota.  By carrying a canoe over the short mile and half marshy gap between the two rivers one can travel from the Atlantic Ocean to the Gulf of Mexico or up the Missouri River out to the Rocky Mountains by boat.  Before the development of roads travel by boat was the only way one could carry any amount of goods from one place to another.  Jean Nicolet discovery of the interconnecting river systems was the key to France’s explorations of North America.  The Arrow on the map points to the short gap at Portage Wisconsin.
