Note: The map on the next page of Wisconsin shows the interconnecting rivers systems in the area. The left side of th…

blocks.text: On 1 Oct 1634, after arriving back in the Québec settlement, Hélène Desportes married Guillaume Hébert, who had stayed in the Québec settlement when it was under English control.
In 1634, Anne Antoinette de Liercourt was born to Philippe de Liercourt and Jeanne (Patin) in Beauvais, Hauts-de-France, France.
In 1634, Louis Marier was born to Louis Marier and Marguerite (Peigne) in Saint-Symphorien-de-Tours, Centre-Val de Loire, France.
Note:  In 1634 the first recorded case of the disease of “measle”, occurred in a Native Huron village.  It is believed the deadly disease arrived with the new French settlers’ children.

In 1635, Mathieu Abraham Côté, his wife Francoise Genevieve (Loisel) and their children, including twenty-five-year-old son Jean Côté left Mortagne, Perche, France and sailed to the Québec settlement in Canada.

On 17 Nov 1635, Jean Côté and Anne Martin were married in the home of Robert Gillard by a Jesuit Priest in the Québec settlement in Canada.  Anne Martin was one of the 262 French women who had signed up to be a “filles à marier” women and had sailed on the ship "Le Sallemande" with her widower father Abraham Martin.  Unlike the others that were recruited by Robert Gillard, Jean Côté was not a trained skilled craftsman.  The fact that Jean Côté was able to marry shortly after his arrival indicates he was not under a three-year employment contract with Robert Gillard.
Note:  On 25 Dec 1635, (Christmas Day) Samuel de Champlain died.  Samuel had led the Canada colonial effort from the beginning and he was now gone.

In 1635, Abraham Martin was given twelve acres of land by the “Company of One Hundred Associates” and another fourteen acres ten years later.  The combined land was on the upper portion of the Québec settlement, on Saint Genevieve Hill.  The land will later become the “Plains of Abraham” where in 1759 France lost Québec Colony to the English when English General Wolfe defeated the Marquis de Montcalm ending the “French and Indian War”.
Note:  In 1636, Charles Huault de Montmagny arrived in the Québec settlement replacing Samuel de Champlain as the new Governor of the Canada Colony.

Note:  On 15 Jan 1636, the “Company of One-hundred Associates” granted seigneury (property of a Lord) of the Îie Jésus Island to Jesuit Fathers.  The Jesuit’s soon lost interest in the Îie Jésus Island and focuses their work on establishing the Ville-Marie settlement on the larger Île de Montréal Island.
On 13 Feb 1636, Jean Pichet was born to Pierre Pichet and Anne Marie (Pineau) in Poitou-Charentes, Nouvelle-Aquitaine, France.
On 3 Jun 1636, Gervais le Normand and Lêonarde Élênore Joinault were married in Igé, Normandy, France.
On 15 Aug 1636, Jeanne Rousseliere was born to Louis Rousseliere and Isabelle (Pariset) in Möeze, Nouvelle-Aquitaine, France.

In Aug 1636, Jean Côté was granted a lot on the Grande-Allee, a boulevard, which today is considered the “Champs Elysees” of Quebec City by the new Governor Montmagny of Quebec.  (Champs Elysees is the main street in Paris France that runs under the Arc de Triomphe).  Jean did not accept the governor’s offer and instead settled in the Beauport community established by Robert Giffard, east of Quebec City.  Out in the open country there was constant fear of being attack and killed by Native warriors while working in one’s field or at home at night.  For this reason, Jean Côté was offered by his friend Noël Langlois Sr., who had earlier received prime Beauport land from Robert Giffard, a portion of Noël land to build a home near Noël family residence for their mutual protection.

In 1636, Saintes Cloutier (Dupont) arrived from Mortagne, Perche, France with her seventeen-year-old son Pierre and ten-year-old daughter Anne Cloutier in the Québec settlement, where they joined up with Saintes’ husband and the children’s father Zacharie Cloutier, who had immigrated two years earlier with their oldest son Jean to the Québec settlement.
In 1636, Suzanne Debure was born to Vincent Debure and Suzanne (Golin) in Rouen, Normandy, France.
In 1636, Jeanne Mignon was born to François Mignon and Marie (Bélanger in La Rochelle, Normandy, France.
In 1636, Charles Garnier was born to Guillaume Garnier and Françoise Charlotte (Deschallais) in Tournebu, Normandy, France.
On 3 Feb 1637, Zacharie Cloutier took formal possession of his land that fulfilled the contract he had sign when agreeing to come with Robert Giffard to Canada.
On 17 Apr 1637, Jean Normand was born to Gervais le Normand and Lênarde Élênore (Joinault) in Mortagne, Normandy, France.
On 17 May 1637, Jacques Gaudry died in Perche, France leaving his wife Charlotte (Chevalier) a widow with seven-year-old son Nicolas Gaudry and one-year-old son Jacques.

On 29 Jun 1637, after honoring their agreement, Robert Giffard, conceded to Noël Langlois Sr. and Francoise (Garnier), 253 acres of land in Beauport, just west of the Montmorency Falls where Noël was employed as a “carpenter” and engaged in farming and the lumber business while also worked as a “river navigator” when needed.  Together Noël and Francoise had ten children.  See below a photo of the large Montmorency Falls today:

blocks.caption: On 29 Jun 1637, after honoring their agreement, Robert Giffard, conceded to Noël Langlois Sr. and Francoise (Garnier)…

blocks.text: On 7 Jul 1637, Marie Lambert was born to Jean Joseph Lambert and Marie (Membertou) in Vernoux-en-Gatine, Nouvelle-Aquitaine, France.

On 12 Jul 1637, Robert Drouin and eleven-year-old Anne Cloutier, were married in the Québec settlement, Canada.  Because of her young age Anne remained living with her parents until she was fourteen.  Soon after his marriage Robert Drouin began to buy land on the northern shore of the St. Lawrence River in the parish (county) of Chateau Richer.  Robert made bricks for “Hotel Dieu”, the hospital in Québec settlement and many other of the buildings in settlement.

On 23 May 1637, Jean Nicolet ended his exploring activities and settled in the Trois-Rivières settlement and work as a “clerk” for the “Company of One-hundred Associates”.  Jean had brought his half native “Métis” Nippissing daughter Euphrosine Madeleine Nicolet with him.  Jean received a grant of 160 acres of land in the outskirts of the Trois-Rivières village.  A few months later Canada Governor Charles de Montmagny ennobled Jean Nicolet as Sieur de Belleborne and awarded him a concession of land where the grand “Le Chateau Frontenac Hotel” now is located in Québec City.  See photo of the “Le Chateau Frontenac Hotel” today below:

blocks.caption: On 23 May 1637, Jean Nicolet ended his exploring activities and settled in the Trois-Rivières settlement and work as…

blocks.text: On 7 Oct 1637, thirty-year-old Jean Nicolet and eleven-year-old Marguerite Couillard were married in the Québec settlement.  Why Jean Nicolet did not bring his native Nippissing partner Jeanne Bahmahmaadjimiwin Giisis, who was the mother of their nine-year-old Métis daughter Euphrosine Madeleine Nicolet with him is unknown.  Jeanne while they were living in the Nipissing Indian village had worked as a clerk, interpreter and intermediary for wealthy French merchants that visited the village.  Most likely she did not come with Jean because she did not want to leave her home and her Nipissing people.

On 12 Jul 1637, François Bélanger and thirteen-year-old Marie Guyon, were married in the Québec settlement, Canada.  François and Marie and her parents had sailed together with Robert Giffard in 1634 from Perche, France.  François and Marie settled in Robert Giffard’s seigneury of Beaupré, which was east of Château-Richer at the mouth of the Sainte-Anne-du-Nord River.
On 6 Aug 1637, Catherine Fortier was born to Jean Fortier and Julienne (Coeffes) in La Rochelle, Normandy, France.
In 1637, sixteen-year-old Marie Crevet left Normandy France and sailed as one of the 262 “filles à marier” women to the Québec settlement in Canada to marry.
On 25 Oct 1637, Robert Caron and Marie Crevet, a “Filles á marier” were married in Notre-Dame, Québec settlement, Canada.
In 1637, Sébastien Gingras was born to Hilaire Gingras and Françoise Louis (Saint-Lo) in Fontenay-le-Comte, Pays de la Loire, France.

In Mar 1637, Jean Guyon his wife Mathurine (Robin) and their children received the promise 845 acres land in nearby Buisson River from Robert Gillard fulfilling the agreement they had made back in Perche France.  Jean Guyon assumed the noble surname of “Sieur du Buisson”.  He was a “mason” by trade but also farmed his new land and served as a “notary”.  He drew up the first marriage contract in the new Québec colony for Robert Drouin and Anne Cloutier a year earlier in 1636.

In 1637, Zacharie Cloutier his wife Saintes (Dupont) and their children took possession of 845 acres land in Beauport fulfilling the agreement they made back in Perche France with Robert Gifford who had recruited them to immigrate to Canada.  Beauport is 3 miles down the St. Lawrence River where the Montmorency River drops over a magnificent water fall into the St. Lawrence River.  Zacharie was a “master carpenter”.

Note:  In 1637, the smallpox disease arrived in the Native Huron village.  Smallpox was the most destructive disease brought to North American by Europeans.  There were a variety of infectious diseases existing in North America prior to the arrival of Christopher Columbus, however the fact that the native people were a single race, limited in total number and there was only a small number of domestic animals living with the Native people, which could carry diseases, had limited the Native population contact with major disease epidemics.  The fact that there was also a limited interactions between the Native American native populations (as compared to areas of Europe and Asia (Eurasia), help limit the spread in disease in North America.  Eurasia (Europe and Asia) on the other hand was a crossroads among many distant, different races of peoples separated by hundreds and sometimes thousands of miles.  Repeated warfare by invading populations and trade between different distant groups of people throughout the Eurasia lead to a wide spread infectious diseases.  As a result of chronic exposure to the various diseases, the of people in Eurasia who survived gradually developed natural immunity (protection) for the diseases.  The early Europeans that came to the New World carried their deadly endemic diseases with them, which killed an estimated 80 to 90% of the North American native people . This impact of the deadly Old-World diseases on the people in the New World people was inevitable, if it had not come from Europe, it would have come from Asia.  The New World could not have stay isolated once sea going ship were developed.
On 23 Jan 1638, Françoise Hébert was born to Guillaume Hébert and Hélène (Desportes) in the Québec settlement.

On 11 Jun 1638, Louis Gagne and Marie Michel were married in St Martin, Igé, Perche, France.  Louis had learned to read and write and worked as a “miller” (a person who operated a mill, with a rock grinding machine that gowned grain seeds into flour) in Guemansais, France.  Louis and Marie had two children while they were living in Igé, France.
On 26 Jul 1638, Anne Lemoine was born to Pierre Lemoine and Judith (Duchesse) in Dieppe, Normandy, France.
On 12 Aug 1638, Louis Robert was born to Andre Robert and Catherine (Bonin) in La Rochelle, Normandy, France.
In 1638, Nicolas Bélanger was born to François Bélanger and Marie (Guyon) in the Québec settlement in Canada.
In 1638, Paul de Rainville and Pauline Rolline Poête were married in St. Thomas church in Touques, Normandy, France.
On 12 Jul 1639, Martin Adroche Côté was born to Jean Côté and Anne (Martin) in the Québec settlement in Canada.  The mother’s madden name was used for their son’s first name.
On 3 Sep 1639, Marguerite Langlois was born to Noël Langlois Sr. and Françoise (Garnier) in the Québec settlement in Canada.

On 23 Sep 1639, thirty-five-year-old Guillaume Hébert died in the Québec settlement, leaving his wife Hélène (Desportes) alone to raise a two-year-old son Joseph, a one-year-old son Françoise Hébert and a new one-month-old daughter Angelique.  Guillaume and Hélène had been married only five years when Guillaume died.  The reason for Guillaume’s death was not recorded.  When French pioneers died from disease, this cause was rarely mentioned in their death records.  Robert Giffard, the colony’s doctor was treating patients at the Québec settlement hospital at the time for smallpox and Guillaume was helping Robert and probably contacted smallpox.
In 1639, Laurent Migneron was born to Pierre Migneron and Marie (Plosson) in Chizé, Nouvelle-Aquitaine, France.

In 1639, twenty-three-year-old Noël Morin arrived from Saint-Etienne, Auvergne-Rhone-Alpes, France in the Québec settlement.  Noël was a “cartwright” (someone who makes and repairs carts and wagons) a trade he learned from his father Claude Morin while growing up in France.  Noël was able to sign his name.

In 1639, Guillaume Couillard built a flourmill and was appointed the “inspector” to assess the status of the Canada’s farms and their ability to feed the growing number of settlers in the colony and the new settlers that were arriving.
On 27 Dec 1639, a marriage contract for Noël Morin and Hélène Desportes was signed in the Québec settlement before Martial Piraube.

On 9 Jan 1640, Noël Morin and twenty-year-old Hélène Desportes were married in the Québec settlement, Canada.  Noël made his living as a “boiler maker” someone making large metal kettles or boilers in the Québec settlement.  Noël gave his new bride a dowry of 200 livres.  Hélène brought a house measuring 24 by 18 feet, located near the church of Notre-Dame in Québec settlement, with 1.7 acres of land near Mont-Carmel and a garden measuring 40 Perches (1/4 acre) to the new marriage, plus her three small children.
In 1640, thirty-three-year-old Nicolas Bonhomme left his home in Rouen, Normandy, France and sailed to French Canada.
In 1640, twenty-eight-year-old Catherine Gouge signed up to be a “Filles á marier” and left her home and in Thury, Normandy, France and sailed over the Québec settlement in Canada.
On 2 Sep 1640, Nicolas Bonhomme and Catherine Gouge were married in the Québec settlement, Canada.
In 1640, Michel Messier was born to David Messier and Marie (Barre) in Rouen, Normandy, France.
In 1640, Jean Carrier was born to unknown parents in Saintes, Nouvelle-Aquitaine, France.
In 1640, Jean Baptiste Halay and Mathurine Valet were married in Chartres-de-Bretagne, Brittany, France.

In 1640, Robert Drouin received a contract to make 7,000 bricks to be delivered in Beauport landing on the St. Lawrence River near his workshop.  Robert was to be paid two poincons (casks holding about 90 gallons) of wheat.  The order help Robert and his family, but was not enough to fully support them.  Robert also built a dry dock for his neighbor Martin Grouvel’s boat and installed plank flooring, clear land and planted fields for other neighbors.
In 1640 Catherine Hurault was born to Jean Hurault in La Flèche, Pays de la Loire, France.

On 21 Jan 1641, Agnés Morin was born to Noël Morin and Hélène (Desportes) in the Québec settlement in Canada.  Agnés was the first of twelve children Hélène and Noël will raise.  With the three children from her first marriage and her additional twelve more children, Hélène with the help of her aunt Marguerite (Matin) earned the profession of “sage-femme” a French “midwife” (a woman trained to assist women in childbirth).
In 1641, thirty-seven-year-old Raymond Pagé and twenty-five-year-old Madeleine Bergeron were married Quierzy, Hauts-de-France, France.
In 1641, Jacques Vezina and Marie Boisdon were married in La Rochelle, Normandy, France.
In 1641 Jean Côté and Noël Langlois Sr. sold 500 bales of hay to the “Company of Canada”, indicating they were both operating as successful farmers.
In 1641, Marie Godard was born to Robert Godard and Antoinette (Grandpierre) in Mortagne, Perche, France.
In 1641, Jacques Lareau and Anne Fosse were married in Rouen, Normandy, France.
In 1641, Anne Bellesoeur was born to Germain Bellesoeur and Marie Barbe (Lucas) in Paris, Île-de-France, France.
In 1641, twenty-one-year-old Marie Marguerie arrived in the Québec settlement as one of the 262 “filles à marier” women who came to the Québec settlement in Canada to marry.
On 23 Aug 1641 Marie Marguerie married Jacques Hertel in the Trois-Rivières community.

The local Canada seigneuries (manors) were aligned (connected) to the area’s Catholics parishes.  The map on the next page shows the Drouin family living in the Chateau-Richard Parish of Beauport and the Côté family living further east in the Ste-Anne Parish of Beaupré.  The layout of the farms in the Beaupré coast established a pattern that used through French Québec on land boarding a river bank or ocean coastal shore.  The individual family farms lots were narrow, but deep.  The family farm house was built near the water so the distance between neighbors was not too great and there was easy access to the water for transportation of farm products and people.  In the summer people traveled by canoe or by walking along the beach; in winter they walked in snowshoes.  The closeness of neighbors helped families joined together to fight off Iroquois warrior attacks, which occurred frequently.

The local Canada seigneuries (manors) were aligned (connected) to the area’s Catholics parishes. The map on the next…
The local Canada seigneuries (manors) were aligned (connected) to the area’s Catholics parishes. The map on the next…

blocks.text: In 1641, Robert Drouin and six other families including Jean Côté’s family had completed their contract period with Robert Giffard and had become dissatisfied with Robert Giffard’s unwellness to allow other families to use the rich lowland along the St. Lawrence River to graze their livestock.  Robert Drouin, Jean Robert Drouin, Jean Côté and the others soon left Beauport and moved on to the new Beaupré seigneury which was being establish further east on the north side of the St. Lawrence River across from the Île d'Orléans Island.

sections.title: Ville Marie
sections.teaser: In 1641, Jeanne Mance a French nurse funded the recruitment of forty-eight immigrants in France to build a new settlement in Canada and baptize the native pe…

blocks.text: In 1641, Jeanne Mance a French nurse funded the recruitment of forty-eight immigrants in France to build a new settlement in Canada and baptize the native people in the area.  Paul de Maisonneuve Chomedey, a Catholic priest, was hired to led the recruited group.  After leaving France and spending a year in the Quebec settlement, the forty immigrants, led by Paul Chomedey, left the Quebec settlement and traveled 160 miles up the St. Lawrence River and started a new missionary settlement, they named “Ville Marie” at the confluence of the St. Lawrence and Ottawa River, which forms a large island called “Ile de Montréal”.  In 1642, a fortress was quickly built and the Ville Marie settlement soon became a center for the fur trade and French expansion into Canada.
In 1642, Louis Gesseron Sr. and Marie Guitard were married in La Rochelle, Normandy, France.

On 27 Oct 1642, forty-four-year-old Jean Nicolet canoed from Lake Nipissing 600 miles across Lake Huron and Lake Superior to Lake Winnebago in Wisconsin.  On his returned canoe trip Jean drowned in the St. Lawrence River when his boat capsized in a sudden windstorm.
On 7 Dec 1642, Louis Gesseron Jr. was born to Louis Gesseron Sr. and Marie (Guitard) in La Rochelle, Normandy, France.
In 1642, Louis Lefébvre was born to Vincent Lefebvre and Suzanne (Golin) in Rouen, Normandy, France.
In 1642, Jean Besset was born to Jean Bessette and Madeleine (Surget) in Cahors, Occitania, France.
In 1642, Francois Allard was born to Jacques Allard and Marie (Frérot) in Blacqueville, Normandy, France.

Note:  The French had been generally successful in establishing good relations with the Native American population starting with their first settlements in Acadia in back in 1604 and Québec in 1608.  The French sought out the native people with missionary efforts and trading posts.  The French worked to learn the native languages and customs that allowed them to effectively communicate with the native people.  The small number of French settlers compared to the large waves of English immigrants that were arriving in the English colonies made the French appear to be a much smaller threat to the native people’s way of life.  The French formed long-lasting alliances with the native peoples of Acadia, the St. Lawrence River Valley, the Ottawa Valley and the Great Lakes.  Often the French were called upon to help manage disputes among their native allies, and they were frequently successful.  The process, however, became entangled in a series of wars with the Native Iroquois Confederation.  The Iroquois were longtime adversaries of several of the French Native American allied tribes and also sought to  control of the North American fur trade.  Between 1640 and 1701, the French and the Iroquois Confederation were at war much of the time.

sections.title: Iroquois Fight Back
sections.teaser: In 1643, the Iroquois Confederation began attacking, using guerrilla raids (quick surprise attack against a larger force and then a quick escape), on the new…

blocks.text: In 1643, the Iroquois Confederation began attacking, using guerrilla raids (quick surprise attack against a larger force and then a quick escape), on the new Ville Marie and the Trois-Rivières settlements.  The Iroquois had recently received a large number of Dutch firearms, they called “fire sticks” making them a much more formable adversary.  The Canada Colony had about 350 inhabitants at the time and the “Iroquois Five Nation Confederation” had more than 12,000 members, including 2,200 highly disciplined warriors.  The Iroquois seized control of the fur trade and blocked French access to the interior of North America.  The Iroquois also prevented other native tribes from trading with the French.  From 1645 to 1665 the Canada colony and its settlements were under constant attacks.  The Canada settlers could not fish, hunt, clear land or plough their fields without fear of being ambushed.  About 10% of the Canada population and most of its livestock were killed during this twenty-year period.

In May 1643, Jean Dumay left his wife Miotte (Lacombe) in France and took his two sons, seventeen-year-old Etienne Sr. and fifteen-year-old Andre and sailed on the ship “Espêrance” from Dieppe, Normandy, France and arrived in mid-August in the Québec settlement in Canada.  Jean Dumay worked for Robert Giffard when he arrived in the Québec settlement, helping other new settlers as they arrived get settled in their new homes and community.

In 1643, Jean Leblanc sailed from France and arrived in the Québec settlement to work on a three-year of service contract as a domestic servant for Guillaume Couillard and his second wife Marguerite Couillard.  Guillaume who had partnered with a Nipissing Native women was the father of a “Métis” daughter Euphrosine Madeleine Nicolet

On 21 Nov 1643, Jean Leblanc and Euphrosine Madeleine Nicolet, (a “Métis” the daughter) of Guillaume Couillard, were married in Québec settlement, Canada.  Jean Leblanc began working as a domestic servants for Guillaume Couillard.
In 1643, Vincent Chrétien was born to Jacques Chrétien and Catherine (Niverd) in Tours, Centre-Val de Loire, France.
In 1643, Jean Baptiste Bourgery and Marie Gendre were married in Aunis, Nouvelle-Aquitaine, France.

In 1643, Guillaume Couillard built a “Lime Kiln” that was used to convert limestone into lime by baking limestone at high temperature.  The lime was used to make cement, concrete and mortar and was also used as a soil additive to increase the productivity of the farmlands.
In 1643, Jacques Ménard sailed from Rennes, Brittany, France to the Québec settlement in Canada.

In 1643, Mathieu Choret signed a three-year contract in France to go and work for the Jesuits in Canada.  He sailed from La Rochelle France and arrived in the Quebec settlement and then moved on and settled in Trois-Rivières, where he worked as a “domestic servant” for the Jesuits.  Mathieu worked for the Jesuits for three years earning 100 livres each year.

In 1644, the small settlement of Ville Marie was struggling to survive the repeated Iroquois warrior attacks.  To help the situation the French settlers brought large guard dogs from France that had an extraordinary knack of detecting and tracking down Iroquois warriors hiding in the forests.  The leader of the pack dogs was named “Pilote” and she would cry out and howl, and the other dogs would quickly follow, anytime Iroquois warriors neared the Ville Marie settlement.  The arrival of the guard dogs alerted the settlers in time to respond to the guerrilla attacks the Iroquois warriors employed against the settlement.
On 26 May 1644, Léonard Pilote and Denise Gauthier were married in La Rochelle, Normandy, France.

In 1644, Louis Gagne and his wife Marie (Michel) left Perche France and sailed to the Québec settlement and then settled in the Château-Richer settlement for two years, before moving on to Beaupré.  Louis worked on a farm as an indentured servant to pay for his and his wife Marie’s trip from France.  His service was complete on 31 Mar 1653.  Louis next worked as a “ploughman” (someone who plough land with metal plough pulled by horses or oxen) by trade and later became a “business leader” of the firm “St-Charles”.
In 1644, Marie de Rainville was born to Paul de Rainville and Pauline Rolline (Poête) in Touques, Normandy, France.
About 1644, Pierre Saint-Denis and Vivienne Brunel were married in Dieppe, Normandy, France.

From 1644 to 1647, François Bêlanger and Masse Gravel worked a concession (land given to them by the Governor) together farming on the Beaupré coast.  In 1647, Masse Gravel left the farm.  Later on, 24 May 1655, the two settled up and François bought Masse's half of the farm and land for the sum of one hundred livres per arpent, to be paid in 10 installments of 116 livres 6 sols 8 deniers (for a total of 350 livres), each due 24 June, Saint-Jean-Baptiste Day.  François secured this debt by pledging all of his property as well as the present and future inheritance of his children.  On 23 Mar 1660, Masse Gravel declared that his former partner had paid up in full and owed him nothing more.  In this same year, both of them became “church wardens” in the parish of Chateau-Richer.  The first notarized act found which includes François was drawn up on 8 Sep 1647 by Claude Lecoustre.  François was required to pay Pierre Legardeur of Repentigny the sum of one hundred livres for the purchase of some wheat.  To guarantee the loan, he again put up all of his property as security.  Based on the major land transactions François was involved in it is clear he had given up the mason trade and was spending all his time farming.
On 21 Mar 1645, Marie Anne Leclerc was born to Jean Leclerc and Marie (Blanquet) in Dieppe, Normandy, France.

On 10 Apr 1645, Jean Dumay contracted to purchase 5 acres of frontage property along the St. Lawrence River from Claude Estienne.  For unknown reasons Jean Dumay on 8 Oct 1646, terminated the land contract .

On 26 Apr 1645, Noël Morin and Hélène (Desportes) were granted by Governor Montmagny, 42 acres of land on the outskirts of the Upper Town in the Québec settlement in a place called “Le Cocteau Sainte-Geneviève”.  Noël built a two-room 20 ft. by 30 ft house with two heated rooms, a cellar and attic.  A shop was added as a separate structure and a barn and stable were also built.  A family garden was created and fifty acres of the land was cultivated and then farmed.  The Morin’s homestead was a short distance from the Québec Church of Notre-Dame.  The Noël Morin and Hélène’s children were taught by the Jesuits and the Ursuline’s (Catholic order of nuns).

In 1645, Robert Giffard officially granted Jean Côté the land he had been living on for the past ten-years.  The property had a 191 yards feet wide frontage the St. Lawrence River and extending back four and half miles into the back boundary of Robert Giffard’s seigneury.  At this time Jean Côté acquire a lot in the upper Québec settlement adjacent to the church of Notre-Dame.  The lot was 150 feet wide and 60 feet deep, located at the corner of Rue Tresor Street and Rue Baude Street in the Québec settlement, which today is located in the  heart of today’s Québec bustling tourist area.  See a photo on the next page of the corner location in Québec City today:

blocks.caption: In 1645, Robert Giffard officially granted Jean Côté the land he had been living on for the past ten-years. The prope…

blocks.text: In 1645, Jean Gautier was born in Blancafort, Tarragona, France (an autonomous community of Catalonia, Spain).
In 1645, Barbe Halay was born to Jean Baptiste Halay and Mathurine (Valet) in Chartres-de-Bretagne, Brittany, France.
On 6 Mar 1646, Marie-Jeanne Anguille was born to Michel L Anguille and Etiennette Toucheraine in Touraine, Centre-Val de Loire, France.

On 31 Oct 1646, Mathieu Choret completed his three-year contract working for the Jesuits in Trois-Rivières and sailed back to La Rochelle, France.  His purpose in returning to France was to seek a wife and then return back to Canada.
In 1646, Marie Lelong was born to Mathurin Lelong and Perrine (Morel) in Rennes, Brittany, France.

On 18 Jul 1646, nineteen-year-old Jean Guay left Berneuil, Nourvelle-Aquitaine, France and sailed out of La Rochelle on the ship “Cardinal” to Canada to work in the service of the Jesuits in their work with the Hurons Native people.  Upon his arrival he was taken in by Jesuits of the Québec settlement where he remained for two-years using his woodworking carpenter skills to make various wooden items requested by the Jesuits.

In 1646, Jean Dumay who had been working as a coordinator helping new French settlers when they arrived in the Quebec settlement get established in their new homes sailed back to his wife Miotte (Lacombe) France, leaving his two grown sons Etienne Sr. and Andre in Canada.  Jean Dumay stayed in France living until age of ninety-five.

In 1646, in the “Corpus Christi Day” (God’s Day) parade in the Québec settlement, the two oldest settlement tradesmen Jean Guyon and Zacharie Cloutier led a procession of bell-ringing marchers in front of a banner whose bearer wore a hat of flowers.  The Cross that followed was carried by a twenty-year-old man wearing an “alb” and a “stole”.  On each side were two children in surplices and stoles.  They were followed by six men carrying torches that represented the tradesmen of the colony: carpenters, masons, sailors, toolmakers, brewers and bakers.  Jean Guyon placed a shield on his torch featuring the tools of his trade: a hammer, compass and ruler.  See below a man wearing an “alb” and “stole”:

blocks.caption: In 1646, in the “Corpus Christi Day” (God’s Day) parade in the Québec settlement, the two oldest settlement tradesmen…

blocks.text: In 1646, Mathieu Gervais was born to Pierre Gervais and Catherine (Saillard).  Pierre was a “baker” in Saint-Maur-des-Fosses, Île-de-France, France.
In 1646, Charles Guillebourg sailed from Perche France and arrived in the Québec settlement, Canada.

On 1 Apr 1647, a seigniory was granted to the order of Jesuits by the “Company of One Hundred Associates” that was given the name “La Prairie”.  The land was on the south side of the St. Lawrence River across from the island of Île de Montréal.
On 3 Feb 1647, Jeanne Drouin was born to Robert Drouin and Anne (Cloutier) in Chateau Richer in Québec, Canada.

On 26 Feb 1647, in La Rochelle, France Mathieu Choret and Sébastienne Veillon were joined together with their parents and friends, at Pierre Rouyer’s, to sign their marriage contract in front of the royal notary Pierre Teuleron and his two clerks.  Shortly after their wedding Mathieu and Sébastienne left La Rochelle and sailed to the Québec settlement in Canada.  The passage for Mathieu and Sébastienne was 75 livres, which was nearly a man’s yearly wages, which provided only minimum service on the voyage.  Mathieu and Sébastienne stocked up on the least perishable and most nutritive foods to carry with them on the long ocean trip.

On 25 Jun 1647, Raymond Pagé with his wife Madeleine (Bergeron) and their two sons left Quierzy, Hauts-de-France, France and sailed to the Québec settlement in Canada.  Raymond and Madeleine had been recruited to go the Canada by Father Pierre Bailloquet, a Jesuit Priest from Bordeaux France.  Raymond, Madeleine and Father Pierre Bailloquet sailed together on the same ship, which also carried the first horse to the Canada Colony.  Raymond Pagé and his family settled in the Québec settlement where he was very active in property transactions: buying, renting, and trading properties in the Québec settlement using his own and borrow money.

On 5 Aug 1647, Pierre Tremblay left Mortagne, Perche, France and sailed to the Québec settlement in Canada.  Pierre had enlisted to go to Canada for 75 livres a year for 3 years.  Pierre made his living as a “cultivator” (someone who tills and plows farmland), which was the profession of his father back in France.  Below is a painting of the growing Québec settlement, the fort up on the hill, the upper and lower city area.

blocks.caption: On 5 Aug 1647, Pierre Tremblay left Mortagne, Perche, France and sailed to the Québec settlement in Canada. Pierre ha…

sections.title: Ocean Voyage
sections.teaser: In the 1600s, sailing in a ship from France to Canada was a 3,600-mile-long journey, which normally took about nine weeks to complete, depending on the winds…

blocks.text: In the 1600s, sailing in a ship from France to Canada was a 3,600-mile-long journey, which normally took about nine weeks to complete, depending on the winds.  Taking into account the most favorable navigational seasons for sailing , it was best to set sail from France before May 1 and sail back from Québec before the end of September.  Sailing from France west to Canada always took longer than sailing back to France.  The ocean winds blew from the east an average 100 days a year and blew from the west an average 260 days a year.  The ships were not very large; they varied from 122 feet to 164 feet in length and 26 feet to 49 feet across.  Generally, they carried between100 and 300 passengers on a trip.  For the captain and a few dignitaries, there were a few cabins, but for the regular passengers there was only one the steerage area that was normally 5 feet 2 inches high, allowing only the shortest to walk upright and there were no reserved family or personal areas.  The luckiest passengers slept in hammocks, the others on the floor.  The steerage area was a very dark area with no windows and no candles because of fire danger.  The air in the steerage area air was cold, humid and rancid with an abominable stink.  Personal hygiene was impossible.  Buckets were used for toilets.  At night, those in hammocks were up off the hard floor, but had to deal with rolling of the waves.  The ship was full rats and seasick people, real sick people and dying people.  A partition separated the passenger side of the steerage area from stable area where the animals going to the Canada settlements were kept.  The odors and the noises coming from the animals added to the confusion and misery of the voyage.  The ship had no dining area; passengers had to eat where they were when it was mealtime.  There also was no heating system on the ship.  A single stove was used for the cooking, if the ship was not shaken too much, otherwise the food was served cold.  The steerage area was usually overcrowded with people and luggage, forcing everyone to literally walk on and over each other for weeks.  In the area below the steerage area, there were wicker baskets filled with stones that were used as ballast to limit the ship’s roll, large drinking water barrels and gun powder barrels, which were needed for the ship’s guns to protect the ship from pirates during the voyage.

During the nine week trip the passengers lived together in a confused jumble, a place marked by disorderly accommodations, where the luggage of the passengers and the various articles to be delivered to the colony were all stored.  There were barrels of food for the trip in the steerage area.  The maritime law required the ship to carry 60 rations per person and water barrels (66 liters).  The ship’s menu was:  "sailor biscuits", bacon, peas, salted cod or dried haring, olive oil, butter, mustard, vinegar, wine, brandy and candied fruits.  If one wanted anything else, they had to carry it in their personal luggage.  The ship’s fresh water quickly becomes fermented and viscous (sticky) and the passengers were forced to rely on hard cider and the treat of wine on Sunday to keep hydrated.  To pass the time, the ship’s sailors sometimes organize parties on the top outside deck.  They would play music and the passengers could dance.  On calm days, the passengers were allowed to walk on the topside deck, talk among themselves, and if able read or write.  The men devote themselves to games of chance: card games, dice, chess and checkers.

The highlight distraction of the crossing was the baptism that took place when the ship reached the Newfoundland’s Grands Banks.  Here passengers who were crossing the Atlantic for the first time were initiated by being doused with a bucket of cold water poured on them.  When the ship spotted an iceberg, and the sea was calm, small boats would be launched from the ship to allow passengers to go and climb on the ice and gather pieces ice to make fresh water.  See on the next page a typical French merchant ship in the 1600s:

blocks.caption: The highlight distraction of the crossing was the baptism that took place when the ship reached the Newfoundland’s Gr…

blocks.text: Normally after about two months, the passengers would see the coast of North America for the first time, but the trip was far from over.  The Québec settlement was still very far away and the next part of the trip started when the ship entered the wide gulf of the St. Lawrence River and began perhaps the most dangers part of the trip.  Sailing up the St. Lawrence River was a very difficult navigational task.  Unexpected strong gusty winds, frequent thick fog, and many shallow rocky reefs all had to be overcome.  On top of the menacing hazard in the river its swirling choppy waters made almost everyone, even those that had not gotten seasick in the open ocean, now seasick.  The remaining voyage from Cape Breton in Nova Scotia to the Québec settlement was over 1,000 miles long and took about two weeks with favorable wind to travel.  Traveling up steam on the large powerful river required strong westerly winds the French called the “nordet winds”.

The ships normally made a stopover of a few days in “Gaspé” settlement a small village on the top of present-day New Brunswick.  The ship’s water tanks were filled with fresh water and vegetables were taken onboard.  The Gaspé settlement maintained several gardens to provide fresh food for the weary ship passengers.  When the ship left Gaspé and headed on up the St. Lawrence River the ship entered an area called the “marine cemetery”.  From Anticosti Island at the mouth of the large river up to the Québec settlement was an area where many sea tragedies occurred.  The French historian Jean La France made an inventory for the French Government and found 22 significant shipwrecks occurred in this area.  The worst disaster occurred in 23 Aug 1711, when a British fleet commanded by Rear Admiral Sir Hovenden Walker was sailing up the river to attack Québec City and British ships blundered onto the rocks of Île-aux-Oeufs.  The fleet had 19 warships and 41 support ships carrying over 11,000 men.  Over 950 men perished when the ships hit the rocks in the river.

On many voyages the ship’s passengers and cargo were let off at the village of “Tadoussac” and transferred to smaller boats that could more easily navigate the remaining 140 miles up the hazardous river.  All boats had to navigate very careful when they enter the waters just before the Île de Orléans Island, a very dangerous section of the “marine cemetery”.  When a ship or boat finally arrived at the Québec settlement it had to anchor out away from shore, in a quarantine area, while the ship was checked to see if anyone was infected by a communicable disease.  If a problem disease was discovered the ship’s passengers would be required to stay on board for up to forty additional days in quarantine.  If the ship passed the quarantine check, but still had non communicable sick passengers they were taken to the Hospital d’hôtel Dieu in the Québec settlement, for treatment until they were healthy again.

The map below is a copy of the chart Jacques-Nicolas Bellin, an engineer-hydrographer to the French King Louis XIV, made for sailing from the Newfoundland’s Grand Banks to the Quebec settlement.  Anticosti Island is shown on the right in the middle of the St. Lawrence River.

blocks.caption: The map below is a copy of the chart Jacques-Nicolas Bellin, an engineer-hydrographer to the French King Louis XIV, m…

blocks.text: In 1647, forty-seven-year-old widow Thomine Bigot (Chastel) and her children, including her daughter Francoise Bigot, left Tourouvre, Perche, France and sailed to the Québec settlement in Canada.  Thomine’s husband and father of her children, Jean Bigot, had died eight-year earlier in Canada while Thomine and the children were living in France.  Thomine’s daughter Francoise had signed up back in Perche to be a “filles à marier” woman, who was expected to marry when she arrived in Canada.  Thomine herself died on 19 Sep 1647 shortly after their arrival in the Québec settlement, leaving her 16-year-old daughter Francoise Bigot to care for her 13-year-old brother Jean and 11-year-old sister Jeanne.

On 19 Sep 1647, thirty-eight-year-old Charles Guillebourg, and sixteen-year-old Francoise Bigot were married in the Québec settlement, Canada.  Francoise Bigot was one of the 262 “filles à marier” women that sailed to Canada to marry.  Charles Guillebourg was a servant of Noël Juchereau in the Québec settlement.  Charles lived eleven more years before dying in 1658.  Francoise remarried and lived a full life dying in 1706 at age 75.
In 1647, Guillaume Couillard was one of five men who were recommended to the French King for designation as a noble.

In 1647, forty-six-year-old widow Charlotte Gaudry (Chevalier) and her son twenty-six-year-old son Nicolas Gaudry, a French soldier and her eleven-year-old son Jacques left Mortagne, Perche, France  and sailed to the Québec settlement in Canada.  Charlotte’s husband Jacques Gaudry had died ten-years earlier back in Mortagne, France.

In 1647, Mathieu Choret and his wife Sébastienne (Veillon) arrive in the Québec settlement from La Rochelle, Normandy, France.  For Mathieu this was his second trip to Canada.  On his first trip Mathieu had worked three years as a “domestic servant” for the Jesuits in the Trois-Rivières settlement.
In 1647, Jacques Samson was born to Toussaint Samson and Catherine (Chevalier) in St. Gratian, Normandy, France.
In 1647, Anne Dequain was born to Jules Dequain and Henritte (Fermills) in Sceaux, a suburb, of Paris, France.
In 1647, twenty-one-year-old Françoise Morin from Bretagne, France arrived in the Québec settlement as “Filles à Marier” who had agreed to a marriage contract before she left France.
In 1647, Gervais le Normand his wife Leonarde Elenore (Joinault) and their ten-year-old son Jean Normand left Mortagne, Normandy, France and sailed to the Québec settlement, Canada.
In 1647, Jeanne Renard was born to Etienne Renard and Marie (Louvet) in Saint-Maclou de Rouen, Normandy, France.
In 1647, Ignace Bonhomme was born to Nicholas Bonhomme and Catherine (Gouge) in the Québec settlement in Canada.

sections.title: Beaver Wars
sections.teaser: During the 1600s, the “Beaver Wars” or the “French and Iroquois Wars” occurred as a series of conflicts in the St. Lawrence River Valley and the lower Great…

blocks.text: During the 1600s, the “Beaver Wars” or the “French and Iroquois Wars” occurred as a series of conflicts in the St. Lawrence River Valley and the lower Great Lakes region of North America.  The wars pitted the Iroquois Confederation’s (Native American tribes who spoke the Iroquoian language) against the French and their Native American allied tribes (Algonquian and Hurons) that spoke the Algonquian language.  The Iroquois were a confederacy made up of the Mohawk, Oneida, Onondaga, Cayuga and Seneca tribes, who inhabited the lands in what is now upstate New York along the shores of Lake Ontario east over to Lake Champlain, Lake George and the Hudson River.  The Iroquois Confederation, was led by the Mohawk tribe.  The Iroquois had been supplied with European weapons by their Dutch and English trading partners, while the Algonquians and Hurons were supplied with arms by their French allies.

In the wars the Iroquois Confederation effectively destroyed the Algonquian speaking tribes in an extremely brutal mode of warfare.  The Iroquois did not just defeat the Algonquian speaking tribes, they exterminated (completely destroyed ) them, killing the fighting warriors, their wives and children in acts of genocide.  The result of the annihilation of the Algonquians tribes put the Iroquois Confederation in the position as the dominant Native American power in the St. Lawrence River Valley, including British New England’s western frontier and the Ohio River Valley native hunting grounds.

Between 1648 and 1649, the Iroquois tribal Confederation slaughtered about 30,000 weakened Native Hurons and tortured and killed four French Jesuit priests.  The Native American Huron tribe that had been the large powerful Native Nation that back in 1603 when it welcomed Samuel de Champlain and his few settlers into the St. Lawrence River Valley, was now gone.  The Québec colonialists, who had relied on the Huron’s crops for their food were now forced to grow their own food.

The conflict subsided a bit when the Iroquois lost their Dutch allies in the New Amsterdam Colony after the English took over the Dutch Colony in 1664.  The French then attempted to gain the Iroquois as an ally against the English, but the Iroquois refused to break their alliance with the English and frequently fought against the French.  The English-Iroquois alliance would reach its zenith during the later 1754 “French and Indian War”, which saw the French being expelled from North America.

The series of “Beaver Wars” and the subsequent commercial over-trapping of the areas where the beavers lived devastated the northeastern North American beaver population.  The loss of the beavers impacted the whole northeastern North America natural ecosystem that had relied on the beavers’ water controlling dams.  The loss led to ecological disaster and an irreversible environmental change in the St. Lawrence River and Ohio River Valleys.

Back in France in 1648, a series of civil wars occurred.  The fighting started when French King Louis XIV issues six new taxes.  The French nobility apposed the new taxes and refused to pay them.  The “Thirty-Year-War” had finally ended with the signing of the “Peace of Westphalia” freeing the French army to return to France and restore civil order.  The civil wars in France that followed created great poverty in the country and made living in Canada look much more appealing.

On 28 Jan 1648, Etienne Dumay Sr. and Françoise Morin were married in Québec settlement in Canada.  Françoise Morin was one of the 262 “filles à marier” women that sailed to Canada to marry.  Etienne Dumay Sr. was working for Robert Giffard at the time of the marriage.  Shortly after their marriage Etienne and  Françoise left the Québec settlement and move 150 miles up the St. Lawrence River to the new Ville-Marie settlement.

On 4 Feb 1648, Anne (Cloutier) died in the Québec hospital.  Anne was the wife of Robert Drouin and had become sick in their home in Beaupré and was taken to the hospital.  With the death of his wife, Robert Drouin decided to leave his farm in Beaupré, which he rented out and move back with his two young daughters five-year-old Genevieve and one-year-old Jeanne Drouin to Québec settlement.  Robert soon met strong-willed widow Marie Chapelier, who made it clear she did not want to live out in the country.  Robert and Marie were married and in their marriage contract Marie made sure that Robert agreed to find a house in Québec City for the family’s new home.

In 1648, Marie Chapelier had a cousin who worked for the Jesuits out at Cap-de-la-Madeleine, which granted Robert Drouin a parcel of land that was located seventy-five miles up the St. Lawrence River.  The location did not meet the terms of the marriage contract that Robert Drouin had agreed to but apparently satisfied Marie as it was located in the Trois-Rivieres, the second largest settlement in French Québec at the time.  While living in Trois-Rivieres, Robert worked a “fur trader” in the area while, he and Marie had eight children.  Something happened between new wife Marie and Robert’s first two children from his first marriage with Anne (Cloutier) and the two girls, the youngest one-year-old daughter Jeanne Drouin, were given to Zacharie Cloutier and his wife Sainte (Dupont) who were the two young daughter’s grandparents, who lived in Château-Richer, to raise away from their new stepmother Marie (Chapelier).
In 1648, Catherine Rivet was born to Pierre Rivet and Matie (Sorgeaut) in Étampes, Île-de-France, France.
In 1648, Antoine Dubois was born to Pierre Dubois and Philippe (Guillionne) in Varaize, Nouvelle-Aquitaine, France.
In 1648, Mathurine Goard was born to Gilles Goard and Catharine (Leger) in Saint-Sulpice, Occitania, France.
On 1 Mar 1649, Anne Le Seigneur was born to Guillaume Seigneur and Jeanne Madeliene (Sauve) in Rouen, Normandy, France.

On 4 Jun 1649, Noël Morin recruited Noël Bélanger to find him a hired man in France.  Noël Bélanger recruited Pierre Paillereau, a “laborer” from Villedoux France, who sailed to the Québec colony to work for Noël Morin.
In 1649, Pierre Bonneau and Marie Lambert were married in Vernoux, Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes, France.
In 1649, Joseph Bonneau was born to Pierre Bonneau and Marie Madeleine (Duchesne) in Vernoux-Sur-Boutonne, Poitou-Charentes, France.
In 1649, Anne Saint-Denis was born to Pierre Saint-Denis and Vivienne (Brunel) in Dieppe, Rouen, Normandy, France.
In 1649, Etiennette Loriot was born to Jean Loret and Françouse Lefroy in Paris, Île-de-France, France.

In 1649, Charles Guillebourg was listed as a “servant” of Noël Juchereau in the inventory of Noël’s estate.  Charles was owed 583 livres for the unpaid service he had rendered over his three-year period of service.
In 1649, Jacques Dion was born to Pierre Lecompte Dion and Gabrielle (Ranger) in Marans, Charente-Maritime, France.
Note:  In 1650, the Québec settlement had grown about thirty homes.

On 6 Feb 1650, Antoine Rouillard and Thomas Touchet promised to build on Noël Morin’s land a framework house 30 by 24 feet, with 6 feet under the house beams.  Noël paid 250 livres for the work, in addition of 20 minots (minot was an old unit of dry volume equivalent to three bushels per minot) of peas Noël gave to the two hired carpenters.
Note:  A French “Minot” is a unit of measure that is equal to 39.154 liters or 10.34 gallons.

On 18 Nov 1650, daughter Nicole was born to Etienne Dumay Sr. and his wife Françoise’s (Morin) in Sillery, Québec, indicating the family had left Ville-Marie settlement and relocated to Sillery district of Québec settlement before their new daughter’s birth.

Note:  The Sillery settlement was named for Noël Brûlart de Sillery, a famous Hospitaller knight who died in 1640.  Noël had been a wealthy and successful French diplomat, who had renounced all worldly goods and became a Catholic priest.  In 1637, he gave funds for the establishment of a settlement for First Nation people in Canada to convert them to Catholicism.  A community called “Sillery” was established at a cove in the St Lawrence River where the Algonquin native people gathered in the summer to catch spawning eels and salmons.  Forty Algonquin Christian families move into the new settlement where they lived most of the year, except for the fall hunting season.  The new settlement residents included Algonquins from as far away as the Ottawa River Valley.  French Missionaries came to Sillery to study with the indigenous resident people to learn their languages before going to more distant settlements.  By 1640, the Augustinian Catholic sisters had established a convent in the Sillery community, which hosted non-permanent residents of the community, especially refugees of the “Beaver War”.  However, the Sillery native indigenous community failed when of the community's native people fell victims to European introduced infectious diseases like smallpox.
In 1650, Guillaume Labelle was born to Jean LaBelle and Marie Loue in Lisieux, Normandy, France.
In 1650, sixteen-year-old Anne Antoinette de Liercourt arrived in the Québec Settlement as one of the 262 “filles à marier” women to marry one of the available men in the area.
In 1650, Quentin Moral sailed from France and arrived in the Québec settlement.  Quentin was a soldier in the “mobile patrol” and a Lieutenant du Roy (Of the King).

In 1651, the Ville-Marie settlement, after repeated Mohawk Iroquois attacks, was reduced to less than 50 French inhabitants.  Paul Chomedey, the governor of the Ville-Marie colony settlement sailed back to France to recruit more men for the failing colony.  The governor had already decided that should he fail in his recruiting efforts, he would abandon Ville-Marie and move everyone back to the larger Québec settlement.

In 1651, the small Trois Rivières settlement was rocked by fighting.  Father Jacque Buteux had been attempting to enforce strict Jesuit Catholicism on the local native people .  Iroquois warriors attacked the town on 6 March 1651, but were repulsed by the French's Huron native allies’ warriors.  On 10 Mar 1651, the Iroquois killed Father Buteux north of the town and later on 8 Jun 1651 killed to two Hurons Natives.  On 19 Aug 1651, the Iroquois attack again, this time killing the governor and twenty-two other French settlers.  The hostilities led to the collapse of the beaver pelt trade.  The defense of Trois Rivières settlement was in the hands of a few friendly Huron native warriors employed by the French to fend off any final Iroquois raid on the settlement.
On 4 Mar 1651, Marie Genevieve Guillebourg was born to Charles Guillebourg and Francoise (Bigot) in Sillery, Québec, Canada.
In 1651, thirty-four-old Pierre Maheu arrived by ship in the Québec settlement from Mortagne, Perche, France.

On 22 May 1651, Pierre Maheu contracted himself to Jean Juchereau for five-years in return for £45 and a pair of new shoes each year.  On 8 Sep 1655, in the company of Jean Trudel, Pierre Maheu rented from brother Nicolas Juchereau, the land and animals of Jean Juchereau in Saint-Denis for the promise to provide fifty minots of wheat and twenty-five minots of peas and seventy pounds of butter from the three rented cows each year.  Four days later, Nicolas Juchereau reduced the price of their lease to forty minots of wheat and twenty-five minots of peas.  Saint-Denis was located 100 miles east down the St. Lawrence River on the south river shore from the Québec settlement.  Later on, 3 Dec 1651 Pierre Maheu along with Jean Trudel, both commit themselves to felling trees for Germain Le Barbier on 4 acres of wood at £25 per acre.
In 1651, thirty-three-year-old Guillaume Fournier left France and sailed to the Québec settlement in Canada.

On 20 Nov 1651, thirty-three-year-old bachelor Guillaume Fournier and thirteen-year-old Françoise Hébert were married in the new Cathedral Notre-Dame-de-Québec, in the Québec settlement, that had just been completed by the Jesuits in stone after the first chapel built by Samuel de Champlain back in 1633 had burned down.  Guillaume Fournier was from a noble French family and was marring Françoise Hébert’s the granddaughter of Louis Hébert one of the original founders of Québec, which was significant.
On 7 Dec 1651, Noël Langlois Jr. was born to Noël Langlois Sr. and Françoise (Garnier) in Beauport, Canada.
In 1651, René Dumats was born to Claude Dumas and Francoise (Leger) in Tours, Centre-Val de Loire, France.
In 1651, Catherine Bureau was born to Jacques Bureau and Marguerite (Verrier) in Paris, Île-de-France, France.
In 1651,  Blaise Juillet and Anne Antoinette de Liercourt were married in the Québec settlement.  Anne Antoinette was one of the 262 “filles à marier” women who had agree to go to Canada and marry.
In 1651, Father Jean de Quen arrived in the Québec settlement and founded a mission for the fishermen, 10 miles downriver on the north shore of the St. Lawrence River called L’Ange-Gardien.

In 1651, Jean de Lauzon, was appointed Governor of the Canada.  Back in 1613, as a prominent lawyer in France, Jean de Lauzon had been appointed a counsellor in the French Parliament. Jean served in several government positions, including president of the Grand Conseil, the intendant (governor) of a Provence Guyenne in France, and later the intendant of Province of Dauphiné.  Jean de Lauzon had been developing interests in the Canada Colony. He was a founding member and became the director of the “Company of One Hundred Associates”, which was monopoly company controlling the colony.  Jean de Lauzon used his influence within the company to obtain land for himself and his sons in the colony.  By 1640, the Jean de Lauzons had become the biggest landowners in the Canada Colony.  Jean’s properties included the Island of Montréal, where the Ville Marie settlement was located and the Island of Île d’Orléans, which was a major island farming area in the middle of the St. Lawrence River across from the Québec settlement.
On 15 Jul 1652, Marie Madeleine Leblanc was born to Jean Leblanc and Euphrosine Madeleine (Nicolet) in Québec settlement in Canada.
On 15 Jul 1652, Zacharie Cloutier received a grant of land from Governor Jean de Lauzon in Château-Richer, where he later eventually settled in 20 Dec 1670.
In 1652, Louise Vezina was born to Jacques Vezina and Marie (Boisdon) in Saint-Rogatien, Nouvelle-Aquitaine, France.
In 1652, seventeen-year-old Jeanne Mignon arrived by ship in the Québec settlement as was one of the 262 “filles à marier” women who went to Canada to marry.

On 10 Nov 1652, Jean Guay and Jeanne Mignon were married in Québec Settlement, Canada.  Jeanne was a “filles à marier”.  Jean Guay worked as a “jointer and carpenter” (someone who builds wooden components such as stairs, doors and window frames).  Jean and Jeanne set up home in Point Lévis, located on the south shore of the St. Lawrence River at the confluence of the Chaudière River with the much larger St. Lawrence River, opposite the Québec settlement, where Jean farmed and built wooden furniture for a living.
On 31 Dec 1652, Francois Lareau was born to Jacques Lareau and Anne (Fosse) in Dieppe, Normandy, France.

In 1652, Jacques Hertel died in the Trois-Rivières settlement leaving his wife Marie Hertel (Marguerie) alone with their three young children.  Twenty-one-year-old Marie Marguerie had arrived in Canada back in 1641 as a “filles à marier” woman and had quickly married Jacques Hertel.

In 1652, Lt. Quentin Moral and widow Marie Marguerie were married in Trois Rivières, Canada.  Marie, had been a “filles à marier” woman, when she first arrived back in 1641 in Canada, she had married hers first husband Jacques Hertel.  Jacques Hertel had arrived with Samuel de Champlain in back in 1615 and like Jean Nicolet had lived with an Algonquin Native American tribe to learn their language and customs.  Marie Hertel (Marguerie) brought her three young children to the marriage with Lt. Quentin Moral.  Canada at this time had only 244 souls and the vast majority of them were single men.  It is not surprising that Marie selected Lt. Quentin Moral to marry, him being king's lieutenant, shortly after her first husband's death.  Lt. Quentin Moral became a judge-provost (government administrator) of Cap-de-la-Madeleine and lieutenant for civil and criminal matter in the Trois-Rivières settlement.  Cap-de-la-Madeleine was a small separate settlement across the Saint-Maurice River from the Trois-Rivières settlement.
In 1652, Jean Baptiste Bourgery his wife Marie (Gendre) and their eight-year-old son Pierre Bourgery left La Rochelle, France and sailed to the Québec settlement in Canada.
On 21 Apr 1652, after a very hard winter, 16 French indentured servants, sailors, and others deserted the Trois Rivières settlement and left Canada and returned to France.

On 21 Aug 1652, Iroquois warriors attack the Trois Rivières settlement again and were repulsed after an eight-day siege.  With the survival of the settlement assured, Lt. Quentin Moral turned to other the matter at hand.  His new wife Marie (Marguerie) had inherited from her first husband 200 acres of land in Trois-Rivières.  Quentin focused on making a living by gaining title of Marie's land.  However there seems to have been an issue with the rights to the land.

In 1652 Robert Drouin, wife Marie (Chapelier) and their eight children left Cap-de-la-Madeleine settlement and the Trois-Rivières area and moved back to the Québec settlement.  The Drouin family had farmed for the Jesuits in the settlement.  Desirous of keeping them on the farm the Jesuits granted the Drouin family a portion of the Jesuit’s extensive lands in Trois-Rivières for their own to farm, but Robert declined and returned to the Québec settlement.

On 23 May 1653, Pierre Gaudin, who was living in Châtillon-sur-Seine, Bourgogne-Franche-Comté, France, when he enlisted to go to Canada with several companions.  As a “master carpenter”, Pierre was offered 100 livers per year to go and work in Canada.  On 20 Jun 1653, Pierre received 127 livres and 6 sols in advance pay.  Pierre left France on the ship “Saint-Nicolas” and sailed over to the Québec settlement in Canada.  Four months later on 22 Sep 1653, Pierre Gaudin arrived in the Québec settlement.

On 15 Nov 1653, Jean de Lauzon, the Governor of Canada, turned over to Noël Morin a quarter-league (.8 miles) of frontal property by a league deep (3.5 miles), beginning an arpent below the La Caille River that flowed into the St. Lawrence River.  Thus, Seigneur Noël Morin became the owner of a portion of the seigneury of La Rivière-du-Sud, today part of the town of Montmagny, Québec.  Noël’s acquisition of the large area of prime land made him a fief (owner of a landed estate held on condition of feudal service) coming with rights and duties.  Noël was required to render faith and homage to the “West Indies Company”.  Noël named his new domain “Saint-Luc”.  Noël and wife Hélène (Desportes) never moved out to the Saint-Luc, preferring to stay and live in Québec settlement.  Their large Saint-Luc estate was later divided among their sons and son-in-law Guillaume Fournier.

In 1653, Jean-Baptiste, the first mayor of the Québec settlement, concedes to Pierre Maheu land of 1.7 acres on of frontage on the Lièver River one mile wide and half a mile depth in L'Ange-Gardien, which was located 10 miles east of Québec settlement.  Pierre worked as a “weaver of clothing” in the L'Ange-Gardien settlement.

In 1653, twenty-three-year-old Paul Vachon, arrived from Poitou, Vendee, France in the Québec settlement in Canada.  According to family tradition Paul arrived with gold coins sewn into the lining of his coat.  Paul was a “mason” by trade and gained the necessary skills to become a “seigneurial notary”(seigneurial attorney).
On 22 Oct 1653, Paul Vachon and Marguerite Langlois were married in Beauport, Québec, Canada.  Paul Vachon was working in Beauport as a notary and clerk to the area court.
On 16 Nov 1653, Governor Paul Chomedey and his one-hundred new Frenchmen recruits arrive in the Ville-Marie settlement essentially guaranteeing the survival of the small remote settlement.

On 17 Nov 1653, thirty-two-year-old Nicolas Gaudry and twelve-year-old Agnés Morin were married in the Québec settlement in Canada.  (There was a real scarcity of marriageable women in Canada at this time.)

In 1653, Toussaint Giroux left Mortagne, Perche, France and sailed to the Québec settlement in Canada.  Toussaint had signed a contract with Robert Gillard to cover the cost of the voyage to Canada by working for three years as an “ell fisherman” on the St. Lawrence River and as a “house servant” for Robert Gillard.

sections.title: Grande Rescue
sections.teaser: After the Iroquois attacks on the Trois Rivières settlement in 1651, the need to defend the newly created settlement of Ville-Marie (later City of Montréal)…

blocks.text: After the Iroquois attacks on the Trois Rivières settlement in 1651, the need to defend the newly created settlement of Ville-Marie (later City of Montréal) from an Iroquois attack became apparent.  Many who had first come and built the settlement left after their contracts ended and the birth rate in the settlement was also almost zero.  There were only about 50 people living in Ville-Marie and the situation was desperate.  In exchange for a piece of land, French nurse Jeanne Mance gave all the money she had acquired over the years to build a hospital to Catholic Priest Paul de Maisonneuve.  Back in 1641 Paul had helped Jeanne recruit forty-eight immigrants from France to come over and settle in the new Ville-Marie settlement.  Paul de Maisonneuve took Jeanne’ money and sail back to France and recruited one hundred and two men and fourteen women from Picardie, Champagne, Île-de-France, Normandy, Anjou and Maine region areas of France.  In the spring of 1653, approximately 150 men had signed contracts before notaries with the “Company of Ville Marie”.  On 22 Sep 1653, a ship with the new settlers landed at Québec settlement after 64 days at sea.  Governor de Lauzon of Canada tried to hold the newly recruited rescue party in the Québec settlement, but finally relented and the "Grande Rescue" group finally arrived at the Ville-Marie settlement on 16 Nov 1653.  The newcomers were people with skilled trades and the now a much larger French community took hold and the Ville Marie settlement was saved.

On 15 Nov 1653, Noël Morin was granted a small “arriere fief” (French for tenant in chief) over his land, located within the larger Rivière-du-Sud seigneurie on the south shore of the St. Lawrence River at Montmagny.  Noël was able to rent out his land to earn an income.  Noël Morin was known as “Sieur de Saint-Luc”.  As a seigneur Noël Morin and his wife Hélène (Desportes) were honored in a number of ways.  The social life of the seigneuries, as well as the colony itself, was centered around the Catholic church.  For this reason, many of the honors paid to a seigneur were accorded through the church.  The seigneur was granted a special pew in the church that was larger than the rest of the other pews and located in the most prominent place in the church, the front row.  Noël and his family would have been mentioned by name in the prayers from the priests in the church pulpit.  In various church ceremonies the seigneur was positioned at the head of the line and upon his death was buried inside the church beneath his seigneur pew.

On 16 Nov 1653, Jacques-Hugues Picard arrived in the Ville Marie settlement as part of the “Grande Rescue” French military force.  Jacques-Hugues was skilled in clearing land and sawing lumber as a “top sawyer”.

In 1653, thirteen-year-old Michel Messier, a young French solder, arrived in the Ville Marie settlement as one of the 102 settlers that were members of the “Grande Rescue”.  The Iroquois attacks continued, and Michel Messier was captured in the fall of 1654.  The next summer, a Mohawk chief returned Michel and other captured Frenchmen to the Ville Marie settlement in exchange for Iroquois chiefs that were being held by the French.
In 1653, François Bélanger was the mayor of the Longue Pointe in Canada, which later became the parish of Saint-Anne-de-Beaupré.

Note:  The Basilica of Sainte-Anne-de-Beaupré was located along the St. Lawrence River 19-miles east of Québec City and today is one of eight national shrines of Canada.  The basilica has been credited by the Catholic Church with performing many miracles of curing the sick and disabled people.  The basilica is an important Catholic sanctuary, which is visited by about a half-million pilgrims each year.  The basilica was initially built as a shrine to honor Saint Ann.  On 8 Mar 1658, settler Étienne de Lessard donated two frontal acres from the west end of his property to the Catholic Church, so that a chapel could be built.  The first reported miracle at the site happened during the shrine's construction when a man named Louis Guimont was hired to help build the shrine even though he suffered from rheumatism.  After placing three stones upon the shrine's foundation, Louis Guimont was cured of all his rheumatism ailment.  This was followed by other testimonies of healed people, and the shrine soon grew in popularity.  In 1876, the basilica opened for worship.  See below the current Basilica of Sainte-Anne-de-Beaupré:

blocks.caption: Note: The Basilica of Sainte-Anne-de-Beaupré was located along the St. Lawrence River 19-miles east of Québec City an…

blocks.text: In 1653, Jean Bonneau was a member of the Huguenot French Protestants that were being persecuted by the Catholic leaders of France.  Jean Bonneau left his wife Catherine (Poulet) and grown children in Mesnil, Picardie, France and sailed to Canada.  Jean Bonneau was the first of the Bonneau/Goodwater branch of Winifred Eloise Coss’ family tree.

In 1653, Jean Guyon and his wife Mathurine (Robin), who had been living in the Québec settlement since 1634, gave their house and garden back in France to the parish of Saint Jean de Mortange in return for being named benefactors of the church.
In 1653 Catherine Hurault arrived alone in the Montréal settlement as a thirteen-year-old “filles à marier” girl.
In 1653, twenty-five-year-old Jean Lemarché arrived in the Montréal settlement as a soldier as part of the 102 members of the “Grande Rescue”.

On 21 Jan 1654, less than two years after Lt. Quentin Moral and Marie (Marguerie) marriage, Marie's twelve-year-old oldest son Joseph-François Hertel from Marie’s first marriage began clearing trees off the disputed land Joseph had inherited from his father Jacques Hertel that he wanted to farm to support his mother and his young sisters.

On 2 Feb 1654, Lt. Quentin Moral signed a new contract with Governor Maisonneuve promising to move and live in the Ville Marie settlement for a total sum of 600 livres.  Quentin was also granted 30 acres of land in Coteau Saint-Louis next to René Bondy on the outskirts of the Ville Marie settlement.  Lt. Quentin Moral made the transition from a military officer of the king of France to that of a civil and criminal lawyer, perhaps not difficult, since lawyers were not allowed to immigrate to Canada.

On 2 Feb 1654, Pierre Gaudin committed to settle permanently in Ville Marie and acknowledged that he had received from Governor Maisonneuve the sum of 600 livres.  On the same day, Pierre was granted land of 30 acres at Coteau Saint-Louis, next to that of René Bondy on the outskirts of the Ville Marie settlement.

Between 1655 and 1662, the inhabitants of Trois Rivières settlement, when not fighting off Iroquois attacks, were a quarrelsome bunch.  In the "Prévôté Court de Trois-Rivières" there were 907 cases tried for a population of about 700 settlers living in Trois Rivières.  The "Prévôté Court” was not only used to dispense criminal justice and adjudicate disputes, it also served as a collection agency under the settlement’s barter economy.  66% of the court cases handled in the "Prévôté Court” were for debt settlement and 20% for settling inheritance claims.  Only 20 were for verbal or physical violence.  Lt. Quentin Moral was involved in 29 cases, reflecting not only his role as an attorney but also his quarrelsome nature and perhaps developed from his duties as an officer of the King.  In one case, Quentin was being sued for having shot and killed other citizen’s wandering pigs, probably part of his duties.  A few cases later, Quentin sued Jacques Aubuchon, a master carpenter and the most quarrelsome man in the colony (44 cases), because the Jacques had intentionally shot one of Quentin's pigs, (perhaps as payback?).

Marie (Marguerie) the wife of Lt. Quentin Moral had been married previously to Jacques Hertel in her first marriage.  Marie and Jacques’ son Joseph-François Hertel became the "Hero of Canada", for his exploits defending the Canada Colony against the “First People” (Iroquois warriors) and their British allies.  In Jul 1661, nineteen-year-old, Joseph-François Hertel had rashly left the fort in Trois-Rivières and was capture by four marauding Iroquois warriors who took him to their village, where he was subjected to torture.  His two-year period of captivity was recorded in three letters he smuggled out of the Iroquois village to Father Simon Le Moyne.  In the letters Joseph describes how he was adopted by an older Iroquois woman who taught him their language and customs.  Joseph was finally able to escaped when his adopted mother was gone and no one was watching him.  He returned to Ville Marie settlement and then on to the Québec settlement.  Later Joseph-François Hertel became famous as the commander of all the French allied Native American tribes.  Joseph-François Hertel’s led Native warrior raids were among the most ingenious and fruitful military feats of that period.  Quentin who had four daughters and no sons in his marriage to Marie and may have had a difficult time bearing the fact he was the “Sieur de St-Quentin” and lord over the island his stepson had cleared, which was known as “L'île Saint-Quentin”.  Marie outlived Quentin by 14 years.  Upon her death at age 80, Marie Moral (Marguerie) was buried beside her first husband Jacques-François Hertel.  The “L'île Saint-Quentin” land remained cropland until 1930, when it was converted to recreational use.  Today it is a popular recreational park.  See below a photo of the “L'île Saint-Quentin” island, which is the island on the lower left:

blocks.caption: Marie (Marguerie) the wife of Lt. Quentin Moral had been married previously to Jacques Hertel in her first marriage.…

blocks.text: On 10 Apr 1654, Marie Madeleine Bourgery was born to Jean-Baptiste Bourgery and Marie (Gendre) in Trois Rivieres, Canada.  Marie Madeleine Bourgery will be captured as a young girl by the Iroquois and forced to live with a Native Iroquois family in their village for a number of years.
On 30 Apr 1654, Suzanne Pagé was born to Raymond Pagé and Madeleine (Bergeron) in Château-Richer, Canada where Raymond and Madeleine were now living.

In 1654, thirteen-year-old Marie Godard left Mortagne, Perche, France after signing a contract as one of the 262 “filles à marier” women to marry in Canada and then sailed over to the Québec settlement.  Marie traveled with her sister Jeanne, who also was a “filles à marier” woman.

On 29 Sep 1654, Toussaint Giroux and thirteen-year-old Marie Godard were married in Beauport, Montmorency in the Québec settlement in Canada.  Toussaint Giroux and Marie (Godard) were both worked as servants for Robert Gillard to fulfil their 3-year contracts to cover the cost of their ocean passage from France.  The two had twins a year later while under their 3-year contract period.  Both the young babies died within a week of their birth.

In 1654, eighteen-year-old Jeanne Rousseliere, one of the 262 “filles à marier” women from La Rochelle, Normandy, France and one of the fourteen women who sailed with the members of the “Grande Rescue”, from Moeze, Saintes, Saintonge, France to the Québec settlement and then on to the Ville Marie settlement.
On 27 Sep 1654, a marriage contract was drawn up by notary Lambert Closse for the marriage of Pierre Gaudin and Jeanne Rousseliere.
On 13 Oct 1654, Jean Lemarché and Catherine Hureau were married in the Ville-Marie settlement, Canada.  Catherine was a thirteen-year-old “filles à marier” woman.

On 13 Oct 1654, Pierre Gaudin and Jeanne Rousseliere were married by Father Pierre Pijart in the Ville-Marie settlement, Canada.  Jeanne was a “filles à marier” woman.  Pierre Gaudin was working as a “master carpenter” in Ville-Marie at the time.
In Dec 1654, under Governor Jean de Lauson’s administration, Guillaume Couillard was ennobled (given a noble rank) by the French King Louis XIV, “for his services rendered to the Canada.”

In 1654, Jean Bonneau, who had arrived the year earlier, wrote back to France and encouraged his son Pierre Bonneau to join him on his farm on the Île d'Orléans Island in the middle of the Saint Lawrence River.  Pierre Bonneau accepted his father’s invitation and left his wife Marie (Lambert) and their five-year-old son Joseph Bonneau in Vernoux-Sur-Boutonne, Poitou-Charentes, France and sailed to Canada.  Jean Bonneau and Joseph Bonneau were French Huguenots (Protestants) and were looking for an opportunity to live in a new area that they hoped would be more tolerant of their non-Catholic religion.  (Previously Huguenots had been prohibited from settling in Canada.  Not sure the rules had changed or Jean, and Pierre were hiding their religion. )
Note: Fourteen members of Mary Ann Goodwater’s branch of Winifred Eloise Coss’s tree were part of the three hundred Founding Families on the Île d'Orléans Island.

In 1654, Paul Vachon in association with Mathurin Roy, were both entrusted with the building a chapel and the sick-ward of the Hôtel-Dieu in the Québec settlement.  The first stone of was laid on 15 Oct 1654 by the Governor-General, Jean de Lauzon.  On 4 Jun 1655, Joseph Giffard granted ten acres of land to Paul Vachon, which was later doubled in 1664.

In 1654, widower Olivier Charbonneau and Marie Marguerite Garnier were married in La Rochelle, Normandy, France.  Oliver’s first wife died after bearing three children in four years of marriage.  Olivier was working was a “sewer cleaner” in Marans, Charente-Maritime, France.

In 1654, Robert Drouin, with his second wife Marie (Chapelier) and their children left the Québec settlement where they were both working for the Jesuits and farming on their own land that had been given to them.  Robert sold the house he had built and provided 600 bricks for the Jesuits to thank them for their help they provided his family when it was in need.  Robert next moved his family back to his original farm he had left in Chateau-Richer and was now renting.  Wife Marie’s opposition to living out in the country appeared to have soften, partly because the family fortunes had greatly improved.  Robert and Marie lived on their farm in Chateau-Richer for the next thirty-years and brought up six more children.  Their improved prosperity was demonstrated in the sized and value of the dowries given to their daughters.  When their first daughter from his first marriage married in 1656 Robert could not give her a dowry at all.
