Three years later when daughter Jeanne Drouin was married, Robert was able to give her a milk cow and clothing.  After another three years the first daughter in Robert’s second marriage was given a dowry of mike cow, a calf, clothing and 100 livres in cash.  Eight years later when daughter Marguerite married, she was given a milk cow, clothing, dishes, household utensils and a promise to have a house built for her and her new husband.

On 17 Jan 1655, Ignace Choret was born to Mathieu Choret and Sêbastienne (Veillon) in Beauport, Canada.  Ignace was baptized on 29 Aug 1655 in the Québec settlement months after his twin brother.  Speculation is Ignace was given to a wet nurse after birth while his mother took care of his twin brother.  Once he was returned to his mother he was baptized.

On 17 May 1655, Noël Morin and Hélène (Desportes) were granted a pew by the “Fabrique Corporation of Québec” (a church corporation which acquired, possess and held land for the Catholic Church).  The pew (an enclosed seating area) was located on the north side, in the nave (center aisle), near the pew of Charles Sevestre.  In return for the privilege of having their own pew in the church, Noël’s paid the “Fabrique Corporation”, 1.7 acres of land, which today is the location of the Cathedral-Basilica of Notre-Dame de Québec ("Our Lady of Quebec City"), located at 16, Rue de Buade street in Quebec City, which is the “primatial church” (where the church bishop resides) of the Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Quebec.  See a photo of the Cathedral below:

blocks.caption: On 17 May 1655, Noël Morin and Hélène (Desportes) were granted a pew by the “Fabrique Corporation of Québec” (a churc…

blocks.text: On 19 Sep 1655, Jean Lebeau was born to Mathurin Lebeau and Louise (Garatte) in Saint-Dizier, Grand Est, France.

On 7 Oct 1655, Jean Baptiste Bourgery and his family were given an area 128 ft. by 38 ft. to build a house inside the fort in the Trois Rivières settlement.  The attacks by the Iroquois had been increasing and the community’s settlers had to move inside the fort for protection.
On 23 Oct 1655, Paul Vachon signed an act in his capacity as notary of Notre-Dame-des-Anges.

In 1655, Pierre Saint-Denis left his wife Vivienne (Brunel) and their children including six-year-old daughter Anne Saint-Denis in Dieppe, Normandy, France and sails to the Québec settlement in Canada.

In 1655, Jean Baptiste Halay left his wife Mathurine (Valet) and their three daughters, including ten-year-old Barbe Halay in St-Julien, Le Coudray, Centre-Val de Loire, France and sailed to Québec settlement in Canada for the first time.
On 12 Mar 1656, Ignace Gagne was born to Louis Gagne and Marie (Michel) in the Saint-Anne de-Beaupré settlement in Canada.

In 1656, twenty-six-year-old Marie Anne Le Laboureur, a “filles à marier” woman, sailed from Bayeux, Normandy, France to the Québec settlement in Canada.  Marie had signed a contract to marry Jean Normand when she arrived in Canada.

On 18 Jul 1656, Jean Normand and Marie Anne Le Laboureur, were married in Québec settlement, Canada.  Jean and Marie lived in Charlesbourg; a borough of the Québec settlement located on the northeast perimeter of the settlement that was laid out by Jesuits in a star formation to make it easier to defend against Iroquois warrior attacks.  Jean worked in the community as a “carpenter”.
On 21 Jul 1656, Raphaël  Giroux was born as the third child of Toussaint Giroux and Marie (Godard) in Beauport in Canada.
On 16 Sep 1656, Jean Pilote was born to Léonard Pilote and Denise (Gauthier) in La Rochelle, Normandy, France.
On 3 Dec 1656, Etienne Dumay Jr. was born to Etienne Dumay Sr. and Françoise (Morin) in Sillery in Canada.

In 1656, Marie Bonneau (Lambert) and her seven-year-old son Joseph left France and sailed to Canada following her husband Pierre Bonneau, who had sailed to Canada two years earlier at the encouragement of his father Jean Bonneau.
In 1656, Aimee Caron was born to Robert Caron and Marie (Crevet) in the Québec settlement in Canada.
In 1656, thirty-one-year-old Claude Metru and twenty-eight-year-old Jeanne Crissot were married in Saint-Mern parish Paris, Île-de-France, France.

In 1656, Marie Anne Metru was born to Claude Metru and Jeanne (Crissot) in Paris, Île-de-France, France.  Jeanne (Crissot) died the same year her daughter Marie Anne was born, possibly from the child birth complications.

In 1658, fifty years after the founding of the Québec settlement in Canada by Samuel de Champlain, the population of Canada was just under 3,000 people.  Canada was made up of a handful of “nobles”, forming a small aristocratic administration, a few "bourgeois" merchants and traders engaged in the lucrative fur trade, some missionaries and their aides, and hundreds farmers grouped near the shores of the St-Lawrence River, around the settlements of Québec, Trois-Rivières and Ville Marie.  The population of Canada, although very diversified, had grown very slowly.  The Native peoples living in the St. Lawrence River Valley, felt threatened from both sides (the warring Iroquois and the expanding French settlements) and constantly launched attacks on the expanding French settlements.  The uneasiness in Canada was enhanced further by some bickering that existed among the religious communities and by their political interference in the affairs of the Québec Administrative Council.  All these factors hindered the effective operation of the affairs of the Canada Colony.  The Governor Lauzon was recalled to France in 1656.  His son Charles Lauzon-Charny succeeded him but returned to France two years later.  The King of France, Louis XIV, realized that the only efficient way to protect his interests in the Canada was to increase the number of French settlers there.
36 Month Contracts

In 1656, King Louis XIV of France authorized a system of indentured servants in which a person wishing to immigrate would sign a “contract of engagement”.  They called the contract a "36-mois" (thirty-six months), owing to the three-year period of the contract.  During the contract period the engagé (indentured servant) was denied citizenship, marriage and prohibited from becoming involved in the fur trade.  The purpose of the new contract system was to supply needed laborers to the Canada merchants, allow the newcomers a period of adjustment and an opportunity to learn the skills needed for a lifestyle in Canada they could choose or reject.  If both parties were satisfied, the immigrants could hope to obtain a tract of land where they could settle on in Canada.  The merchants, on the other hand, had to assume the cost of the return trip, food for the three-years of the contract, plus a sum of from 50 to 100 livres depending on the terms of the contract based on the trade and skills of the applicant.
On 9 Apr 1657, Agathe Fournier was born Guillaume Fournier and Francoise (Hébert) in the Québec settlement in Canada.
On 11 Apr 1657, Anne Charbonneau was born to Olivier Charbonneau and Marie Marguerite (Garnier) in La Rochelle, Normandy, France.

On 19 Sep 1657, Pierre Tremblay and Anne Ozanne Achon were married in Québec settlement in Canada.  Anne had just arrived in Québec settlement after completing her voyage from France.  Pierre Tremblay had contracted the marriage thru Claude Aubert with Anne Ozanne Achon, one of the 262 “filles à marier” girls when she was living in France.  The marriage took place in the Notre-Dame Church in Québec settlement.  Anne Ozanne Achon was of Moorish descent.  Léonard Pilote performed as a witness for the wedding.

On 10 Nov 1657, Jean-Baptiste Bourgery died in Trois-Rivières, leaving his wife Marie (Gendre) and their son Pierre Bourgery and three-year-old daughter Marie Madeleine Bourgery alone to fend for themselves.  Marie (Gendre) remarried three months later Florian LeClerc in Trois-Rivières as her second of four husbands.

In 1657, twenty-year-old Catherine Fortier left La Rochelle, Normandy, France and sailed to the Québec settlement, having agree to being a “filles à marier” and contracted to marry Jacques Menard when she arrived in Canada.

On 19 Nov 1657, Jacques Ménard and Catherine Fortier were married as contacted in Trois-Rivières, Canada.  Jacques owned two small plots of land in Trois-Rivières and made his living as a “carpenter and wheelwright” (maker of wheels and carts).  He built several barns and houses in the Trois-Rivières settlement.
In 1657, nineteen-year-old Anne Lemoine left Dieppe, Normandy, France and sailed to the Québec settlement, having contracted as a “filles à marier” in France.
In 1657, Pierre Charles Le Sueur was born to Victor Le Sueur and Anne (Honneur) in Hesdin, Hauts-de-France, France.

On 21 Jun 1657, Léonard Pilote left his pregnant wife of seven months Denise (Gauthier), their four-year-old daughter and their one-year-old son Jean Pilote and in La Rochelle, Normandy, France and sailed to Canada on the ship “Le Taureau”.  Many documents, signed by Leonard, revealed a man of imposing stature who was energetic and friendly.  The positions that he occupied proved that he was educated, well-mannered and that he had every intention of succeeding in business in Canada as evidenced by his activities soon after his arrival.
In 1657, Michel Messier bought twenty-five acres of land on the Île de Ville Marie Island for 900 livres.

On 25 Feb 1658, Michel Messier and Anne Lemoine were married in Ville-Marie settlement in  Canada.  Michel was a soldier in the “Grande Rescue” of Ville-Marie and Anne Lemoine was one of the “262 “filles à marier” women that sailed Canada to marry.

On 7 Oct 1658, shortly after Léonard Pilote arrival in Québec settlement, at the request of merchant Michel Desorcy, Léonard signed a document appointing him Michel Desorcy's Procurator (the ability to handle one’s affairs in their absence).  Léonard was vested with the power to manage the affairs of Michel Desorcy and, therefore, became the emissary of His Majesty the King of France.  Michel Desourcy left the Québec settlement on 27 Jun 1659 and sailed back to La Rochelle, France leaving Léonard in charge of his affairs in Canada.
In 1658, Nicolas Massard arrived in the Québec settlement from France.

About 1658, Toussaint Giroux and his wife Marie (Godard) had completed their 3-year servitude contract period working as “house servants” for Robert Giffard.  Toussaint bought a plot of land in the Bourg de Fargy section of Beauport where he built a house consisting of “a heated room, antechamber, cellar and attic.”  Toussaint was friends with Michel Baugis and the two men became “eel fishermen” together.  They were living next to each other in the 1666 census.  Toussaint Giroux was among 65 people confirmed at the church of Notre-Dame de Québec on 24 Feb 1660.  In 1681, Toussaint had 45 acres of land under cultivation.  Wife Marie (Godard) died on 21 Nov 1684 and Toussaint remarried Marie Therese (Leblanc) on 29 Oct 1686.  They had a daughter born two years later, but the marriage ended in a separation that was granted on 12 Apr 1691.  The reason for the ending of the marriage was because Marie Therese had spent a large sum of money shopping in Québec settlement and caused Toussaint to go into debt, so he arranged to end their marriage.
On 11 May 1659, Catherine Gaudin was born to Pierre Gaudin and Jeanne (Rousseliere) in the Ville-Marie settlement in Canada.

On 8 Sep 1659, twenty-nine-year-old Pierre Maheu and twelve-year-old Jeanne Drouin were contract to married in front of the notary Paul Vachon.  The marriage took place on 10 Nov 1659, in the Québec settlement.

In 1659, Léonard Pilote who had left his pregnant wife Denise (Gauthier) back in France wrote in the spring of 1659; “Denise, it is here you must come.  I have obtained a beautiful grant right next door to Québec.  Join me as soon as you can.  My love to Marguerite!  How big is three-year-old Jean Pilote now?  How goes every little thing with you?"
In 1659, nineteen-year-old Sébastien Gingras, sailed from France to Canada and arrived in the Québec settlement.

On 16 Jun 1659, Paul de Rainville, his wife Pauline Rolline (Poête) and their children including fifteen-year-old Marie de Rainville, arrived in the Québec settlement on the first ship from France of the year.  The ship carried Bishop François de Laval, the new Bishop of the Québec settlement, Jesuit priests and 84 fishermen from Normandy, France in addition to the Rainville family.  Paul de Rainville settled in Beauport on 17 acres of land with St. Lawrence River frontage where he built a house and farmed.  Later in 1664 Paul served as a sergeant in the Beauport’s militia.

In 1659, Jacques Vezina, wife Marie (Boisdon) and their five children including seven-year-old daughter Louise Vezina left LaRochelle, Normandy, France and sailed to Canada.  Prior to the family’s departure daughter Louise Vezina had signed up as one of the "262 filles à marier” women.  The family settled in Chateau Richer settlement in Canada.
In 1659, Jerome Longtin Sr. was born to Andre Longuetin and Jeanne Angelique (Briere) in Paris, Île-de-France, France.
In 1659, Louis Gesseron Sr. left his wife Marie ( Guitard)and their eighteen-year-old son Louis Gesseron Jr. in La Rochelle, Normandy, France and sailed to the Québec settlement in Canada.

In 1659, Jean Pichet sailed from Poitiers, Nouvelle-Aquitaine, France to the Québec settlement.  He was under a three-year contract to someone in the Chateau-Richer on the north shore of the St. Lawrence River near Beaupré.

In early 1659, Olivier Charbonneau was recruited by Jeanne Mance to take himself and his family to Canada for a chance of a hopefully better life.  Olivier as a “manual laborer” who had agreed to a two-year contract to do spade work on Jeanne Mance’s farm in Canada to cover the 50 livres cost for Olivier and his family’s passage to Canada.  The ship that was to carry Olivier and his family got delayed on port and the ship owner increased the ship passage cost to 175 livres plus a special charge for family provisions and baggage.  Olivier did not have the additional money that was now required for the ship passage and had to beg Jeanne Mance to loan him more money.  Jeanne Mance agreed to the larger loan to pay for Olivier’s family passage and their large travel trunk.  In addition to Olivier and Marie there were 18 other related people who had all been recruited by Jeanne Mance to sail to Canada.

On 2 Jul 1659, the group Jeanne Mance had recruited left France and ran into a storm and their ship the “Saint-Andre" lost its rudder forcing them to return back to France.  The “Saint-Andre" set off again and shortly members of the ship’s crew and passengers began coming down with the “Black Plague” and ten people died on the ship.

On 7 Sep 1659, the “Saint-Andre" arrived in the Quebec settlement in Canada and the plague infected passengers on the ship soon spread the deadly disease to the entire Québec settlement.  Olivier, Marie and their children, including two-year-old daughter Anne Charbonneau quickly left the Québec settlement and moved on up the St. Lawrence River to the Ville Marie settlement.  Olivier partnered with Pierre Dagenets to buy 42 acres of land in Boucherville, located on the east end of the Île de Montréal, which they both farmed growing wheat.  They built a water powered mill at Pointe-aux-Trembles that Olivier used to mill to turn cereal grain into flour.

In 1659, Jean Baptiste Halay left the Québec settlement in Canada and returned to his family in Le Coudray, Centre-Val de Loire, France.  Jean and his wife Mathurine (Valet) and their three daughters including fourteen-year-old Barbe Halay soon left France and sailed over to the Québec settlement in Canada.  Jean Baptiste Halay and his family settled in the seignory of Lauzon, northeast of the large Lévis settlement located on the southern bank of the St. Lawrence River across from the Québec settlement.

In 1659, Paul Vachon became a notary in the Beauport seigneurie.  He was already the seigneurial attorney of the Lirec and Île d’Orléans seigneuries.  In the same period, Paul acted as secretary to Charles de Lauzon sieur de Charny, the acting governor of Canada, vicar general and ecclesiastical superior of the Hôtel-Dieu of Québec, taking care of his seigneurial land grants.  Paul Vachon was the first notary of the Île d’Orléans, where he practiced and was one of the three hundred Founding Families on the island.  About the same time, Paul became the “court clerk” of the Beauport and Notre-Dame-des-Anges seigneuries.
Note:  Two-thirds of the immigrants that came to the Canada Colony between 1632 and 1659 for various reasons returned to France, limiting the growth of the new French colony.

In 1660, after the end of the “English Civil War” and the restoration of the English monarchy with King Charles II, the English began to wonder why they had tolerated the presence of the Dutch Colony on a territory wedged between their New England and Virginia Colonies.

On 11 Jan 1660, Nicolas Bélanger and Marie de Rainville were married in Beauport, Canada.  Nicolas worked as a “salt merchant” in Beauport and farmed thirty-four acres of land in Beauport on the St. Lawrence River shoreline.  Nicolas remained faithful to Robert Giffard’s family and stayed in Beauport when his father and brothers moved on

In the 1660s, the Iroquois continued to control the forest areas of Canada, raiding to the edges of the walled settlements of Québec, Trois-Rivières and Villa Marie.  In May 1660, an Iroquois force of 160 warriors attacked Villa Marie and captured 17 of the French colonists.  Amount the captured French colonists were two young French girls, six-year-old Marie Madeleine Bourgery and nine-year-old Jeanne Baillargeon.  The following year a force of 250 warriors attacked and took ten more French captives from the Villa Marie settlement.  Between 1661 and 1662, the Iroquois made several raids against the Abenakis Native tribe that was allied with the French.  A small military force made up of Frenchmen, a few surviving Hurons and Algonquins was formed in Villa Marie to try counter the Iroquois raids, but the Iroquois attacked this new force as soon as it tried venture out the fortified walled settlement.  Only 29 of the Villa Marie Frenchmen survived and escaped the last Iroquois attack.  Five Frenchmen settlers were captured in the raid and tortured to death.  Despite their victory, the Iroquois also suffered a significant number of casualties, and their leaders began to consider negotiating for peace with the French.

The two young French girls Marie Madeleine Bourgery and Jeanne Baillargeon were taken back to an Iroquois village where they were assigned to Iroquois families and brought up as Iroquois native child.  The Iroquois culture was one where the women were the head of the family, particularly in matters involving village life.  The young French girls in every way became Iroquois and lost all contract with their previous French families and culture ways of living.
On 25 Apr 1660, Louis Guay was born to Jean Guay and Jeanne (Mignon) in Québec settlement in Canada.
On 26 Jun 1660, Jean Baptiste Neveu was born in Saint-Germain, Nouvelle-Aquitaine, France.

On 30 Jun 1660, forty-six-year-old Jacques-Hugues Picard and twenty-six-year-old widow Anne Antoinette de Liercourt were married in the Ville Marie settlement in Canada.  Anne Antoinette de Liercourt’s first husband Blaise Juillet and died a few months before in Ville Marie.  Jacques Picard was working as a “sawyer of planks” (one who sawed tree logs into planks and boards) and as a “land-clearer” after he was discharged from his “La Grande Rescue” military duties.
In 1660, Louis Lefébvre and Suzanne Debure were married in Rouen, Normandy, France.

In 1660, Jean Carrier left Saintes, Nouvelle-Aquitaine, France and sailed to the Québec settlement in the Canada Colony.  When Jean Carrier arrived, he got a job working with the Québec Hospitaller’s Nuns.  Most of the French settlers in Canada, who were not farming or in the fur trading business worked for the French colonial government, religious institutions or rich French seigneurs (feudal lords) property managers.

In 1660, Vivienne (Brunel), wife of Pierre-Saint-Denis and their children including eleven-year-old daughter Anne Saint-Denis left Dieppe, Normandy, France and sails to the Québec settlement in Canada where husband Pierre-Saint-Denis had been living for the last five-years.

In 1661, the Canadians living in the Québec, Ville Marie and Trois-Rivières settlements, most of whom were men, were having a frightful time defending themselves against recurring attacks by Iroquois Native warriors.  French casualties were high and many of the French settlers were very discourage.  French King Louis XIV and his government made a decision to support and defend their French Canada Colony with troops and more settlers rather than give up and abandon their colony in America.
On 1 Jan 1661, Marie-Marthe Moral was born to Lt. Quentin Moral and Marie (Marguerie) in Trois Rivieres in Canada.

In Mar 1661, Michel Messier was taken prisoner again by the Iroquois.  At the end of Jun 1661, two boatloads of Iroquois arrived at the Ville Marie settlement bearing a white flag with four captured Frenchmen as proof of their peaceful intentions.  The Iroquois reported that Michel Messier had been burned to death by Onondagas Natives at their village.

On 2 Feb 1661, Louis Gagne was one of eight French settlers who were killed by Mohawk-Iroquois along the south bank of the Mohawk River west of the future site of Fort Hunter in Auriesville, which was 40 miles north of the Dutch Fort Orange in the Dutch Colony and later became the City of Albany New York.  Why Louis Gagne and the other Frenchmen were down in the area is unknown.

In Apr 1661, Raymond Pagé left Château-Richer and move to a two-acre waterfront lot on the Beaupré coast.  His sons Robert and Stephen also move in next to Raymond on their own two-acre waterfront lots.  The big three Canada censuses of the 1600s show the Pagé family remained at the Beaupré coast.  In the 1666 census , Raymond Pagé was working as a “carpenter” in Beaupré.  His sons, Robert, Stephen and William, as well as his twelve -year-old daughter Suzanne Pagé were living with their parents along with thirty-year-old house servant Paul Brusseau.  In the 1667, the census taker added an important detail: Raymond had nine head of cattle and was cultivating fourteen acres of his land.
On 6 Jul 1661, Michele Picard was born to Jacques-Hugues Picard and Anne Antoinette (de Liercourt) in the Ville-Marie settlement in Canada.
In 1661, Marie Angélique Lefébvre was born to Louis Lefébvre and Suzanne (Debure) in Rouen, Normandy, France.
In 1661, Charles Garnier left Caen, Normandy, France and sailed to the Québec settlement in Canada.

In 1661, Jacques Lareau with his wife Anne (Fosse), their nine-year-old son Francois Lareau and their daughter left Dieppe, Normandy, France and sailed to the Québec settlement in Canada.  Jacques settled as a “sharecropper” on a plot of land situated north of the Rivière Saint-Charles in what is now urban Québec City.  Jacques was practicing his trades as a “carpenter” and “wood turner” (someone who uses a lathe to make symmetrical round wood items such as table legs or wooden eating dishes.

sections.title: Île d’Orléans Island
sections.teaser: In 1661, the Île d’Orléans, a large island in the middle of the St. Lawrence River east of the Québec settlement, was sought as a safe refuge by the Hurons (…

blocks.text: In 1661, the Île d’Orléans, a large island in the middle of the St. Lawrence River east of the Québec settlement, was sought as a safe refuge by the Hurons (Wendats), who had been the target of numerous attacked by the Iroquois (Mohawks) warriors.  In 1661, known as a year of terror, the Iroquois launched a massive attack against island of Île d’Orléans and the towns of Ville Marie, Trois-Rivières, Tadoussac and even Québec.  Nearly 100 people, both French colonists and Hurons, were slaughtered.  Sainte-Famille, the first parish on the Île d’Orléans Island, was established in 1661 after a horrible attack.

In 1661, Canada’s new Governor Davaugour dispatched Pierre Boucher to France to seek help from King Louis XIV.  At the time, there was a military regiment of seasoned soldiers in France that had been formed as a private army back in 1644 by Thomas François de Savoie, Prince de Carignan.  This was an army for hire, made up of hand-picked volunteers.  The standards for the regiment were very high, and the soldiers recruited for the regiment all had to be big and strong physically with a strong fighting spirit.  In the hire of the King Louis XIV, this regiment had just returned from a successful engagement against the Turks.  Rather than demobilize the regiment, the King decided to send it to Canada to help the colonists against the ongoing Iroquois attacks.  The Regiment was placed under the command of Henri de Chapelas, Sieur de Salières and was called the “Carignan-Salières Regiment”.

In 1662, Pierre Boucher, who worked for Canada’s Governor Davaugour, returned to France to solicit the support of King Louis XIV and Jean-Baptiste Colbert, the Minister of Finances to save the threatened Canada colony from the ongoing Iroquois attacks.  The sheer terror of the Iroquois warrior raids and resulting massacres was about to end the colonization effort in the new world for the French.  Farming had become almost impossible in the settlements of Ville Marie and Trois-Rivières.  Settlers going out to their fields to work had to have armed guards with them, militia or fellow settlers.  It seemed every tree had an Iroquois warrior lurking behind it.  The Iroquois warrior raids were fast and furious, always leaving blood and fire in their wake.  Pierre Boucher describes the situation this way:  “They can set up an ambush anywhere; a small bush can conceal six or seven of these barbarians.  We are always in fear of attack.  Woman live in constant fear that her husband, who left that morning to work in the farm fields, will be killed or captured and that she will never see him again.”  In 1662 French King Louis XIV finally took personal control over his Canada colony.  The king changes the status of the colony from a proprietary colony run by government established monopoly companies to a fully recognized French province controlled by a French colonial government.

In 1662, the commitment of the French King Louis XIV to his Canada Province changed the process used to build buildings, churches and city homes.  The old wooden building, built mainly by boat builders were replaced by stone structures built in harmonious forms of French classicism built by classically trained “stonecutters”, “masons” and master “carpenters”.  The classical French principles of town planning were also employed, with each town’s overall design and defensive fortification all plotted on paper before being transposed onto the structures on the ground.  The concept of an “upper town” with government building, church cathedrals and aristocracies’ business and homes and a “lower town” with merchants’ shops and homes was retained to uphold the French tradition of the privilege by birthright being separated from the rest of the town people.

Outside the initial main towns of Québec, Villa Marie and Trois-Rivières, the rest of Canada took shape in the form of villages linked into the linear profile of communities along the St. Lawrence River shore.  The seigneurial’s manor & mill, the church, rectory and cemetery were higher up and the farms and craft businesses were lower down near the river.  Military forts were strategically built in a string of strongholds across the southern borders of Canada.

On 10 Aug 1662, Jean Pichet, having completed his three-year contract that paid for his voyage from France, purchased a track of land in the parish of Saint Pierre on the Île d’Orléans in the La Chevalerie estate.  The land was two acres wide and ran from the shore of the St. Lawrence River to the middle of the island, totaling 38.5 acres.  Jean had to clear his land to open an area to build a house and then open the rest his land for farming.  Jean Pichet was one of the three hundred Founding Families on Île d’Orléans Island.

On 11 Sep 1662, forty-two-year-old Jean Leblanc was killed by Iroquois warriors in an attack on Saint-Pierre Parrish on the Île d’Orléans Island.  Jean’s wife Euphrosine (Nicolet) and their ten-year-old daughter Marie Madeleine Leblanc were now living alone in the Québec settlement in Canada.  Four months later, widow Euphrosine Leblanc (Nicolet) married Elie Dussault in the Québec settlement.
On 27 Aug 1662, Marie-Françoise Gaudry was born to Nicolas Gaudry and Agnés (Morin) in the Québec settlement in Canada.
On 24 Oct 1662, Marie Anne Lemarché was born to Jean Lemarché and Catherine (Hurault) in Vieux, the Québec settlement in Canada.
In 1662, Denise Pilote (Gauthier), wife of Léonard Pilote, arrived from France with their nine-year-old daughter Marguerite and six-year-old son Jean Pilote in the Québec settlement in Canada.

In 1662, twenty-year-old Jean Besset, who was living in Cahors, an old Roman town in southern France, that had fallen into decline and now offered few job opportunities decided, joined the “Carignan-Salières Regiment”.  Jean travel 100 miles north from his home in Cahors to the Loire Valley where he joined the “La Tour’s Company”, which was one of the twenty-five companies in the regiment.

In 1662, Pierre Duchesne sailed from Picardie France to the Québec settlement.  Pierre worked as a “servant” for three years to fulfill his contract for his passage to Canada before becoming a colonist in Canada.

In 1662, Pierre Tremblay and Anne Ozanne (Achon) left the Québec settlement and move to 1.7 acres of land in L’Ange-Gardien, 32 miles upstream from the Québec settlement, on the north shore of the St. Lawrence River.

In 1662, Canada’s new Governor Pierre Dubois Davaugour, gave Étienne Dumay Sr. lot number 2276 in the Québec settlement, which Étienne gave to his brother Jean Dumay.  The lot had an unfinished house, which grew into a three-story structure given the address of 29 Champlain Boulevard (later street) in Quebec City, Canada.  The original house was destroyed by English cannon balls during the “French and Indian War” and was rebuilt to what is now there today.  See the historic Dumay house below.  The Dumay family name later changed to Demers.

blocks.caption: In 1662, Canada’s new Governor Pierre Dubois Davaugour, gave Étienne Dumay Sr. lot number 2276 in the Québec settleme…

blocks.text: In 1662, the “filles à marier” program ended mostly as a failure.  Between 1634 and 1662, only 262 single woman/girls had immigrated to Canada and average of just 10 girls a year.

In 1662, François Bélanger was named trustee of the affairs and guardian of the children of the late Olivier Le Tardif, co-seigneur and Justice of the Peace in Beaupré.  That same year, François sold two oxen to Romain de Trepagny, for the sum of 300 livres, payable in silver, in beaver pelts, or in valid currency.  The next year Beaupré formed its first Sovereign Council, on which François served as one of the principal inhabitants of the region, who had the confidence of both the government authorities and the area’s colonists."

On 5 Feb 1663, a series of big earthquakes occurred in the St. Lawrence River Valley.  Today geologists attribute the earthquakes to the earth’s surface rebounding from the release of the weight and pressure on the Earth’s surface as the last large ice fields melted as the Earth warmed.  There was also a large glacial moraine (earthen dam) that had been created from the dirt and rocks that had been push by the glacial movement that created a large lake in the St. Lawrence River valley.

The history of the 1663 earthquake was captured in letters from Jesuit missionaries that were living in the area at the time.  Father Lalemant described the earthquake event as a series of earth shakings that started in the morning and continued through the night.  The quakes did not cause much damage, but some stone house chimneys collapse, but wooden houses were not greatly affected.  The big impact of the series of earthquakes was to loosen the dirt and rock in the glacial moraine earthen dam that quickly eroded sending a wall of water down the large St. Lawrence River valley destroying everything near the river’s edges, including the area of the lower Québec settlement.

On 13 May 1663, Jean Guyon died in the middle of the series of earth quakes that shook the Beaupré settlement.  Jean Guyon had lived in Canada for 26 years and was on his death bed and the earth quakes may hasten his final hour.  Jean Guyon’s death resulted in a family quarrel that lasted five years over the settlement of his estate.  The Sovereign Council finally had to step in and resolved the estate distribution.

The Native Angier’s tribe, who were feared more than the Iroquois, killed 8 people Beaupré in 1663.  François Bélanger family was spared from the Angier warrior attacks.  François Bélanger later became, according to the census of 1667, one of the most prosperous farmers in Beaupré with 42 acres of land and 13 head of cattle in his stable.

On 4 Mar 1663, Charles Garnier was hired by Guillaume Couillard to clear the land on the river coast of Beaupré.  When Guillaume Couillard died on 4 March 1664, his widow Guilleumette (Hébert) leased eight concessions of land to eight individuals.  Charles Garnier was one and received 3.3 acres fronting property on shore of the St Lawrence River that extended 5.5 miles depth, in what is today the parish of Boischatel.  Charles Garnier also received the hunting and fishing rights within the boundaries of his concession.  Each year Charles gave Guilleumette (Hébert) 80 sols and 48 deniers for the land he was renting.  Charles Garnier’s land was located between rented properties of Jean Grignon and Jacques Nourry.

In 1663, Michel Messier managed to escape from the Iroquois again, after having been reported to have been burned to death.  Michel returned to the Ville Marie settlement and continued to fight against the Iroquois war parties and distinguished himself in the fighting.

In 1663, six-year-old Pierre Charles Le Sueur left Hesdin, Calais, France, an area King Louis XIII had recently taken from the Dutch.  Pierre accompanied a group of Jesuits on a ship that brought him to Canada.  Pierre was sent to the Jesuit mission at Sault Ste Marie, which was located on St. Mary’s River that leaves Lake Superior and flows into Lake Huron, which was an important water route between the two large Great Lakes.

Note:  In 1663, the establishment of a Conseil Souverain, officially restructured the Canada colony and turned the colony into a Royal Province of France ended the Company of the “One Hundred-Associates” involvement in Canada.  From this time on Canada was control by a Governor appointed by the King of France, who was the ceremonial and military head of the French colony.

sections.title: Filles du Roi or “King’s Daughters”
sections.teaser: In 1663, King Louis XIV of France initiated a new program to recruit and transport marriageable women to the Canada colony.  The new recruiting program call…

blocks.text: In 1663, King Louis XIV of France initiated a new program to recruit and transport marriageable women to the Canada colony.  The new recruiting program call the “Filles du Roi” took unmarried women who were living in the cities of Paris, Rouen and other northern cities of France to the French colony in Canada.  French merchants and ship outfitters were paid fifty livres by the French government to cover the cost of the women’s voyage to Canada.  The “Filles du Roi” or “King’s Daughters” program was set up under the sponsorship of the French government as part of the overall strategy of strengthening the Canada Colony until it could stand on its own without economic and military dependence on France.  Young girls in orphanages and girls of single women were recruited to the new program to offer them a better life in Canada.  A screening process required each girl to present her birth certificate and a recommendation from her parish priest or local magistrate stating that she was free to marry.  The girls needed to be twelve-years-old or more and needed to be healthy and strong to undertake the hard frontier work they were expected to encounter.

The big attraction of the “Filles du Roi” program for the girls that took part was they would have  a choice in selecting their new husband as opposed to the “arranged” marriage process that was present in France at the time that was used to select a husband for the young girl regardless of her wishes.

The sea voyage across the North Atlantic Ocean was a long, primitive, and a difficult undertaking. There were no private rooms or even cubicles for the passengers; they all traveled in the ship's hold (cargo space).  There were also the odors of the farm animals that traveled with them--live chickens, pigs and cows--that were brought along to be slaughtered as needed for meals during the duration of the trip.  These conditions might have been tolerable when the weather permitted one to stroll about the deck and breathe the fresh ocean air.  But if the weather turned bad, passengers were confined to the ships hold and were and unable to vent the odors of human and livestock waste.  This was a breeding ground for all manner of bacteria, from which fever, illness and infections such as boils could and did develop.  Once the livestock and fresh food supply was exhausted, the only food available was a kind of hard biscuit that was not very tasty or nourishing, designed only to provide sustenance (keeping one alive). The passengers frequently suffered dysentery, scurvy and the loss of their teeth from this poor diet, which lack of fruits and vegetables.  An estimate 10% of the young “Filles du Roi” girls died during the long sea voyage.

The girls who were chosen had their passage to Canada paid for them and were given some clothes.  They were also given a case with incidentals: ribbon, needle and thread, pins, scissors, comb, gloves, stockings, knives, handkerchief, bonnet, and 2 livres in cash (the French livre was the equivalent of about $14.00 U. S. dollars).  Once the girls arrived in Canada, they were given clothing more suited to the colder Canadian climate.  They were also given lodging and schooled in the tasks that would be required of them as wives in the colony; tasks included cooking, sewing and the making of natural medicines.  This effort would be considered the first formal "home economics" training done in North America.  Men seeking wife would come to the houses where “Filles du Roi” women were staying to select and choose their new wife.  The house mothers--the most notorious of these was Marguerite Bourgeoys--would, in the absence of the young girls’ own families, counsel the girls on making a good choice of their new husband.  A sharp young girl would pay attention as their fellow filles were being courted and learned exactly what to ask the men when their turn to select came up, such as: "How big is your farm?"  "How many children do you have?  How old are you?"  "How many cows do you own?"  "Are your chickens good egg layers?"  The answers provided by the men allowed the girls to quickly decide if a potential suitor was right for them.  However, this game of 20 questions was not exclusive used by the women.  The men also had concerns, mainly about the size of the dowry the girl was bringing to the marriage.  The Canada was new, raw and primitive and a good match was based less on mutual attraction and more on the sum total of goods and skills from both sides that would be brought to the marriage.  The more resources a couple had between them, the better the chances for their survival.  Once the choice was made and the marriage took place, the new couple would receive a gift from the King of France of 50 livres ($700) as a marriage dowry the new wife brought to the marriage.

Once the young “Filles du Roi” girls were married and established in the Canada colony they usually had large families.  Twelve children or more was common and welcome.  The French government granting incentives to couples with large families.  A family with 10 children qualified for 300 livres ($4,200) a year; families with 12 or more could net 400 lives ($5,600) per year.  This government money was considered the first "child tax credit".  Along with caring for all of their children, the “Filles du Roi” women also took care of housecleaning, laundry, cooking and tending the family’s garden, etc.  The young women quickly learned how to cook, preserve, and eat animals they'd never seen before.  They also had to make their own soap and candles.  They were expected to work alongside their husbands at harvest time and learned how to preserve the farm crops that were gathered.  All these household actives were really hard physical work.  The “Filles du Roi” women also faced tremendous losses in this untamed, primitive land teeming with wild animals and hostile Native warriors, both of which could be a danger to the new settlers.  The spread of illness and disease in the new land where doctors would be few and far between was also a major concern.  It was not uncommon for a woman to lose more than one child to stillbirth, sickness, or misfortune.  Some of the women were widowed multiple times; and the reverse also occurred, with men having to bury more than one wife.

In all about 768 women were recruited and sent to Canada as “filles du Roi” women.  Twenty-four of the King’s Daughters are directly connected to the Mary Ann Goodwater’s Branch of Winifred Elois Coss’s Family tree.
On 22 Sep 1663, thirty-eight girls of the first group of filles du Roi women arrived in Québec City.
By 1663, the Québec settlement had grown from the small settlement of thirty homes back in 1650 to become small city in thirteen-years with over one hundred homes and over 500 residents.
In 1664, an English fleet seized the Dutch “New Netherland” colony in America and divided the seized area into the New York and New Jersey English colonies.

In Jan 1664, Florian LeClerc, the second husband of Marie (Gendre) died in Trois-Rivières, leaving Marie to raise her seven children, including twelve-year-old daughter Marie Madeleine Bourgery, while Trois-Rivières continued to come under Iroquois warrior attacks.
On 15 Aug 1664, Marie-Madeleine Vachon was born to Paul Vachon and Marguerite (Langlois) in Québec City in Canada.

On 28 Oct 1664, Charles Garnier was living on his land on the Beaupré coasts along the St. Lawrence River about ¾ of a mile east of the Montmorency River junction.  Father Louis Ango des Maizerets celebrated Mass in the house of Jean Trudelle, before the election of the first vestrymen for the parish of L’Ange Gardien, French for “the guardian angel” settlement.  Charles was singled out as one of the 22 most eligible men in the area, referring to the fact his was single and a successful farmer.

On 28 Oct 1664, Pierre Mahue, Raymond Paget and Jacques Goulet, purchase the land of Pasquier Nony dit Larose in the Chateau Richer settlement, for the price of £800.  Later in the 1667 census, Pierre was shown to have four animals with horns and 8.3 acres of land and was working as a “weaver” (someone who turned yarn into fabric).  In 1664, Pierre Maheu was made a churchwarden in the Chateau Richer church, which was 15 miles east and down the St. Lawrence River from the Québec City.

On 21 Dec 1664, after a marriage contract ceremony before notary Claude Auber.  Charles Garnier and Louise Vezina were then married in Chateau Richer, Canada.  Louise’s parents Jacques Vezina and Marie (Boisdon) deposited 200 livres ($2,800) in the marriage basket, as well a clothing and linens, dishes and household utensils for the newlyweds.  Louise Vesina was one of the 262 “filles à marier” women”.  (The new “filles du Roi” process was just starting and a few of the older “filles à marier” women were still in the process of coming to Canada to marry).  The life of Charles and Louise was hard and challenging.  Six of their children died as children.  In 1703, a smallpox epidemic occurred that killed two of their daughters’ husbands.

sections.title: Carignan-Salières Regiment
sections.teaser: In 1665, the pleas of the colonists of Canada for assistance in their struggle with the Native American Iroquois Confederation of tribes were answered with t…

blocks.text: In 1665, the pleas of the colonists of Canada for assistance in their struggle with the Native American Iroquois Confederation of tribes were answered with the arrival of the first large number of French regular troops in Canada, the Carignan-Salières Regiment.  The Regiment had been delayed in La Rochelle France, to allow Catholic Jesuits to convert the Protestant Huguenots soldiers who were members of the regiment to Catholicism.  An additional delay occurred to allow time to construct canoes the regiment would need to travel up the St Lawrence River.

Between Jun and Sep 1665, some of 1,200 soldiers and their officers arrived in Québec, under the leadership of Lt. General Alexander de Prouville, Sieur de Tracy.  The Regiment established a series of forts along the Richelieu River and conducted a successful campaign into the land of the Mokawk-Iroquois.  The defeat of the Mohawk-Iroquois, led to a long period of peace for the French colony, which permitted it to prosper.  King Louis XIV's plan also allowed the Carignan-Salières Regiment soldiers at the completion of the conflict to stay and become settlers in Canada.  404 of the Regiment’s soldiers accepted offers of land in Canada and remained in the colony, many of whom married the newly arrived “filles du Roi” girls.  Seven (7) of the Carignan-Salières Regiment’s soldiers are directly connected to the Mary Ann Goodwater Branch of the Winifred Eloise Coss’ family tree.
In 1665, Jean Talon arrived with the Carignan-Salières Regiment as Quebec Colony’s first administrative officer over the settlement’s agriculture, education, justice, and trade.

On 28 Feb 1665, Pierre Gaudin received a concession (contract to work) in “Our Lady of the Angles church” in Charlesbourg north of the Québec City.  Pierre moved his family including seven-year-old daughter Catherine Gaudin from Montréal to Charlesbourg a borough of Québec City.
On 26 May 1665, forty-six-year-old Léonard Pilote died in Beauport, Canada.

On 18 Jun 1665, René Dumats and Mathieu Gervais, French soldiers in the “Carignan-Salières Regiment” arrived in Canada on the ship “L’Aigle d’Or” as a member of the “Grandfontaine Company”.  Nineteen-year-old Mathieu Gervais was assigned to the Fort Chambly on the Richelieu River near Québec City.

On 19 Jun 1665, Jean Bessett a French soldier in the “Carignan-Salières Regiment” left La Rochelle, France on the ship “Le Vieux Simêon” and arrived in Québec City, Canada as a member of the “La Tour Company” commanded by Captain Marquis.  The trip across the Atlantic Ocean was a difficult one lasting more than 100 days.  Upon arriving the La Tour Company spent the winter in the Québec City.  Jean used the dit nickname of “Brisetout” when he became a settler in Canada.
In 1665, Anne Bellesoeur sailed from France as a contracted “filles du Roi” woman and arrived in the Québec City.
On 12 Oct 1665, Nicolas Massard and Anne Bellesoeur were married in Québec City.  Anne was able to contribute a £50 dowry to the marriage as part of her “filles du Roi” contract.

On 14 Sep 1665, Louis Robert, a French soldier in the “Laubia Company” of the “Carignan-Salières Regiment”, sailed out of the port La Rochelle on the ship “Le Saint-Sébastien”, a large 250-ton ship which carried the “DePortes”, “Dugué”, “La Varenne” and “Laubia” companies of the “Carignan-Salières Regiment”.  When the ship arrived in Québec City the soldiers were transferred small boats and canoes and rowed and paddled up the St. Lawrence River to the Trois-Rivières settlement, which was experiencing repeated Iroquois warrior attacks.  Louis Robert was billeted (live with) widow Marie Gendre and her seven children, including thirteen-year-old daughter Marie Madeleine Bourgery in the Trois-Rivières fort.
On 17 Nov 1665, Sébastien Gingras and Marie Genevieve Guillebourg were married in Sillery, Canada.

In 1665, eighteen-year-old Jacques Samson, left Normandy, France with his older brother Gabriel and sailed to Canada.  Jacques was under a three-year contract to cover the cost of his trip to Canada and worked as a “house servant” for Gervais Bush in Sillery a community on the north shore of the St. Lawrence River in Québec City.

In 1665, Vincent Chrétien left Tours, Centre-Val de Loire Tours, France after his two brothers were beheaded by young French King Louis XIV who was seeking glory on the battlefield.  Vincent arrived from France and settled in Sainte-Famille on the I’Île d’Orléans Island.  Vincent Chrétien would become one of the three hundred Founding Families on the Île d’Orléans Island.

In 1665, Michel Messier was named Sieur de Saint Michel when the Sulpicians (congregation of secular Roman Catholic priests founded in 1642 by a priest of St. Sulpice, Paris, to train candidates for holy orders) gave Michel twenty-five acres of land above the twenty-five areas he already owned.  On his land Michel raised seven head of cattle and grew crops on twenty of the acres.  Michel Messier was made a lieutenant in the Ville Marie settlement’s militia.
In 1665, Catherine Rivet left Rennes, Brittany, France as a “filles du Roi” woman and sailed to Québec City to marry.
The Île d’Orléans Island was divided into six Parish Municipalities.  See a map on the next page shows the Parish’s in the island.

blocks.caption: The Île d’Orléans Island was divided into six Parish Municipalities. See a map on the next page shows the Parish’s in…

blocks.text: In 1665, Louis Lefébvre with his wife Suzanne (Debure) and their four-year-old daughter Marie Angélique left from Rouen, Normandy, France and sailed by ship to Québec City.  Marie Angélique Lefébvre was listed as a “filles du Roi” woman even though she was only four-years-old when she arrived in Québec.  She did not marry for ten more years.  Wife Suzanne’s sister, Angelique, also traveled with family on the trip to Canada.  Signing up their four-year-old daughter as a “filles du Roi” woman may have been what was needed to allowed the family pay for the trip to Canada.

In 1665, Françoise Langlois (Garnier) wife of Noël Langlois Sr. died in an accident.  Nine months later Noël remarried an old friend, Marie Crevet, the widow of Robert Caron.  In the marriage contract Noël agreed to look after Marie’s young daughter Aimee Caron.  In time chemistry developed between Aimee and Noël’s son Noël Jr.  Noël Langlois Sr. sheltered soldiers of the “Carignan-Salières Regiment” who were in Canada to protect the settlers from the waring Iroquois.  One of the soldiers was a dashing young officer, Francois Laumonier de Traversy.  The Iroquois killed Traversy while he was hunting on the shores of Lake Champlain.  Young Noël Jr. decided to take a “dit name” to differentiate himself from his father, he chose the name Traversy.

In 1666, the first “Census of Canada” was conducted in Québec (and indeed in North America).  It was organized by Jean Talon, the first Intendant of Canada, between 1665 and 1666.  A statue of Jean Talon is located in front of the Quebec Parliament Building.  Talon and the French Minister of the Marine, Jean-Baptiste Colbert had brought the colony of Canada under direct royal control in 1663, and Colbert wished to make it the center of the French colonial empire.  To do this he needed to know the state of the population, so that the economic and industrial basis of the colony could be expanded.  Jean Talon conducted the census largely by himself, travelling door-to-door among the settlements of Canada.  He did not include Native American inhabitants of the colony, or the religious orders such as the Jesuits and Récollets.  According to Talon's census there were 3,215 people in Canada in 538 separate families.  The census showed a difference in the number of men at 2,034 versus 1,181 women.  Children and those who were unmarried were grouped together and there were 2,154 of them, while only 1,019 people were married (42 were widowed).  547 people lived in the Québec City, 455 in Trois-Rivières settlement, and 625 in Ville Marie.  The largest single age group, 21-30-year-olds, numbered 842.  763 people were professionals of some kind, and 401 of these were servants, while 16 were listed as "gentlemen of means".

On 6 Jan 1666, twenty-seven-year-old Laurent Migneron in Chizé, Nouvelle-Aquitaine, France, where he was living and working as a “carpenter”, went before government official Claude Auber and gave everything he owned to Marie Monnier as a deposit to French King Louis XIV.  Shortly after the transaction was completed Laurent Migneron sailed over to Canada.  A mandate by King Louis XIV, exercised by Governor Francois Montmorency, granted Laurent Migneron a parcel of land 28 miles northeast of Quebec City in the parish of Saint Joachim of the community of Beaupré.  The land was registered later in 1680 as being located west of the Saint Anne River where it empties into the St. Lawrence River.
On 7 Jan 1666, Pierre Duchesne and Catherine Rivet were married in Montmorency, Canada.  Catherine Rivet was one of the newly arrived “filles du Roi” women.
In 1666, eighteen-year-old Mathurine Goard arrived in Ville Marie settlement as one of the  “filles du Roi” women.

On 9 Jan 1666, the soldiers of the French “Carignan-Salières Regiment” invaded the Iroquois homeland south of Lake Ontario.  The French invasion force of 600 solders was led by the new Governor General of Canada, Daniel de Remy de Courcelle.  A party of Algonquin Native warriors were expected to guide the French army but they failed to appear.  The French soldiers, carrying their arms and supplies on their backs were unaccustomed to marching in snow-shoes in deep powdery snow and quickly became exhausted.  The intense cold weather made their ordeal even worse.  Without native guides the French army got lost and frequently had to retrace their path.  By 15 Feb 1666, the 600 French soldiers were completely lost and luckily stumbled upon the Schenectady settlement Province of New York, which was 20-miles northwest of the town of Albany New York that was recently taken from the Dutch by the British, two-years earlier.

Later in July 1666, being aware that the French army was preparing a new attack on Mohawk lands, a group of Mohawk warriors ambushed some French officers in Lake Champlain area, killing seven and taking four prisoner.  In response, Canada’s Governor de Tracy had several members of an Oneida Iroquois delegation, who were in the Quebec City at the time, held as captives.  Chief Canaqueese and three other Mohawk leaders traveled to the Quebec  City to exchange prisoners with the French and ratify a peace treaty between the Iroquois and the French.  These Mohawk leaders were troubled by the lack of support that their English and Dutch allies were providing, and had decided to seek peace with the French.  The delegation arrived on 28 Aug 1666, but the talks soon broke down and Governor de Tracy imprisoned the four Mohawk leaders.

By the fall of 1666, violence between the French and Mohawk resumed.  A second French invasion force led by Viceroy Alexandre de Prouville, with of about 1,300 men of the “Carignan-Salières Regiment” moved out looking for Iroquois.  They found deserted Mohawk villages and destroy them and the crops stored and growing in the area.  Viceroy Prouville seized all the Mohawk lands in the name of the king of France.  Chief Canaqueese was released from custody by Intendant Jean Talon, and sent back to Mohawk country with an official offer of peace.  The peace offer required the Mohawk’s to release some of their Huron, Algonquin, and French captives, who they had been adopted into Mohawk tribes’ villages and the Iroquois native society.  After Chief Canaqueese made a series of diplomatic missions in spring of 1667 to negotiate with French, on provisions of a peace treaty between the Mohawks and French an agreement was finally achieved and the Mohawk released some of their captives.  The peace settlement lasted for more than a decade.  Some of the Mohawk people later migrated to Ville Marie settlement area and settling nearby in the southern part of the St. Lawrence Valley, including Chief Canaqueese himself in 1667.

Chief Canaqueese was a  war chief and intercultural mediator who lived in the Mohawk Valley, an area of central present-day New York State.  He was of mixed race, with a Mohawk mother and Dutch father.  Canaqueese was brought up and identifying himself as Mohawk.  He was an important  between the  and  since he spoke the Dutch, Algonquian and the Iroquoian languages.  The Mohawk people lived in a matrilineal kinship system where children were considered born into their mother's clan and deriving their status from their mother family.  The mother's eldest brother was more important to the children than their biological father.  Chief Canaqueese was brought up by his Iroquois Mohawk mother and the Mohawk tribal people, while he also understood Dutch culture from his father.

During the fighting between Iroquois and the “Carignan-Salières Regiment”, French prisoners were found in an Iroquois village and where rescued.  In the case of freeing twelve-year-old Marie Madeleine Bourgery from the Iroquois a problem occurred when Marie ran into the forest and hide not wanting to be taken captive by the French soldiers.  Marie was found and force to leave her Iroquois adopted mother.  Marie during her six-years of captive with the Iroquois had completely adopted the native Iroquois way-of-life and did not want to leave and return to her French mother and family.  The French soldiers took twelve-year-old  Marie Madeleine Bourgery back to Québec City and placed her with the “Ursuline Sisters” to be rehabilitation back into French culture.

Note:  The Ursuline Sisters were a religious order founded in the sixteenth century for the education of young girls.  In the early part of the seventeenth century an urgent appeal came from the Canada Colony for religious women to undertake the arduous task of training native Indian girls in Christian habits of life.  Madame de la Peltrie, a French widow, offered herself and all that she had to fund a mission in Canada.  In May, 1639, Madame de la Peltrie sailed from the French port of Dieppe France in company with three Ursuline Sisters and three Catholic nuns.  In the Québec settlement, she founded the first institution of learning for the Native people the French had come in contact with.

Once peace was achieved with the French, the Iroquois Confederation began a westward expansion of conquest.  The tribes such as the Lakota (Teton Sioux) were pushed across the Mississippi River onto the American Great Plains, where they adopted the horse culture and nomadic lifestyle for which they later became known.  In the Ohio River Valley region, the Shawnee and Miami tribes, were quickly overrun and the Shawnee tribe was pushed into Miami tribe’s territory.  To better defend itself the Miami tribe joined in a confederacy with the local Pottawatomie tribe and the Illinois Confederation containing the Cahokia, Kaskaskia, Michigamea, Peoria, and Tamaroa tribes.

The Iroquois improved on their warfare as they continued to attack even farther west from their homeland.  Iroquois war parties often traveled by canoes on rivers at night, and then the Iroquois warriors would sink their canoes and fill them with rocks to hold them on the river bottom.  They would then move through the woods to a target and burst from the wood to cause the greatest panic in an opposing Native village.  After their attack, they would refloat their canoes and leave before any significant resistance could be put together.  The lack of European firearms put the opposing tribes at a great disadvantage allowing the Iroquois to defeat larger number to defending warriors.  Several tribes gave up and moved west beyond the Mississippi River, leaving much of the Ohio Valley, southern Michigan, and southern Ontario depopulated of its original Native people.  The Iroquois’ advance and expansion was finally stopped by a confederation of Native tribes north of Lakes Huron and Superior.  See the map below showing the location of the major Native American tribes

blocks.caption: The Iroquois improved on their warfare as they continued to attack even farther west from their homeland. Iroquois wa…

blocks.text: On 26 Apr 1666, Charles Maheu was born to Pierre Maheu and Jeanne (Drouin) in Chateau Richer, Canada.

On 6 Sep 1666, Laurent Migneron and Anne Saint-Denis were married in Chateau-Richer, Canada.  Anne's parents Pierre Saint-Denis and Vivienne (Brunel) gave the couple a milk cow and Anne’s bride garments as wedding gifts.  Both Laurent and Anne signed their names with “X” indicating they could not read or write.   The new family began life in a small wooden shack with few worldly goods.

On 25 Nov 1666, Louis Robert and Marie Madeleine Bourgery were married in Trois- Rivières, Canada.  Louis Robert a soldier who had lived with Marie’ s mother Marie (Gendre) in the Trois-Rivières fort during the Iroquois raids wanted to marry the young twelve-year-old Marie Madeleine Bourgery who had just completed her rehabilitation with the “Ursuline Sisters” after her release from her captivity with the Iroquois.  Because of her young age Marie Madeleine continued to live with her mother during the beginning of her marriage until she was sixteen.  Louis lived in Trois-Rivières working as a “cobbler” (shoemaker).
In the 1666 census for Beauport Canada Nicolas Bélanger was listed working as a “salt merchant”.

Note:  Historically, a saunier, (French salt merchant) harvested salt from salt marshes.  Because of the high taxes levied on salt in France, salt merchants faced intense competition from contraband sellers of salt.  The contraband traffickers traded in salt without paying the government tax.  French had put in place a high salt that affected all citizens (salt was used for cooking, preserving food, making cheese and raising livestock), the salt tax was one of the most hated taxes in France’s history.  Hundreds of French convicted salt contraband salt traffickers were deported to Canada to work as “forced labourers”.  The salt tax was repealed during the French Revolution and reinstated by Napoleon Bonaparte in 1806, the salt tax was finally abolished in France in 1945.

In the 1666 census for Quebec City, Jean Carrier was listed working in the Hotel-Dieu Hospital of Québec.  At the hospital Jean meet twenty-one-year-old Barbe Halay, who was in the care of the Catholic hospital’s sisters.  Barbe Halay was being cared in the hospital according to records by Catholic Bishop Lordship Francois de Laval and the Jesuits’ because, she was “possessed by the devil”.  Barbe was living under the guardianship of Mother Catherine de Saint-Augustin.  After several attempts at exorcism, the “evil angel” was finally driven out of Barbe.  After her release Barbe worked for a few years at the Hôtel-Dieu de Québec.

Note:  Exorcism was a process used to cast out demons from someone.  The Christians exorcism process was performed by a member of a Christian Church thought to be graced with special powers or skills.  The exorcist used prayers and religious material, such as set formulas gestures, symbols, icons, amulets, etc.  The exorcist often invokes God Jesus or several different angels to intervene with the exorcism.

In 1666, Denise Pilote (Gauthier), a year after her husband Léonard Pilote died, assigned a guardian, Jean Drouard, to administer the inheritance left by her husband to their three children.  Jean Drouard was a domestic of the Jesuits (working in the Jesuit’ quarters cleaning and performing menial tasks).  Denise’s young ten-year-old son Jean Pilote, joined Jean Drouard in performing domestic tasks for the Jesuits.  Denise Pilote (Gauthier) remarried in Feb 1667, Robert Lefébvre who, promised to provide for her and her three children.

In 1666, Jean Pichet and Marie Madeleine Leblanc were married on the island of  Île d'Orléans in Canada.  Marie’s mother Euphrosine Madeleine Nicolet was the daughter of the famous explorer-interpreter Jean Nicolet and is his Nipissing Indian princess partner, Jeanne Bahmahmaadjimiwin Giisis.

In the 1666 Canada Census, Toussaint Giroux and his family were living in Beauport in a house Toussaint had built and Toussaint was worked as a “weaver” (someone who turned yarn into fabric).  Toussaint had two cows and sixteen acres of land he was farming.
In the 1666 Canada Census, François Bélanger owned thirty-four acres of cropland along the Beaupré shoreline on the north side of the St. Lawrence River.
About 1666, Louis Marier left Tours, Centre-Val de Loire, France and sailed to Canada and settled in Ville Marie settlement.
On 2 Jan 1667, Marie Charlotte Garnier was born to Charles Garnier and Louise (Vezina) in Chateau Richer, Montmorency, Canada.
On 20 Feb 1667, Anne Marie Massard was born to Nicolas Massard and Anne (Bellesoeur) in the Seignory of Lauzon, Canada.
On 31 Mar 1667, Louis Marier and Mathurine Goard, one of the “filles du Roi” women, were married in Ville Marie settlement in Canada.

On 11 Apr 1667, Madeleine Duchesne was born to Pierre Duchesne and Catherine (Rivet) in Saint Francois Parish on the Île d'Orléans Island in Canada.  Pierre and Catherine were one of the three hundred Founding Families on the Île d'Orléans Island.

In 1667, eighteen-year-old Anne Le Seigneur, whose father Guillaume Le Seigneur had recently died was now an orphan and was selected to be one of the “filles du Roi” girls who were sent to the Québec Canada to marry one of the many eligible bachelors looking for a wife in Canada.

On 7 Jul 1667, Anne Migneron (St. Denis) and three other women were taken before a Judge because to insults they had made to Nicolas Huot and their hitting him with a wooden stick.  The four women were sentenced to stand before the Judge of Beaupré, in the presence of six witnesses, and beg for a pardon from Nicolas Huot.  They were also fined 25 livres each for the moral attack.

On 25 Jul 1667, Martin Adroche Côté and Suzanne Pagé were married in Chateau-Richer, Canada.  Martin Côté and Suzanne (Pagé) lived in the Saint Peter Parish on the Île d’Orléans island across from Suzanne’s parents and her siblings.

In the 1667 Canada Census, Pierre Maheu and his wife Jeanne (Drouin) and their one-year-old son Charles were living in the parish of L’Ange-Gardien in Beaupré, Canada, on a ten-acre farm with four livestock.

In 1667, Paul Vachon had two exceptional commissions entrusted to him in quick succession.  On 3 Nov 1667, Marie-Barbe de Boullongne, Governor General d’Ailleboust’s widow, appointed Paul Vachon a seigneurial attorney and notary in her Argentenay fief on the Île d’Orléans and a week later on 10 Nov 1667, Bishop Laval named Paul to the same offices in his Beauport and Île d’Orléans seigneuries.  Paul as an attorney, did not forget he came from country stock, and never failed to work his land.  In 1666 Paul had an “indentured domestic,” twenty-two-year-old Michel Aubin, who worked Paul’s farm where he raised 7 head of cattle and cultivated 20 acres of farmland.  Later in 1681 Paul still had an “indentured domestic”, 13 head of cattle, and 35 acres of farmland under cultivation.  In addition to his land at Beauport, Paul had another eight acres on the island of Île d’Orléans, which was worked by another tenant farmer.

In the 1667 Canada Census, Pierre Saint-Denis and his wife Vivienne (Brunel) and their son were living on 60 acres with 38 animals in Beaupré on the north shore of the St. Lawrence River down steam from Québec City.

In the 1667 Canada Census, Noël Morin and Hélène (Desportes) were living on Île d'Orléans Island on 40 acres with twelve head cattle and a “domestic worker” they had hired named Jean Ballie.  Noël, who was a trained “cartwright” (someone who makes and repairs carts and wagons) and skilled in making large metal kettles or boilers had an apprentice Zacharie Jolliet working for him learning “cartwright” skills.

In the 1667 Canada Census, Nicolas Gaudry and his wife Agnes (Morin) and their children, including daughter Marie Françoise Gaudry, were living on a farm in a rural area of Québec City on 6.7 acres with 4 cows.

In the 1667 Canada Census, Jean Baptiste Halay, his wife Mathurine (Valet) and their two children, were listed as living in Lauzon, Lévis, Canada.  Twenty-two-year-old daughter Barbe Halay was gone and now living and working in Québec City after her stay with the Quebec Hospitaller’s Nuns, where the devil had been successfully driven out of her.
In 1667, Paul Vachon and his wife Marguerite (Langlois) moved out to the Île d'Orléans Island to farm.
In 1667, Jean Gautier sailed from Blancafort, Centre-Val de Loire, France to Quebec City and then he moved on to the Ville Marie settlement.

In 1667, seventeen-year-old Guillaume Labelle accepted an offer by Jean Talon, who was recruiting young men to go to Canada to start a new life.  Guillaume Labelle left Lisieux, Normandy, France and sailed on a small ship, crowded with other passengers, baggage and livestock giving the small ship the appearance of an overcrowded Noah’s Ark.  After reaching Quebec City, Guillaume moved on and settled in the Ville Marie settlement.  Guillaume was not taught how to read and write and signed his name with an “X”.

In the winter of 1667 Canada Census, sixteen-year-old Joseph Bonneau had left his parents was working as an “indentured servant” in the home of Noel Jeremie in Sillery, Canada.  Joseph Bonneau had contracted himself to a 36-month contract to work for Noel Jeremie.  During this contract period Joseph Bonneau learned the “mason trade” (building structures with bricks or stones).

In the 1667 Canada Census, Robert Drouin, and his second wife Marie (Chapelier) and their five children were living at home in Chateau-Richer in Canada.  Robert owned six head of cattle and nine acres of cultivated farmland.  See below a later photo of Robert Drouin’s brick home:

blocks.caption: In the 1667 Canada Census, Robert Drouin, and his second wife Marie (Chapelier) and their five children were living a…

blocks.text: In 1668, the Mohawk-Iroquois sued for peace and the soldiers of the “Carignan-Salières Regiment” were demobilized and returned to France, but about 400 French soldiers chose to stay and remain in Canada.  The French King Louis XIV granted the sum of one hundred francs and food for one year to each soldier who chose to stay in Canada.

On 11 May 1668, Monsignor de Laval, an extraordinary Catholic bishop visited the Ville-Marie settlement and confirmed 48 young candidates, one of whom was eighteen-year-old Guillaume Labelle.  The conformation ceremony (affirmation of one belief) took place in the simple Notre-Dame chapel.  Conformation is one of three sacraments in the Catholic faith the others are Baptism and Holy Communion.

On 3 Jul 1668, with the demobilization of the “Carignan-Salières Regiment”, Jean Besset had to make a decision of either staying in Canada and figuring out how to make a living or sailing  back to France and try to start over.  Jean elected to stay in Canada.  Antoine Adhemar drew up his first notarial act, a marriage contract for Jean Besset in Fort Chambly where he was living and nineteen-year-old Anne le Signeur a “filles du Roi” girl, who had arrived in Canada a year earlier.  Jean Besset and Anne Le Seigneur were married in Chambly, Canada.  Anne was accompanied by squire Jacques D’Harcinval, a nobleman and an officer in the “Carignan-Salières Regiment”, who witnessed the ceremony in her favor.  Jean-Baptiste de Poitiers, a soldier in the Regiment was best man for Jean Besset and his companion-in-arms.  The couple spent the winter of 1668/9 at Fort Saint-Louis on the hill above Québec City, which was used primarily as a warehouse for storing supplies and munitions.

Note:  Back in 1609, Samuel de Champlain passed through an area that later became the site of the town of Chambly.  Samuel wrote the following in his journal:  “The approach to the rapids is a sort of lake into which the water flows down, and it is about three leagues in circumference.  Nearby are meadows where no Natives people live, by reason of recent wars.  At the rapids there is very little water, but it flows with great swiftness, and there are many rocks and boulders, so that the Native people cannot go up by water; but on the way back they run them very nicely.  All this region is very level and full of forests, vines and butternut trees.  No European Christian have ever visited this land and we had all the misery of the world trying to paddle the river upstream.”

Chambly was the home to the massive Fort Chambly, built with local stone between 1709 and 1711 in the style of classic French fortifications.  The fort was built on land south of the St. Lawrence River at the mouth of a large basin, on the site of successive wooden forts dating back to 1665.  Fort Chambly was the largest in a series of fortifications on the shores of what was known as the Iroquois River (later known as the Chambly River, finally becoming the Richelieu River).  The site was originally called Fort Saint-Louis, but soon came to be known by the name of its first commanding officer, Jacques de Chambly, to whom the surrounding seigniory was granted in 1672.  It was intended to protect Canada in general (and Ville Marie in specific) from attacks of hostile native warriors and English soldiers.  The map below shows the current locations of the cities of Chambly and Boucherville and the painting on the right is of Fort Chambly:

images.caption: Chambly was the home to the massive Fort Chambly, built with local stone between 1709 and 1711 in the style of classi…
Chambly was the home to the massive Fort Chambly, built with local stone between 1709 and 1711 in the style of classi…

blocks.text: On 19 Oct 1668, Marie Therese Bélanger was born to Nicolas Bélanger and Marie (de Rainville) in Beauport, Canada.
In 1668, twenty-three-year-old Marie Anne Leclerc left Dieppe, Normandy, France and sailed to Québec City, Canada, as one of the "filles du Roi" women.
In 1668, Vincent Chrétien and Marie Anne Leclerc were married in St Famille-de Île d'Orléans, Canada.

In 1668, Lt. Michel Messier and his brother-in-law Jacques le Moyne received the fief (land) of “Cap de la Trinite”.  When they divided the land in 1676, Lt. Michel Messier’s portion was called “Cap Saint Michel”.  Michel added another large parcel of land next to his “Cap Saint Michel” estate in 1678.

Note:  In 1669, King Louis XIV (the Sun King) of France made a considerable effort to populate Canada.  He ordered 64,000 livres be given to the Canada colony to recruit 500 men and women and sent twelve mares, two stallions and fifty sheep to the colony.
On 10 Jan 1669, Joseph Normand was born to Jean Normand and Marie Anne (Le Laboureur) in Québec City, Canada.

On 21 May 1669, Jeanne Françoise Ménard was born to Jacques Ménard and Catherine (Fortier) in Trois-Rivières settlement in Canada.  Shortly after, Jacques Ménard moved his family up the St. Lawrence River near the Ville Marie settlement to Boucherville, located on the southern shore of the St. Lawrence River at the urging of Pierre Brucher de Grosbois, the founder of the new town.

On 22 Jun 1669, forty-eight-year-old Nicolas Gaudry died in Sillery, Canada leaving his wife twenty-eight-year-old  Agnés (Morin) alone to raise their eight children, including seven-year-old daughter Marie-Françoise Gaudry.

On 7 Sep 1669, the Sovereign Council for Chateau Richer confiscated the land of Jacques Nourry, a neighbor of Charles Garnier.  Jacques Nourry had been executed back in 19 Apr 1669 after being found guilty for molesting the four-year-old daughter of Nicholas Leroy.  The confiscated land was given to Charles Garnier, thus doubling his land holdings.

On 10 Oct 1669, in Ville-Marie settlement, notary Bénigne Basset signed an “amnesty of debtors” in the hope of encouraging people to live in Ville Marie settlement.  Most current settlers’ debts were enormous; each family’s debt was larger than their annual wages.  The debts were wiped out by Nurse Jeanne Mance and her generous donors.

On 19 Oct 1669, eighteen-year-old Joseph Bonneau least land from Etienne Beaufils in the Saint-Francois parish at the east end of I’Île d’Orléans Island between Pierre Duchesne and the area’s manor house.  Joseph Bonneau had recently completed his 36-month indentured servitude working for Noel Jeremie.  During this period Joseph learned the “mason” trade.
On 21 Oct 1669, Louise Tremblay was born to Pierre Tremblay and Anne Ozanne (Achon) in L’Ange-Gardien, Québec, Canada as their sixth child.
On 23 Oct 1669, Jean Migneron was born to Laurent Migneron and Anne (Saint-Denis) in Beaupré, Canada.
In 1669, twenty-two-year-old Anne Dequain arrived from France by ship in the Québec City as a “filles du Roi” woman, agreeing to marry in exchange for a dowry from the French King.

On 28 Oct 1669, Francois Lareau and Anne Dequain were married in Québec City, Canada.  Francois made his living as a “carpenter” in Québec City.  Francois and Anne set up their home in Charlesbourg a borough of Québec City west of Beauport.

On 10 Nov 1669, Olivier Charbonneau, after ten years of working in Ville Marie settlement, was finally able to pay off the loan Jeanne Mance had given him to pay for his family’s ship passage from France to Canada.  Through the generous gift of Nurse Jeanne Mance, Olivier was able to keep his savings he had accumulated to pay off his debts.
In 1669, François Bélanger was named Captain of Militia of the Beaupré coast.

Governor Frontenac patterned the Québec militia after the Coast Guard militia in France.  At this time colonial settlements were spread out on the hillsides along the St. Lawrence River in cleared spaces.  A “Captain of the Hill” was appointed to protect several inhabited areas.  As the areas grew larger, they were divided into parishes (counties) and a Captain of Militia was assigned to each parish.  The Militia Captains received orders directly from the Canadian Governor and then passed the information on to the parish residents.  The Militia Captains were also responsible for monitoring their area, conducting military exercises and leading their troops in battle, when necessary.  The Militia Captains received no pay but were honored as representing the government in their area.  The system of “Captains of the hill” along the St. Lawrence River continued on and became the basis of future lines of communications (later railroad, telegraph and telephone systems).

In 1669, after Pierre Gaudin’s work in Charlesbourg was completed and he moved his family back to Lachine, a community on Montreal Island next to the Ville Marie settlement, where he was tasked with building the first chapel for the community.
In 1669, Catherine Bureau left France and sailed to Canada as one if the “Fille du Roi” women to married a Frenchman in Canada.
