In 1669, three French ships carrying supplies and five-hundred men and two women to Canada ran in to a horrific storm off Tadoussac, 135 miles east of the Québec settlement were the St. Lawrence River ends and the Gulf of Saint Lawrence begins as the rivers flows into the Atlantic Ocean.  All the supplies for the colony and most of the passengers and crews were lost in storm.  The storm was probably a hurricane or a strong “Nor’easter” storm.  Marie de Tadoussac wrote a letter to her son about the storm.  The letter still exists.  She described the effect of the storm as follows: “Finally Monsieur Talon has arrived at Québec.  He thought he'd be shipwrecked a second time near Tadoussac, where a year before a strong storm tossed his ship onto the rocks, and set it on its side.  This year his ship remained fixed between the frightful rocks in a place where the tide rises and lowers.  They remained there stuck until it was refloated.  Everyone began to make vows, and to ask our Lord for mercy.  The ship which should have broken into pieces, and everyone lost, was refloated without any rupture; on the contrary by unexpected good fortune, in the spot where it had leaked during the whole crossing, in such a way that they had been obliged to continually pump.“

The Québec settlement was also impacted by the horrific storm as described later in a letter written by Marie de Tadoussac:  “The houses of the lower town of Québec had been greatly damaged, the tide being swelled so extraordinarily, that it rose up to the third floor of houses.  There are still some houses knocked down in the upper town.  The storm had been so violent everywhere that our house shook as if in an earthquake.  The roof and the rafters of the building of our servants had been carried away.  Our wood fences, and those of Monseigneur the Bishop, the Nursing Sisters and the Reverend Fathers have been blown down. ---“

In 1670, the additional support ordered by King Louis XIV for Canada finally arrived when four ships left La Rochelle France, headed by Monsieur Talon, loaded with 250 tons of supplies for the Canada Colony.  The ships arrived in Canada on 20 Aug 1670.  A second group of ships with an additional 300 tons of supplies left later on 30 Jun 1670 and arrived in the Québec City on 15 Sep 1670.  The ship “le Saint-Jean-Baptiste” also sailed that year with150 “filles du Roi” (daughters of the King) adding to the number of settlers in Canada.

Beginning in the 1670s, the French began to explore and settle in the Ohio River Valley, north of the Ohio River out west to its junction with the Mississippi River.  The French established a trading post on the south shore of Lake Michigan called “Tassinong” named after a Potawatomi native village in the area.  The Iroquois warriors quickly destroyed the French trading post to retain control of the fur trade with the Europeans.
On 5 Feb 1670, Catherine Marier was born to Louis Marier and Mathurine (Goard) in the Ville Marie settlement in Canada.

On 18 Mar 1670, Jean Côté was born to Martin Adroche Côté and Suzanne (Pagé) in Saint-Famille-de-I’Île d’Orléans, Canada.  Jean Côté was the grandson of Jean Côté who arrived back in 1634 with Robert Giffard.

On 18 Oct 1670, Joseph Bonneau purchased from Pierre Letat 2.5 acres of farmland with a cabin, shed, outdoor oven, open meadow  and tillable land, on the island of I’Île d’Orléans for 150 French livres, payable in three installments.  The cold Canadian winter was approaching and Joseph had to re-mulch the cabin with earth and fir branches, cut firewood for the cabin’s fireplace to keep warm during the long cold winter coming.  Pierre Letat learned that Canada Intendant Jean Talon was in great need of wood timbers and wood planks and proposed to Joseph Bonneau they worked together and make the needed timbers and planks using a “pit-saw”.  Intendant Jean Talon agree to pay Joseph and Pierre 30 livres for a hundred timbers and 20 livres for one hundred planks.  The two men completed the job just before Easter in the spring.  Joseph Bonneau, used his half of money to buy three more arpents of land on the island.
Note:  A “pit-saw” is a dugout pit over which a trunk of a tree is positioned to be sawed with a long two-handled whipsaw pulled and pushed by men holding handles on the end of the saw.

On 31 Oct 1670, Joseph Bonneau and Marie-Anne Lelong signed a marriage contract, with joint ownership of property.  Bride Marie-Anne was a “Filles du Roi” woman who brought to the marriage a dowry in property of 200 livres with an additional 50 livres as a gift from the king of France.  The next day Joseph Bonneau and Marie-Anne Lelong were married in the church of Sainte-Famille on the island of I’Île d’Orléans.
On 4 Nov 1670, Jean Carrier and Barbe Halay were married in the Cathedrale de Québec City in Canada.  See on the next page photos of the Cathedrale today:

images.caption: On 4 Nov 1670, Jean Carrier and Barbe Halay were married in the Cathedrale de Québec City in Canada. See on the next…
On 4 Nov 1670, Jean Carrier and Barbe Halay were married in the Cathedrale de Québec City in Canada. See on the next…

blocks.text: In 1670, Zacharie Cloutier sold his land in Beauport and moved his family to Chateau Richer on farmland on the north side of the St. Lawrence River across from the Île d'Orléans Island.

In 1670, Jacques Samson having completed his three-year contract of service, partnered with Louis Lefébvre to sell fish in the Lauzon market in Québec City.  Their fish shop sold eels, salmons and sturgeons both fresh and smoked.  The fish shop did well and Jacques bought a house and grounds in Lauzon from Etienne Landeron.  The sale contract was dated 25 Aug 1670.  The price of the house and grounds was 300 cords of firewood delivered each year for the next three years to Etienne Landeron.  The same day Jacques bought 750 pounds of grain in exchange for addition firewood.
About 1670, Jean Jacoti was born to Pierre Jacoti and Marie (Bonnevico) in Perigueux, Nouvelle-Aquitaine, France.

sections.title: Start of the Hudson’s Bay Company
sections.teaser: On 2 May 1670, the “Hudson’s Bay Company” (HBC) was chartered making it the oldest incorporated joint-stock merchandising company in the English-speaking wor…

blocks.text: On 2 May 1670, the “Hudson’s Bay Company” (HBC) was chartered making it the oldest incorporated joint-stock merchandising company in the English-speaking world.  HBC was a fur trading business that was entwined with the colonization of North America, particularly in Canada.  In the 1600s, fur trading emerged as a major commercial enterprise in North America due to European demand for fur felt hats made from beaver pelts.  Two French traders were the first to propose the idea of forming a trading company to reach the interior of the North America via Hudson Bay.  After failing to obtain French support on their idea the French traders went to England in 1665 and interested Prince Rupert, the cousin of England’s King Charles II in their idea.  Prince Rupert persuaded his cousin the king and several English merchants to back the venture.  The “Hudson’s Bay Company” was granted wide powers, including exclusive trading rights in the territory crossed by rivers flowing into Hudson Bay.  This vast region was named “Rupert’s Land” after Prince Rupert.

Unlike most contemporary trading businesses, the HBC evolved as a joint-stock company with a centralized bureaucracy.  At the annual General Meeting shareholders elected a governor and a committee to organize and run the fur trading operations.  The elected governor was responsible with oversee the Rupert Land’s trading posts in North America.  After trapping beavers during the fall and winter when the beaver pelts were at their highest quality, Native people travelled to the HBC trading posts to barter their gathered beaver pelts for European manufactured goods such as metal tools, guns, textiles and foodstuffs.

On 4 Jan 1671, fifty-five-year-old Noël Morin and fifty-one-year-old wife Helene (Desportes), who were living in Québec City realizing they were getting older and signed an agreement giving their household possession and their farm animals to their sons twenty-one-year-old Alphonse and sixteen-year-old Charles on the condition that they would care for their parents until their deaths.  Life, however, doesn’t always go as planned and young son Charles died later in the year.

On 12 Jan 1671, thirty-year-old widow Agnés Morin married twenty-four-old Ignace Bonhomme in Québec City in Canada.  Agnés brought her eight children, including eight-year-old Marie-Françoise Gaudry from her first marriage to Nicolas Gaudry to the new marriage.  Agnés and Ignace Bonhomme had eight more children.

In 1671, twenty-nine-year-old Louis Gesseron Jr., arrives in Québec City where his father Louis Gesseron Sr. had been living since his arrival in 1659.  Louis Gesseron Jr. had remained in La Rochelle France with is mother Marie (Guitard) until her death earlier that year.
On 28 Sep 1671, twenty-nine-year-old Louis Gesseron Jr. and fourteen-year-old Agathe Fournier were married in Québec City in Canada.

On 12 Oct 1671, René Dumats and Marie Lelong were married in Québec City, Canada.  Twenty-five-year-old Marie Lelong as a “Filles du Roi” who had just arrived in Québec City to married René Dumats a French soldier of the “Carignan-Salières Regiment” as part of a marriage contract, they both had agreed to in advance.

In 1671, the victories of the “Carignan-Salières Regiment” over the Mohawk-Iroquois native warriors allowed Noël Morin to feel comfortable enough to establish a homestead on his seigneury out in the forests south of Ville Mare settlement on the Richelieu River.  Owners of sub-seigneuries were required to pay homage to the owner of the larger seigneury.  Accordingly, on 12 Nov 1671 Noël Morin rendered faith and homage to Louis Couillard, the owner seigneurie de la Rivière-du-Sud, of which Noël’s fief Saint-Luc was a part.

At the end of the year in 1671, twenty-one-year-old Guillaume Labelle, who was living in the Villa Marie settlement declared himself ready to start a family.  On the morning of 22 Nov 1671, he wore his Sunday best cloths and went to the home of Sir Jacques Leber, a merchant and husband of the "honorable" Jeanne LeMoyne, accompanied by Jean Petit a resident of Longueuil and a tailor and Benigne Basset, a notary for the Île de Montréal.  Already there waiting for them was Olivier Charbonneau, future father-in-law and Marie Marguerite (Garnier) his wife together with Simon Cardinal, Michelle Garnier, Jacques Cardinal, Mathurin Thibodeau and Catherine Avrard, Charles LeMoyne, lord of Longueuil and Catherine Thierry, François Dollier de Casson a Sulpician priest and "district ecclesiastic superior", and the young bride, fifteen-year-old, Anne Charbonneau.

The next day Guillaume Labelle and Anne Charbonneau were married in the Ville-Marie settlement.  Anne brought to the marriage 300 livres and her parents Olivier Charbonneau and Marguerite pledged 3 blankets, 4 chicken and 60 livres.  Six days after the wedding, on 29 Nov 1671, Guillaume Labelle bought a plot of land from Louis Mary called “Ste-Marie”.  The following year Guillaume returned the original land he acquired from Louis Mary and acquired 60 square acres from Côte St-François for a considerable sum of 500 livers.  Guillaume Labelle’s new lot was number 116 and was situated near his father-in-law, Olivier Charbonneau’s property lot number 120.  Guillaume promised to pay surgeon René Sauvageau, an old soldier of the “Carignan-Salières Regiment” and the owner of the land by sawing 100 roof planks of wood and delivering them by the next Easter as the initial 55 livers payment on the land.  Guillaume Labelle, however, had problem making his next year’s payment and an agreement was reached allowing Guillaume to live on the front edge of the property while he continued to work on the remaining payments he owed.
In 1671, twenty-two-year-old Etiennette Loriot left her Paris home as a “filles du Roi” woman and sailed to Canada to marry an eligible man and raise a family.

In 1671, fifteen-year-old Marie Anne Metru sailed from her home in Paris France, with her father Claude Metru.  Marie’s mother Jeanne (Crissot) had died giving birth to Marie.  Marie had signed up as one of the “filles du Roi” girls.
In 1671, Marie-Jeanne Arquille left France and sailed to Québec City, Canada.
In 1671, Jean François Allard left France and sailed to Québec City, Canada.
In 1671, Jean François Allard and Marie-Jeanne Arquille were married in the Notre-Dame Church in Québec City in Canada.
On 26 Nov 1671, twenty-four-year-old Jacques Samson and fifteen-year-old Marie Anne Metru were married in Québec City, Canada shortly after Marie Anne’s arrival in Québec City with her father.
On 19 Mar 1672, sixty-six-year-old Jean Baptiste Halay was found dead in the woods and was burred the same day.

Note:  The governor of Canada in the 1670s was an ex-military man named Louis de Baude de Frontenac, who was said to be contentious and arrogant.  Governor Frontenac took charge of the French colonies in 1672, having spent much of his life in the French royal court.  He had certain demands for protocol when he became the governor in Canada, and he often clashed with the people around him, including the Canada’s Sovereign Court, which handled all judicial matters.
In 1672, seventeen-year-old Jean Lebeau left France and sailed to Québec City in Canada.  Upon his arrival Jean continued up the St Lawrence River to the Ville Marie settlement where he settled.

On 15 May 1672, Jean Lebeau married twenty-three-year-old Etiennette Loriot in Chambly on the south shore of the St. Lawrence River south of the Ville Marie settlement.  Etiennette was one of the “filles du Roi” women.
On 12 Sep 1672, André Allard was born to Jean Francois Allard and Marie-Jeanne (Arquille) Charlesbourg, Canada a village north of Québec City.
On 25 Oct 1672, Noël Langlois Jr. and Aimee Caron were married in Beauport, Canada.

On 3 Nov 1672, Mr. Talon the Québec Administrative Officer granted 30 acres to Guillaume Fournier and Francoise (Hébert) on the south shore of the St. Lawrence River.  Guillaume Fournier was one of the pioneers and founders of the parish of St. Thomas of Montmagny and later the town of Montmagny, which was named after Charles de Montmagny the governor of Canada at the time.  Montmagny was located on the south shore of the St. Lawrence River and was an important expansion of the French Canada Colony.  The second church in the parish of St. Thomas of Montmagny was built on three acres of land Guillaume Fournier gave the parish as a gift.  The first parish church services had been held in Guillaume Fournier’s house that was one story 22 feet long and 18 feet wide structure with an attic, two windows on the north side and one window on the south side.

In 1672, Louis de Buade, the current Governor of Canada, organized a local militia in Québec City.  François Bélanger was again made Captain of the Beaupré militia.  The Governor in recognition for his service granted Francois new lands on the south shore of the St. Lawrence River in the Islet Parish, with the title of “seigneury of Bélanger”.
In 1672, Jacques Dion left Marans, France and sailed to Québec City in Canada.
In 1672, Jeanne Renard left Rouen, Normandy, France and sailed to Québec City in Canada.

On 26 Apr 1672, Jacques Dion and Jeanne Renard were married in Québec City in Canada.  Jacques Dion had recently immigrated from France to Canada.  Jeanne Renard was one of the “filles du Roi” woman, who has also recently left France and sailed to Canada to marry.

On 4 Jun 1672, French King Louis XIV issued an ordinance requiring all uncleared seigneurial lands in Canada to be returned to the royal domain.  The measure provided an immediate incentive for landowners to get out and develop their property.

The 1672, King Louis XIV ordinance requiring all uncleared seigneurial lands to be returned to the royal domain spurred Noël Morin to clear and improve his Saint Luc seigneury.  Noël gave a parcel of his land to his son Alphonse Morin and another parcel to his son-in-law Guillaume Fournier, the husband of his wife Helene’s daughter from her first marriage to Guillaume Noël and then granted portions of his estate of Saint Luc to his wife Helene’s (Desportes) children.

Note:  Starting in 1673, the process of disposing human waste in Québec City by throwing it from the windows and doorways of homes and cottages into the city’s streets, which was the custom back in France was finally stopped in Québec City.  In 1673, legislation was put in place making it the law that new homes had to be built with latrines (Indoors) or privies (outdoors) on the property.  Older homes, where there was no room for a latrine, required the owner to clean the street in front of their home every day.  The practice in the Lower Town in Québec City of allowing pigs to roam freely was also stopped.  French King Louis XIV’s grand Palace of Versailles had no bathrooms and those who were there without personal servants with chamber pots were force to use the stairways as their bathroom in the palace.
On 22 Feb 1673, Joseph Bonneau sold the 2.5 acres of forested land he had acquired back in the fall of 1670 from Maurice Arrive for 24 minots of wheat from Gregoire Grondin.

In the Summer 1673, Noël Langlois Jr., called himself, “Sieur de Traversy”.  Noël Jr. loved to hunt, fish and the military life and he accompanied Governor Frontenac on an expedition west to the area of present-day Kingston, at the beginning of the St. Lawrence River where it leaves Lake Ontario.  Because Noël Jr. distinguished himself on this venture Governor Frontenac promised him a Seigneury.  When reminded of his promise, Frontenac granted a fief on the south shore of the St. Lawrence River.  Noël Langlois Jr. called his land “Port Joli”.  It was not good farmland, but was excellent for the hunting and fishing, which Noël Jr. enjoyed.  It also contained old large oak trees that provided large oak spars, used to hold up a ship’s sails, for his brothers' shipbuilding business.  Noël Jr. never was able to develop his Seigneury.  His father Noël Langlois Sr. was old and wanted him to stay at home to look after him and his second wife.  To this end Noël Langlois Sr. assigned all his assets and debts to the son.  To straighten out the tangled affairs left by his father, Noël Jr. sold his Seigneury “Port Joli”.  Noël Jr. could have kept the Seigneury if he had sold the homestead in Beauport after the death of the father in 1684, but he chose not to.

On 14 Oct 1673, after the breakup of the “Carignan-Salières Regiment”, many of the ex-soldiers received land concessions to induce them to remain in the Canada Colony and take up farming.  Jean Besset received title to land in Chambly.  A notarized document reveals that Monsieur de Chambly ceded him a tract with 26 acres of frontage land on the Richelieu River that was 1.8 miles in depth in the territory of Saint-Mathias de Rouville south the St. Lawrence River on the west bank of the Richelieu River, south of Chambly.

In 1673, the “Jesuit Mission in Beauport de la Province de Québec” was established.  Beauport was Québec City’s most eastern borough and was located where the Beauport and Montmorency Rivers flow into the St. Lawrence River at Montmorency Falls.

On 18 Jan 1674, Marie Suzanne Samson was born to Jacques Samson and Marie Anne (Metru) in St-Joseph-de-la-Pointe-Lévy, Canada a part of the Seignory of Lauzon located on the south bank of St Lawrence River.
On 27 May 1674, Pierre Julien Dion was born to Jacques Dion and Jeanne (Renard) in Québec City in Canada.

On 22 Jul 1674, Jean Besset sold his land in Chambly to Jean Brocherieux dit LaSoulaye and a week later bought a concession from François Prudhomme of 68 acres of land in Sault St-Louis south across the St. Lawrence River from the town of Ville Marie for 80 silver livres.

On 16 Oct 1574, Anne (St Dennis) wife of Laurent Migneron died giving birth to her fourth child daughter Anne Migneron in Beaupré, Canada.  A few months later Laurent Migneron decided to remarry, but before he was allowed to marry again, he was required to settle his estate with his three children: five-year-old son Jean Migneron, three-year-old-son Pierre and seven-month-old daughter Anne.  On 11 May 1675, before lawyer Paul Vachon, all of Laurent Migneron’s property was recorded.  The recorded information stated: “On their land, painfully cleared, is a wooden shack in which stands a working fireplace with all the utensils; steel pots, old dished, plates, a chest with key in which all of passed away wife Anne’s garments lay”.  The was no mention of a bed, decorations or religious device.  The family farm animals were listed as “2 black cows, 2 bulls, 2 veal caves and a sow”.  Anne (St Dennis) father Pierre St Dennis served as a witness for the three young children.  Lawyer Paul Vachon divided the land between widower Laurent Migneron, who received half and his and Anne’s two other children who received the other half.  After the property settlement was completed,  Laurent was allowed to remarry and on 20 May 1675, Laurent Migneron married widow Marie Guilluame an original “Filles du Roi” women in Beaupré, Canada.

On 21 Jan 1675, thirty-year-old Jean Gautier and fourteen-year-old Marie Angélique Lefébvre, were married in Québec City, Canada.  Marie was included in the list of “filles du Roi” women when she arrived in Canada back in 1665, when she was only four-years old with her parents Louis Lefébvre and Suzanne (Debure).  Jean Gautier worked as a “Taillandier”, (someone who made sharp tools such as scissors, shears and axes out of metal) in Québec City.
On 21 Jun 1675, Anne-Agnès Bonhomme was born to Ignace Bonhomme and Agnes Morin in St Michel, Québec City in Canada.

On 20 Jul 1675, Antoine Dubois who had been a garrison soldier protecting Trois-Rivières settlement sold the land he was given for his service for £100.  He next bought 1.7 acres of frontage land on the St Lawrence River that was 42 acres deep in the Seigneury of Tonnancourt, by working for the seller until he had worked £25 worth of time.  Antonine then cancel the contract he had just signed on 18 Aug 1675, as he readied himself for participating in a military operation against raiding Iroquois.  On 17 Sep 1675, Antonine gives his possessions, in case he is killed, to his future father-in-law Sieur Quentin Moral.  Antonine survived the fighting and return and settles in Saint-François-du-lac, which was located on the south of the St. Lawrence River 15 miles west of the Trois-Rivières settlement.
On 6 Apr 1675, Marie Anne Langlois was born to Noël Langlois Jr. and Aimee (Caron) in Beauport, Canada.

On 29 Oct 1675, Pierre Boucher, Lord of Boucherville, who was looking for settlers who would become tax payers to populate his property on Île Jésus island selected Guillaume Labelle and his father-in-law Olivier Charbonneau to care for his seigneurial farm on Île Jésus Island.  The farm was 45 acres of cultivated land, with building, 300 sheaves of dry Bundled wheat, 600 bales of dry baled hay, 4 oxen and a poultry yard.  On 13 Aug 1677, Pierre Boucher signed over the deed of the farm to Guillaume Labelle changing him and his wife Anne (Carbonneau) from a poor sharecropper to successful prosperous landowner.
In 1675, Joseph Bonneau owed 25 minots of different grains to Michel Lecourt, a merchant from Québec City.  Joseph paid his debt on time and in silver coins.
On 24 May 1676, Marguerite Messier was born to Lt. Michel Messier and Anne (Lemoine) in Chambly, Canada.

On 7 Jun 1676, Pierre Gaudin sold his property in Lachine, a settlement next to Villa Marie, to Mathurin Thibdeau.  Pierre Gaudin his wife Jeanne (Rousseliere) then moved to French Acadia (Cape Breton Island) to build a mill for Michel La Vallière the French lord of Acadia.  After the mill was built Pierre and Jeanne moved on and settled in Port Royal settlement in Acadia, Canada.

On 13 Aug 1677, Pierre Boucher signed a deed conceding land ownership to Guillaume Labelle of the land he and his wife Anne (Charbonneau) and their three young children were farming on Île Jésus Island.  Guillaume was the island’s first land owner and his land was assigned lot number 8 near the Pierre Boucher’s manor house.  Clearly Pierre Boucher like Guillaume and wanted him and his family to be his neighbor.

On 31 Aug 1676, thirty-year-old Mathieu Gervais, a discharged French soldier of the “Carignan-Salières Regiment” and fifteen-year-old Michele Picard were married in Ville-Marie settlement in Canada.  Michele had been born in the Ville-Marie back in 1661.  Mathieu was living and farming on 4 acres of land in La Prairie, Canada when he married.  La Prairie was located on the south side of the St. Lawrence River across from the Ville Marie settlement.

In 1677, sixty-five-year-old François Bélanger and his wife fifty-three-year-old Marie (Guyon) had 12 children.  Ten of children were either on their own or in the process of leaving the family home.  François accepted a concession of land and a position as seigneur of Bonsecours, which would later become known as “L'Islet”.  The property was located downstream from Île d'Orléans.  Taking such a step was often expected of a young man but not for a man sixty-five-years-old, it shows his determination and a strong desire to provide for the stable establishment of his sons.  François had been involved in protests and the demands of an active lifestyle.  One would have expected him to be looking for a more tranquil life, not starting new careers.  To reward the former officers of the military, Jean Talon, the intendant of Canada granted a large land tract, which were established as seigneur lordships.  Jean Talon's successors followed the same practice and François Bélanger asked for and obtained a new grant for his land.

With this new grant, François and Marie gave their farm and buildings at L'Ange Gardien to their son Charles.  François new grant was located on the south side of the St. Lawrence River, where it seemed there was a better future.  This grant included title to the domain and a Seigneurie, which was granted to him by Louis de Buade, Count of Frontenac (the Governor) and his aide Duchesneau, on 1 Jul 1677.  This grant, called “Bonsecours”, consisted of a league (3 miles) of river frontage land by two leagues (6 miles) in depth on the south bank of the St. Lawrence River which, today, is part of the parish of L'Islet, Québec.

François Bélanger became the Lord of the Domain of “Bonsecours”.  Two of his sons, who had come with him to the south shore, left soon after to set up their own farms nearby.  After forty years of struggle and constant work, François had suddenly become the Seigneur of “Bonsecours) (L'Islet).  This concession was placed on record by the Sovereign Council on 24 Oct 1680.  Later in 1709, Gedeon de Catalogne (an engineer), after reading the description on the plans of seigneuries and lands of the governments of Québec, Trois Rivieres and Montreal, spoke of it as follows:  "The land there is rather level, sprinkled with plowed up stones, and marginally produces all sorts of grains, vegetables and pasturage.  The fruit trees produce abundantly, and the natural woods are a mixture of all species."

In Feb 1678, thirty-seven-year-old Agnès Bonhomme (Morin) was charged with “uttering insulting words against the Canada’s Governor Louis de Frontenac.”  Exactly what Agnès said or did not say is not clear in records, but she was arrested and made to stand trial.  Governor Frontenac himself may have pressed the charges, and Agnès said on the witness stand that her guards “ill-treated” her.  It was the Canada’s Sovereign Court that had control of the proceedings and after hearing several witnesses, the court dismissed the case.  Governor Frontenac wasn’t pleased and he took his rage out on the clerk who served the court.  First, he cornered the court clerk after a dinner and demanded he share what was going on in the closed court session.  The clerk refused and Governor Frontenac next forced the information out of the clerk and got even more angry when he learned the court proceeding were not going as he had hoped.  He ended up grabbing the clerk by his coat, and ordering his guards to lock him up; the clerk was detained for several days.  In the end Agnès was exonerated (freed) by the Sovereign Court.
On 27 Jun 1678,  Jean Pilote and Marie-Francoise Gaudry were married in Québec City in Canada.
On 19 Jul 1678, Jean Besset returned back to Chambly and purchased 3.4 acres of frontage abandoned by Arnaud Piat dit LaFleur in exchange for 70 livres.
On 5 Dec 1678, Françoise Agnès Gingras was born to Sébastien Gingras and Marie Genevieve (Guillebourg) in Sillery, Canada.
On 26 Dec 1678, Charles Carrier Sr. was born to Jean Carrier and Barbe (Halay) in Lauzon, Levis, Canada.

In 1678, Jean Dumay Jr. bought 13 acres of land for his brother Étienne Dumay Sr., repaying his brother for the house that Governor Pierre Dubois Davaugour gave Étienne in Québec City, which Étienne then gave to his brother Jean.

In Mar 1679, Joseph Bonneau committed to work for a month during the summer in the services of Marie Baril, wife of Francois Sauvin.  For his work Joseph was promised 15 livers plus food and lodging.  Later on, 9 Dec 1679, Michel Lecourt rented a piece of land to Joseph Bonneau allowing Joseph to for the first time to practice his trade as a “mason”.
On 14 Jan 1680, Marie Agnes Pilote was born to Jean Pilote and Marie-Francoise (Gaudry) in L’Ancienne-Lorette, Canada.

Note:  Exploration of the lands west of the Great Lakes started by adventures like Jean Nicolet was interrupted by the Iroquois Wars against the French and other Native American tribes.  Now that the Iroquois were no longer a major obstacle to exploration in the west men like Pierre Charles Le Sueur were free to explore the areas in the middle of the North America.

In 1680, twenty-three-year-old Pierre Charles Le Sueur left the Jesuit mission in Sault Ste Marie, which was located on the St. Mary’s River that leaves Lake Superior and flows into Lake Huron to pursue the lure of the fur trade.  Pierre was soon involved in trade with the Sioux Nation on the upper Mississippi River, learning the skills need for living in the frontier.

In 1680, France the most powerful country in Europe, sent over to Canada the “Troupes de la Marine”, a regular military force under the command of the French Ministry of the Navy.  The troops were organized in France and sent to Canada.  Many of the soldiers eventually settled in Canada and replacements for the Troupe were recruited from young Canadians.  The units of the “Troupes de la Marine” were more professional than the local Canadian colonial militia.  The “Troupes de la Marine” quickly adapted the Native American-style forest warfare.  In 1687 the Marquis de Denonville launched an attack against Iroquois-Seneca villages with 832 soldiers, 1,030 militia and 800 Native American allies in the largest military operation to date.  The operation successfully pushed the Iroquois back to their home territory south of Lake Ontario  (Western New York).
Note: By 1681, Québec had grown into a city with over 1,300 people and a census was taken.
On 21 Nov 1681, Raphaël Giroux and Marie-Madeleine Vachon were married in Beauport in Canada.

Note:  The” Laurentian Highlands” in western Quebec are the largest exposures of Precambrian rocks (oldest Earth rocks) in the world.  They are two-billion-year-old relics of metamorphosed granite and gneiss (pronounced “nis”) that have been subjected to heat and pressure, uplifted and eroded by time and the effects of glaciation.  Drainage in the region was affected, resulting in the maze of large and small lakes, marshy hollows, dramatic cliffs, rocky outcroppings, winding streams and swift-flowing rivers with rapids and waterfalls.  Within this scoured bedrock and uninterrupted boreal forests is living in a cool damp climate which makes up most of the area’s primal landscape.  The aerial view below shows a dense panorama of dark evergreen interspersed with lighter green deciduous trees such as sugar maple and beech, brownish-yellow bogs and gray rocks.  The ancient landscape has two huge meteor craters, the Manicouagan and the Malbaie, that dent the land with circular depressions.  Rich mineral deposits of silica, lime, dolomite, copper, gold and nickel are present in the area resulting a prosperous mining industry in the area.  See an aerial view of the Manicouagan crater below:

blocks.text: In the 1681 census, Robert Drouin, wife Marie (Chapelier) and their children were farming in Chateau-Richer on sixteen acres of cultivated farmland with one indentured servant.  Robert had become a successful farmer, but never give up his interest in making bricks.  In 1682, at age seventy-five, Robert invited his son Etienne to come by in his spare time and help “make some bricks”.
In the1681 Canada Census, Nicolas Bélanger owned 35 acres of land in Beauport, with four indentured servants, making Nicolas one of the most prosperous settlers in Beauport community.

In the 1681 Canada Census, Jean Pichet and his wife Marie Madeleine (Leblanc) and their family were residing 20 acres of land with eleven head of cattle, in the Parish of Saint-Pierre-Paul on the Île-d'Orléans Island in the middle of the St. Lawrence River east to Québec City.

In the 1681 Canada Census, Jacques Samson and his wife Marie (Metru) and their four children including seven-year-old daughter Marie Suzanne Samson along with two maidservants were living in Lauzon on the south shore of the St. Lawrence River across from Québec City.
In the 1681 Canada Census, Pierre Maheu and his wife Jeanne (Drouin) were living L’Ange-Gardien in Beaupré on a 25-acre farm with 5 horned beasts.

In the 1681 census, Guillaume Labelle his wife Anne (Charbonneau) and their three children were living on 50 acres of land on Île Jésus Island.  The family had a musket hanging above their hearth, 2 horned beasts in the stable and 4 acres of their land under cultivation.
In the 1681 Canada Census, fifty-five-year-old widower Étienne Dumay Sr “ was living alone in Sillery in Canada on 13 acres of farmland with 2 horned beasts.

In the 1681 Canada Census, sixty-year-old Guillaume Fournier and his forty-six-year-old wife Françoise (Hébert) and their eight remaining children were living in Bellechasse a community on the south side of the St. Lawrence River twenty mile up the river from the town of Montmagny.  The family had three guns, 12 either goats or cows on their 10-acre farm.

In the 1681 Canada Census, Pierre Saint-Denis and wife Vivienne (Brunel) were living with their children in the Parish of Sainte-Famille on the Île-d'Orléans Island.  Pierre owns a gun, eight horned animals and fifteen arpents of land.
The map on the next page shows the islands that are in the St. Lawrence River.  The City of Montréal is located Île de Montréal.  The Île Jésus Island is located north of Île de Montréal Island.

blocks.caption: The map on the next page shows the islands that are in the St. Lawrence River. The City of Montréal is located Île de…

blocks.text: In the 1681 Canada Census, Jean Pilote and wife Marie-Françoise (Gaudry) and their one-year-old daughter, Marie Agnes were living in Lauzon Seigneury in the Lévis settlement located south across the St. Lawrence River from Québec City.

In the 1681 Canada Census, Jean Carrier, wife Barbe (Halay) their three children including two-year-old Charles Carrier Sr., were living in the Lauzon Seigneury in the Lévis settlement. The Carrier family was farming 15 acres of land, owned one musket and five head of cattle.

In the 1681 Canada Census, Nicolas Bélanger, wife Marie de (Rainville) and their children were listed living on 34 acres of land in Beauport making Nicolas one of the most prosperous settlers in the area.

In 1681, Pierre Charles Le Sueur, who was a fur trapper and trader in the western Great Lakes area, was arrested and jailed in the Ville Marie settlement for trading fur pelts illegally.  Pierre was soon freed and in 1683 he had joined a party fur traders who left Ville Marie in 15 canoes and traveled across the Great Lakes to Green Bay, a large bay on the west side of Lake Michigan and then on to the headwater of the Mississippi River that Jean Nicolet had discover back in 1634.  Pierre found or received some samples of bluish clay from the upper Mississippi River, which he took France for analysis.  A French chemist, Alexandre L'Huillier, deemed the clay sample was high-grade copper ore.  Pierre Le Sueur returned to Canada to mine the copper, but was convicted of overreaching his trade privileges and was put in prison.  Eventually, however, Pierre was given a royal French commission to open a copper mine (although some suggested he was more interested in "mining animal furs").
In 1681, Jean Pilote and wife Marie-Françoise (Gaudry) and their one-year-old daughter Marie Agnes were living in Lauzon in the Lévis settlement.

In the 1681 Canada Census, Joseph Bonneau and his first wife Marie-Anne (Lelong) were living on Saint-Laurent Coast in the parish of Saint-Francoise where they had 3 head of cattle and 14 acres under cultivation.
On 15 Dec 1681, after the 1681 census was taken, Marie Labelle was born to Guillaume Labelle and Anne (Charbonneau) in Ville-Marie settlement in Canada.

In the 1681 Canada Census, seventh-seven-year-old Raymond Pagé and his wife Madeleine (Bergeron) were living alone in Beaupré and Raymond was still working as a “carpenter”.  Their four married children were all living close by.  Sons Robert, Guillaume and William were immediate neighbors; daughter Suzanne and her husband Martin Adroche Côté and their son eleven-year-old Jean Côté were living in the Saint-Famille Parish on the I’Île d’Orléans Island across the St. Lawrence River from Beaupré.
On 7 Feb 1682, Noël Lareau was born to Francois Lareau and Anne (Dequain) in Québec City in Canada.
On 27 Feb 1682, François Chrétien was born to Vincent Chrétien and Marie Anne (Leclerc) in the Saint Francois Parish on the Île d’Orléans Island in Canada.
On 1 Jun 1682, Marie Gesseron was born to Louis Brulotte Gesseron Jr. and Agathe (Fournier) in Lauzon, Lévis, Canada.
On 14 Sep 1682, Guillaume Labelle purchased from Gilles Carré, the harvested grains (cereal crop) on Pierre Boucher’s land.  The grain was paid off with 100 bushels of wheat.

On 24 Oct 1682, Joseph Bonneau and Maurice Arrive’ signed a contract with Louis Rouer to build a house with stone walls in the Lower-Town of Québec.  The partners were paid 5 livres for each “toise” (2.13 sq yards) of masonry put down.

On 21 Nov 1682, Anne (Fosse), wife of Jacques Lareau died and Jacques remarried Jeanne Caille, the widow of the owner of the land Jacques was farming.  Because of this, Jacques obtained ownership of his own farm through his new wife.
In 1682, Nicolas Bourdet was born to Alexandre Bourdet and Madeleine (Michel) in Rouen, Normandy, France.
On 23 Nov 1682, Antoine Dubois and Marie-Marthe Moral were married in Trois-Rivières settlement in Canada.
On 19 May 1683, Antoine Dubois sold his license to trap beavers to Augustin Lafond for £150, payable in beaver pelts.
On 24 Oct 1683, Joseph Bonneau and Maurice Arrive’ who had earned the reputation of being good masons were hired by Jean Lerouge another mason and a surveyor.

In 1683, Jean Pilote after signing a contract with Aubert de la Chesnaye and accompanied by four other Frenchmen left on a fur trade expedition to the Illinois wilderness.  Upon their return, with a large load fur pelts, the group of Frenchmen were ambushed and taken prisoners by Iroquois warriors, who confiscated their seven canoes, their fur pelts and all their provisions.  The Iroquois next escorted the group to Fort St-Louis outside of Québec City where they were released.  The Iroquois’ left the five Frenchmen fur-trappers with only one rifle and a little powder.

On 13 Feb 1684, thirty-one-year-old Marie-Anne Lelong, first wife of Joseph Bonneau died from a bad case of influenza.  Joseph Bonneau and Marie-Anne had five children: Pierre, Joseph Jr., Marie-Jeanne, Anne and Antoine.  Early deaths affected all five of Joseph and Marie-Anne’s children.  Son Pierre died at age 26, son Joseph Jr. and his two children died from a smallpox epidemic in 1702, daughter Marie-Jeanne lost her husband after one year of marriage and spent the rest of her life in service to the Seminary of Québec, and youngest four-year-old daughter Anne died and the last son Antoine died at age twenty-two from a great pestilence (a disease endemic).

On 27 Feb 1684, Seigneur Crevier de Saint-François granted Antoine Dubois 5 acres of frontage property in the Saint-François Parish on the l'Île-d'Orléans island. The land ran across the entire width of the island.

On 11 Apr 1684, thirty-five-year-old widower Joseph Bonneau and seventeen-year-old Madeleine Duchesne were married in the Saint-François Parish on l'Île-d'Orléans Island in Canada.  At this time Joseph Bonneau had taken on the bit name “LaBecasse” which meant a “woodcock bird” in French.  The Bonneau family was well known for hunting of snipe ground birds.  The Snipe are notoriously hard to hunt, hence the expression “Off on a snipe hunt” means “off wasting your time”.
On 2 Oct 1684, Joseph Bonneau contracted to make a fireplace in the home of Abraham Methot, in return for 1,600 pickled eels, which Joseph shared with his partner Maurice Arrive.

On 30 Apr 1684, Mathurin Lebeau was born to Jean Lebeau and Etiennette (Loriot) in Fort St-Louis, Chambly in Canada.  Fort St-Louis was built back in 1655 by Captain Jacques de Chambly an officer in the “Carignan-Salières Regiment” to protect the Canada colonists from Native Iroquois warrior attacks.
In 1684, Jerome Longtin Sr. left Paris, France and sailed to Canada and then traveled up the St. Lawrence River to the Ville Marie settlement in Canada.
On 16 Oct 1684, Jerome Longtin Sr. and Catherine Marier were married in the Ville Marie settlement in Canada.
On 22 Nov 1684, Marguerite Gervais was born to Mathieu Gervais and Michele (Picard) in La Prairie, Canada.
On 23 Dec 1684, Claude Gautier was born to Jean Gautier and Angélique (Lefébvre) in Québec City, Canada.

In 1684, Lt. Michel Messier took part in Governor Joseph-Antoine de Lefébver de La Barre’s expedition against the Iroquois as the commander of a boat named “The General”.  Upon his return to Ville Marie the Governor awarded Lt. Michel Messier with a license for fur trading in the Ottawa native country on the northern shore of Lake Huron with the Ottawa Native tribe.  Michel after making his will in May 1685 travel to the Ottawa native country.
On 9 Feb 1685, Louis Pichet was born to Jean Pichet and Marie Madeleine (Leblanc) in Saint-Pierre-Parish on the l'Île-d'Orléans Island in Canada.

On 1 Jun 1685, Robert Drouin died at age seventy-eight in Chateau-Richer.  His second wife Marie (Chapelier) died twenty years later after many legal battles with her step-children’s families over the disposition of Robert Drouin’s estate.
On 26 Jul 1685, Francois Besset was born to Jean Besset and Anne (Le Seigneur) in Chambly, Canada.
On 2 Sep 1685, Marie Louise Dumats was born to René Dumats and Marie (Lelong) in La Prairie, Canada.

On 26 Nov 1685, Ignace Choret and Marie Therese Bélanger were married in Beauport, Canada.  Ignace was working in Beauport as a “shoemaker” and farmer.  Thirty-seven-year-old Ignace Choret died on 6 Oct 1701 in the hospital of Québec shortly after his twin brother Pierre died.
In 1685, Jerome Longtin Jr. was born to Jerome Longtin Sr. and Catherine (Marier) in the Ville Marie settlement in Canada.

In 1685, without hesitating, Joseph Bonneau agreed to be part of a project with Nicolas Veilleux, Alexandre Boissard, and Nicholas Menanteau.  Jean Gritton, a merchant from the Lower-Town in Québec City and owner of ships making the crossing between La Rochelle, France and Québec City had decided to open a shipyard at I’lle-aux-Coudres, for which he needed at least 2,000 pine planks.  Joseph Bonneau and the other partners immediately started work in order to finish making the needed pine planks by 15 Apr 1685.
On 25 Nov 1686, Etienne Dumay Jr. and Jeanne Francoise Ménard were married in Boucherville, Canada.
In 1686, Marie Jeanne Choret was born to Ignace Choret and Marie Therese (Bélanger) in Québec City, Canada.
On 30 August 1687, forty-six-year-old Agnès Bonhomme (Morin) died and her husband Ignace Bonhomme remarried the widow of Agnès’ first husband Nicola Gaudry’s brother Jacques Gaudry.

In 1687, a large military French force entered what is now western New York State, burning villages belonging to the Seneca Native people, an Iroquoian nation allied with the English.  The French destroying vast stores of harvested corn the Seneca estimated to have been about 1.2 million bushels.
In 1687, Jean Baptiste Neveu left France and sailed to Québec City, Canada.
On 16 Feb 1688, Jean Baptiste Neveu and Catherine Gaudin were married in Lachine, Canada.
On 26 Apr 1688, Charles Maheu and Marie Charlotte Garnier were married in L’Ange-Gardien, Canada.

On 4 Aug 1689 more than 1,500 Mohawk Iroquois warriors attacked the island of Île de Montréal at the village of Lachine, in revenge for the attack on the Seneca two years earlier. The attack was encouraged by the English, since England was now at war with France.  The Lachine village had been established about nine miles upstream (west) from Ville-Marie settlement, above the Lachine Rapids, back in 1667.  The Lachine village burned to the ground and an estimated 80 French settlers were killed.  Jean Baptiste Neveu and Catherine (Gaudin) somehow survived the attack or had left Lachine before the attack.

On 6 Nov 1689, Ignace Gagne and Louise Tremblay were married in L’Ange-Gardien, Canada.  The marriage is the second for Ignace, who had married earlier Barbe Dodier on 5 Nov 1680 in Quebec City.  Barbe died in Feb 1689.  Ignace Gagne and his new wife Louise (Tremblay) move farther down the St Lawrence River to Baie-St-Paul, Canada.

In 1689, Pierre Charles Le Sueur was assisting Nicolas Perrot at Fort Antoine, (lower Lake Pepin on the Mississippi) when Perrot ceremonially took possession of the Mississippi's headwaters for France.

sections.title: King William’s War
sections.teaser: In 1689, the “King William’s War”, started in North America between France and England.  The war was a continuation of the war that was going on in Europe at…

blocks.text: In 1689, the “King William’s War”, started in North America between France and England.  The war was a continuation of the war that was going on in Europe at the time between France and England.  The English captured Port Royal in Acadia from France with little resistance.  A year later in Oct 1690, the “Battle of Québec” was fought between Canada and British Massachusetts Bay Colony’s militia.  It was the first time Québec's defenses were tested.  Following the easy capture of Port Royal, the British New Englanders hoped to seize Québec itself, the capital of Canada.  The loss of the Acadian fort shock the French and Governor-General Louis de Buade de Frontenac ordered the immediate preparation in the Québec City for siege.  The English New England forces surrounded the fortified City of Québec.  When a New Englander envoy delivered the terms of surrender to the city, the Governor-General famously declared that his only reply would be by "the mouth of my cannons”.  On 18 Oct 1690, English Sir William Phips started bombarding the Québec City with cannons on 37 English war ships in the St Lawrence River.  The shelling had little effect, and after a defeat at Beauport three days later, the English retreat.  The expedition's ships, commanded by Sir William Phips, were nearly destroyed by French cannons on the Quebec City walls.  See below a painting of the “Battle of Québec”:

blocks.caption: In 1689, the “King William’s War”, started in North America between France and England. The war was a continuation of…

blocks.text: Note:  For the British/American Colonies full prospective on the four “French and Indian wars” see the Edith Powers’ Family Tree Story back at the beginning of this document.

In 1689, during an Iroquois raid on a large Miami Native settlement, in what will become the State of Illinois, the Iroquois’ destroyed the settlement and captured a large number of Miami native prisoners.  The Miami tribe asked for help from the other members of the Anishinaabe Confederation, which were native tribes like the Miami, which spoke the Anishinaabe language that was part of the larger Algonquian language group.  A large group of other native tribes joined with the Miami and Illinois tribes to confront the ongoing Iroquois raiding menace that impact the lives of all the peaceful native tribes in the region.  A large combined force of the Anishinaabe Confederation, using newly acquired European firearms tracked down the Iroquois raiders camped near the southernmost bend of the St. Joseph River (called South Bend Indiana today).  The combined Anishinaabe Confederation forces overwhelmed the Iroquois killing almost all of the Iroquois warriors.  The result of the destruction of the Iroquois raiding force was the creation of a large depopulated region that the Iroquois had controlled.  The Iroquois were never able to reestablish a permanent presence in the area and overtime the original native tribes used the opportunity to slowly return to their ancestral homes.  See a map below that shows the North American tribes that spoke the Anishinaabe language that were part of the larger Algonquian language group.

blocks.caption: In 1689, during an Iroquois raid on a large Miami Native settlement, in what will become the State of Illinois, the I…

blocks.text: On 29 Mar 1690, Pierre Charles Le Sueur and Marguerite Messier were married in Boucherville, Canada.
In 1690, Marie-Claude Dubois was born to Antoine Dubois and Marie-Marthe (Moral) in Trois-Rivières, Canada.

On 11 Aug 1691, the “Battle of La Prairie” occurred when 120 English militiamen and 146 Native warriors from the Mohawk and Mohican tribes attacked the French colonial settlement of La Prairie in Canada.   The French governor, responded by amassing 700 French militiamen and Native American allies at Fort La Prairie, located on the south shore of the St. Lawrence River.  The fort had been built three years earlier to protect the new and growing La Prairie French colonial settlement.

The English militiamen surprised the much larger French force in a rainstorm just before dawn, inflicting many casualties before withdrawing towards the Richelieu River.  The English attacking force was intercepted by a French force of 160 men to block the road to the major French fort of Ville Marie, which was the center of the French fur operation.  The two sides fought in vicious hand-to-hand battle for approximately an hour, before English gave up and retreated to escape.  The French victory stop the English, allowing the French to retained French control over number of French settlements in the St. Lawrence Valley.  The site today is a National Historic Site of Québec .
On 18 Oct 1691, Dominique Bonneau Sr. was born to Joseph Bonneau and Madeleine (Duchesne) in Montmorency, Canada.
On 10 Jan 1692, Louis Guay and Marie Suzanne Samson were married in Lévis, Canada.
On 18 Feb 1692, Jean Jacoti and Anne Marie Massard were married in Québec City, Canada.  (Jean Jacoti must have arrived in Canada before this marriage, but no record of his arrival has been found.)

In 1692, Lt. Michel Messier was once again granted permission to fur trade, this time he gave his wife Anne (Lemoine) the power of attorney as he was expecting grave dangers during his next trip.  After his return Michel did not apply for further fur trading licenses.  Instead, Michel settled on one of his fiefs and lived until the age of 85.

In 1692 Toussaint Giroux spent a month in the Québec hospital for unknown reasons.  In 1711, he sold a tract of his land to his grandson Raphaël on the condition he care for him in his old age and pay a sum of money to each of his children after his death.  His estranged ex-wife Therese Leblanc tried to block this arrangement in the courts, but lost.

On 1 Oct 1692, Guillaume Labelle was hospitalized at the Hotel-Dieu of Québec City for 40 days.  The condition of his need for hospitalization is unknown.  His daughter Catherine Labelle had been born in 5 Jul 1692 in Lachenaie, a village next to Ville Marie, indicating Guillaume was living on Villa Marie Island at the time.  Traveling to Québec City would take time and effort, indicating Guillaume and his family probably had moved away from Ville Marie possibly to avoid the ongoing Iroquois attacks on the settlement.  It appears that at this time Guillaume Labelle had move his family to Côte de Beaupré on the north bank of the St. Lawrence River across from the east end of l'Île-d'Orléans island on land he had bought from the “Episcopal Corporation”.
On 18 May 1693, Pierre Francois Giroux was born to Raphaël Giroux and Marie-Madeleine (Vachon) in Beauport, Canada.

In 1693, Pierre Charles Le Sueur was sent west by Canada’s Governor General Louis de Buade, Comte de Frontenac, to establish forts around Lake Superior and work on establishing peace with local the native tribes.  Pierre had become fluent in several native languages, which was crucial to his success in trading and exploring.  In 1693, Pierre who spoke both the Sioux and Chippewa languages settled a dispute between the two warring native tribes.  Pierre founded a trading post at La Point in the Chequamegon Bay on the west side of Lake Superior, where Pierre found copper ore.  A large Native American village, known as “Chequamegon” and been established back in 1649, after the “Beaver Wars” as a refugee for Petun, Huron and Ottawa Native people fleeing to escape the Iroquois attacks.  Today the “Bad River Band Chippewa Indian Reservation” is located on the south shore of Chequamegon Bay.  In the 2010 census 6,945 Ojibwe Native people were living on the reservation.
On 29 Jan 1694, Louis Neveu was born to Jean Baptiste Neveu and Catherine (Gaudin) in Sainet-Anne-de-Bellevue, Canada on the western end of the Île de Montréal Island.
On 8 Feb 1694, Jean Côté and Marie Anne Langlois were married in Beauport, Canada.
On 15 Mar 1694, Jacques Robert Sr. was born to Louis Robert and Marie Madeleine (Bourgery) in Boucherville, Canada.
On 14 Dec 1694, Marie Anne Côté was born to Jean Côté and Marie Anne (Langlois) in Saint-Pierre-de-l'Île-d'Orléans, Canada.

On 9 Feb 1695, Jean Migneron and Marie Labelle were married in Saint-Joachim-de-Shefford within La Côte-de-Beaupré, Canada where Marie’s parents Guillaume Labelle and Anne (Charbonneau) were now living.  Guillaume was listed as an employee of the “Episcopal Corporation”.
On 17 Mar 1695, Marie Angelique Maheu was born to Charles Maheu and Marie Charlotte (Garnier) in L'Ange-Gardien, Canada.
On 22 Nov 1695, André Allard and Marie Anne Lemarché were married in Charlesbourg, Québec, Canada.

In 1695, Pierre Charles Le Sueur was commanding the French fort that had been constructed at Chequamegon Bay an inlet of southwestern Lake Superior and had established another French fort on the Brule-St. Croix River, which provided water route from the Great Lakes to the Mississippi River.  Pierre next built another French fort on an island in the Mississippi River, 960 miles above the junction with the Illinois River.  In mid-July of 1695, Pierre returned to Ville Marie settlement with the native Chiefs of Native Ojibwe and Dakota tribes.

Today the “Brule-St. Croix River Portage Trail” is a popular hiking trail for walking, birding and snowshoeing in winter.  See on the next page a metal plack located at the start of the portage trail.

blocks.caption: Today the “Brule-St. Croix River Portage Trail” is a popular hiking trail for walking, birding and snowshoeing in win…

blocks.text: On 22 Jan 1696, Marie Francoise Gagne was born to Ignace Gagne and Louise (Tremblay) in Baie-Saint-Paul, Canada on the north side of the St. Lawrence River 60 miles east and downstream from Québec City.
On 21 Apr 1696, Marie Le Sueur was born to Pierre Charles Le Sueur and Marguerite (Messier) in Québec City, Canada.

On 20 Jan 1697, Pierre Julien Dion and Anne-Agnès Bonhomme were married in Québec City, Canada.  This was the Pierre’s first of three marriage.  Pierre and Anne had five children all boys including their last son François Xavier Dion.  Wife Anne-Agnès died in 1703 and Pierre married his second wife and they had six additional children before his second wife died.  Pierre married a third wife and they had four more children.
On 24 Oct 1697, Marie-Anne Jacoti was born to Jean Jacoti and Anne Marie (Massard) in Québec City, Canada.

In 1697, Pierre Charles Le Sueur presented a report to the King of France about the advantages of mining copper and lead in the La Point area in the far western bay of Lake Superior.  Pierre found he had opposition to his proposal from those who were supporting the Native tribes in the area, preventing Pierre from getting his request approved, but he did create interest in the area he had found and its rich metal ore resources.
In 1698, French colonists begin to settle east of the Beauport River on the North side of the St. Lawrence River, attracted by a flour mill and good agriculture land.
In 1698, Louise Migneron was born to Jean Migneron and Marie (Labelle) in Ville-Marie settlement in Canada.
In 1698, Jeanne Dumay was born to Etienne Dumay Jr. and Jeanne Françoise (Ménard) in St-Nicolas, Canada.
On 15 Jun 1699, Charles Carrier Sr. and Marie Gesseron are married in Lauzon, Lévis, Canada.

In 1699, Pierre Charles Le Sueur was with the group of French explorers that were traveling 1,200 miles up  from  on the Gulf of Mexico, stopping to overwinter at Fort Perrot above Lake Pepin on the Mississippi River.  In the spring Pierre went upstream as far as Saint Anthony Fall (now the city of Minneapolis Minnesota).  After trading with the local Dakota tribal bands in the area, Pierre and a group of 20 men went further up the Mississippi River to an area the local native people called: "minisota", meaning "cloud reflected water".  This area was known to later explores as the St. Pierre River, which today is the Minnesota River.  Pierre and the group continued up to the Blue Earth River, where they built Fort L’Huiller (near Mankato, Minnesota), named for the chemist who declared the ore in the area to be copper.  They overwintered at Fort L’Huiller, trading for furs with merchandise they had brought for trade with the local Native tribes.  Pierre Le Sueur and the others found the open prairies full of American bison and learned to subsist (live) as the native people largely on a bison meat diet.

Note:  In 1700, around 10,000 people of French origin were living and farming in the lower St. Lawrence River valley in Canada.  By comparison over 250,000 people were living in the English American colonies at the same time.
On 2 Nov 1701, Charles Carrier Jr. was born to Charles Carrier Sr. and Marie (Gesseron) in Lévis, Canada.

In May 1701, Pierre Le Sueur left the group of Frenchmen at Fort L’Huiller under the command of d'Eraque and accompanied by large group Native Dakota men traveled back down the Mississippi River to Fort Charlotte in Mobile (later will become Mobile, Alabama) for further analysis of the ore they had gathered from the Blue Earth area, which turned out not to be copper and was worthless.  Later that year, Fort L'Huillier was attacked by Native Sac and Fox warriors and three Frenchmen were killed and the fort was then abandoned.  Pierre Le Sueur sailed over to France to secure a commission to serve as a local magistrate in what is now Alabama.  Pierre was supposed to leave France on the ship “Loire” in 1703 but he did not actually sail until the next spring aboard the ship “Pélican”.  The “Pélican” was carrying nurses and women to Louisiana and stopped at Havana Cuba where Pierre contracted yellow fever and died on 17 Jul 1704 and was buried in the parish church of San Cristóbal."  Pierre Le Sueur is namesake of Le Sueur, Minnesota, Le Sueur River and Le Sueur County Minnesota.

On 4 Aug 1701, a peace treaty the “Great Peace of Ville Marie” was signed between Canada and 39 First Nations tribes of North America by Louis-Hector de Callière, governor of Canada and 1,300 representatives of 39 First Native tribes.  The treaty resulted in 16 years of peaceful relations and trade before war started again.  About this time the Ville Marie settlement was renamed after the Island of Montréal it was located on becoming the “City of Montréal”.

In 1701, with peace finally established with the signing of the “Great Peace of Ville Marie”, Guillaume Labelle and his family, including twenty-three-year-old daughter Marie Labelle, returned to their 50 acres farm on Île Jésus Island.  See a photo on the next page of the eastern point of the large Île Jésus Island and the St Lawrence River that created the island:

blocks.caption: In 1701, with peace finally established with the signing of the “Great Peace of Ville Marie”, Guillaume Labelle and h…

sections.title: Queens Anne’s War
sections.teaser: In 1702, the “Queen Anne’s War” in Europe had France and England fighting each other again.  The fighting carried over to the New World with French military…

blocks.text: In 1702, the “Queen Anne’s War” in Europe had France and England fighting each other again.  The fighting carried over to the New World with French military and English colonists fighting a second “French and Indian War”  In Spanish Florida, English Province of South Carolina militia attached the French Fort Louis de la Louisiane (near present-day Mobile, Alabama) and the fighting almost nearly wiped out all the Native American population in the area.  The New England colonists fought against French colonists and their allied Native American tribes also in Acadia and Canada.  The walled Québec City was repeatedly targeted by New England colonial forces, and the French Acadian capital Port Royal was taken by the New English colonists in 1710.  The French colonists and the Wabanaki Confederacy (confederation of five Native tribes (the Mi'kmaq, Maliseet, Passamaquoddy, Abenaki, and Penobscot) fought to stop New England expansion into Acadia at the Kennebec River, considered by the French to be the border with Maine.

In 1702, Paul Vachon experienced the grief of seeing his family afflicted by the 1702-3 small-pox epidemic in Canada.  Paul had been a widower since 24 Sep 1697, and in six months he lost four of his children, a daughter-in-law, and six grandchildren.  Paul himself succumbed to the small-pox scourge that hit Beauport, Canada the next year on 24 Jun 1703.
On 17 Aug 1703 François Xavier Dion was born to Pierre Julien Dion and Anne-Agnes (Bonhomme) in L'Ancienne-Lorette, Canada.
On 12 Nov 1704, Jerome Longtin Jr. and Marie Louise Dumats were married in La Prairie, Canada.

On 1 May 1707, “Acts of Union” was passed by both the English and Scottish Parliaments joining England and Scotland, which had both shared the same king since 1603.  England/Wales and Scotland joined to form the new country of Great Britain.
On 29 Mar 1709, Marie-Véronique Guay was born to Louis Guay and Marie Suzanna (Samson) in Lauzon, Lévis, Canada.
On 10 Nov 1710, Louis Pichet and Marie Anne Côté were married in Saint-Pierre-Parish on Île-d'Orléans Island in Canada.
In 1710, Joseph Normand and Marie Jeanne Choret were married in Québec City, Canada.

In 1711, a “Québec Expedition”, was sent by the Great Britain on a secret mission to attack Québec City during the “Queen Anne's War”.  Englishman Robert Haley, chief minister to the British crown wanted to demonstrate Britain’s new military policy emphasizing its strength at sea.  Robert Haley sent British Admiral Walker to attack Québec City, but the admiral had difficulty acquiring experienced pilots and accurate charts for navigating the waters of the lower Saint Lawrence River.  The expedition reached the Gulf of Saint Lawrence without incident, but foggy conditions, tricky currents, and strong winds combined to drive the fleet toward the north shore of the river near a place now called Pointe-aux-Anglais, where the English warships were wrecked.  Following the disaster, Walker abandoned the expedition's objectives and returned to Great Britain.
On 15 Dec 1711, Marie Anne Normand was born to Joseph Normand and Marie-Jeanne (Choret) in Québec City in Canada.
On 7 Feb 1712, Noël Lareau and Marie Agnes Pilote were married in in Québec City in Canada.
On 22 Jun 1712, Marie Josèphine Allard was born to Andre Allard and Marie Anne (Lemarché) in Charlesbourg in Canada.

In 1713, the “Queen Anne’s War” in Europe and North America ended with the signing of the “Treaty of Utrecht” in Europe.  Some terms were ambiguous in the treaty, and concerns of various Native American tribes were not included, thereby setting the stage for future conflicts.  The main result of the war was England emerged as a major imperial, military, commercial and financial power.  The French and English colonist both took advantage of the relative peace that followed the “Treaty of Utrecht” and gradually extended the western frontiers into the center of North America.  The French established a colony of Louisiana at the mouth of the Mississippi River and built forts up the Mississippi River all the way to the Great Lakes.  The alliances the French had built with the Native American tribes were neglected during this expansion period alienating (turning off) trust of the native people.  The Native Americans were divided between the attraction of the European advanced trade goods and the resentment over both the dependence the goods created and the loss of additional ancestral homeland caused by the flood of new European settlers, which upset the native people’s way of life.

In 1713, L'Acadie an area on the west side of the Richelieu River, across from Saint-Jean-sur-Richelieu in Quebec, 30 miles from the city of Montréal and about the same distance from the United States border at the head of Lake Champlain was settled by French people who were forced to leave the Islands of Nova Scotia, Newfoundland and the land on the southern shore of Hudson Bay in 1713, at the end of “Queen Anne’s War”.
On 20 Feb 1713, Mathurin Lebeau and Marie Le Sueur were married in Boucherville, Canada.
On 20 Dec 1713, François Chrétien and Louise Migneron were married in Montréal City, Canada.

In 1713, the territory of New France or Canada consisted of the Québec, Louisiana and Acadia Colonies.  Québec , the most developed colony was divided into the districts of Québec, Trois-Rivières and Montréal.  See a map below of New France Canada in 1713:

blocks.caption: In 1713, the territory of New France or Canada consisted of the Québec, Louisiana and Acadia Colonies. Québec , the m…

blocks.text: On 5 Feb 1714, Pierre Francois Giroux and Marie Angelique Maheu were married in Beauport, Canada.
On 8 Feb 1714, Joseph Lareau was born to Noël Lareau and Marie Agnes (Pilote) in Québec City, Canada.
In 1714, Nicolas Bourdet left Rouen, Normandy, France and sailed to the Québec City in Canada.  Nicolas was a soldier in the Company of the “Knights de Beaucours”.

Note:  Josué Dubois de Beaucours was a military officer and the chief engineer of Canada and governor of the towns of Trois-Rivières and Montréal City.  He joined the French navy when his was twenty.  In 1684, he was named a midshipman (first rank of French officer).  In 1687, he was discharged from the naval service and the following year became a lieutenant (second rank of officer) in the colonial regular troops of Canada.  In 1689, he was charged with improving the defenses of the town of Trois-Rivières due to the threat of attacks by Iroquois warriors.  In 1692, Lt. Beaucours led a successful expedition against the Iroquois Nation.  Later that year, he drew up plans for the fortification of Québec City against a possible English attack.  In 1701, he was promoted to captain and, in 1702, went to work on building forts in the Montréal City area.  In 1704 and 1705, he led a group of 120 French men which took part in an expedition against the English in Newfoundland that culminated in the failed Siege of St. John’s.  On his return, Captain Beaucours continued to work on fortifications in the Montréal and Québec cities and in 1712, he was awarded the French “cross of Saint-Louis” and named chief engineer for Canada.
On 17 Apr 1714, Nicolas Bourdet and Marie-Anne Jacoti were married in Québec City, Canada.
In May 1714, Claude Gautier and Marie Francoise Gagne were married in Baie-Saint-Paul, Canada.  (“Baie” translates to Bay.)
In 1714, Louis Neveu and Marguerite Gervais were married in Sainte-Anne-de-Bellevue, Montréal City, Canada.
On 9 Feb 1715, Francois Besset and Marie-Claude Dubois were married in Chambly, Canada.
In 1715, Louis Francois Neveu was born to Louis Neveu and Marguerite (Gervais) in Montréal City, Canada.
On 16 Feb 1716, Louis Lebeau was born Mathurin Lebeau and Marie (Le Sueur) in Boucherville, a suburb of the Montréal City, Canada.
On 3 Jul 1716, Dominique Bonneau Sr. and Françoise Agnès Gingras were married in Québec City, Canada.
On 20 Jan 1717, Marie Anne Bourdet was born to Nicolas Bourdet and Marie-Anne (Jacoti) in Québec City, Canada .
On 25 Apr 1718, Jacques Robert Sr. and Jeanne Dumay were married in Boucherville, Canada.
On 29 Jul 1718, Marie Angéllque Besset was born to Francois Besset and Marie-Claude (Dubois) in Chambly, Canada .
On 30 Apr 1720, Jean-Baptiste Giroux was born to Pierre Francois Giroux and Marie Angéllque (Maheu) in Beauport, Canada.
In 1720, Nicholas Louis St Sauveur was born to Jean St Sauveur and Jeanne (Rodier) in Paris, Île-de-France, France.
On 12 Aug 1721, Marie-Catherine Longtin was born to Jerome Longtin and Marie Louise (Dumats) in La Prairie, Canada.

On 19 Sep 1721, Marie Francoise Gautier was born to Claude Gautier and Marie Francoise (Gagne) in Baie St Paul, Canada.  Baie St Paul, was a small community on the north side of the St. Lawrence River 60 miles downstream east of Québec City, Canada.
On 11 May 1722, Dominique Bonneau Jr. was born to Dominique Bonneau Sr. and Francoise Agnès (Gingras) in Baie-Saint-Paul, Canada .

Note:  Back in 1142, the Iroquois (“People of the Longhouse") were a powerful northeast Native American Nation.  They formed the “Iroquois Confederacy” or “Five Nations”, comprising the Mohawk, Onondaga, Oneida, Cayuga, and Seneca tribes.  In 1722, they later added the Tuscarora tribe from the Southeast into their confederacy, as they were also Iroquoian-speaking, and the expanded confederation became known as the “Six Nations Confederacy”.
On 14 Oct 1723, Marie Angélique Chrétien was born to François Chrétien and Louise (Migneron) in Trois-Rivières, Canada .

On 25 Aug 1725, off the rocky coast of near Louisbourg, Nova Scotia where strong sea currents often make it difficult for ships to approach the shore, a hurricane raged through the area.  The next day, the debris from a heavy tonnage vessel was found along the coast.  It was identified as the ship “Chameau.”, with 310 passengers aboard all of whom perished.  Only 180 bodies were recovered from the sea and buried at La Baleine, Nova Scotia. The fact that most were wearing nightclothes indicates the violence and suddenness of the ship wreck.
On 22 Oct 1727, Marie Anne Pichet was born to Louis Pichet and Marie Anne (Côté) in Saint-Pierre Parish on the l'Île-d'Orléans Island in Canada.
On 17 Nov 1727, Charles Carrier Jr. and Marie-Veronique Guay were married in Lévis, Canada.
On 12 Jun 1729, Jacques Robert Jr. was born to Jacques Robert Sr. and Jeanne (Dumay) in Chambly, Canada.
On 26 Feb 1731, Charles-Joseph Carrier was born to Charles Carrier Jr. and Marie-Veronique (Guay) in Lauzon, Lévis, Canada.
On 9 Jan 1735, François Xavier Dion and Marie Josèphine Allard were married in Charlesbourg, Québec, Canada.
On 3 Nov 1735, Joseph Lareau and Marie Anne Normand were married in Québec City, Canada.
On 29 Sep 1741, Marie Anne Lareau was born to Joseph Lareau and Marie Anne (Normand) in Chambly, Canada.
On 8 Nov 1741, Dominique Bonneau Jr. and Marie Francoise Gautier married in Baie-Saint-Paul, Canada.
On 24 Apr 1741, Louis Francois Neveu and Marie Anne Bourdet were married in Québec City, Canada.
On 15 Nov 1741, Marie-Anne Neveu was born to Louis Francois Neveu and Marie Anne (Bourdet) in the Montréal City, Canada.

On 30 Jan 1743, Benjamin Bonneau Sr. was born to Dominique Bonneau Jr. and Marie Francoise (Gautier), in Petite-Rivière, Canada.  Petite-Rivière was located 10 miles west of Baie-Saint-Paul on the north shore of the St. Lawrence River where Dominique Bonneau Jr. and Marie Francoise (Gautier) were married.

Note:  Petite-Rivière is located on the river from which it takes its name.  Samuel de Champlain gave the river its name “little River”, Petite-Rivière in French.  The river was called the “Simkook”, by the Native Mi’kmaq people living in the area.  The small settlement was founded by Pierre du Gua de Monts back in 1632.

In 1745, Governor William Shirley of the English Province of Massachusetts Bay, received important intelligence information on the weak defenses that were in place French held Nova Scotia from prisoners that had been exchanged with the French.  Governor immediately mobilized 4,000 New Englanders militia solders under the command of William Pepperell.  The New Englanders attacked and captured the import port of Louisbourg on Nova Scotia Island and mainland Acadia area from the French.  The following year France sent a fleet of 76 ships with 3,000 soldiers to retake Louisbourg, Nova Scotia and Acadia, but the fleet was devastated by a hurricane and an out brake of smallpox in the French soldiers in the French fleet that spread to the supporting French colonists and their allied Native American tribes.  The remaining French ships and soldiers were forced to returned to France.  The contact between the sick soldiers caused more deaths than all the fighting that occurred in the area.  The British set up a naval blockade of the French ports in Canada, which dramatically cut the flow of goods to the France colony and upset the trading that had been occurring with the Native American tribes.  The shortage of European goods drove the Native American tribes to switch to the Great Britain to trade their furs for European items the Native American tribes wanted.  The series of events starting with the hurricane, the spread of smallpox disease, the successful British naval blockade and the switching of sides of many of the Native American tribes set the stage of the upcoming disaster that was about to occur for the French in North America.  See a map on the next page of Acadia:

blocks.caption: In 1745, Governor William Shirley of the English Province of Massachusetts Bay, received important intelligence infor…

blocks.text: In 1745, Louis Lebeau and Marie Angéllque Bessette were married Chambly, Canada .
In 1745, Noël Lareau and his brother Joseph moved their families south to the Richelieu River where land was plentiful and settlers were needed to defend the Montréal City from Iroquois attacks.
On 16 Aug 1746, Jean-Baptiste Giroux and Marie-Catherine Longtin were married in La Prairie, Canada .
In 1747, Marie Catherine Giroux was born to Jean-Baptiste Giroux and Marie-Catherine (Longtin) in La Prairie, Canada.

On 12 Feb 1748, Nicholas Louis St Sauveur and Marie Angélique Chrétien were married in Montréal City, Canada.  No information has been found Nicholas Louis St Sauveur arrival in Canada and his actives prior to his marriage.
On 11 Sep 1748, Louis Charles Lebeau was born to Louis Lebeau and Marie Angéllque (Besset) in Chambly, Canada.

In 1748, a Native Cree girl was born to Payukoonisk “The Fathom”, a Whitefish Native Cree man and Ollie Ani-Waya Chicamagu, a Cree woman, in Swampy Cree Native settlement in York Factory, Manitoba, Canada.

Note:  York Factory was a settlement Hudson Bay Company trading post located on the southwestern shore of the Hudson Bay in northeastern Manitoba, Canada, at the mouth of the Hayes River.  York Factory was one of the first fur-trading posts established in 1684 by the Hudson Bay Company and used in the fur-trading business for more than 270 years.  Manitoba is a Cree word meaning “Spiritual and fundamental Life Force”.  See a later 1925 photo of York Factory and map of Rupert’s Land controlled by the Hudson Bay Company below:

images.caption: Note: York Factory was a settlement Hudson Bay Company trading post located on the southwestern shore of the Hudson B…
Note: York Factory was a settlement Hudson Bay Company trading post located on the southwestern shore of the Hudson B…

blocks.text: On 10 Jun 1749, Marie Marguerite Moreau was born to Ignace Francois Moreau and Marie Jeanne Audet in Saint-Jean Parish on Île-d’Orléans Island in Canada.

In 1750, British North America colonies had grown to becoming a full-fledged independent country, something they would actually become a few decades later, with 1 million inhabitants.  Meanwhile, Canada was still viewed mostly as a cheap source of natural resources for France and had only 60,000 inhabitants.  Nevertheless, Canada was territorially larger than the English thirteen colonies, but had a population of only 6% in size.  There were occasional warfare breakouts occurring along the borders between the British North America colonies and Canada, with the French supporting Native American warrior raids on the English American colonies.
On 6 Nov 1752. Charles-Joseph Carrier and Marie Anne Pichet were married in Saint-Pierre Parish on the Île d'Orléans Island in Canada.
On 3 Mar 1753, Thomas Dion was born to François Xavier Dion and Marie Joséphine (Allard) in L’Ancienne-Lorette, Canada as their tenth and last child.
In 1754, North America was divided into British, France and Spanish colonies.  See a map on the next page showing the major French and British fighting actions in the “French and Indian War”:

blocks.caption: In 1754, North America was divided into British, France and Spanish colonies. See a map on the next page showing the…

sections.title: French and Indian War
sections.teaser: On 28 May 1754, a war started when a British Colonial Militia unit led by a 22-year-old Lt. Colonel George Washington ambushed a small French force in the “B…

blocks.text: On 28 May 1754, a war started when a British Colonial Militia unit led by a 22-year-old Lt. Colonel George Washington ambushed a small French force in the “Battle of Jumonville Glen”.  The French reacted to the attack on their soldiers and the “French and Indian War” started.  The war in North America was between the France’s Canadian Provinces and their native Indian allies against Great Britain and its North American colonies.  Both the French and English sent large armies from Europe to fight in North America.  One of the first results of the war occurred when the French Acadians living in Nova Scotia refused to swear allegiance to the British king and were expelled from their homes.  The French Acadians living in Nova Scotia were forced to scatter throughout the North American.  Some of the Acadian (pronounced ‘a-cay-DJYEN in French) settled in the French Louisiana colony.  The word “Cajun” derives from the word “Acadian”.
