On 2 Dec 1755, Joseph Carrier was born to Charles-Joseph Carrier and Marie Anne (Pichet) in Saint-Joseph-De-La-Pointe, Lévis, Canada.

Both the French and the British sent more soldiers to North America.  France sent a new commander, Louis-Joseph Montcalm to led the French forces.  In May 1756, Great Britain and France declared war on each other.  The French had the upper hand in the fighting in 1756 and 1757 in North America.  The British soon sent more military forces and the British Royal Navy took control of the Atlantic shipping lanes, preventing France from sending more military help.

On 3 Dec 1757, Marguerite St Sauveur was born to Nicholas Louis St Sauveur and Marie Angélique (Chrétien) in Longueuil, Canada.  Longueil is located on the south shore of the St. Lawrence River across from Montréal City.

In 1757, General Montcalm, with 3,600 French and Canadian troops and 1,500 Native warriors attacked Fort William Henry at the south end of Lake George in the Province of New York.  General Montcalm permitted the British soldier who had sundered to return to their lines after receiving a promise they would not fight for 18 months.  The French Native American allies felt the action was wrong and attacked the unarmed British soldiers on their way back.  The ensuing massacre became a rallying cry for the British.  Many of the Native warriors that had joined both the French and British military forces in the attack and fall of Fort William Henry in New York became exposed to an out brake of “small pox”, which resulted in the deadly disease being spread back across the Native people in the Ohio River Valley and out to Native tribes living beyond the Mississippi River to the west.

The conflict quickly exploded across the British and French colonies in North America and extended to the seizure of hundreds of French merchant ships at sea by the British Navy.  The war soon changed to a European style fight with large armies facing each other in the Canadian heartland.  The British placed William Pitt, who was the leader of the House of Commons, in charge of British war effort.  The British forces included 20,000 regular British soldiers and 22,000 colonial militia troops.  The British soon captured Fort Duquesne in the Ohio River Valley.  The Iroquois Confederation perceiving a shift in favor of the British and ended their neutral position in the war and joined with the British.  The Iroquois involvement quickly resulted in a series of loses of the French forts in the Iroquois areas.

In 1758, the British troops captured the fort at Louisbourg, gaining control over the mouth of the St. Lawrence River.  Some of the British units successfully incorporated the Native American style of fighting in the back country they learned from observing the Iroquois warriors.

In Jul 1759, General Montcalm consolidated his French forces around Québec City.  The British forces confronted the French Army on the “Plains of Abraham” just outside Quebec City.  Both General Montcalm and the British commander Major General James Wolfe were killed in the major battle.  Marshal Francois de Lévis took over command of the French forces and continued the fight and defeated the British forces on the “Plains of Abraham”, named after farmer Abraham Martin.  The arrival of the British fleet forced General Lévis to retreat upriver to the Montréal City area.  The British forces soon surrounded the remaining French regular forces and the “Troupes de la Marine” in Montréal City, which fell to the British forces.

On 8 Sep 1760, the French surrendered to the British ending French rule in Canada.  The “Treaty of Paris” finalized the full surrender.  On 7 Oct 1763, Great Britain’s King George III issues a “Royal Proclamation” setting out the terms of government for the newly captured French territory, as well as defining the geographic boundaries of the territory.

After the French surrender, British Brigadier-General Charles Lawrence orders the expulsion of the remaining French Acadians from all of eastern Canada (Maritime Provinces of Nova Scotia, New Brunswick, and Prince Edward Island).  Over the next decade an estimated 11,400 French Canadian Catholics were deported to the Thirteen British Colonies, Europe and the French Colony of Louisiana.

Note:  See the Story of Carolee Afton McCance’s family, the Story of Morris Rankin Batie’s family for more information on the "French and Indian Wars” between Britain and France and their North American colonies.
On 20 Apr 1761, Jacques Robert Jr. and Marie Anne Lareau were married in Chambly, Canada .  The war between French Canadians and the British had been going on all around them at this time.

sections.title: British Québec
sections.teaser: In 1763, after the “Treaty of Paris” Great Britain took over Canada under the rule of Royal Governor James Murray who was benign, guaranteed the French Canad…

blocks.text: In 1763, after the “Treaty of Paris” Great Britain took over Canada under the rule of Royal Governor James Murray who was benign, guaranteed the French Canadians they would be allowed to retain their traditional rights and customs.  Great Britain’s King George III issued a “Royal Proclamation” united the three Québec districts into the “Province of Québec”.  It was the British who were the first to use the name "Québec" to refer to a territory beyond Québec City.  The British tolerated the Catholic Church and protected the traditional social and economic structure of the new Province of Québec.  The people of Québec Province responded with one of the highest birth rates ever recorded, 65 births per thousand per year.  Most of the existing French laws were retained within a new system of British courts, all under the command of the British governor.  The goal was to satisfy the “Francophiles” (French settlers), albeit to the annoyance of British merchants back in Great Britain.

Until 1763, the Hudson Bay Company (HBC) struggled with the French for control of the fur trade in southern Rupert’s Land.  In the early years, a series of naval and land battles took place on the Hudson and James Bays.  In 1713, as part of the “Treaty of Utrecht”, France acknowledged England’s claim to the Hudson Bay area.  For the next 60 years, the HBC erected posts at the mouths of all major rivers flowing into the Hudson and James Bays.  HBC’s reluctance to establish trading posts in the interior of Rupert’s Land allowed competitors to outflank the HBC by travelling into the interior to trade with Native peoples rather than waiting for them to come to the coast.  The Native trappers used this intense rivalry to extract greater returns for their furs.

After the 1763 “Treaty of Paris”, that force France out of North America, the HBC’s French rivals were replaced by a much more formidable opposition, the Montréal-based “Overland tide Network” taken over by the British.  By 1774, HBC trade had been undercut enough that the governor and the committee embarked on an aggressive policy of inland expansion beginning with the building of Cumberland House on the lower Saskatchewan River and the development of the Red River Colony in Manitoba, Canada.  In 1821, a merger of the two companies was arranged, and the British Parliament confirmed and extended the HBC’s monopoly privileges in North America out to the Pacific Coast.
On 23 Jul 1763, Jacques Robert III was born to Jacques Robert Jr. and Jeanne (Dumets) in Chambly, in the Province of Québec, Canada.
On 25 Jul 1763, Louis Charles Lebeau and Marie-Anne Neveu were married in Chambly, Province of Québec, Canada, Canada .
On 5 Feb 1764, Marie Charlotte Lebeau was born to Louis Charles Lebeau and Marie-Anne (Neveu) in Chambly, Province of Québec, Canada.
On 19 Feb 1770, Benjamin Bonneau Sr. and Marie Catherine Giroux were married in Saint-Philippe, Province of Québec, Canada.  (Saint-Philippe parish is located 13 miles southwest of Chambly.)

In 1774, the “Québec Act of 1774” was enacted by Great Britain’s Parliament setting up procedures for the governance in the “Province of Québec”.  The Act officially recognized: French civil laws in British Québec Province, Roman Catholicism as the official religion in Québec and French as the official language in Québec.  Among the other components of the “Québec Act” were the restoration of the use of the French civil law for private matters, while maintaining the use of the English common law for public administration (including criminal prosecution), rewording  of the oath of allegiance in the Québec Province so that it no longer made reference to the Protestant faith, and guaranteed free practice of the Catholic faith.  The Act's principal purpose was to prevent French Québec from joining the 13 American Colonies in their rebellion against British rule.  The fact that the “Québec Act” granted the French colonists some independence and control over civil matters helped incited resistance to British rule in the American colonies, which observe the obvious differences between the way the French Canadians and the loyal British Americans colonists were being treated.

sections.title: American Revolutionary War
sections.teaser: In 1775, the “American Revolutionary War” broke out and British Canada became a target for American forces, that sought to liberate the French population fro…

blocks.text: In 1775, the “American Revolutionary War” broke out and British Canada became a target for American forces, that sought to liberate the French population from British rule.  In Sep 1775 the American Continental Army began a two-pronged invasion of Canada, with one army’s goal of capturing the Montréal City while another army traveled through the wilderness of what is now the State of Maine toward the goal of capturing Québec City.  The two armies joined forces, but were defeated in the “Battle of Québec”, in which the American Continental General Richard Montgomery was killed.  The Continental Army was driven back out of Canada and back into New York by the arrival of a large army of British soldiers and mercenary hired German soldiers in June 1776.

On 15 Feb 1776, Benjamin Franklin, Samuel Chase and Charles Carroll travel to the Québec City and offer to make the French speaking area the 14 American colony in the effort to separate the French people in Canada from Britain.  The French Canadians seriously considered the proposal, until the American forces suffered a defeat in their effort to capture Québec City.
On 3 Feb 1777, Joseph Carrier married Marguerite St Sauveur in Chambly in Province of Québec, Canada.

On 30 Mar 1779, André Carriére was born to Joseph Carrier and his wife Marguerite (St Sauveur) in Boucherville, Province of Québec, Canada.  André later changed the spelling of his last name from Carrier to Carriére.  In 1780, André Carriére was baptized by his parents in Laval, Province of Québec, Canada.  The towns of Laval and Boucherville that are 20 miles apart on the Île de Montréal Island.

On 18 Jun 1781, Jacques Robert III and Marie Charlotte Lebeau were married in St-Mathias-sur-Richelieu, Province of Québec, Canada.  Jacques was identified in the marriage “bann” (announcement) as working as a “laborer”.

On 3 Sep 1783, the “Treaty of Paris” was signed ending the American Revolutionary War.  With the war over, the American colonists who had remained loyal to Great Britain were now on the losing side of the war.  Many of the loyal British colonists were expelled or voluntarily moved up to the British provinces in Canada.  Nova Scotia swelled with new residents and the province was partitioned creating a new separate New Brunswick Province.  Many other British loyalists moved out to the area north of the Great Lakes in western portion of the Province of Québec area to settle.  The city of York (present-day Toronto) became the center of activity and later in 1791 became the capitol of Upper Québec.  Between 1782 and 1784, over 5,000 loyal British colonists from the new “Confederation of the United States” entered what is now Ontario Province Canada.  Great Britain granted the new newly arriving British supporting American colonists, 200 acres of land and other items with which they could use to rebuild their new lives.  Great Britain also set up native people’s reserve lands area north of Lakes Superior and Lake Huron for the Native Mohawks Iroquois allies who had fought on the British side in the war and lost their land in the State of New York.
On 7 Jun 1784, Benjamin Bonneau Jr. was born to Benjamin Bonneau Sr. and Marie Catherine (Giroux) in Saint-Philippe, La Prairie, Province of Québec, Canada.
On 22 Feb 1785, Marie Anne Robert was born to Jacques Robert III and Marie Charlotte (Lebeau) in Saint-Philippe, La Prairie, Province of Québec, Canada.

In 1787, Marie Angelique Dion was born to thirty-four-year-old Frenchman Thomas Dion and a thirty-seven-year-old Native Cree partner women in St. Boniface, Manitoba, West Canada.  The Native Cree women’s name is unknown and the records only identifier her as the daughter of Payukoonisk, a Native Whitefish Cree man named “The Fathom” and Ollie Ani-Waya Chicamagu a Native Shawnee women.

Note:  The “Cree” were a North American Indigenous people and one of the largest “First Nations” tribes that lived west of Lake Superior in the current Canadian Provinces of Ontario, Manitoba, Saskatchewan and Alberta.  In the 2016 Canada census there were 356,655 Native Cree people living in Canada.  See below a photo of Cree camp taken in 1893:

blocks.caption: Note: The “Cree” were a North American Indigenous people and one of the largest “First Nations” tribes that lived wes…

blocks.text: Note:  As have been noted in the history so far of the Native Americans who were living in North America when the European explores, traders and eventual settlers arrived did not go well.  The more technically advanced Europeans with their old-world diseases eventually force the native Americans to change their traditional ways of living or eventually live controlled Indian reservations.  By far the most successful Native Americans were the ones that adopted the European ways of living and joined in with the Europeans in merging the two cultures by parenting children Native/European children.  Frenchman Thomas Dion and his Cree Métis daughter Marie Angelique Dion and Jean Nicolet’s and his Nipissing partner Jeanne Bahmahmaadjimiwin Giisis and their Euphrosine Madeleine Nicolet Métis daughter are examples where to Native Americans were able to merge with the new Europeans and thrive, passing their genes to future generations.
The family tree on the next page shows four generations back from Winifred Eloise Coss of the Coss family

blocks.caption: The family tree on the next page shows four generations back from Winifred Eloise Coss of the Coss family

blocks.text: Note:  In 1789, the French Revolution occurred and the King of France and was removed and a Republic was established resulting in political turmoil in the country that led to a dictatorship under Napoleon Bonaparte.
Note:  In 1790, the United States conducted its first census of the whole country’s population.
In the 1790 U. S. Census, Thomas Johnson, wife Elizabeth (Smith) and their twelve-year-old daughter Esther Johnson were living in Worcester in Massachusetts.

No 1790 U. S. Census has been found for Josiah Powers Jr. and his family, including sixteen-year-old Nathaniel Powers.  Based on the 1793 birth of their sixth child, son Roswell, the Powers family had moved from Westminster in Vermont, twenty miles south to Chesterfield, in the State of New Hampshire before 1793.

In 1791, Warren Coss was born to unknown parents in Lowell, Massachusetts.  Information found on Warren Coss is limited to later U. S. Census records giving information on his birth.  (Warren Coss’ birth and marriage to Edith Powers were previously covered in the Story of Edith Powers family tree and are repeated here to connect the Edith Powers and Mary Ann Goodwater’s families together.)
On 2 Dec 1795 James Falconer and Mary Morrison were married in Inverness, Scotland.  Inverness is located 111 miles south of the town of Thurso on Scotland’s north coast where James was born.

Note:  In the Scottish town of Forres, which is located 25 miles east of Inverness Scotland, is the Falconer Museum.  The Falconer Museum is named after two brothers, Hugh and Alexander Falconer, who were born and lived in the town of Forres.  Hugh Falconer who lived from1808 to 1865 became prominent within the Scottish scientific community.  A real Victorian polymath (walking encyclopedia), Hugh was a contemporary of Charles Darwin, spending much of his scientific career in the country of India.  His work led to new ideas about the antiquity of man.  He introduced tea as a cash crop to India.  Older brother Alexander Falconer who lived from 1797 to 1856, became a merchant in Calcutta but later returned to his homeland.  In his will Alexander bequeathed £1000 for the establishment of a museum in Forres Scotland of objects of art and science he had gathered.  Hugh and Alexander Falconer lived at the same time James Falconer, but no connection has been found  between the brother and James.

In 1791, the “British Constitutional Act of 1791”, also known as the “Canada Act”, was enacted which divided the Province of Québec into two new separate provinces:  Protestant British speaking “Upper Canada” (the future Ontario Province) and a Catholic French speaking “Lower Canada” (the future Québec Province).
In 1800, the “Act of Union” added the Kingdom of Ireland to the Great Britain forming a new country of the “United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland” or “United Kingdom”.
On 13 Jul 1800, Nathaniel Powers and Ester Johnson were married in the town of Westminster in Vermont.

On 30 Oct 1800, Edith Powers is born to Nathaniel Powers and Esther Johnson in Athens, Windham Co., Vermont.  Athens and Windham Vermont were about eight-miles apart.  The birth of Edith Powers starts the process of joining the large Edith Powers and large Mary Ann Goodwater branches of Winifred Eloise Coss’ Family tree.

In the 1800 U. S. Census, Josiah Powers and wife Mary (Robbins) and their two remaining children were living in Marlboro, Windham Co., Vermont.  The town of Marlboro is about 25 miles south of the town of Westminster, Vermont.

In 1803, the “Louisiana Purchase” occurred when the United States purchased France’s interest in North America for $15 million dollars.  The purchases added 827,000 square miles of land west of the Mississippi River, doubling the size of the United States.  Emperor Napoleon sold the Louisiana Territory, which he had secretly acquired three years earlier from Spain in 1800.  Napoleon’s interest in making the deal was because the African slaves in the French Colony of Haiti had revolted and over thrown their French masters.  A slave’s life in Haiti was usually hard, short and miserable.  The slaves died from yellow fever disease and their horrible slave mistreatment.  The French had to replace their entire slave population that died in Haiti every 20 years.  The slave revolt shocked France.  Haiti was a source of great wealth for France, 40% of the sugar used in Britain and France came from Haiti.  Napoleon sent a French army to Haiti to stop the slave rebellion, but the effort failed.  When President Thomas Jefferson offered the buy the city of New Orleans, Napoleon responded by offering the entire Louisiana Territory to get rid of Frances problems in North America.

Before 1805, André Carriére left Boucherville near the Montréal City and traveled 1,400 miles west to Staint Boniface, Manitoba, where the Hudson’s Bay Company was actively involved in the fur trading business in northern Canada.

In 1805, twenty-six-year-old André Carriére and eighteen-year-old Marie-Angélique Dion were married in Staint Boniface, Manitoba.  Marie Angelique Dion’s mother was identified as a Cree Native American from a Manitoba “Métis” community in the Red River Colony.  Frenchmen who settled and lived in the Canadian frontier commonly married or partnered with native women to have families.

On 22 Feb 1808, Angus Falconer was born to James Falconer and Mary (Morrison) in Eddrachilles Parish, in Sutherland Co., Scotland.  Scotland has been under strict British rule at the time since its defeat of the “Jacobite Rebellion” 1746.  Scotland was also starting to convert from a farming economy to more industrial economy with the start of the industrial revolution.

Note:  The Falconer family name derived from the Scottish name of one who breeds and trains falcons for hunting.  Many of the great houses of Scotland had their own falconer.  The principal ‘Falconer’ family clan claimed to be falconers of King William the Lion (1165-1214) beginning with Ranulph the Falconer, son of Walter of Loutrop.
In the 1810 U. S. Census, Josiah Powers and wife Mary (Robbins) and their four children were living in Marlboro, Windham Co., Vermont.
Note:  The 1810 U. S. Census was problematic, many of the census records were destroyed in a fire.

On 7 Jan 1811, Benjamin Bonneau Jr. and Marie Anne Robert were married in Mont-Saint-Hilaire in the Province of Québec in Lower Canada.  The town of Mont-Saint-Hilaire was located on the east of the Richelieu River, which flows north from the State of New York into Canada where it merges with the St. Lawrence River, east of the City of Montréal.
On 15 May 1811, Josephine Morrison was born in the parish of Eddrachilles, Sutherland Co., Scotland to unknown parents.

On 9 Nov 1811, Benjamin Bonneau III was born in Benjamin Bonneau Jr. and Marie Anne (Robert) in  St-Mathias, Sur-Richelieu in the Province of Québec in Lower Canada.  The next day 10 Nov 1811, Benjamin Bonneau III was baptized in St-Mathias Church by his parents Benjamin Bonneau Jr. and Marie Anne (Robert).  The city of St-Mathias, Sur-Richelieu is located on the East side of the Richelieu River, 3 miles north of City of Chambly and 6.5 miles south of Mont-Saint-Hilaire.

Note:  The Richelieu River was an important river that connected the City of Montréal, which is located on the north side of the St. Lawrence River, down to Lake Champlain in the northern portion of the current State of New York.  Cities like St-Mathias, Sur-Richelieu, Mont-Saint-Hilaire and Chambly in the Province of Québec are located on the Richelieu River providing a convenient transportation route between the French Province of Québec Canada down to New England States in the United States.  See a map below of the Richelieu River that flows from the United States north into Québec Canada:

blocks.caption: Note: The Richelieu River was an important river that connected the City of Montréal, which is located on the north s…

blocks.text: In 1811, Thomas Douglas the 5th Lord Selkirk was granted 120,000 squire miles of land (five time the size of Scotland where Lord Selkirk was from) that was located at the junction of the Red and Seine Rivers for a new “Red River Colony” by the Montréal based “North West Company”.  The site today is the city of Winnipeg Manitoba Canada.  The new “Red River Colony” included the northern portions of land in present-day States of North Dakota and Minnesota and much of the southern Province of Manitoba in Canada.  The new colony was developed as an agriculture colony in the large Red River Valley region to reduce Canada’s reliance on British shipments of food from Great Britain to feed the growing number of English and French settlers living in central Canada.  See the maps below of the Red River Valley and Lord Selkirk’s Red River Colony:

images.caption: In 1811, Thomas Douglas the 5th Lord Selkirk was granted 120,000 squire miles of land (five time the size of Scotland…
In 1811, Thomas Douglas the 5th Lord Selkirk was granted 120,000 squire miles of land (five time the size of Scotland…

blocks.text: In 1811, thirty-two-year-old André Carriére was listed as working for the North West Company in the new Red River Colony in Manitoba, Canada.

sections.title: War of 1812
sections.teaser: On 18 June 1812, the new United States of America declared war against United Kingdom beginning the “War of 1812”.  Although often treated as a minor footnot…

blocks.text: On 18 June 1812, the new United States of America declared war against United Kingdom beginning the “War of 1812”.  Although often treated as a minor footnote to the bloody European war between France and Britain, the “War of 1812” was crucial for the future of United States.  The victory in the war effectively destroyed the Native Americans' ability to resist the United States’ expansion into the large Ohio River valley.  General Andrew Jackson crushed the Native Creek tribe in the “Battle of Horseshoe Bend” in Alabama, while General William Henry Harrison defeated the Native Tecumseh’s Confederation who were allies of the British army at the “Battle of the Thames” in Ontario, Upper Canada.  Abandoned by their British allies, the Native Americans tribes reluctantly surrendered most of their lands north of the Ohio River and in western Alabama to the United States’ government.
See a map on the next page showing the large Ohio River Valley:

blocks.caption: See a map on the next page showing the large Ohio River Valley:

blocks.text: Note:  See the Story of Morris Rankin Batie’s Family, and the Story of Carolee, Afton McCance’s Family for more details on the “War of 1812”.

On 16 Sep 1813, Mary Louise Carriére was born to André Carriére and Marie Angelique (Dion) in Staint Boniface community in the Red River Colony in Manitoba Canada.  Mary Louise’s mother Marie Angelique was a 50% Native Cree and Shawnee “Métis” women and her daughter Mary Louise Carriére was a 25% of mixed race “Métis” women.

On 8 Jan 1815, the “Battle of New Orleans” was fought in the “War of 1812” between the British Army under Major General Sir Edward Pakenham and the United States Army under Major General Andrew Jackson.  The battle occurred 5 miles southeast of the French Quarter of the city of New Orleans.  The battle was the climax of the five-month “Gulf Campaign” by the British military in their efforts to take over possession of New Orleans, West Florida and the Louisiana Territory.  The battle took place 18 days after the signing of the “Treaty of Ghent”, which formally ended the “War of 1812” between Great Britain and the United States.  The news of the new treaty agreement had not yet reached the United States from Europe.  Despite a large British advantage in the numbers of trained experienced soldiers, the American volunteer forces defeated a poorly executed assault by the British Army in slightly more than 30 minutes.  The Americans suffered roughly 60 casualties, while the British suffered roughly 2,000 casualties, including the deaths of their commander, Major General Sir Edward Pakenham.

While Britain and the United States had been negotiating to end the “War of 1812” in Ghent Netherlands in Europe, Henry Bathurst, the British “Secretary of State for War and the Colonies”, sent secret orders to General Pakenham, commanding him to continue the war even if he heard rumors of peace because the British Secretary did not want to stop miss an opportunity for major British victory over the United States.  Great Britain and their Spanish allies wanted to reclaim the territories sold to the United State in the “Louisiana Purchase” because they did not recognize any land deals made by Napoleon (first the 1800 transfer of the Louisiana Territory from Spain to France and then the later 1803 transfer of the Louisiana Territory from France to the United States).  If the British had won the “Battle of New Orleans”, they would have claimed all territories west of the Mississippi River that United States had bought from France in the 1803 “Louisiana Purchase”, preventing the expansion of the United States beyond the Mississippi River.  The United States might have also lost Florida to Spain.

The victory in the “War of 1812” allowed the United States to rewrite its boundaries with Spain and solidify control over the lower Mississippi River and the Gulf of Mexico.  Although the United States did not defeat the British Empire, it had fought the world's strongest power to a draw.  Spain recognized the significance of this fact and in 1819 Spanish leaders abandoned Florida and agreed to a United States boundary running across the North American continent to the Pacific Ocean.

On 20 Oct 1818 , the “Convention respecting fiseries, boundary and the restoration of slaves” or the “Treaty of 1818” was signed between the United States and the United Kingdom.  This treaty resolved standing boundary issues in North America between the two nations.  The treaty allowed for joint occupation and settlement of the Oregon Territory.  The two nations agreed to a boundary line at the 49th parallel north separating the United States and Canada, in part because a straight-line boundary would be easier to survey than the pre-existing boundaries based on watersheds of rivers.  The Great Britain ceded all of Rupert’s Land south of the 49th parallel and east of the Continental Divide, including all of the Red River Colony south of the 49 parallel, while the United States ceded the northernmost edge of the Missouri Territory north of the 49th parallel.  See a map below showing the new 49th boundary line between Canada and the United States:

blocks.caption: On 20 Oct 1818 , the “Convention respecting fiseries, boundary and the restoration of slaves” or the “Treaty of 1818”…

blocks.text: In 1818, a group of French missionaries living in a Roman Catholic mission in the Red River Colony settlement, worked with the Ojibwe and Cree Native people in the Red River area to introduce them to Christianity, particularly the 72 native women who had married French Canadian men and were currently living in the settlement area.  By 1823, there were about 350 mixed French/Native American families called “Métis” families living in and around Red River Colony settlement and another 450 “Métis” families living in the present North Dakota area.  “Métis” was a French word developed back in the early fur trading period to identify people of mixed European and Indigenous Native parents.

In the 1820 U. S. Census, Nathaniel Powers was living in Westminster, Windham Co., Vermont.  With Nathaniel was a women age 20-45, his wife Esther (Johnson), four daughters ages 16-25 including twenty-year-old daughter Edith Powers, two daughters under 10-year-old, one son 16-25, four sons 10-15 and three sons under 10-year-old.
No 1820 U. S. Census record has been found for Warren Coss.

In 1821, the Montréal based “North West Company” (NWC) was force to merge with the “Hudson’s Bay Company” (HBC) by the British government.  An HBC service records for the Red River Colony shows forty-five-year-old André Carriére working for NWC from 1811-1821 and HBC 1821-1831.  In 1827-1828, André Carriére traveled from the Red River settlement of the York Factory up on the shore of Hudson Bay.  In 1829-1830, André Carriére was an Inland Summer Tripman.  See a photo of a HBC Service Document below:

images.caption: In 1821, the Montréal based “North West Company” (NWC) was force to merge with the “Hudson’s Bay Company” (HBC) by th…
In 1821, the Montréal based “North West Company” (NWC) was force to merge with the “Hudson’s Bay Company” (HBC) by th…

blocks.text: A second HBC record exists that lists André Carriére as being the son of Joseph Carriére and Marguerite (St. Sauveur) of Boucherville, District of Montréal, who was married to Marie Angelique Dion, the daughter of Thomas Dion and a “Métis” Native Cree women.  See on the next page a photo of an HBC marriage document for André Carriére:

blocks.caption: A second HBC record exists that lists André Carriére as being the son of Joseph Carriére and Marguerite (St. Sauveur)…

blocks.text: On 30 Sep 1824, Warren Coss and Edith Powers were married in Lowell, Middlesex Co., Massachusetts.
Note:  The marriage of Warren Coss and Edith Powers joined the very short Coss family tree branch and the very long Powers family tree branch of Winifred Eloise Coss’s family tree.

On 19 Jul 1827, Joseph Warren Coss was born to Warren Coss and Edith (Powers) in New Hampshire.  No birth certificate has been found and different birth locations are recorded on different United States census and military records.  The birth place on “Find a Grave” is listed as New Hampshire without a city location.  Joseph’s one-year-old older sister Martha Coss died in Peterborough, Hillsborough Co., in New Hampshire two months later after his birth, which was most likely the place where Joseph Warren Coss was born.  Normal records for Joseph Coss and Warren Coss are missing, which appears to be a problem with other New Hampshire records prior to 1850.

In 1827 Red River Settlement Census, André Carriére was living in lot #164, as a 45-year-old Roman Catholic, with 1 women, 3 sons (+16), 3 son (-16), 3 daughter (-15), 1 house, 1 stable, 3 horses, 2 cows, 1 ox, 2 calves, 1 cart, 1 plough, 1 harrow, 1 canoe and 3 acres.
Note:  In 1828, the U. S. president election was the first-time non-property-holding white males were allowed to vote in the majority of States in the United States.

On 13 Apr 1829, Henry Raymond was born in Yorkshire, England to unknown parents.  Henry Raymond is the start of the short Raymond family tree branch.  No information on Henry’s arrival in Canada had been found.
On 24 Feb 1830, Angus Falconer and Josephine Morrison were married in Eddrachilles Parish in Sutherland Co, Scotland.
In the 1830 U. S. Census, Warren Coss and his wife Edith (Powers) and their children including, two-year-old son Joseph Warren Coss, were living in Lowell, Middlesex Co., Massachusetts.

In the 1830 U. S. Census, Nathaniel Powers was living in Westminster, Windham, Vermont.  With Nathaniel was a women age 50-59, wife Esther, one daughter age 20-29, one daughter age 15-19, one daughter 10-19, two sons 20-29, one son 15-19  and two sons under 10-14.

On 17 May 1831, twenty-year-old Benjamin Bonneau III and eighteen-year-old Mary Louise Carriére, daughter of André Carriére and Marie Angelique (Dion), were married in the Saint-Athanase Church in Iberville, Québec Province, Lower Canada.  No information has been found on when Mary Louise Carriére left St. Boniface, Manitoba and traveled 1,422 miles east to the town of Iberville, which is 30 miles south from Boucherville, where her father André Carriére was born.  André’s work record with the Hudson Bay Company ends in 1831 and eighteen-year-old Mary Louise may have left Manitoba and moved back to the Québec area, where the rest of her father’s Bonneau family relatives were still living.  The Red River Valley, where the St. Boniface settlement was located, was subject to major flooding events during the annual spring thaw and Mary Louise may have decided to find a more stable area to live in.  The Red River Colony also had a major whooping cough epidemic in 1827-28 and Mary Louise may have decided she wanted to live somewhere else.
On 11 Feb 1832, Sarah Cannon was born to William Cannon and Elizabeth (Lambourn) in East Ilsley, Berkshire, Great Britain.  On 15 Oct 1833, Sarah was baptized by her parents in East Ilsley.

Between 1832 and 1833, Benjamin Bonneau III and Mary Louise (Carriére) left Iberville, Québec Province Lower Canada and immigrate 50 miles south across the border to the State of Vermont in the United States.  No immigrating record has been found for the border crossing.  The move of locations is based on the birth records of Benjamin and Mary’s 1st and 2nd children.  It appears they snuck across border.

In 1832, fifty-three-year-old André Carriére was still living in the Red River Settlement in Manitoba, Canada.  André Carriére was later reported in 1838, 1840, 1843, 1846 and 1849 Manitoba census records as living in the Red River Settlement in Manitoba.  No information was included in the census about his wife Marie Angelique and their children.  Later on, 26 Jul 1861, eighty-two-year-old André Carriére died in St Norbert, Manitoba.  His seventy-four-year-old wife Marie Angelique also died the same year in St Boniface, Manitoba.  The two locations are 10 miles apart.  Disease outbreaks were fairly common in the Red River Colony.  Smallpox, measles, whooping cough and influenzas epidemics occurred in the in the late 1700s and throughout the 1800s.  No records have been found showing the causes of André and Marie deaths.  See on the next page an image of a Red River Colony Catholic Settlement’s 1846-47 census record for André Carriére:

blocks.caption: In 1832, fifty-three-year-old André Carriére was still living in the Red River Settlement in Manitoba, Canada. André…

blocks.text: Between 1833 and 1836, Benjamin Bonneau III, wife Mary Louise (Carriére) and their children left Vermont and traveled 375 miles west to the town of Lancaster, Erie Co., New York, which is 10 miles east of Buffalo, New York.  The move date is based on the birth records of their 2nd and 3rd children.
On 16 Feb 1833, Thomas Franklin was born to William Franklin and Hannah (Bolton) in Mixbury, Oxfordshire, Great Britain.  Thomas was baptized on 17 Mar 1833 by his parents in Mixbury.
In 1833 and 1834, Warren Coss was listed in the Lowell, Massachusetts town’s directories as ( a machine shop worker) in Lowell.

On 1 Jun 1833, settlers were allowed to legally occupy land claims in Iowa after “Black Hawk Purchase” was completed that paid the Black Hawk Native tribe $20,000 a year for 30 years for their land.  The new settlers in Iowa sent back word to relatives and friends in Illinois, Ohio and Indiana and other States, about the rich and beautiful country awaiting the people in Iowa.

On 3 Aug 1835, Elizabeth Falconer was born to Angus Falconer and Josephine (Morison) while the family was on a ship sailing from England to the town of New London on Prince Edward Island in the Maritime Province of Canada.
On 12 Feb 1836, Elizabeth Falconer was baptized by her parents Angus Falconer and Josephine (Morison) in New London on Prince Edward Island.

On 4 Jul 1838, the U.S. Congress approved the “Iowa Territory” that was composed of the present states of Iowa, Minnesota west of the Mississippi River and the Dakotas east of the White Earth and Missouri Rivers.  Four-hundred square miles of land was set aside in the large area for the Sacs and Foxes Native American Tribes.  Settlers were now allowed to settle beyond the area defined earlier in the 1833 “Black Hawk Purchase”.  A great tide of people surged into the new lands of the “Iowa Territory”.  See a map on the next page of the 1838 “Iowa Territory”:

blocks.caption: On 4 Jul 1838, the U.S. Congress approved the “Iowa Territory” that was composed of the present states of Iowa, Minne…

blocks.text: On 15 Apr 1839, Mary Ann Bonneau, was born to Benjamin Bonneau III and Mary Louise (Carriére) in Lancaster, Erie Co., New York.  The place of birth comes from multiple U.S. Census records and find-a-grave document.  No birth record has however been found.  Mary Ann Bonneau’s, whose last name will later change to Goodwater.  The last name change occurred before the 1850 U. S. Census when her father Benjamin answered the census recorder’s questions and Benjamin gave his last name as Goodwater.  Benjamin four years later completed his U.S. Nationalization and listing his name as Benjamin Goodwater.  See a photo of Mary Louise (Carriére) below:

blocks.caption: On 15 Apr 1839, Mary Ann Bonneau, was born to Benjamin Bonneau III and Mary Louise (Carriére) in Lancaster, Erie Co.,…

blocks.text: Note:  The French family name of Bonneau was perverted to Bono (easer to say and spell) when Benjamin Bonneau III was living in Québec City.  The French name Bonneau translates to “Good water” in English and Benjamin adopted the English translation of his name, and it appeared as Benjamin’s last name on the 1850 Census in Wheelock, Caledonia Co., Vermont.  Mrs. Delina Bonneau wife of Vitral Bonneau, not related to Benjamin, had also used Goodwater as an alias name when she completed her request in 1880 for U.S. Civil Was Pension upon the death of her husband, indicating other Bonneau’s also ran into issues with the Bonneau French name.

Note:  Between 1840 and 1930, roughly 900,000 French Canadians left the Québec Province in Lower Canada and immigrated to the United States.  They poured into the New England states in droves, entire families consisting of parents with over a half dozen children.  Most spoke only French and were met at train depots throughout New England by kin who arrived months, or years earlier.  Jobs were waiting for them--even the children as young as eight-year-olds could easily find employment.  Why did so many leave the Québec Province , it was simply the fact that the farm lands in Lower Québec could no longer support the growing population.  Between 1784 and 1844, Québec's population had increased by about 400%, while the agricultural acreage had risen only by 275%, creating a large food deficit in the area.  As a result, there were thousands of landless farmers searching for a way to feed their family and they left for better opportunities in New England.

In the 1840 U. S. Census, Nathaniel Powers was living in Westminster, Windham, Vermont.  With Nathaniel was a woman age 60-69, wife Esther, one daughter age 30-39, one granddaughter age 5-9, one granddaughter daughter under 5, one grandson 15-19 and two sons under 10-14.

In the 1840 U. S. Census, Warren Coss was living in Athens, Windham, Vermont.  With Warren was a woman age 30-39, wife Edith and their children, including a daughter age 5-9, a son Joseph Warren Coss age 10-14 and son William Coss son age 5-9.

Back in 1815, the “Treaty of Ghent” ended the “War of 1812” and reaffirming the United States jurisdiction over the “Michigan Territory”, which included the future State of Wisconsin.  The establishment of American settlements in the Wisconsin Territory was delayed by two Native American tribes fighting in 1827 the “Winnebago War” and later in 1832 the “Black Hawk War” in hopeless efforts to try and stop the settlement of their ancestral homelands.  The defeat of the Native American tribes living in Wisconsin open a large area for new settlements.  On 3 Jul 1836, the “Wisconsin Territory” was created by an act of the U. S. Congress.  The first settlers were drawn to the new area to prospect and mine “lead ore” in southwest of the territory.  By the 1840’s southwest Wisconsin was producing more than half of the lead in the country.  See a map below showing the 1836 Wisconsin and Michigan Territories:

blocks.caption: Back in 1815, the “Treaty of Ghent” ended the “War of 1812” and reaffirming the United States jurisdiction over the “…

blocks.text: The Wisconsin frontier evolved through multiple phases over three hundred years.  Starting as the ancestral homelands of Native American people, followed by arrival of the French who were interested in fur-trading and later the British who were interested in mining the rich minerals and logging the forests in the area.  The arrival of the railroad in the 1850s brought the end of the frontier period and farming soon became the primary use of the land.

Note:  The first countrywide census in Great Britain occurred in 1841.  The first national census in Canada started in 1871.  Individual Canadian provinces census did take place on irregularly bases prior to 1871.
Note:  In 1841 the terms Upper Canada and Lower Canada were changed to Canada West, previous Upper Canada and Canada East previous Lower Canada.

Sometime after 5 May 1841, when the third son Whitcomb Nathaniel Coss was born in Windham, Vermont to Warren Coss, wife Edith (Powers) the left Windham Vermont and moved 1,020 miles west to the rural town of Palmyra in Jefferson Co., Wisconsin.  The family probably used multiple modes of transportation starting with the Erie Canal that could take them to from Albany New York to Buffalo New York on Lake Erie.  Crossing Lake Erie by boat would have taken them to Toledo, Ohio.  Railroads were still very new and were not yet in place between Toledo, Ohio and Chicago, Illinois so the Warren Coss family would have had travel by stage or wagon to Chicago and then out to Palmyra community in Wisconsin.

In the 1841 England Census, eight-year-old Thomas Franklin was living with his widowed mother Hannah Franklin (Bolton) and his older brother and sister in Mixbury, Oxfordshire, Great Britain.  Thomas’ father William Franklin had died back in 1834.

In 1841, Angus Falconer, wife Josephine (Morrison) and their children, including six-year-old daughter Elizabeth Falconer were now living in Port Egin, Bruce Co. in Ontario West Canada.  The family’s location was based on the birth of their second son John William Falconer on 19 Jan 1841.  Port Elgin was located over 1,300 miles west of New London out on Prince Edward Island.  The family was required to travel up the St Lawrence River, across Lake Ontario and across Ontario Province to the west shore of Lake Huron to reach Port Egin which was name of the harbor on the easter shore of Lake Huron for the community of Saugeen, Bruce Co. Canada West.  The Saugeen community was named after the original “First Nation” Native Saugeen people who lived in the area.  See 1857 maps of Bruce County on the next page.  On the left is a blowup of Bruce County in green with Saugeen location identified by the blue arrow:

images.caption: In 1841, Angus Falconer, wife Josephine (Morrison) and their children, including six-year-old daughter Elizabeth Falc…
In 1841, Angus Falconer, wife Josephine (Morrison) and their children, including six-year-old daughter Elizabeth Falc…
In 1841, Angus Falconer, wife Josephine (Morrison) and their children, including six-year-old daughter Elizabeth Falc…

blocks.text: On 12 Mar 1843, Charlotte Pollard was born to Joseph Pollard and Mary (Jefferson) in Guisborough, Yorkshire, Great Britain.

In 1843, Mary Collingwood was born to Edward Collingwood and Sarah (Gray) in the small village of Running Waters in Durham, Great Britain.  On 26 Sep 1843, Mary Collingwood was baptized by her parents in Shadforth, Durham, Great Britain, which was located less than a mile east of Running Waters.

In 1843, Warren Coss and wife Edith (Powers) and their children, including eighteen-year-old Joseph Warren Coss left Athens, Windham, Vermont and moved 1,000 miles west to Palmyra, Jefferson Co. Wisconsin.  The family probably used multiple modes of transportation; however, the Railroad lines were now open and could have taken all the way the town of Rockford that was 80 miles west of Chicago, leaving the family 60 miles short from their destination, the community of Palmyra in Wisconsin.

Note:  Winneshiek County, Iowa was organized in 1847 and open to settlers in 1848.  The county was located in the top Northeast corner of Iowa.  Winneshiek County has a rich cultural history as a number different European immigrants settled and established farms within its boundaries.  The county was named after a Native chief of the Winnebago tribe in the area.
In 1847, twenty-three-year-old Sarah Collingwood (Gray), the mother of Mary Collingwood, died in Sunderland, Durham, Great Britain, which is located 25 miles east of Shadforth, Durham, Great Britain.

In the 1850 U. S. Census, Warren Coss and wife Edith (Powers) and their six children were living in Palmyra, Jefferson Co., Wisconsin.  Warren was farming on his $400 valued farmland.  The fact that the census included the value on the land Warren and Edith were living on indicates they owned the land.  The censuses prior to 1850 did not include land value estimates so land ownership was not possible to determine, except in the probate process, which occurred upon death in “will” distributions.  Warren Coss’ twenty-three son Joseph Warren Coss was no longer living with the Warren family.  At some point prior to 1850, Joseph Warren Coss had left the family and moved out 190 miles farther west from Palmyra Wisconsin to Winneshiek County, Iowa.

In the 1850 U. S. Census, Benjamin Goodwater III and wife Mary Louise (Carriere) and their five children, including eleven-year-old daughter Mary Ann Goodwater were living in Wheelock, Caledonia Co., Vermont.  Benjamin was farming and had changed his family’s last name from Bonneau to Goodwater.

Note:  The small town of Wheelock, located 35 miles south of the border with Canada, was named after Eleazar Wheelock, the founder of Dartmouth College.  Through a provision setup in 1830 by Eleazar, any full-time resident of Wheelock who was accepted as an undergraduate in Dartmouth College could attend the college entirely free of tuition.

The Benjamin Bonneau III’s family fit the profile of poor subsistence Canadian farmers looking for a better life.  The Bonneau family starting in Canada when Jean Bonneau’s arrival in the Ville Marie settlement back in 1653, where he farmed for a living.  Jean’s son Pierre and his family followed his father to Canada a year later.  Pierre’s son Joseph had an opportunity to acquire land on the Île d’Orléans Island when the area opened up to settlers.  Joseph’s son Dominique Sr. was the 5th son of Joseph’s second marriage and one of Joseph’s twenty-two children.  Clearly Dominique Sr. was not going to inherit much and had to leave his father’s farm on the Île d’Orléans Island and find a new life for himself.  Over the next four generations leading up to Benjamin Bonneau III, the Bonneau families lived in different farming locations indicating each generation worked on someone else’s land as “tenant farmers”.

After 12 Sep 1850, when the 1850 U. S. Census for Benjamin Goodwater III and his family was recorded, the family was now living in Ossian, Winneshiek Co., Iowa.  Benjamin and his family had left Wheelock, Caledonia Co., Vermont and traveled an incredible 1,300 miles west to Iowa.  The new United States railroad networks were underway by 1850 and ships were crossing Lake Erie at the time.  The family probably used multiple modes of transportation starting with the Erie Canal that would take them to from Albany New York to Buffalo New York on Lake Erie.  Crossing Lake Erie by boat would have taken them to Toledo, Ohio.  Railroads would next take them from Toledo out west of Chicago to Rockford Illinois where the railroad ended, leaving the family 200 miles from their Iowa destination.

In the 1851 Canada Census, Angus Falconer, wife Josephine (Morison), six children, including sixteen-year-old daughters Elizabeth Falconer, were listed living in Chatham, Kent Co., Ontario in Canada West.  Angus was working as a “weaver” (making cloth), wife Josephine and daughters Elizabeth and Mary were working as “seamstresses”(turning cloth into cloths).

In the 1851 Canada Census, twenty-two-year-old Henry Raymond was farming on 100 acres of land of which 50 acres were cultivated in wheat in Houghton, Norfolk Co., Ontario, Upper Canada.  The 1851 Canada Census is the first record that has been found on Henry Raymond after his birth in England.

Note:  "The Township of Houghton was located in the southwest corner of Norfolk County in Ontario Canada.  It was triangular in shape, its northern portion forming an acute point running up between the Townships of Walsingham and Middleton on the east, and the Township of Bayham on the west.  The base of the triangle was on the northern shore of Lake Erie.  The first settlers in the area were British loyalist that left the United States in 1789 after the end of the American Revolutionary War.  Today Houghton is a neighborhood Port Burwell, Norfolk Co. Ontario Canada.

In the 1851 England Census, eighteen-year-old Thomas Franklin was working as a “house servant” on the Cold Harbor Farm in Mixbury, Oxfordshire, England.  Thomas was listed living with four other “house servants”, indicating the family Thomas was working for were wealthy.

In the 1851 England Census, six-year-old Mary Collingwood was living in the home of Ralph Peverell and his wife Mary and their ten-month-old son in St Andrew, Auckland, Durham, England.  Mary was listed as a “visitor” with the Peverell family and was attending school.  Mary Collingwood was listed as having been born in Running Waters in Durham Great Britain, which was located 13 miles north of St Andrew, Auckland, Durham.

In 1851, the first “Treaty of Fort Laramie” was signed in an attempted to resolve disputes between Native American tribes and the U. S. Government, as well as among individual Native tribes themselves, in areas that will become the states of Montana, Wyoming, Nebraska, North Dakota and South Dakota.  The treaty specified that the tribes would make peace among one another and allow outside access to their Native lands for activities such as travelling, surveying, and the construction of government outposts and roads.  As part of the treaty the individual Native tribes would be responsible for wrongs committed by their own people.  In return, the U. S. Government agreed to protect the Native tribes on their own land and pay an annual of $50,000 annuity each year to the Native tribes.

On 2 Apr 1853, twenty-seven-year-old Joseph Warren Coss and fifteen-year-old Mary Ann Goodwater were married in Winneshiek Co., Iowa.  This marriage connects the two major branches of Winifred Eloise Coss’s family tree by joining the Goodwater’s and the Prowers’s branches through Joseph Warren Coss’s mother Edith Powers.
About 1854, Henry Raymond and Elizabeth Falconer were married in Saugeen, Bruce Co. Ontario Upper Canada.  No marriage record has been found.

Note:  The 1854, immigration to the State of Iowa was unparalleled at the time.  In 1854, News Papers throughout the United States contained long accounts, full of exclamation points, telling their readers about the opportunities to own land in Iowa.  The roads leading to open farmland in Iowa were crowed with families in canvas topped wagons.  The new land open to settlement was quickly filled with people living in white canvas tents.  Ferries over the Mississippi River were busy both day and night conveying pioneer families from Illinois to Iowa.  New family cabins began to be built like magic.

On 29 Mar 1854, Benjamin Goodwater III completed the U.S. Nationalization process to become a U.S. Citizen in Winneshiek Co., Iowa.  Benjamin had relocated his family, including fifteen-year-old daughter Mary Ann Goodwater, from Ossian, Iowa and acquired a homestead 10-mile east in Frankville, Winneshiek Co., Iowa in an unincorporated farming community in the northeast of Iowa 20 mile south of the Minnesota border and 20 miles east of the Wisconsin Border.  See a photo below of Benjamin Goodwater immigration card:

images.caption: On 29 Mar 1854, Benjamin Goodwater III completed the U.S. Nationalization process to become a U.S. Citizen in Winnesh…
On 29 Mar 1854, Benjamin Goodwater III completed the U.S. Nationalization process to become a U.S. Citizen in Winnesh…

blocks.text: See below a copy of an 1886 map of Frankville, Winneshiek Co, Iowa.  Benjamin Goodwater III’s farm is highlighted in yellow on the bottom of the map.

images.caption: See below a copy of an 1886 map of Frankville, Winneshiek Co, Iowa. Benjamin Goodwater III’s farm is highlighted in y…
See below a copy of an 1886 map of Frankville, Winneshiek Co, Iowa. Benjamin Goodwater III’s farm is highlighted in y…

blocks.text: In 1854, the old French feudal seigneurial system in Québec Lower Canada was finally officially abolished, after Benjamin Goodwater III and Mary Louise (Carriere) and their children had left Québec seeking land and opportunity in the United States.
On 28 May 1855, Thomas Franklin and Sarah Cannon were married in Toot Baldon, Oxfordshire, Great Britain.

On 8 Nov 1855, Edgar Putnam Coss was born to Joseph Warren Coss and sixteen-year-old Mary Ann (Goodwater) in Sauk Centre, Steans Co., Wisconsin.  Sauk Centre, Wisconsin was located 260-miles northwest of Winneshiek Co., Iowa where Joseph and Mary Ann had been married and lived 20 months earlier.

The 1856 Iowa Census, listed Benjamin Goodwater III, his wife Mary Louise (Carriére) and their children now living in Glenwood, Winneshiek, Iowa, eleven miles north of Frankville, Winneshiek Co., Iowa.  Daughter Mary Ann Coss (Goodwater) was gone having married Joseph Warren Coss two years earlier.

In the 1860 U. S. Census, Warren Coss, wife Edith (Powers) and their twenty-six-year-old twins, daughter Hellen and son Henry, and their nineteen-year-old son Whitcomb Nathaniel were living in Hebron, Jefferson Co., Wisconsin.  Additionally, six-year-old Henry Goodwater and three-year-old Georginaus Goodwater were living with the Coss family.  Warren Coss was working as a “machinist” on land valued at $500 with his personal estate worth $200.

Note:  The Hebron, Wisconsin community was started back in 1835 as a lumber milling town.  A large saw mill was built to convert the abundant forest of trees into lumber for building materials.  On 4 Nov 1844 Joseph Powers (no connection to Edith Powers) purchased the saw mill.  The town named after the Palestinian city on the west bank of the Jordan River, grew rapidly and soon had six saw mills and 300 people living in the area.  Hebron was located 8 miles northwest of the town Palmyra, Wisconsin another town named after bible references, where David Johnson Powers built a saw mill in 1849.  The closeness Hebron and Palmyra in Wisconsin brought the Powers and Coss families together.  See a current photo below of the Prince’s Point Wildlife area that is located between the small towns of Hebron and Palmyra in Wisconsin:

blocks.caption: Note: The Hebron, Wisconsin community was started back in 1835 as a lumber milling town. A large saw mill was built t…

blocks.text: Note:  Beginning about mid 1800s many of the Winifred Eloise Coss family began to give and use nick names, such as Ed, Sadie, Hannah, Annie, Wini.  The nick names sometime appeared in historical records and other time were omitted.  Generally, the name that appeared on the record being reference have been used.

In the 1860 U. S. Census, Joseph Warren Coss, wife Mary Ann (Goodwater) their five-year-old son Edgar Putnam “Ed” Coss and three-year-old son William Coss were now living in Cherry Grove, Goodhue, Minnesota, 157 miles southeast from Sauk Centre, Steans Co., Wisconsin.  Joseph was farming on land valued at $252 with $300 of personal wealth.  Living next to Joseph Warren Coss family farm was the family of Noman Goodwater, indicating the Coss and Goodwater families knew each other.

In the 1860 U. S. Census, Benjamin Goodwater III and Mary Louise (Carriére) and their three remaining children were living in Glenwood, Winneshiek Co., Iowa.  Benjamin was successfully farming on $1,200 valued farmland and had $375 personal estate value.  Benjamin had changed his name, but he still was listed as someone from France in the census.

In the 1861 Canada Census, Henry Raymond, wife Elizabeth (Falconer) and their four children were living in Saugeen, Bruce, Canada West.  Henry was working as a “blacksmith”.  The Raymond family were living in a framed two-story house.

Note:  Today the town of Saint Nobert is a bilingual (French and English) community located on the southern edge of Winnipeg, Manitoba, Canada, where the Red River Colony was located.  In 1869, the Saint Nobert village was at the center of the “Red River Rebellion” that was a sequence of events that led up to the establishment of a new provisional government led by Métis leader Louis Riel.  The “Red River Rebellion” was the first major crisis the new federal Canadian government had to address when the three British North American provinces: Province of Canada, Province of Nova Scotia and the Proving of New Brunswick were united into the “Dominion of Canada”.

The new expanded “Dominion of Canada” had appointed an English-speaking governor for the Red River Colony who was opposed by the French-speaking mostly-Métis inhabitants of the Saint Nobert settlement.  Before the land was officially transferred to Canada the new governor sent a group of surveyors out to plot the land according to the United States square mile township system.  The Métis, led by Louis Riel, prevented the survey group from entering the territory.  The Métis next created a provisional government to which they invited an equal number of Anglophone (English speaking) representatives.  Louis Riel negotiated directly with the new federal Canadian government to establish a new Manitoba Canadian province.

Louis Riel next arrested members of a pro-British Canadian faction leaders who had resisted the new Métis provisional government.  One of the arrested leaders was a protestant northern Irishman named Thomas Scott.  Riel's government tried and convicted Scott for insubordination and he was executed.  The new federal Canada government and Louis Riel’s provisional government soon negotiated an agreement to stop hostilities.  In 1870, the Canadian Parliament passed the “Manitoba Act”, allowing the Red River Colony to enter Dominion of Canada as the new “Province of Manitoba”.  The Manitoba Act also incorporated some of Louis Riel's demands, such as the provision to create separate French speaking schools for Métis children and the protection of the Catholic religion in the new province.

In the 1861 Ontario Canada West Census, fifty-three-year-old Angus Falconer and his wife Josephine (Morison) and their children were living in the community of Saugeen in North Bruce Co., Ontario Canada West.  Angus was “fishing” for a living with fishing equipment valued at $150.

In the 1861 Canada West Census for Bruce County, Henry Raymond, wife Elizabeth (Falconer) and their four children were living in Saugeen, Bruce Co., Canada West.  Henry was working as a “blacksmith”.  Henry and Elizabeth were members of the Church of England and lived in a framed house.  Henry Raymond’s place of birth was listed as England.
In the 1861 England Census, Thomas Franklin, wife Sarah (Cannon) and their two young children were living in Plumstead, Kent, England.

In the 1861 English Census, forty-two-year-old Edward Collingwood and his thirty-six-year-old wife Jane Collingwood (Banks) with their seventeen-year-old daughter Mary Collingwood, fifteen-year-old daughter Jane, thirteen-year-old daughter Margret and one-year-old son John Thomas were listed as living in the home in Cornforth, Durham, United Kingdom.  Mary Collingwood birth place was listed as Shadforth, Durham, England.  The village of Shadforth is located less than that a mile east of the village of Running Waters, where the 1851 English Census listed Mary Collingwood was born.  Additionally, Mary Collingwood was baptized by her parents in Shadforth.  Widower Edward Collingwood had remarried Jane Banks in 1858 after losing his first wife Sarah Gary and was farming on 35 acres of land for a living.

sections.title: Dakota Territory
sections.teaser: The “Dakota Territory” consisted of the northernmost part of the land acquired back in the 1803 in the Louisiana Purchase from France and the later 1818 agre…

blocks.text: The “Dakota Territory” consisted of the northernmost part of the land acquired back in the 1803 in the Louisiana Purchase from France and the later 1818 agreement with British Canada that established the border between Canada and the United States at the 49th parallel.  The name “Dakota” refers to the Dakota branch of the Native American Sioux Nation people who were living in the area at the time.  Most of new “Dakota Territory” was formerly part of the Minnesota and Nebraska territories.

When Minnesota became a state in 1858, the leftover area between the Missouri River and the and Minnesota's western boundary was left unorganized, from the United States government’s perspective but not the perspective of the Sioux Nation.  In 1858, the Yankton Sioux Native American tribe ceded (gave up) most of its eastern South Dakota land to the United States.  The early settlers that were living in the area formed a provisional government, but failed to received United States territory status.  In 1861, President-elect Abraham Lincoln’s cousin-in-law John Blair Smith Todd lobbied for territory status for the area and the U.S. Congress formally created “Dakota Territory” on 2 Mar 1861.  President Lincoln appointed his old friend and neighbor from Springfield, Illinois William Jayne the first Dakota Territory governor.  See on the map below the 1861 “Dakota Territory”:

blocks.caption: When Minnesota became a state in 1858, the leftover area between the Missouri River and the and Minnesota's western b…

blocks.text: On 20 May 1862 President Abraham Lincoln signed the “Homestead Act”.  The act granted the U. S. Federal Government more than 270 million acres of land for homesteading.  To make a homestead claim, a potential homesteader had to pay a filing fee of $10 to make a temporary claim on the land, plus $2 for commission to the land agent and an additional $6 final payment to receive an official patent on the land.  Land titles could also be purchased from the government for $1.25 per acre following six months proven residency.  See maps below showing the lands that were taken from the Native American people and was opened to settlers:

blocks.caption: On 20 May 1862 President Abraham Lincoln signed the “Homestead Act”. The act granted the U. S. Federal Government mor…

blocks.text: As new lands became available in Iowa, Minnesota and Wisconsin the 1850s, they were quickly claimed by new Euro-American settlers that initially used the land for “subsistence farming”, the growing and raising the food needed to allow the family to survive.  By 1860 most of the land in the three states was all claimed.  The American Civil War disrupted the “subsistence farming” system as men were called up to serve in the U.S. Army.  Between 1850 and 1860 new railroad lines were constructed in the three states that change the farming process from primarily one of feeding the farm family to “commodity farming” were farm products were grown and raised to be sold for a profit.  How well the settlers’ families adopted the new entrepreneurial farming opportunity resulted in some successful farms growing in size and the less successful farming families’ fathers and sons having to work on the more successful farms as farmworkers for wages.

The change from subsistence farming to commodity farming required access to money.  Growing crops and raising animals exceeding the farm family’s own minimal needs required the planting of larger areas of farm fields and raising more farm animals to sell, which each required up-front money to pay the costs of seeds, farm animals and salaries of farmworkers before money was made from selling the farm’s products.  The money problems were made worse because most new farming family had no money.  If fact most were already in debt after paying their relocation costs and $24.25 for five acres of homestead farmland and each additional acre of land cost $1.25 more.

In 1862, to help the United States fund the Civil War the U.S. Congress passed the “Legal Tender Act”, authorizing the printing of $150 million in new U.S. paper money, colored in distinctive green color on the backside, which was quickly nicknamed  the “Greenback”.  The “Legal Tender Act” was followed up by Congress creating a National Banking System, which was  authorized to issue banknotes backed by the U.S. Government.  Government Reserve Banks were next formed in the country’s largest cities holding money reserves to backup local banks established in the country’s smaller towns.  With a new banking system in place settler families were able now to borrow money to buy homestead farming property.  If the settler farmer family was unable to grow or raise extra farming products they could sell, there was little chance they could pay off their borrowed bank loan.

Based on the frequency of moving from location to location by the Winifred Eloise Coss’s families, most if not all of the families were unable to became successful commodity farmers and instead worked on the larger more successful commodity farms as farm labors and farm support craftsmen.  The Midwest farming operation went from boom to bust during the second half of the 1800s, causing more difficult for small farms and farm workers in acquiring personal wealth.  As will be seen later Benjamin Goodwater III, was one Winifred Eloise Coss’s family tree members who was able to succeed and grow his family’s wealth.

On 17 Aug 1862, the “Dakota War”, also known as the “Sioux Uprising” started along the Minnesota River, after the U. S. Federal Government failed to deliver the annuity payments that had been promised to Sioux Nation back in the 1851 in the “Treaty of Fort Laramie”.  The Santee Sioux warriors reacted by pillaging the village of New Ulm in Brown Co. Minnesota and attacked Fort Ridgely located on a hill overlooking the Minnesota River southwest of Fairfax, Minnesota.  The Sioux warriors killed over 800 German farmers, including men, women and children.  The Sioux Nation was eventually defeated on 23 Sep 1862 in the “Battle of Wood Lake” in Yellow Medicine Co., Minnesota.

Most of the Sioux warriors who took part in the fighting at “Battle of Wood Lake” escaped into the “Dakota Territory” and continue to attack settlers.  303 Santee Sioux Nation warriors finally surrendered on 26 Sep 1862 at Camp Release to the U. S. Army.  In the Military murder trials that followed and all 303 Santee Sioux warriors were found guilty of killing settlers and sentenced to death.  After a closer review of the 303 of the death sentences all but 38 were overturned by the U. S. Federal government and only the 38 of the warriors’ leaders remained guilty and were all hanged to death on 26 Dec 1862 in the Town of Mankato, Minnesota in United States’ largest mass-execution.

Over the next two years, other Sioux warriors continued to attack settlers in the Dakota Territory in a series of large raids.  The final two battles were fought in July 1864 in Dunn County, Dakota Territory and in Aug 1864, in the Dakota Territory’s western badlands on the border with Montana near the present town of Medora, North Dakota.  The U. S. Army, which was deeply involved in the “American Civil War” at the time in 1864, spared some soldiers and managed to force the surrender of most of the Dakota tribal bands by the end of Dec 1864.  The U. S. Congress next passed legislation that abolished the Native America reservations in the Mankato area, which resulted in the loss of the ten-mile-wide strip of land on each side of the Minnesota River the Sioux Nation had retained when the ceded most of their land back in 1851.  With the loss of the last southern Minnesota land held by the Sioux Nation tribe, new rich farmland became available for settlers.  The “Sioux Uprising” occurred because to the broken promises that were made to the tribe by the United States government and the horrible living conditions the Sioux people were forced accept on their previous ancestral homeland.  Their treatment was very unfair and quickened the almost total demise of the Sioux Native American people.

In May 1862, Sarah Mary "Sadie" Pearson was born in Cornforth, Durham United Kingdom to William Pearson and nineteen-year-old Mary Collingwood, who were not married at the time.  The fact that her parents were not married cause last name confusion for Sarah Mary who was listed with the last name “Pearson” in some documents and “Collingwood” in other documents and also listed with both last names or no last name in even other documents.

On 2 Jul 1864, the U. S. Congress chartered the Northern Pacific Railway Company, with the goal of connecting the Great Lakes with Puget Sound in Washington Territory on the Pacific Coast, thus opening vast new lands for farming, ranching, lumbering and mining.  The new railroad line linking Washington and Oregon territories to the rest of the country.  The chartered private railroad company was encouraged to build a new railroad line quickly by granted the railroad company 60 million acres (94,000 squire miles) of land to sell to homesteaders in exchange for railroad company building a private rail line into United States’ undeveloped western territories.

In Oct 1864, William Pearson and twenty-two-year-old Mary Collingwood married in the town of Durham, in Durham Co. Great Britain.  William was the father of Mary Collingwood’s two-year-old daughter Sarah Mary Collingwood/Pearson.

On 16 Feb 1865, Private Joseph Warren Coss enlisted in Company M in the 1st Minnesota Heavy Artillery Regiment as a thirty-eight-year-old private, with blue eyes, brown hair and being 5”9” tall.  The 1st Minnesota Heavy Artillery Regiment had organized on 1 Sep 1864 and mustered out (sent to war) on 27 Sep 1865.  The regiment was commanded by Colonel William Colville who was ordered by Major General Ulysses S. Grant to reenforce, protect and control the vital railroad junction in Chattanooga, Tennessee during the winter of 1864/65.  Chattanooga and its rail lines were critical to hold to allow supplies to flow to General William Tecumseh Sherman, who had just launched his Atlantic campaign to march across George to the port city of Savannah thus cutting the Confederacy in half.  The 1st Minnesota Heavy Artillery Regiment was composed of twelve companies, each containing 140 soldiers and officers.  The 1st Minnesota Heavy Artillery Regiment was assigned to the key heavy guns that were in place to protect Chattanooga from an expected counter-attack by General Braxton Bragg’s Confederate Army.  The Confederate Army assessed the Chattanooga defenses and never attempted an attack.  1st Minnesota Heavy Artillery Regiment, with their mission accomplished, remained in Chattanooga until the close of the “Civil War”.  Starting in June 1865 companies of the regiment began mustering out of service and by Oct 1865 the 1st Minnesota Regiment no long existed.  Private Joseph Warren Coss stayed in the regiment until the end mustering out on 27 Sep 1865.

In the 1865, “Minnesota, US, Territorial and State Census”, Joseph Warren Coss, wife Mary Ann (Goodwater) and their children including ten-year-old son Edgar Putnam “Ed” Coss were living in Concord, Dodge Co., Minnesota.  Joseph was working as a ”carpenter”.

On 15 Sep 1866, William James Raymond was born to Henry Raymond and Elizabeth (Falconer) in Port Elgin, Bruce County, Ontario, Dominion of Canada.  Port Elgin is the harbor area in the town of Saugeen, Bruce Co., Canada.

Note:  In 1867, the “British North American Act” created the Dominion of Canada, a confederation of Ontario, Québec, Nova Scotia and New Brunswick under the Crown of the United Kingdom of Great Britain.
In 1867, Henry Raymond was listed in “Directory of the County of Bruce, Canada” as a “blacksmith”.

Note:  Canada has a long history of using different forms of money.  Prior to the arrival of the first Europeans the Native American people in the area used wampum made from North Atlantic channeled whelk shells and white and purple beads made from the quahog or Western North Atlantic hard-shelled clams and animal furs as money for trading, which both continued to be used when trade with Europeans began.  During the period of the French colonization, French coins were introduced, as well as the first paper currency in the New World.  During the later British colonization period, new British coins and banknote were introduced and used as money.  In 1867, with the formation of Canadian Confederation, the Canadian dollar was established, which was linked to the value of the United States dollar.
In Jun 1869, Warren Coss died in Concord, Jefferson Co., Wisconsin.
On 17 Sep 1869, thirty-two-year-old William Pearson, husband of Mary Collingwood and the father of Sarah Mary “Sadie” Pearson, died in the town of Coxhoe, in Durham Co., United Kingdom.

In the 1870 U. S. Census, Benjamin Goodwater III and Mary Louise (Carriére) and their son were living in Frankville, Winneshiek Co., Iowa.  Benjamin was doing well farming on land valued at $3,000 with a personal estate value of $1,008.  Benjamin was listed as U. S. Citizen who could read or write.  Benjamin’s wealth had increase about three times since 1860, up from his then $1,200 valued farmland $375 personal estate wealth.

In the 1870 U. S. Census, Joseph Warren Coss, wife Mary Ann (Goodwater) and their fourteen-year-old son Edgar Putnam “Ed” Coss, twelve-year-old son William C. Coss and nine-year-old son Charles were living in Getty, Stearns Co., Minnesota.  Joseph was working as a “stone mason”.  Joseph’s property was valued at $1,000 and his estate was valued at $350 in personal wealth.  Joseph’s family was now living 180 miles north of their 1860 residence in Cherry Grove, Minnesota where they were farming ten-year earlier.

In the 1870 U. S. Census, sixty-six-year-old widow Edith Coss (Powers) was living in Palmyra, Jefferson Co., Wisconsin in the home of her thirty-year-old daughter Victoria Esther Kincaid (Coss) family.

Between 1870 and 1880, a “Dakota Boom” occurred as a surge of new settlers arrived in North Dakota.  The surge of new settlers tipped the scale against the defending Native Sioux population, which was soon widely outnumbered.

Early in 1871, Thomas Franklin, and his very pregnant wife Sarah (Cannon) and their five children left Plumstead, Kent, England and sailed to Canada and settled in Ingersoll, Oxford South Co., Ontario, Canada.
On 27 Mar 1871, daughter Anne Elizabeth Franklin and her twin, son George Warren Franklin, were born to Thomas Franklin and Sarah (Cannon) in Ingersoll, Oxford South Co., Ontario, Canada.

In the 1871 Ontario, Canada Census, thirty-eight-year-old Thomas Franklin, wife Sarah (Cannon) and their seven children, including one month old Ann Elizabeth Franklin were listed as living in Ingersoll, Oxford South Co., Ontario, Canada, 100 miles west of Toronto Ontario, Canada and 20 miles north of Lake Erie.  Thomas was listed as working as a “laborer”.

In the 1871 Ontario, Canada Census, sixty-three-year-old Angus Falconer and his wife Josephine (Morrison) and their last two children, twenty-one-year-old Janet Falconer and fifteen-year-old Hector Falconer were living in Saugeen, North Bruce Co., Ontario, Canada.  Angus was still “fishing” for a living.

In the 1871 Ontario, Canada Census, Henry Raymond, wife Elizabeth (Falconer) and their eight children, including five-year-old son William James Raymond were living in Saugeen, North Bruce Co., Ontario Canada.  Henry Raymond was working as a “Blacksmith” in Saugeen.  The family’s four oldest children are all going to school.  The three younger children including two-year-old son William James as not attending school.
In 1871, André Carriére, property appears on a map of Staint Boniface community in the Red River Colony in Manitoba Canada.  See copy of the map below:

images.caption: In 1871, André Carriére, property appears on a map of Staint Boniface community in the Red River Colony in Manitoba C…
In 1871, André Carriére, property appears on a map of Staint Boniface community in the Red River Colony in Manitoba C…

blocks.text: In the 1871 English Census, twenty-five-year-old widow Mary Pearson was listed as the head of the household and working as a “dress maker” in Cornforth, Durham, Great Britain.  Eight-year-old daughter Sarah Mary “Sadie” Pearson and her five-year-old sister Elizabeth, were both living with their mother and attending school.

In 1871, the Northern Pacific Railway Company reach the Dakota Territory, connecting the area by rail to the central and eastern portions of the country.
