In early Jun 1872, the Northern Pacific Railway reached the town of Fargo in the Dakota Territory.  The next year the railroad reach, the town of Edwinton, the present-day city of Bismarck, North Dakota.
In 1872, individual counties were established in Dakota Territory.  On 4 Jan 1873, Bottineau County was organized, named for Pierre Bottineau an early Minnesota frontiersman.
On 28 Nov 1875, forty-year-old wife Elizabeth (Falconer) of Henry Raymond died in Port Elgin, Ontario, Canada.

In the 1875, “Minnesota, US, Territorial and State Census”, Joseph Warren Coss, wife Mary Ann (Goodwater) and their four children including twenty-one-year-old Edgar Putnam “Ed” Coss were listed as living in Little Sauk, Todd Co., Minnesota.
Note:  On 25/26 Jun 1876, the “Battle of the Little Bighorn” or “Custer’s Last Stand” took place along the Little Bighorn River in southeaster Montana Territory.

In 1876, Joseph Warren Coss, wife Mary Ann (Goodwater) and their children, including their oldest son Edgar Putnam “Ed” Coss left Getty, Stearns Co., Minnesota and traveled 230 miles north up to Grand Forks, in the Dakota Territory.  Joseph and Mary Ann opened the first hotel in the frontier town of Grand Forks, where Native Americans were living nearby in tepees and hunting American Bisons.

Note:  Grand Forks is the oldest major city in the Dakotas.  Prior to French explorers’ arrivals, the area where the city now sits (at the forks of the Red River and the Red Lake River) was an important meeting and trading point for Native American tribes in the area.  The early French explorers and fur-trappers called the area "Les Grandes Fourches" (meaning "The Grand Forks").  By the 1740s, Grand Forks was an important rendezvous point for French fur trappers, which continued until the “French and Indian War” gave the area to Britain.  In 1800, British fur trader Alexander Henry, an agent for the North West Company of Montreal, located a temporary camp at Grand Forks and by 1808 a permanent British post was established.  The United States acquired the whole Dakota territory from Great Britain in the “Treaty of 1818”.  See below an early photo of Grand Forks town business center:

blocks.caption: Note: Grand Forks is the oldest major city in the Dakotas. Prior to French explorers’ arrivals, the area where the ci…

blocks.text: On 22 Feb 1877 the “Le Métis” newspaper reported on the 56th Golden Wedding anniversary of Mr. and Mrs. Landry, a couple living in Saint-Boniface, Manitoba Canada.  After the wedding ceremony the attendees went over to Daniel Carriére’s house who was the son-in-law of the celebrating married couple where an excellent breakfast was served.  Daniel Carriére was son of André Carriére and Angelique Dion.  Placide Carriére, the son of Daniel Carriére, and grandson of the married couple read to his grandparents an address which expressed congratulations and best wishes for their continued happiness.  Fifteen letters from grandchildren were also read.  Several speeches were spoken in several different languages: French, English, Cree, German, Italian, Salteaux and Montagnais during the event.
In 1877, Thomas Franklin and his wife Sarah (Cannon) attend the wedding of their son William T. Franklin to Euphemia Gray in Lambton Co., Ontario, Canada.

On 4 Aug 1877, Henry Raymond and his wife Elizabeth (Falconer) were present in Port Elgin, Bruce Co., Ontario Canada for the marriage of their seventeen-year-old daughter Josephine Raymond to twenty-four-year-old Alexander John Vandrick.  The fact that Elizabeth (Falconer) is listed on the Ontario Canada Marriage document conflicts with Elizabeth’s death and grave headstone, which list her death back in 1875.
In 1877, fifteen-year-old daughter Sarah Mary Collinwood probably with her thirty-four-year-old mother Mary Collingwood and Mary’s new husband Mr. Kenmir left England and sailed to America.
On 23 1878, thirty-nine-year-old Mary Ann (Goodwater), wife of Joseph Warren Coss died in Grand Forks, Grand Forks Co., Dakota Territory.

In 1879, seventeen-year-old  Sarah Collingwood applied for and became a naturalized United States citizen, based on information recorded in the later 1900, 1910 and 1930 U.S. Census records for her and her later husband Edgar P Coss.  The three U.S. Census record list 1879 as the year Sarah Collingwood became a naturalized United States Citizen.  The census record also lists England as the country of Sara’ birth and the country where Sara’s parents were born.  No record has been found for when Sarah’s widow mother Mary Collingwood emigrating from England to the United States.

On 14 Apr 1880, twenty-four-year-old Edgar Putnam “Ed” Coss and seventeen-year-old Sarah Collingwood were married in Grand Forks, Grand Forks Co., Dakota Territory.  At the time, Edgar was driving the stagecoach 15-miles between the city of Grand Forks and Fisher’s Landing east in Minnesota.  Fisher’s Landing was a boat landing on the Red Lake River that settlers used to arrive in the area by boat.  Edgar Putnam Coss had met Sarah Mary Collingwood when her mother Mary Kenmir (Collingwood) and her second husband Mr. Kenmir were traveling on Edgar’s stagecoach going from Fisher’s Landing over to Grand Forks on their way up to Canada.  Sarah and her mother Mary Kenmir (Collingwood) and step-father husband stayed at Edgar’s parents, Joseph Warren Coss and Edith (Powers)’s, hotel in Grand Forks.  In discussions at the hotel Mary and her husband were talked into staying in Grand Forks instead of going on up to Canada allowing Sarah to also stay in Grand Forks giving her the opportunity to get to know and later marry Edgar Coss.

Note:  The information for the story of the events that occurred when Edgar Putnam “Ed” Coss and Sarah Mary Collingwood met on the stage to Grand Forks comes from the information Lorraine Powers Geiger (Coss) wrote in her family history.  Lorraine was unclear about who Sara Mary’s parents were, but she does refer to both of them which implies there were two parents indicating Mary Collingwood had remarried after the death of her first husband William Pearson.  No birth record has been found for Sarah Mary Collingwood and English and United States census have been used to determine Sarah’s birth and emigration date.

In the 1880 U.S. Census, twenty-two-year-old Edgar Putnam “Ed” Coss and eighteen-year-old wife Sarah were living in Grand Forks, Grand Forks Co., Dakota Territory.  Edgar was now farming in the farmland out of the City of Grand Forks.

In the 1880 U.S. Census, seventy-nine-year-old widow Edith Coss (Powers) was living in Palmyra, Jefferson Co., Wisconsin in the home of her son Henry Coss and his family.  Edith was bed written and had lost her sight.  Edith lived seven years longer dying in 1887.

No 1880 U.S .Census record had been found for fifty-two-year-old Joseph Warren Coss.  With the death of his thirty-nine-year-old wife Mary Ann (Goodwater) three years earlier, Joseph may have been living with one of his married children family.

In the 1881 Ontario Canada Census, fifteen-year-old William James Raymond was living the home of Angus Currie and Elizabeth Currie (Raymond) in Port Elgin, North Bruce Co., Ontario, Canada.  Elizabeth Currie (Raymond) was William Raymond older married sister.  Living with William Raymond in the Currie home was his seventeen-year-old older sister Mary Raymond, his twelve-year-old younger brother Alex Raymond and his six-year-old younger sister Evi Raymond.  The Angus Currie was working as a carpenter in Port Elgin.  William Raymond’s forty-year-old mother Elizabeth (Falconer) had died six years earlier on 28 Nov 1875, the same year her daughter Evi Raymond was born.  Mother Elizabeth (Falconer) had also lost two daughters within a year of their birth prior to the birth of her last daughter Evi.  Fifty-two-year-old Henry Raymond, the father of all the Raymond children was not with his children and has not been found in an 1881 Canada census records.

In the 1881 Ontario Canada Census, seventy-three-year-old Angus Falconer, his wife seventy-year-old Josephine Falconer (Morison) and their sixteen-year-old granddaughter Rebecca Falconer were living in Saugeen, North Bruce Co., Ontario, Canada.  Angus Falconer was still “fishing” for a living, which he had been doing for more than thirty years.  Angus and Josephine were living next door to their son John Falconer and his wife and their children, who were the parents of granddaughter Rebecca Falconer.

Sometime between the time the 1881 Ontario Canada Census was taken (The day and month when the census was conducted has been found on census document) and 17 Dec 1881 when Josephine Falconer (Morison) died in Hoople, Walsh Co, North Dakota, seventy-three-year-old Angus Falconer, his wife seventy-year-old Josephine Falconer (Morison) left Saugeen, North Bruce Co., Ontario, Canada and traveled 1,200 miles to Crystal, Pembina Co., North Dakota.  There is a good chance Angus and Josephine took their twelve-year-old grandson William James Raymond with them to help on the long trip.  The reason for their leaving Canada, may have been concern over getting small pox.  Young grandson William was living with his older sister Elizabeth Currie (Raymond) and her husband Angus Currie’s family.  William Raymond’s forty-year-old mother Elizabeth Raymond (Falconer) had died back in 1875, when William was six-year-old.  William’s father Henry Raymond was gone having left William with his older sister and her husband Angus Currie to raise.  See a photo of Angus Falconer, Josephine Falconer (Morison) below:

blocks.caption: Sometime between the time the 1881 Ontario Canada Census was taken (The day and month when the census was conducted h…

blocks.text: Note:  By the end of the 1700s inoculation against smallpox had become a popular preventive process in Europe.  Yet, epidemics of smallpox continued to affect the people in Canada and Montréal City was hit particularly hard in the 1880s . The epidemic of 1885 was especially dramatic; its scale persuaded municipal authorities to make smallpox vaccination mandatory, but medical opinion was divided into pro and anti-vaccination camps, with the latter accusing the former of spreading the deadly disease.  Many in Canada refused to be vaccinated.  In Sep 1885, a riot broke out in the Montréal City and some protesters tore down pro-vaccination posters and ransacked the homes of the official medical vaccinators, attacked the city hall, the city’s pharmacies stores and the homes of municipal government magistrates.  The extent of the smallpox epidemic took 3,164 lives with 2,117 of them being children.  The fear of reoccurring epidemics in Canada persuaded many Canadian families to move south into the United States where vaccinations were commonly given to everyone.

In the 1881 Ontario, Canada Census, Thomas Franklin, wife Sarah (Cannon) and their six children, including ten-year-old Anne Elizabeth “Hannah” Franklin and her twin brother George Franklin were living in Petrolia, Lambton Co., Ontario, Canada.  Thomas was working as a “laborer” in Petrolia.  Back in 1858, the small town of Petrolia in southwestern Province of Ontario Canada was the place where the oil industry started in North America.  Oil was discovered when James Williams struck a large oil deposit in Oil Springs while digging a shallow water well.

The unincorporated farm community of Arvilla in the North Dakota Territory was names after Arvilla Estella Hersey the wife of Dudley Hersey.  The Hersey family made a fortune in the lumber milling, first in Maine and later in Minnesota.  In 1881, Dudley Hersey bought 4 sections (2,560 acres) of farmland west of the town of Grand Forks in the Dakota Territory and built an elegant “mansion house”, suitable for a rich gentleman farmer.  The house had 42 rooms for visiting guests and hot and cold running water in the era of hand-pumps.  The house was fully protected from mosquitoes by netting.  Adjacent to the house was a billiards-hall; and situated nearby were “archery and tennis grounds, swings, tents and quite retreats protected by shade canopies” of top-notch quality.  There were 22 buildings in all on the Hersey farm, which included a separate dormitory and dining hall for the farm’s workers, wagon, carriage and machinery storage halls; five horse barns, a cow barn, pig barn, chicken coop, ice house, and a slaughterhouse.  The Hersey Farm raised thoroughbred Clydesdale workhorses and high-class Jersey milk cows.  There were also 100 hogs; and several hundred Plymouth Rock hens, all of prime stock.  Dudley Hersey also established a townsite near his large farm and names it after  his wife Arvilla.  Dudley Hersey died in 1900 and his wife Arvilla moved away and the magnificence farm quickly faded away.

Since Arvilla did not exist before Dudley Hersey bought the land in 1881, there is a strong possibility sixteen-year-old William James Raymond went and lived in Arvilla, work for Mr. Dudley in the construction of his large home mansion, farm buildings and also probably worked on the new farm as a farmhand after everything was finished .  The was little else to do in Arvilla.  See a photo of the “mansion house” below:

blocks.text: On 30 May 1884, Angus Falconer became a United States citizen in Grand Forks, Dakota Territory.

In the 1885, North Dakota U.S. Territorial and State Censuses, thirty-year-old Edgar Coss, twenty-three-year-old wife Sarah Coss (Pearson) and their three children were living in Grand Forks, Grand Forks Co., Dakota Territory.  Edgar Coss was farming for a living.  Living next to Edgar Coss’s family was his fifty-four-year-old widower father Joseph Warren Coss, who was working as a “carpenter”.  Also living with Joseph was thirteen-year-old sister Mamie Coss and a twenty-one-year-old boarder Elizabeth Pearson from England.
In 1885 Ontario, Canada Voter Lists, fifty-six-year-old Henry Raymond was living in the Houghton Township in Ontario.
On 10 Mar 1889, William James Raymond and Anne Elizabeth “Annie” Franklin were married in Arvilla, Grand Forks Co., Dakota Territory.
On 27 Feb 1889, eighty-one-year-old Angus Falconer died in Crystal, Pembina Co., North Dakota.  The small town of Crystal was located 55 miles north of Arvilla in North Dakota.
Note:  On 2 Nov 1889, North Dakota and South Dakota became the 39th and 40th States in the United States.
In the 1889-1890 Grand Forks, North Dakota Directories, Edgar P. Coss was listed as living at 726 S 4th in Grand Forks,  Edgar was working as a “Teamster”.

Note:  Back on 14 Jul 1862, the U.S. Congress passed the “An Act to grant Pensions “.  The act awarded pensions to injured soldiers in the Civil War and to surviving widows and minor children of soldiers killed in the war.  In 1890 the “Disability Act of 1890” was passed that removed the restriction of war-related injuries, allowing pensions to be awarded to solders solely on time of service in the Civil War.
On 15 Mar 1890, Joseph Warren Coss, who served in the 1st Regiment of the Minnesota Heavy Artillery, filed for his Civil War pension in North Dakota.
In the 1890, “ Special Schedule for Surviving Soldiers, Sailors, and Marines, and Widows”, Joseph Warren Coss was listed as serving as a private in the 1st Minnesota Regiment.
On 21 Jul 1890, Charles Witecomb Coss was born to Edgar Putnam “Ed” Coss and Sara Mary “Sadie”(Collingwood/Pearson) in Grand Forks, Grand Forks Co., North Dakota.
Note:  Edgar Putnam “Ed” Coss and Sara Mary “Sadie”(Collingwood/Pearson) named four of their thirteen children with middle names of older relatives: Pearson, Collingwood, Powers, Warren.

Note:  The United States census of 1890 was taken beginning June 2, 1890, but most of the 1890 census materials were destroyed in 1921 when a building caught fire and in the subsequent disposal of the remaining damaged records.
On 7 Oct 1891, Josephine (Morison), wife of Angus Falconer died in Hoople, Walsh Co., North Dakota.

On 12 Feb 1898, Sarah (Cannon), wife of Thomas Franklin and mother of Anne Elizabeth “Annie” Franklin died in Grafton, Walsh Co., North Dakota.  The Grafton Township was located 43 miles north of Arvilla and 19 miles southeast of Hoople in the farmlands of North Dakota.
On 2 Aug 1900, Ethel Cannon Raymond was born to William James Raymond and Anne Elizabeth “Annie” (Franklin) in Arvilla, Grand Forks Co., North Dakota.

In the 1900 U. S. Census, William James Raymond, wife Anne Elizabeth (Franklin) and their five children, including nine-month-old daughter Ethel Canon Raymond were living in Arvilla, Grand Forks Co., North Dakota.  William Raymond was working as a “blacksmith”.

In the 1900 U. S. Census, Joseph Warren Coss was living with his second new wife Margaret (Thompson) in Grand Forks, North Dakota.  Joseph and Margaret had married the year before in Grand Forks.  Joseph’s first wife Mary Ann Goodwater had died twenty-two years earlier back on 23 Sep 1878 in Grand Forks.  See a map below showing of the small farm towns in eastern North Dakota:

blocks.caption: In the 1900 U. S. Census, Joseph Warren Coss was living with his second new wife Margaret (Thompson) in Grand Forks,…

blocks.text: In the 1900 U. S. Census, forty-three-year-old Edgar P. Coss, thirty-eight-year-old wife Sarah M. Coss (Pearson) and their six sons; William P., Edgar P. Jr, Joseph W., Charles W., Clifford and Clarence G. and their two daughters Gertrude and Adelaide were living at 726 South Fourth Street, Grand Forks, North Dakota.  Edgar Coss was working as a “Teamster” (someone of drives horse drawn wagons).  See photos on the next page of Edgar Putnam and Sarah Mary “Sadie” (Pearson/Collingwood):

images.caption: In the 1900 U. S. Census, forty-three-year-old Edgar P. Coss, thirty-eight-year-old wife Sarah M. Coss (Pearson) and…
In the 1900 U. S. Census, forty-three-year-old Edgar P. Coss, thirty-eight-year-old wife Sarah M. Coss (Pearson) and…

blocks.text: On 1 Feb 1906, seventy-six-year-old Henry Raymond died in Saugeen Shores, Bruce Co., Ontario, Canada.  Back in 1881, Henry had stayed behind when his son William James Raymond left with his grandparents Angus Falconer and Josephine Falconer (Morison) and move to Arvilla, Grand Forks Co., North Dakota.

On 11 Feb 1908, Joseph Warren Coss died in the morning of apoplexy (sudden death that begin with a sudden loss of consciousness, which can occur in a heart attack or stroke) in Grand Forks, North Dakota at his sister Victoria Kincaid’s home.  Joseph was buried back in the Hillside Cemetery in Palmyra, Jefferson Co., Wisconsin, with a Union soldier’s tombstone.  See below a photo of younger brother Henry Coss on the left and Joseph Warren Coss on the right

blocks.caption: On 11 Feb 1908, Joseph Warren Coss died in the morning of apoplexy (sudden death that begin with a sudden loss of con…

blocks.text: In the 1910 U. S. Census, fifty-five-year-old Edgar Putman Coss, forty-eight-year-old wife Sarah Mary (Collingwood/Pearson) and their sons Clifford and Clarence, daughters Gertrude, Loretta and Lorine were living at 229 Boulevard Ave., Grand Forks, North Dakota.  Sons twenty-year-old Joseph Warren and eighteen-year-old Charles Whitcomb are missing in the census record.  Edgar Coss was working as a “street laborer”.  Sarah Mary and her parents were listed as being born in England and Sarah Mary becoming a Naturalized U.S. Citizen in 1878.  Living next door was twenty-nine-year-old William P Coss and his wife Agusta and two-year-old daughter Wilma.  In the next house was fifty-one-year-old single William Worth Coss, the younger brother of Edgar Coss.

In the 1910 U. S. Census, William Raymond, wife Anne Elizabeth (Franklin) and their four children, including ten-year-old daughter Ethel Cannon Raymond were living in Arvilla, Grand Forks Co., North Dakota.  William Raymond was farming for a living.

In the 1915 North Dakota, U. S. Territorial and State Censuses, Edgar Putman Coss, wife Sarah Mary (Pearson) and their sons Joseph W., Charles W., Clifford H. and Clarence G. and daughters Gertrude and Lorine P., were living, Grand Forks, North Dakota.  Sons Joseph Warren and Charles Whitcomb, who were missing in the 1910 UC Census were back with the family.

On 3 Oct 1916, twenty-six-year-old Charles Witecomb Coss and Josephine Olson were married in Grand Forks, Grand Forks Co., North Dakota.  Little is known about twenty-two Josephine Olson beyond what is in the preserved their Marriage License.  Charles and Josephine where together just two months before Charles joined the Canadian Expeditionary Force in World War I.  Josephine died before Jul 1920.

On 9 Dec 1916, Charles Witecomb Coss enlisted in the Canadian Expeditionary Force in Winnipeg, Manitoba, Canada.  Charles served in the 78th Battalion two and a half years, until being discharged in Winnipeg, Manitoba, Canada on 12 Jun 1919.  The fighting in the war had ended at 11am, 11 Nov 1918 with the signing of an armistice, but the official ending of the war did not occurre until 28 Jun 1919 with the signing of the “Treaty of Versailles”.

Note:  The 78th Battalion (Bn) was a Canadian infantry Battalion, maned with recruits from Winnipeg, Manitoba.  During the 9 thru 12 Apr 1917,attack on Vimy Ridge in France the 78th Bn was a follow-up battalion to the 38th, 72nd, and 73rd Battalions and was given the mission of exploiting the  gains made in the initial attack of the other three battalions.  Unfortunately, an intact German trench of soldiers held up to the attack and the 78th was hit by a German counterattack.  The78th Battalion lost 75 men killed, 261 wounded and 159 missing in action.  See on the next page photo a tank being used by the 2nd Canadian Division and Machine gunner operation during the fighting of the Viny Ridge battle:

blocks.text: On 21 Jul 1920, thirty-year-old widower Charles Witecomb Coss and twenty-one-year-old Ethel Cannon Raymond were married in Arvilla, Grand Forks Co., North Dakota.

In the 1920 U. S. Census, Edgar Putnam Coss, wife Sadie Coss (Pearson) and sons, Charles, Clifford and Clarence; daughters Lorraine, Loretta and granddaughter Virgil were living Grand Forks, Grand Forks Co., North Dakota.  Edgar Coss was working as a “excavator contractor” digging basements and foundations for buildings; sons Clarence and Charles were working as Grand Forks “firemen” and Clifford was a “laborer”.  See below photos of Edgar Putnam Coss  and Sara Mary “Sadie” Coss (Pearson):

images.caption: In the 1920 U. S. Census, Edgar Putnam Coss, wife Sadie Coss (Pearson) and sons, Charles, Clifford and Clarence; daug…
In the 1920 U. S. Census, Edgar Putnam Coss, wife Sadie Coss (Pearson) and sons, Charles, Clifford and Clarence; daug…

blocks.text: In the 1920 U. S. Census, William Raymond, wife Anne Elizabeth (Franklin) and their twenty-five-year-old son Eddie Raymond were living on Halsey Street in Arvilla, Grand Forks Co., North Dakota.  William was working as a “Grain Buyer” who stored harvested grain in grain elevators for later sale.

On 30 Aug 1922, Winifred “Wini” Eloise Coss was born to Charles Whitcomb Coss and Ethel Cannon (Raymond) in Arvilla, Grand Forks Co., North Dakota. Charles Whitcomb Coss was working as a fireman in Grand Forks.  See below a photo of Charles Whitcomb Coss:

blocks.caption: On 30 Aug 1922, Winifred “Wini” Eloise Coss was born to Charles Whitcomb Coss and Ethel Cannon (Raymond) in Arvilla,…

blocks.text: On 1 Apr 1925 in the North Dakota U.S. Territorial and State Censuses, Charles Witecomb Coss, wife Ethel Cannon Coss (Raymond) and their three-year-old daughter Winifred Eloise Coss had left Arvilla were now living in Grand Forks, Grand Forks Co., North Dakota.

In the 1925, North Dakota, U.S. Territorial and State Censuses, sixty-nine-year-old Edgar P. Coss, sixty-three-year-old wife Sarah Mary (Collingwood/Pearson), twenty-seven-year-old son Clarence Grover Coss and his twenty-five-year-old wife Ann Coss were living in a boarding house with twenty-one other residents in Grand Forks, Grand Forks Co., North Dakota.

In the 1925, North Dakota, U.S. Territorial and State Censuses, fifty-six-year-old William James Raymond and his fifty-four-old wife Anne Elizabeth (Franklin) and thirty-two-year-old son Edward Raymond were living in the Arvilla township in Grand Forks Co., North Dakota.

In 1927 Henry D. Geiger helped his two sons Winfield “Happy” Geiger and Grant G. Geiger with an early inheritance by setting the two brothers up as owners of the Geiger Motor & Oil business in Kenmare.  Later 1936 the business was expanded by adding a car dealership and car garage selling Plymouth cars.

On 18 Dec 1927, Charles Witecomb Coss’ twenty-eight-year-old wife Ethel Cannon Coss (Raymond) died from a stomach illness in the Deaconess Hospital in Arvilla, Grand Forks Co., North Dakota.  Charles Witecomb Coss was left to raise his three children five-year-old daughter Winifred Eloise Coss, three-year-old son Charles Witecomb Coss Jr., and four-month-old son Clarence Pearson Coss.  See photos below of Ethel Cannon Coss (Raymond) and her five-year-old daughter Winifred Eloise Coss and three-year-old Charles Whitcomb Coss Jr. in front of the Coss family home in Arvilla and a second photo of young Wini:

images.caption: On 18 Dec 1927, Charles Witecomb Coss’ twenty-eight-year-old wife Ethel Cannon Coss (Raymond) died from a stomach ill…
On 18 Dec 1927, Charles Witecomb Coss’ twenty-eight-year-old wife Ethel Cannon Coss (Raymond) died from a stomach ill…

blocks.text: In 1928 in the (beta version) of the U.S. City Directories, Charles W. Coss and his wife Ethel Coss (Raymond) were listed as living at 309 Division Ave., Grand Forks, North Dakota.  Charles was working as a “Driver” at the Grand Forks city Fire Department.  Ethel had died back in 18 Dec 1927 and the city directory must not have been updated.
In 1928 in the (beta version) of the U.S. City Directories, listed Edgar P. Coss and his wife Sarah M. Coss (Pearson) were living 31 Euclid Ave, Grand Forks, North Dakota.

In 1928, thirty-eight-year-old Charles Witecomb Coss gave his five-year-old daughter Winifred Eloise Coss to his twenty-five-year-old sister Lorraine Powers Geiger (Coss) and her husband Winfield “Happy” Geiger who were living 243 miles west in Kenmare, Ward Co. North Dakota to raise.  At the time Winfield and Lorraine had a two-year-old daughter Delores Loretta Geiger.  Lorraine was pregnant with her son Robert Duane Geiger, who was born on 6 Jan 1929 and died a month later.  Fortunately, Lorraine and Winfield welcome young Winifred “Winni” who had lost her mother and now was separated from her family and Winni soon became a full member of the growing loving Geiger family.

sections.title: Great Depression and Dust Bowl
sections.teaser: On 24 Oct 1929, the American Wall Street stock market crashed, as nervous investors sold their stocks and the U.S. and World economy slowed and prices of con…

blocks.text: On 24 Oct 1929, the American Wall Street stock market crashed, as nervous investors sold their stocks and the U.S. and World economy slowed and prices of consumer goods fell.  At the same time a major drought occurred in the western half of the United States causing farm crops to fail compounding the economic and banking problems in the country.  North Dakota farmers were especially hit hard by the Depression and prolong drought.  The years 1934 and 1936 were especially dry.  Crops and grasses did not grow.  Hungry cows lost weight and thousands had to be sold and many died of starvation.  Scorching dry winds picked up particles of soil and created “snowdrifts” made of dirt.  The black clouds also appeared in the sky, which were not rain clouds, but instead were of millions and millions of grasshoppers that swarmed all over the land eating any bit of vegetation (plant life) that lay in their path.  Most North Dakota farmers had obtained a mortgage from a bank in order the buy the farmland they were living and farming on.  Thousands of North Dakota farmers lost their farms because they could not grow crops to sell to pay the banks on the loans they had taken on.  During the 1930s, over 121,000 people moved out of the state and 12,308 farms were lost in North Dakota.

As a rural farming state, North Dakota suffered greatly when the prices received for farm produce declined.  The search for a solution brought about different movements in the 1920s and 1930s.  Farmers banded together to market their produce and reduce the costs of farming.  A “North Dakota Farmers Union” was formed and quickly spread across the state’s prairies.  The local Farmers Unions, built grain elevators and organized petroleum cooperatives that served the needs of an increasingly mechanized rural farming economy.  In 1932, the cooperative group helped form a militant defense organization the “Farmers Holiday Organization”, to take direct action against low commodity prices and farm foreclosures.

The militancy in rural North Dakota farm areas quickly spread into state politics.  A revitalized Nonpartisan League (NPL) emerged in 1932, electing the colorful populist William Langer as the state’s Governor.  William Langer took bold actions when he assumed his Governor’s office in 1933; he slashed state spending, imposed moratoriums on mortgage foreclosure sales, and embargoed shipment of grain from the state.  However, his disregard of law brought Federal investigations, and in 1934 he was convicted of campaign law violations and removed from office; Lt. Governor Ole Olson finished the governor term.  That same year, a divided NPL lost the Governor's election to Democrat Thomas Moodie, who assumed office in 1935, but was quickly disqualified when an investigation discovered that he did not meet state residency requirements.  Lt. Governor Walter Welford succeeded ousted Thomas Moodie, becoming the fourth Governor in seven months.

Despite economic problems, crop failures, dust storms, and weather extremes, North Dakota visibly modernized during the 1930s.  Government spending increase on projects that built a new skyscraper state capitol building, improved the state’s highways and roads, created state parks, and expanded city services throughout the state.

The Great Depression of the 1930s both slowed progress and sped change.  Heavy farm debt and low farm commodity prices caused farm foreclosures and bank failures.  Successful farmers who had better handle their financial situation, were able enlarged their farm holdings by buying foreclosed farm property.  The rural population in the state diminished while the state’s cities grew.  Thousands of North Dakotans lost their farms and were forced either moved into the cities and towns or out of the state altogether.  It has been estimated that over 70% of the state's people required one form of public assistance during the period.

Finally in the 1940s more favorable weather returned and farm crop yields were profitable again.  With more commodities to sell, farmers benefitted even more from the higher prices stimulated by United States entry into World War II.  Within a span of five years, the farm debt in the state dropped markedly and by the end of the war in 1945 North Dakota residents had accumulated the largest per capita bank deposits in the nation.

In the 1930 U. S. Census, Edgar P Coss and his wife Sarah Coss (Pearson) were living on 31 Euclid South Avenue in Grand Forks, Grand Forks Co., North Dakota.  Living with Edgar and Sarah were their thirty-seven-year-old widower son Charles Witecomb Coss and his five-year-old son Charles Witecomb Coss Jr. and two-year-old son Clarence Coss.  Also living with Edgar and Sarah was their other thirty-two-year-old single son Clarence Pearson Coss.  Sons Charles and Clarence were both working as Grand Forks firemen.  Seventy-five-year-old Edgar P Coss was unemployed.  Living in the house next to Edgar’s house was the Ina Pearson and her twelve-year-old daughter Pauline Pearson with seventy-nine-year-old Carrie Amble and a sixty-two-year-old “roomer”’.  The spelling of the last name “Pearson” indicates a family relationship existed, but one has not been found.  Today the home address of Edgar Putman Coss’ home on 31 Euclid Avenue is located in the middle of a nature area on the flood plain of the Red River in the city of Grand Forks on the border with Minnesota.

In the 1930 U. S. Census William Raymond, wife Anne Elizabeth (Franklin) and their thirty-eight-year-old son Eddie Raymond were living in Arvilla, Grand Forks Co., North Dakota.  Sixty-one-year-old William was no longer working and he and wife Anne were living with their son Eddie who was working as a “labor”.  Two other unrelated men were also living in the house as “roomers” and were both working as “labors”.  William Raymond no longer was a “Grain Buyer” who stored harvested grain in grain elevators for later sale.  The Great Depression of 1929, had major impacts on the price of farm grain products in North Dakota and many private grain elevators were put out of business.

In the 1930 U. S. Census, Henry D. Geiger and his family were living in the town of Cavalier, Pembina Co. North Dakota and farming on land Henry owned out in the nearby farmland.  Henry and his family were living in Henry built that he ordered through the Sears’ Catalog, that was valued at $6,000.  People living in the 1930s in rural areas used the mail order “Sear Catalog” the way people today use “Amazon” on the Internet to order everything they needed that was not in their local stores.  See on the next page a photo of the mail order house Henry built in Cavalier and a photo of a page in the Sears Catalog for a house.

In the 1930 U. S. Census, Henry D. Geiger and his family were living in the town of Cavalier, Pembina Co. North Dakot…

blocks.text: In the 1930 U. S. Census, Winfield Geiger, wife Lorraine (Coss) and their four-year-old daughter Delores Geiger and Lorraine’s seven-year-old nice Winifred Eloise Coss, Lorraine’s brother Charles Witecomb Coss’ daughter and two other young women “roomers” ages twenty-five and twenty-three were living at 219 First Avenue East, Kenmare, Ward Co., North Dakota.  Winfield Geiger owned a Service Station (gas station) in Kenmare and lived in a house Winfield owned worth $3,400.  Today the home at the address no longer exists and has been replace railroad tracks that run on the shore of Middle Des Lacs Lake in Kenmare.

Note:  The Great Depression affected almost everyone living in North Dakota.  The practice of taking in roomers (renters) in one’s home help cover one’s living costs was common and help both the renter and the roomer survive the desperate times.

On 11 Mar 1932, sixty-two-year-old William James Raymond died in Arvilla, Grand Forks Co., North Dakota.  The death certificate for William James Raymond was completed by his wife Anne Elizabeth (Franklin) who did not know the names of Williams’ parents.  Anne Elizabeth may not have been well enough to recall their names for two days later sixty-year-old Anne Elizabeth (Franklin) also died in Arvilla.

In the 1940 U. S. Census, Winfield “Happy” Geiger, wife Lorraine (Coss) and their four children and their seventeen-year-old niece Winifred Eloise Coss were living in a house valued at $3,000 Kenmare, Ward Co., North Dakota.  Seventeen-year-old niece Winifred was a junior in high school.  Winfield Geiger owned a gas service station and Pontiac car dealership in Kenmare.

In the 1940 U.S. Census forty-two-year-old Clarence Coss was listed as the head of the house hold at 31 Euclid Street, Grand Forks in Grand Forts Co., North Dakota.  Living with Clearance was his seventy-eight-year-old widow mother Sarah M. Coss (Collingwood) and his forty-eight-year-old older brother Charles Coss and his two sons sixteen-year-old Charles Jr. and twelve-year-old son Clarence P. Coss.  The Coss brothers Clarence and Charles were working a Grand Forks city fireman.  Clarence was earning $1,580 as the Foreman of the fire department and Charles was earning $600 as a fireman.  See photo below of Charles Coss with horses used in Grand Forks Fire Department.

blocks.text: On 27 Apr 1942, fifty-one-year-old Charles Witecomb Coss Sr., received his U. S. World War II Draft Card.  Charles was described as 5’10” tall, 170 lbs. with gray hair and blue eyes, in  Grand Forks, Grand Forks Co., North Dakota.

In the 1950 U. S. Census, Morris Rankin Batie, wife Winifred Eloise (Coss) and six-year-old son Charles Batie and four-year-old daughter Gloria Batie were living at 22th Ave., Sacramento, California.  Morris was working as an Air Traffic Controller at the Sacramento Metropolitan Airport.

In the 1950 U. S. Census, fifty-two-year-old Clarence Coss was living at apartment #31 on Ewslive St in Grand Forks, Grand Forks Co., North Dakota.  Clarence’s fifty-nine-year-old older brother Charles Witecomb Coss was living with his younger brother as a lodger.  Clarence was working 72 hours a week as a fireman and Charles was working 20 hours a week as a gardener.
See the Story of Morris Rankin Batie’s Family for information on Winifred Eloise Coss and Morris Rankin Batie family history.  See below photo of Morris and Wini:
